clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old athlete 4 weeks post-knee surgery. Knee pain and swelling at surgical site. Limited knee motion and functional activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to return to sport. Walking with limp. Kneeling and squatting limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Knee ligament tear from skiing injury. Reconstruction with autograft hamstring. Early post-operative phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee flexion 80 degrees, extension lag 5-10 degrees. Quadriceps strength 3+/5. Hamstring strength weak 3/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays show graft positioning adequate. Tunnel placement appropriate. MRI shows intact graft.

puzzles.explanation: Post-ACL Reconstruction shows early post-operative period (4 weeks) with expected swelling, weakness, and need for intensive neuromuscular rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young adult with facial droop following Lyme disease diagnosis in endemic area.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Loss of facial symmetry, difficulty with oral hygiene. Water falls from affected side during drinking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent tick bite and erythema migrans rash. Positive Lyme serology. Facial paralysis developed 2 weeks after rash.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Unilateral lower facial weakness greater than upper, incomplete eye closure. Tear dysfunction, hyperacusis possible.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows denervation, imaging and CSF unremarkable. Nerve biopsy shows Borrelia on culture.

puzzles.explanation: Lyme disease-associated facial nerve palsy with asymmetric facial involvement and confirmed Borrelia infection in CSF.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged woman with intermittent hip pain after returning to recreational dancing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain during hip adduction and internal rotation; worse with repetitive flexion movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong history of hip tightness; increased activity tolerance recently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion passive range of motion 100 degrees with pain; positive impingement sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows labral fraying without full-thickness tear; acetabular version abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: FAI can develop insidiously with activity increase. Labral pathology often accompanies bony impingement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports ear pain, jaw stiffness, and headaches; symptoms worse in morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain provoked by talking and chewing; morning symptoms suggest nocturnal grinding.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of bruxism and stress; associated neck tension and migraine-type headaches.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Jaw deviation on opening, severe masticatory muscle tenderness, tooth wear visible, limited mouth opening.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal disc position but joint surface changes, EMG shows nocturnal muscle hyperactivity. Tooth wear documented.

puzzles.explanation: TMJ disorder presents with myogenous pain from muscle dysfunction and bruxism with morning symptoms and cervical association.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young female athlete reports amenorrhea, decreased performance, and multiple stress fractures.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant reduction in body weight and compulsive exercise with restricted eating pattern.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Began with sports-related weight loss goals, progressed to disordered eating, and multiple injuries from excessive training.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Low body weight, signs of energy deficiency, absence of menstrual cycle, poor bone health markers, muscle weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan shows significantly reduced bone density, multiple stress fractures, and metabolic panel shows deficiencies.

puzzles.explanation: Overtraining syndrome presents as relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S) with disordered eating, amenorrhea, and multiple stress fractures.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old with prior chemotherapy presents with exertional dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance discovered during routine follow-up.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to exercise at previously tolerated intensities. Mild dyspnea with brisk walking. Asymptomatic at rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Breast cancer treated 18 months ago with anthracycline-based chemotherapy. No prior cardiac history. Excellent overall cancer prognosis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Well-appearing, vital signs stable. No edema, no JVD, no crackles. Heart sounds normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows EF 42%, global hypokinesis without regional wall motion abnormality. BNP normal. Troponin I mildly elevated.

puzzles.explanation: Cardiotoxicity from cancer chemotherapy (anthracycline-induced cardiomyopathy). Early detection through screening reveals reduced EF and reduced strain before clinical symptoms develop.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Distance runner reports groin or anterior hip pain that worsens during long runs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered by running distance, severe limp develops. Pain radiates to knee area.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Significant training volume increase, female athlete with energy deficiency. Menarche delayed or amenorrhea.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pain with FABER test, positive anterior hip pain with extension, weakness in hip abductors, functional leg length discrepancy.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays may appear normal initially, CT or MRI shows stress fracture on femoral neck. Compression-side fractures seen.

puzzles.explanation: Femoral stress fracture shows compression-side femoral neck fracture in female endurance athlete with energy deficiency.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult with insidious progressive back pain and progressive height loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive pain worsens over time, activity intolerance develops, and postural changes limiting function.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple previous compression fractures, chronic condition, and associated with corticosteroid use.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Increased thoracic kyphosis, restricted spinal movement, and multiple levels of vertebral tenderness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows multiple level compression fractures, significant kyphotic deformity, and dEXA scan confirms osteoporosis.

puzzles.explanation: Vertebral compression fracture involves multiple fractures with progressive deformity and chronic pain management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Elderly patient with cervical fracture-dislocation from fall causing severe quadriplegia.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: All four limbs paralyzed, requiring mechanical ventilation, and total care dependence.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall from standing in elderly patient with osteoporosis. C6-C7 dislocation with complete cord injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Complete quadriplegia (all muscle groups grade 0/5), complete sensory loss at C6 level, absent reflexes, respiratory failure.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT shows C6-C7 dislocation with significant misalignment and MRI confirms complete spinal cord transection.

puzzles.explanation: Complete cervical spinal cord injury with total motor and sensory loss requiring mechanical ventilation and full support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old athlete 4 weeks post-knee surgery. Knee pain and swelling at surgical site. Limited knee motion and functional activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking and light jogging started. Cutting and pivoting movements cause instability. Single-leg balance deficient.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Knee ligament tear from skiing injury. Reconstruction with autograft hamstring. Early post-operative phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee flexion 125 degrees, full extension. Quadriceps strength 4+/5. Hamstring strength 4/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows intact patellar tendon graft with good incorporation. Knee shows normal alignment. No meniscal involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Post-ACL Reconstruction shows mid-phase recovery (4 months) with good range and pain control but persistent weakness and proprioceptive deficits limiting return to sport.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged person with widespread musculoskeletal pain that comes and goes across the body.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with stress, cold weather, and poor sleep; improves with gentle movement and warmth.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began gradually after a stressful period; also reports significant fatigue and morning stiffness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Multiple tender points throughout body, normal strength and range of motion, symptoms aggravated by pressure.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: All standard imaging normal; diagnosis based on clinical criteria including widespread pain index.

puzzles.explanation: Fibromyalgia is a centralized pain condition affecting multiple body regions with characteristic tender points, fatigue, and sleep disturbance. presents in typical demographic with clear psychological trigger.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Manual worker reports low back pain triggered by occupational activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with lifting, bending, and prolonged static postures at work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset with job demands, variable intensity with workload. Previous episodes possible.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Limited spinal flexion, weak core muscles, positive straight leg raise bilaterally, muscle guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging may show minor degenerative changes or normal; functional movement assessment abnormal.

puzzles.explanation: Lower back pain work-related shows occupational mechanical pain from lifting and bending demands.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old man with progressive difficulty walking and cognitive slowing over 5 years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walks very slowly with walker, fatigue significantly limits activity, problems with attention.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset without clear relapses. History of single demyelinating event 6 years ago. Progressive worsening since.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Spastic paraparesis (grade 3/5), hyperreflexia, positive Babinski, slow mentation, positive Romberg.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spinal MRI shows cervical cord atrophy; brain MRI shows extensive white matter changes and gadolinium-enhancing lesions.

puzzles.explanation: Primary progressive MS with insidious onset, progressive myelopathy, cognitive decline, and spinal cord involvement characteristic of PPMS.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old male is referred to cardiac rehabilitation with breathlessness, exercise intolerance, and bilateral ankle swelling that worsens throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Becomes breathless walking from the bedroom to the kitchen (NYHA Class III). Reports needing 3 pillows to sleep (orthopnoea) and waking at night gasping for air (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea).

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of myocardial infarction 5 years ago. Hypertension and type 2 diabetes. On multiple cardiac medications including ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, and diuretic.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elevated JVP. Bilateral pitting oedema to mid-shins. Bibasal fine crackles on auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows ejection fraction 28% (severely reduced — HFrEF). BNP markedly elevated at 1200 pg/mL. Chest X-ray shows cardiomegaly, upper lobe pulmonary venous diversion, and bilateral pleural effusions.

puzzles.explanation: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) post-MI with classic signs of fluid overload, orthopnoea, PND, elevated JVP, and severely reduced EF on echocardiography.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Boy age 6 has hip pain and limp; pain referred to thigh and knee.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Antalgic gait, limitation of hip abduction and internal rotation. Weight-bearing difficult.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset, no trauma. Otherwise healthy, similar condition in maternal uncle.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Trendelenburg sign, hip flexion contracture. Limited internal rotation, pain on log roll test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows femoral head flattening and fragmentation; MRI confirms avascular necrosis of femoral epiphysis.

puzzles.explanation: Legg-Calvé-Perthes is idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head epiphysis in children, causing hip pain and gait disturbance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Cancer survivor 2 years after completing platinum-based chemotherapy with persistent numbness and pain in feet.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Chronic affecting sleep, difficulty walking on uneven surfaces, and has completed cancer treatment.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Received cisplatin for ovarian cancer treatment. Neuropathy developed during treatment and has persisted despite completion.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked sensory loss distally, weakness of foot intrinsics, absent reflexes, protective gait, hyperesthesia to touch.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows chronic axonal neuropathy consistent with prior cisplatin exposure and no current chemotherapy agents.

puzzles.explanation: Platinum-induced chronic peripheral neuropathy persisting long after chemotherapy completion with significant functional impairment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with sudden severe pain in back of the lower leg and audible or palpable pop. Immediate inability to push off.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Inability to push off or rise on toes and complete loss of plantarflexion power. Unable to bear weight on affected leg acute functional loss.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden traumatic event and pushing motion at the ankle against resistance or sudden push-off. The tendon was previously normal at the heel age typically over 30.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Thompson test, palpable gap in tendon, complete loss of plantarflexion strength and severe swelling with ecchymosis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI confirms tendon discontinuity and imaging shows acute rupture with gap between tendon ends. Hemorrhage visible.

puzzles.explanation: Achilles tendon rupture is a complete traumatic injury requiring urgent assessment and typically surgical repair.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with sudden severe pain in lower leg, audible or palpable pop, and immediate loss of plantarflexion strength.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Marked loss of ankle plantarflexion, dorsiflexion, and painful with any ankle motion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Develops months after initial injury and unresponsive to standard rehabilitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Capsular restriction pattern, all ankle motions reduced, and talocrural hypomobility.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows synovial thickening, capsular changes, and X-rays may be normal.

puzzles.explanation: Frozen ankle is a rare but recognized complication involving ankle joint capsule fibrosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older patient with chronic knee pain and recurrent episodes of giving way during daily activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Stairs, uneven terrain provoke instability, patient has adapted with activity avoidance, and no recent acute injury recalled.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Remote injury from years prior, progressive compensatory movement patterns, and concurrent symptoms of mechanical catching.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lachman test may be less obvious due to hamstring guarding and pivot shift can be subtle. Patient demonstrates antalgic gait.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows degenerative appearance of ACL remnant with bone marrow edema and secondary osteoarthritis changes.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic ACL insufficiency often presents with subtle instability and secondary degenerative changes rather than acute symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 9-year-old girl presents with recurring joint pain affecting multiple joints and fatigue. Her parents report she bruises easily and has always seemed more flexible than her peers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Ability to perform unusual contortions, frequent joint dislocations that worsen with movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Born with excessive flexibility and family history of connective tissue disorder. Skin fragility.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Beighton score elevated, joint hypermobility generalized, and skin hyperextensibility bruising.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Genetic testing confirms Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and imaging shows structural connective tissue changes.

puzzles.explanation: Hypermobility syndrome from connective tissue disorder involves excessive joint flexibility predisposing to subluxations and pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports multiple regional pain syndromes that mimic nerve distribution patterns.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms fluctuate with activity level; multiple muscle groups affected across different body areas.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing muscle tension from occupational demands; multiple treatment attempts with variable success.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Widespread taut bands, multiple trigger points in different muscles, variable referred pain patterns.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal nerve conduction studies and imaging; symptoms don't follow dermatome distribution.

puzzles.explanation: Myofascial pain syndrome shows chronic, multi-regional presentation mimicking neuropathic pain but with trigger point findings on examination.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Severe non-healing wound in elderly patient with multiple risk factors.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Complete immobility, palliative care focus. Infection management needed.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic pressure injury, failed conservative treatment. Systemic infection risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Large open wound with necrotic tissue, significant drainage. Surrounding erythema.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows extensive tissue involvement, possible bone exposure. Infection confirmed.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced pressure injuries with extensive tissue involvement require multidisciplinary management including wound care, offloading, and infection control.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Teenage boy (16 years old) with progressive proximal and cardiac symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk without wheelchair, severe respiratory insufficiency, and fatigues easily reports palpitations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic tissue injury, failed conservative treatment, and systemic infection risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe proximal and generalized weakness, minimal movement in lower extremities, cardiac murmur, respiratory muscle weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows dilated cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction and emg shows myopathic pattern. Elevated ck >40,000.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced Duchenne muscular dystrophy with severe disability, cardiac involvement, and respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged patient with lateral ankle swelling, ecchymosis, and pain with walking after minor twisting injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with weight-bearing, ankle inversion, limits shoe fitting, and difficulty with uneven surfaces improves with elevation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Simple inversion injury and onset immediate but gradual. Activity-related mechanism no prior ankle problems.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lateral ankle tenderness,, anterior drawer test positive, limited range of motion with inversion, and plantarflexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays rule out fracture and ultrasound shows ligament. MRI demonstrates partial ligament tear with surrounding inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Grade 1-2 lateral ankle sprains cause pain and swelling but maintain functional mobility with appropriate management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient presents with posterior knee pain and vague 'heaviness' in the lower leg during prolonged sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms exacerbated by activities requiring sustained knee flexion and minimal acute instability but functional impairment noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic presentation after remote or minimally recalled injury and gradual onset of calf swelling. Progressive.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Subtle posterior sag, posterior drawer less obvious than acute injury, quadriceps weakness, and atrophy present with long-arc quads.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows chronic PCL changes with possible avulsion fracture and secondary osteoarthritis present in some cases.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic PCL insufficiency may present insidiously with posterior pain and weakness rather than acute instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with malignancy presenting with progressive lower back pain and leg symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worsening despite rest, night prominent, and progressive functional decline.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known cancer history and constitutional symptoms. Recent radiation therapy progressive symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive spinal percussion test, neurological signs variable, and weakness disproportionate to.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows metastatic disease with epidural spinal cord compression.

puzzles.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome is caused by metastatic disease requiring oncology consultation and emergency decompression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adolescent athlete with pain, swelling at tibial tuberosity, onset during growth spurt, and anterior knee pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with jumping, running, and activities involving knee extension against resistance. Worse during rapid growth periods.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Common in growing adolescents involved in repetitive jumping sports, quadriceps, hamstring tightness present, and self-limiting condition.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Prominent tibial tuberosity with localized tenderness, knee extension against resistance reproduces, and palpable apophyseal prominence.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays may show apophyseal or fragmentation and mri shows inflammation at apophyseal insertion. Self-limited condition.

puzzles.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a self-limiting apophysitis common in growing adolescent athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with prominent bony bump at base of great toe, pain at medial first metatarsal head, and associated with tight shoes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: worsens with tight or high-heeled shoes, improves with wide, soft footwear, and walking on hard surfaces aggravates.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual development of deformity and family history common. Often begins in adolescence or early adulthood may worsen with pregnancy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible medial deviation of great toe and bony prominence at first metatarsal head. Bursa inflammation may be present possible second toe crowding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays show increased intermetatarsal angle, hallux valgus angle, joint degenerative changes possible, and assess first metatarsal position.

puzzles.explanation: Hallux valgus is lateral deviation of the great toe with medial first metatarsal prominence causing bunion deformity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Post-traumatic knee pain and instability in chronic presentation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Persistent functional limitation, difficulty with uneven terrain, and giving way episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She has a history of osteoporosis and was previously treated for a distal radius fracture two years ago. She takes calcium supplementation but is not on bisphosphonate therapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Chronic joint effusion, ligamentous laxity present, and crepitus with motion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Long-standing imaging shows healing fracture with post-traumatic arthritic changes.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic tibial plateau fracture sequelae often involve post-traumatic arthritis and ligamentous laxity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old presents with chronic radial wrist pain and persistent functional loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with any gripping, weakness, and activities requiring wrist stability are limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He sustained a fall from height six months ago with residual wrist stiffness. He has no history of inflammatory arthritis or prior hand surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Persistent anatomical snuffbox tenderness, restricted radial deviation, and grip strength significantly reduced.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows scaphoid non-union with sclerotic margins and ct confirms nonunion. MRI shows AVN.

puzzles.explanation: Scaphoid nonunion with possible avascular necrosis, chronic presentation from inadequate initial treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Office worker reports eye strain, dry eyes, and headache after prolonged screen time.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen with prolonged computer use; improve with reduced screen time and frequent breaks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms develop during work hours; associated with reduced blinking and poor screen ergonomics.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced tear break-up time, conjunctival injection, accommodation lag, dry eye findings.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal ophthalmologic imaging; dry eye testing confirms reduced tear production or quality.

puzzles.explanation: Computer vision syndrome shows occupational eye strain from prolonged screen use with dry eye component.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 67-year-old presents with palpitations, dyspnea, and chest discomfort that developed acutely during sleep.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen with prolonged screen use; improve with reduced screen time and frequent breaks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known hypertension and chronic kidney disease. No prior arrhythmia. No recent chest pain history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Irregularly irregular pulse (variable rate 110-140 bpm). Blood pressure elevated at 158/92. Apical heart rate faster than radial pulse.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: 12-lead ECG shows absent P waves, irregular ventricular rhythm, and fibrillatory baseline confirming atrial fibrillation. Troponin normal. Echo shows normal EF with LA enlargement.

puzzles.explanation: Acute atrial fibrillation presentation with rapid ventricular response and hemodynamic compromise. ECG confirms diagnosis with characteristic findings. Hypertension and CKD are predisposing factors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Child or young adolescent with heel pain, pain worse during or after sports. Pain at back of heel.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms with running and jumping, pain worsens with increased activity. Pain improves with rest, activity limitation noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occurs during growth spurts, typically 8-14 years old. High activity level, tight gastrocnemius-soleus complex.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness at calcaneal insertion of Achilles, pain with passive dorsiflexion. Tight calf muscles, pain with squeezing heel.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays may show fragmented apophysis, MRI shows apophyseal inflammation. Self-limited condition resolving with growth plate closure.

puzzles.explanation: Sever's disease is a self-limiting calcaneal apophysitis common in growing athletic children.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent dizziness and imbalance 6 weeks after acute an inner ear infection episode. Vertigo largely resolved but positional symptoms. Spatial disorientation remain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Balance deficits persist and patient uncertain in crowded environments. With complex visual surroundings. Difficulty with gait on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute viral an inner ear infection 6 weeks ago with initial severe vertigo resolved. Residual dizziness and balance problems persisting. Hearing decline documented with audiometry.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dix-Hallpike now negative, Spontaneous nystagmus absent, Romberg test positive but improved from acute phase, Gait shows cautious pattern but not severely restricted, Dynamic gait testing shows deficits with head turns.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Repeat MRI shows resolving labyrinthine inflammation, Mild residual enhancement, Audiometry shows sensorineural hearing loss in high frequencies, Imaging findings consistent with resolving inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Post-acute labyrinthitis requires vestibular rehabilitation emphasizing gait training, balance retraining with visual and proprioceptive compensation, habituation to remaining symptoms, and psychological support for chronic dizziness adjustment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old with history of shoulder dislocation reports repeated episodes with minimal trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Recurrent dislocation with minimal provocation, can sometimes self-reduce. Apprehension with movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple dislocations over years, getting easier to dislocate. Chronic apprehension.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive apprehension, positive relocation test. Sulcus sign, generalized laxity noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows labral damage with Hill-Sachs lesion, glenoid bone loss. Chronic instability changes.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent anterior instability with established labral pathology and bone loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Road cyclist reports hand numbness affecting ring and little fingers after long rides.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms triggered by prolonged cycling grip; improves with hand position changes and padded gloves.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive symptoms with increased cycling volume; associated with ulnar nerve compression at wrist.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's test over ulnar nerve at wrist, weakness in intrinsic hand muscles, sensory loss in ulnar distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show ulnar nerve slowing at wrist; ultrasound shows compression site.

puzzles.explanation: Cyclist's palsy shows ulnar nerve compression at wrist from prolonged cycling hand pressure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 30-year-old female presents with unilateral left-sided buttock pain that she points to with one finger over the posterior superior iliac spine.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: is worse with prolonged standing, walking uphill, and rolling over in bed. Relieved by sitting. Reports when stepping up onto a curb with the left leg.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 4 months postpartum. Vaginal delivery with prolonged second stage of labour. Reports her pelvis felt 'unstable' since delivery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Three or more SIJ provocation tests positive: compression test, thigh thrust (posterior shear), Gaenslen's test, and sacral thrust. Positive active straight leg raise (ASLR) test bilaterally. Lumbar spine examination unremarkable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays are normal. MRI shows mild bone marrow oedema at the left SIJ without erosions or sclerosis, consistent with inflammatory changes rather than structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Postpartum SIJ dysfunction is common due to ligamentous laxity from relaxin. Diagnosis requires 3+ positive provocation tests to achieve adequate specificity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old male tradesperson presents with anterior knee pain and swelling distal to the patella of two weeks duration. The area is tender to touch and warm on palpation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with kneeling on knees; worse with prolonged knee bending.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Related to prolonged kneeling position; name reflects historical occupational cause.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Swelling below patella, tenderness over patellar tendon insertion. Pain with knee flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates bursal enlargement with fluid collection.

puzzles.explanation: Infrapatellar bursitis occurs from repetitive kneeling in a kneeling position.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports progressive foot ulceration with signs of infection 2 months duration. Purulent drainage, malodor, and surrounding cellulitis present. Fever reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Non-weight-bearing status required and use of assistive device essential. Severe activity limitation. Self-care activities difficult.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Type 1 diabetes for 20 years with repeated hospitalizations for infections. Previous partial foot amputation. New ulcer for 2 months with inadequate home care.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Large ulcer with exposed subcutaneous tissue and bone involvement involvement, Surrounding cellulitis is significant, Systemic signs of infection present. Fever, tachycardia, No protective sensation, Pulses absent in dorsum.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows deep ulcer with bone involvement (osteomyelitis present), Subcutaneous gas in tissues (gas gangrene risk), Vascular imaging shows critical limb ischemia, Amputation risk very high and surgical consultation needed.

puzzles.explanation: Complicated diabetic foot ulcer with infection and vascular insufficiency requires urgent multispecialty management including infectious disease, vascular surgery, wound specialist, glycemic control, and preparation for potential amputation with rehabilitation planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old woman presents with widespread joint pain, fatigue, and frequent episodes of dizziness on standing. She reports a lifelong history of easy bruising and skin hyperextensibility.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe activity limitation, fatigue disproportionate to exertion. Orthostatic intolerance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of joint issues, multiple system involvement. Dysautonomia features.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant hypermobility, skin changes. Autonomic dysfunction signs, neurodiverse features common.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Genetic testing confirms EDS; imaging shows degenerative changes despite young age.

puzzles.explanation: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is a complex systemic presentation with significant autonomic and multisystem involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old with severe initial COVID requiring ICU admission reports ongoing dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance 6 months later.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot return to work due to dyspnea with stairs. Exercise intolerance marked. Persistent cough.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe COVID-19 with pneumonia requiring 10 days mechanical ventilation. Recovered medically but symptomatic. Significant ICU-acquired weakness developed.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Decreased respiratory muscle strength. Able to walk 200 meters before dyspnea onset. Oxygen saturation drops to 92% with exertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest CT shows resolved pulmonary edema but persistent ground-glass opacities suggesting ongoing inflammation. PFTs show reduced DLCO and exercise-induced desaturation.

puzzles.explanation: Severe Long COVID presentation following critical illness. Combines persistent respiratory impairment with ICU-acquired weakness and significant functional limitation requiring rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Cyclist with chronic pain at ischium; activity-dependent discomfort.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worse during intervals and climbs; improved with cadence reduction and seat height adjustment.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She has a background of recurrent sacroiliac joint pain and recently increased her cycling training volume by 40% over eight weeks. No prior hamstring injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized ischial tenderness, positive Puranen-Orava test. Pain with resisted hip flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound dynamic imaging shows tendon irritation with movement.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic hamstring tendinopathy in cyclists often relates to bike setup and loading mechanics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old male with polyarticular hand and foot pain. Associated with visible skin rash with silver scales and nail changes including pitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Morning stiffness lasting 1-2 hours. Pain with gripping and walking. Worse with stress.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Skin lesions present 2 years, joint symptoms started 1 year ago. Family history of psoriasis. Stress-related exacerbations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Psoriatic skin plaques on extensor surfaces. Nail pitting and onycholysis visible. Ray-like swelling of fingers (sausage digits).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows pencil-in-cup deformities and osteolysis. Asymmetric joint involvement. MRI shows inflammation and bone erosions.

puzzles.explanation: Psoriatic Arthritis is the classic presentation with visible skin manifestations preceding or accompanying joint disease with characteristic asymmetric pattern and bone changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with severe heel pain following fall from height or crush injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with heel weight-bearing, massive swelling. Unable to ambulate.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mechanism was a fall from height of approximately 3 metres onto a hard surface. He has no prior lower limb fractures and no anticoagulation use.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe heel swelling, loss of heel pad height. Positive bulge sign, possible compartment syndrome.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan shows intra-articular comminution, loss of Bohler's angle. Subtalar involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Calcaneal fractures are severe injuries with significant morbidity and risk of chronic pain and dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with hand, numbness in ring, and little fingers progressing over months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot grip with little finger, ring finger, difficulty with fine motor tasks, and claw hand developing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset. Works at computer with elbow bent. History of ulnar nerve compression at elbow.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Interosseous atrophy, weakness of finger abduction and adduction, sensory loss in ulnar distribution, Froment sign positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows slowing across elbow, ultrasound shows nerve thickening at cubital tunnel, and mRI shows nerve compression.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic ulnar nerve entrapment at cubital tunnel with progressive motor loss, intrinsic hand atrophy, and confirmed compression on imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Child develops foot pain and limited ankle motion during growth spurt.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with walking, sports, there is inability to invert/evert foot, and stiff ankle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset age 10-14, family history of foot problems, and progressive symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Limited subtalar motion, rigid flatfoot, and with inversion heel.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan shows talocalcaneal or talonavicular coalition and osseous or fibrocartilaginous bridge.

puzzles.explanation: Tarsal coalition is an abnormal bone or cartilage bridge between tarsal bones causing pain and motion limitation during growth.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Teenager reports mild back discomfort and notices uneven clothing fit.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Minimal functional limitation and able to participate in school activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Curve detected at age 10, slowly progressive over 3 years, and still growing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left lumbar scoliosis with rib hump, mild pain with spinal palpation, and flexible curve.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serial X-rays show Cobb angle progression from 22 to 35 degrees and immature skeletal age.

puzzles.explanation: Progressive idiopathic scoliosis in growing adolescents may require bracing to halt progression and prevent the need for surgery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with severe back pain, systemic symptoms, and fever present.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation, at rest, and progressive worsening.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Risk factors present: immunosuppression, recent infection, IV drug use, spinal procedure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vertebral tenderness, fever, and elevated inflammatory markers possible neurological deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows disc space narrowing, vertebral body involvement, and inflammatory signal changes.

puzzles.explanation: Spondylodiscitis is a spinal infection requiring emergency recognition and treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old office worker presents with persistent wrist discomfort that has been gradually worsening over the past two years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with sustained typing, gripping heavy objects, and wringing out cloths. Notices an intermittent clicking sensation with certain wrist movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Suffered a fall onto an outstretched hand two years ago that was initially treated conservatively. Symptoms never fully resolved and have progressively worsened despite splinting.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Watson's test with a visible scaphoid shift. Reduced grip strength measured at 60% of the contralateral side. Mild dorsal prominence noted over the mid-carpal region.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray demonstrates scapholunate gap widening greater than 3mm. MRI confirms chronic ligament disruption with surrounding synovitis. CT shows early carpal malalignment.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic wrist sprain with scapholunate instability and carpal malalignment from inadequate healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Healthcare or animal facility worker reports progressive respiratory symptoms at work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worse with allergen exposure (animal proteins, latex, cleaning chemicals).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive sensitization over months to years and initial symptoms mild but worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Baseline obstruction present, significant variation with work exposure, positive allergen testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spirometry shows persistent obstruction, specific ige to workplace allergen, and peak flow diaries show work pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational asthma shows allergic sensitization occupational asthma with progressive airway obstruction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient to lift hand at wrist after waking up from sleep with arm in unusual position.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot extend wrist or fingers, wrist droops downward, loss of sensation on dorsum of hand, and thumb.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset after sleeping on arm in compressed position. 'Saturday night palsy' from sleeping on armchair. No trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist drop with inability to extend wrist, weakness of finger extension, sensory loss over dorsum of hand (first web space), normal elbow strength.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows conduction block at mid-humerus and ultrasound shows nerve compression. X-ray normal.

puzzles.explanation: Acute radial nerve compression palsy from prolonged pressure with classic wrist drop and sensory loss in superficial radial nerve distribution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with chronic intercostal pain and neuralgic quality possible.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: variable and certain movements trigger but pattern not always clear.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic course, may follow previous strain, and scarring or nerve involvement suspected.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Intercostal tenderness, may follow nerve distribution, and some sensory changes possible.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: May show muscle scarring and differential diagnosis includes neuropathic component.

puzzles.explanation: Intercostal muscle strain is chronic with potential nerve involvement (intercostal neuralgia).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with forefoot pain affecting second, third metatarsal heads, pain with walking, and standing activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with weight-bearing and activity modification provides partial relief. Symptoms recur with increased activity footwear choice critical.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Associated with hallux limitus or metatarsocuneiform joint dysfunction and possible longstanding forefoot. Plantarflexor tightness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized metatarsal head tenderness and with great toe dorsiflexion. Intrinsic muscle weakness possible plantar callus formation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging may show degenerative changes at metatarsophalangeal joints and assess first ray plantarflexion function. Radiographs may reveal anatomical variants.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary metatarsalgia may result from biomechanical dysfunction of adjacent joints.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Child age 18 months with residual foot deformity despite early treatment.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking with inverted foot, toe-walking on affected side, and limping gait.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Treated with Ponseti casting from birth, casting discontinued prematurely, and no maintenance splinting.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Persistent varus, equinus, difficulty with heel walking, and limited ankle dorsiflexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows incomplete correction with persistent talus deviation and calcaneal positioning abnormal.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent clubfoot deformity from inadequate treatment requires repeat intervention and compliance with dynamic splinting protocols.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young person with significant chest wall swelling, pain, and concerning presentation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by deep breathing, coughing, and direct pressure to the chest wall. She avoids lying on her left side and finds relief only with complete rest and analgesia.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset of, associated with severe, and multiple investigations performed.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible of costochondral cartilage, significant tenderness, and overlying inflammation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging confirms benign costochondral and no malignancy. Diagnosis of tietze's syndrome.

puzzles.explanation: Costochondritis is Tietze's syndrome with visible swelling requiring imaging confirmation to rule out serious pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old recreational runner presents with persistent lateral ankle pain and a feeling of ankle instability following an inversion sprain three months ago. Symptoms are reproduced by walking on uneven ground.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: worse on uneven terrain, instability sensation, and worse with inversion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Follows ankle inversion injury and persistent despite ankle rehabilitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness in sinus tarsi, positive sinus tarsi palpation, and subtalar instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows signal change in sinus tarsi and CT may show talocalcaneal malalignment.

puzzles.explanation: Sinus tarsi syndrome results from inflammation and instability in the subtalar joint after ankle sprain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old female with polyarticular joint pain affecting hands, wrists, and knees. Associated malar (butterfly) rash across cheeks. Nose.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Morning stiffness and limiting hand function. Joint improves with rest. NSAIDs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset over 6 months. Reproductive age female. Associated constitutional symptoms: fever, weight loss.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Classic malar rash. Joint swelling without erosions (non-erosive arthritis). Oral ulceration.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Positive ANA with high titre. Positive dsDNA antibody and anti-Smith antibody. Reduced complement levels (C3, C4).

puzzles.explanation: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus is the classic presentation in a young female with characteristic malar rash, non-erosive arthritis, and positive serology including ANA and dsDNA antibodies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged female with sharp lateral hip pain after increasing walking distance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain reproducible with side-lying; aggravated by crossing legs while standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in running or gym activity; gluteal weakness noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Exquisite tenderness at trochanter, positive lateral hip pain test. Weak hip abduction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals gluteal medius tendinopathy with associated bursal inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Activity-related GTPS often involves functional weakness of hip stabilizers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old female office worker presents with pain in her left buttock that radiates down the back of her thigh. She has been treated for sciatica but her lumbar MRI was normal.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting, crossing legs, and climbing stairs. Driving is uncomfortable. Pain eases with standing and walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms present for 4 months. No low back pain. No trauma but increased running recently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness on deep palpation reproducing buttock and leg symptoms. Positive FAIR test (flexion, adduction, internal rotation). Positive Pace sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI pelvis shows mild hypertrophy and oedema of the piriformis muscle with close relationship to the sciatic nerve. MR neurography suggests nerve irritation at that level.

puzzles.explanation: Piriformis syndrome (deep gluteal syndrome) causing extra-spinal sciatic symptoms with a normal lumbar spine, positive piriformis-specific provocation tests, and MRI showing the piriformis-sciatic nerve relationship.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older individual with persistent lateral hip discomfort limiting activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Morning stiffness in hip; pain with prolonged standing and uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic presentation; history of previous ankle or knee injuries altering gait.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Limited hip external rotation, palpable trochanteric tenderness. Positive single-leg stance test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates chronic bursal thickening; tendon changes evident.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic GTPS often reflects underlying biomechanical issues and gait abnormalities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Athlete with acute posterior thigh pain during sprinting or kicking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Onset sudden during explosive movement and immediate with weight-bearing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No significant prior symptoms and high-speed activity or overstretching mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ecchymosis visible and with straight leg raise. Positive stretch test palpable defect possible.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI confirms muscle tear with retraction and fluid collection.

puzzles.explanation: Hamstring strain typically occurs during explosive sports movements, with ecchymosis and immediate functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with incidental finding of posterior knee mass and minimal or no symptoms. Discovered during screening or.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Asymptomatic and no functional limitations. Patient concerned about mass activity tolerance normal.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cyst present for extended period without change and no acute episodes. Underlying knee pathology may be minimal often found on ordered for other reasons.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable cyst without significant tenderness, primary knee examination largely normal, and size stable neurovascular intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging confirms simple cyst and no complex features. Primary knee joint may be normal or show minimal degenerative changes.

puzzles.explanation: Asymptomatic Baker's cysts are common incidental findings and may not require intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Post-operative patient with persistent radicular pain despite initial improvement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: returns with specific movements and neurodynamic tests reproduce symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous spinal surgery and initial improvement followed by plateau or worsening. Months to years post-op.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive neural tension tests, with specific movement patterns, and variable neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows scar tissue, nerve root adherent to surrounding structures, and may not explain all symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Nerve root adhesion is a post-surgical complication with persistent radicular pain from scar formation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Runner or athlete reports heel pain that is worse with first steps in morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: triggered by prolonged standing or running and improves with stretching. Orthotic support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset with increased running volume, associated with tight calves, and high-impact activities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tender plantar fascia, limited ankle dorsiflexion, with passive toe extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows plantar fascia thickening, mri shows fascial inflammation, and may show heel spur.

puzzles.explanation: Plantar fasciitis athlete shows typical running-related heel pain with fascial inflammation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old female university student reports diffuse anterior knee pain that has gradually worsened over 2 months since starting running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: is aggravated by going up and down stairs, squatting,. Prolonged sitting with knees bent ('movie '). Running downhill is worse than uphill.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No locking, giving way, or significant swelling. Increased her running volume by 40% over 3 weeks. Wears old running shoes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness on patellar facets with compression. Positive Clarke's test (patella grind). Increased dynamic valgus on single-leg squat.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray unremarkable. MRI shows no structural pathology — no cartilage defects, intact menisci and ligaments. Mild patellar lateral tilt on axial views.

puzzles.explanation: Classic patellofemoral pain in a young female runner with training error, proximal weakness, and characteristic 'movie sign' and stair pain. Imaging is typically unremarkable.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old presents with pain and swelling at the shoulder tip after direct blow.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with adduction across body (cross-body adduction); pain at top of shoulder.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct impact injury, typical from fall or collision. Acute presentation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness at AC joint, positive AC joint compression test. Crossover test positive, assess grade of injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows AC joint displacement, assess for grade 1-3 injury. Comparison views helpful.

puzzles.explanation: Acute AC joint sprain from direct trauma with typical mechanism and clinical findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old presents with severe elbow pain and deformity after a fall onto outstretched arm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Complete inability to move elbow, severe, and obvious angular deformity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism, posterior dislocation is most common, and associated with high-energy injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Obvious posterior displacement and severe swelling. Restricted range of motion neurovascular assessment critical.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray confirms posterior and assess for associated fractures (coronoid, radial head).

puzzles.explanation: Acute posterior elbow dislocation from typical fall mechanism requiring urgent reduction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Soccer or hockey player reports sudden groin pain with kicking or pushing movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with adduction, hip flexion, and kicking; improves with rest and stretching.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute non-contact injury during acceleration or lateral movement; pain radiates along medial thigh.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Adductor tenderness, positive adductor squeeze test, pain with hip adduction against resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows muscle strain with hypoechoic area; MRI shows muscle fiber disruption and edema.

puzzles.explanation: Groin strain shows acute adductor muscle strain from kicking mechanism with positive squeeze test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old with rheumatoid arthritis presents with multiple fingers in finger position.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive deformity affecting function; secondary to chronic joint inflammation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Longstanding RA, gradual development of deformity over years. Multiple fingers affected.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Multiple fingers with PIP flexion and DIP extension, intrinsic tightness. Fixed deformities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows RA changes with joint destruction; MRI shows synovitis and tendon changes.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary boutonniere deformity from chronic rheumatoid arthritis with progressive joint destruction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent shoulder dysfunction and radiation-related skin changes 12 weeks post-operatively. New complaint of cording sensation along inner arm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Continued limitations with overhead activities and reaching. Difficulty with rotational movements. Radiation therapy causing skin irritation and fatigue.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Bilateral mastectomy with reconstruction and axillary dissection 12 weeks ago. Adjuvant radiation therapy recently initiated. Patient reports initial good compliance with early rehabilitation but plateau in progress.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant axillary web syndrome (cording) palpable extending from axilla toward medial upper arm, Erythematous skin changes from radiation, Shoulder internal rotation particularly restricted, Beginning lymphedema in upper arm.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound confirms post-surgical changes and shows early fluid accumulation consistent with lymphedema. Reconstruction integration progressing appropriately.

puzzles.explanation: Post-mastectomy rehabilitation complicated by radiation therapy requires modified approaches to avoid skin damage, aggressive treatment of axillary cording, lymphedema management, and coordination with oncology team for optimal outcomes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old female violinist presents with diffuse arm pain, numbness in two of her fingers, and a sensation of heaviness in her right arm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are provoked by prolonged overhead activities and playing violin for more than 20 minutes. Reports occasional colour changes in her hand during cold weather.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lean build with postural forward head and rounded shoulders. History of whiplash 2 years ago. Symptoms worsening with increased rehearsal schedule over 12 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Roos test (elevated arm stress test) at 90 seconds — reproduces symptoms and hand pallor. Positive Adson's test with diminished radial pulse. Tender anterior and middle scalenes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows a rudimentary cervical rib on the right. Doppler ultrasound demonstrates subclavian artery compression with arm abduction. Nerve conduction studies are normal.

puzzles.explanation: Neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome with vascular component, presenting with ulnar-distribution symptoms, provoked by overhead use, with cervical rib as a structural contributor.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 36-year-old reports persistent fatigue, dyspnea, and cognitive difficulties 4 months after mild initial COVID infection.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Profound fatigue worsening with minimal exertion and lingering for days (post-exertional malaise). Dyspnea on walking. Difficulty concentrating at work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Confirmed COVID-19 infection 4 months prior with mild symptoms initially. Recovered from acute phase but never returned to baseline. Previously very active.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vital signs stable, oxygen saturation normal at rest. Appears fatigued. Cognitive testing shows mild impairment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows no acute consolidation or effusion. High-resolution CT of the chest demonstrates mild ground-glass opacities in the lower lobes bilaterally. Pulmonary function tests show a mild restrictive and diffusion-capacity defect.

puzzles.explanation: Typical Long COVID presentation with post-exertional malaise, cognitive dysfunction, and dyspnea persisting months after acute infection. Symptom variability and exclusion of organic pathology are characteristic.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old 2 months post-completion of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy for melanoma presents with chest pain and dyspnea.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Acute dyspnea and chest discomfort with minimal exertion. Unable to perform usual activities. Palpitations and dizziness noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Stage III melanoma treated with combined nivolumab and ipilimumab immunotherapy. Recent completion of 6-month course. History of autoimmune inflammation during treatment.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tachycardia, elevated blood pressure. Quiet heart sounds. New S3 gallop present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ECG shows diffuse ST elevation and PR depression consistent with myocarditis. Troponin markedly elevated at 4.2 ng/mL. Echo shows global hypokinesis and reduced EF 25%.

puzzles.explanation: Immune checkpoint inhibitor-induced myocarditis with acute severe presentation. Fulminant course with acute heart failure requiring intensive therapy and potential mechanical support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Person reports ankle pain and significant swelling following fall or awkward step.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with weight-bearing, significant functional limitation. Swelling interferes with shoe fitting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recurrent ankle sprains, episodes of giving way. Chronic ankle instability pattern.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Chronic swelling, positive inversion stress with significant laxity, positive anterior drawer with laxity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows multiple ligament involvement (ATFL, CFL), chronic changes, possible peroneal tendon pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Ankle inversion sprain shows chronic instability with recurrent episodes and multiple ligament involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Pregnant patient at 35 weeks gestation reports severe, debilitating pelvic pain affecting all mobility. Pain radiates to lower extremities. Significant impact on function and quality of life.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Ambulation severely limited; walking with severe antalgic gait. Unable to perform any household activities. Sleeping nearly impossible despite multiple positional changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Primigravida with early-onset pelvic pain at 18 weeks. Pain severely progressive; different from typical pregnancy discomfort. Previous musculoskeletal complaints.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant pain with any pelvic motion. Marked diastasis recti with functional weakness. Severe pelvic floor dysfunction with spasm.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Obstetric imaging confirms normal pregnancy progression. No fetal compromise. Pelvic imaging shows pubic diastasis of 3cm.

puzzles.explanation: Severe antenatal pelvic girdle pain with marked diastasis and SIJ laxity requires intensive stabilization, rigid pelvic support garment use, strict activity restriction, close obstetric monitoring, and preparation for potential postpartum complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with progressive great toe stiffness and pain, significant limitation of motion. Functional impairment with walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with push-off, difficulty with running and jumping. Pain interferes with daily walking, conservative measures inadequate.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing stiffness, progressive worsening. Multiple joint involvement, walking pattern significantly altered. Possible surgical history consideration.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe dorsiflexion limitation, pain and stiffness predominate. First MTP joint swelling present, gait adapted for pain avoidance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays show advanced joint degenerative changes, significant osteophytes. Marked joint space loss, bone-on-bone appearance possible.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced hallux rigidus shows significant degenerative changes and may require surgical intervention.

puzzles.answer: Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with risk factors presenting with groin pain and progressive hip dysfunction.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with weight-bearing, rapid functional deterioration, and night prominent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of steroid use, alcohol abuse, previous femoral neck fracture, or radiation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Antalgic gait, hip flexion contracture, positive faber, and fadir tests.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Early MRI shows bone marrow edema and later stages show segmental collapse on X-ray.

puzzles.explanation: AVN is a serious progressive condition requiring early diagnosis to prevent femoral head collapse.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with shoulder pain and progressive deltoid muscle wasting 6 weeks after shoulder surgery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Marked difficulty with shoulder abduction, when trying to raise arm, and post-surgical decreasing but strength declining.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Underwent shoulder repair surgery 6 weeks ago. Symptoms developed post-operatively. Previously normal shoulder function.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe deltoid weakness (grade 1/5), notable deltoid atrophy, sensory loss over lateral shoulder, normal rotator cuff strength.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows complete denervation of deltoid and ultrasound shows deltoid atrophy. MRI shows nerve compression at quadrangular space from post-surgical changes.

puzzles.explanation: Post-surgical iatrogenic axillary nerve injury from rotator cuff repair with severe deltoid paralysis and atrophy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 78-year-old woman is referred after multiple falls over the past three months. She reports that the falls occur both during the day and at night, often without a clear trip or slip.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Loss of confidence, avoidance of activities, and restricted mobility from fear.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recently started new medication, lives alone, and poor home safety vision problems.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Balance impairment and slow gait. Reduced vision abnormal proprioception Notably, mild cognitive slowing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows mild brain atrophy, normal for age, and no acute findings.

puzzles.explanation: Multifactorial falls in elderly result from combined balance impairment, sensory loss, medication effects, and environmental hazards.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Child age 3 has swollen knee joints and morning stiffness lasting over an hour.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty walking after waking, refuses to run, play, and refuses to bear weight on legs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset 2 months ago, no fever or trauma, and otherwise healthy development.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral knee effusions, warm, swollen joints, and restricted range of motion positive ana.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows synovial hypertrophy, effusion, normal bone, and mRI confirms synovitis.

puzzles.explanation: Oligoarticular JIA commonly affects knees with morning stiffness and swelling, requiring anti-inflammatory treatment and careful monitoring for uveitis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Child age 10 has pain around the kneecap during activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with running, jumping, difficulty with stairs, and squatting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No acute trauma, insidious onset, and active in sports rapid growth.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: with compression test, positive j-sign, and weak hip abductors tight quadriceps.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays normal, mri shows no structural damage, and tracking problem on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Patellofemoral pain in children results from biomechanical issues and muscle imbalance, treated with strengthening and activity modification.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 20-year-old college athlete struck in head during game, reports seeing stars.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Briefly dazed but returned to play; later reports difficulty concentrating and dizziness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Head-to-head collision during contact sport. Symptoms emerged over hours. Second impact to head this season.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: GCS 15, normal pupil reactivity, no focal weakness, slow reaction times, balance slightly impaired.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head negative; clinical diagnosis of concussion with post-concussive syndrome.

puzzles.explanation: Mild traumatic brain injury (concussion) in athlete with transient altered consciousness, post-concussive symptoms, and increased vulnerability to second-impact syndrome.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Elderly gentleman with hip discomfort in both groin and lateral regions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stairs and getting up from chair; better with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive symptoms over years; history of hip injury in youth.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Antalgic gait, significant hip flexion contracture. Pain at end-range movements.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows cartilage loss, bone marrow lesions, and joint space reduction.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced hip OA with secondary effects on gait and flexibility. Previous trauma accelerates degenerative changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents with progressive shoulder pain and from cyst compression.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with overhead, adduction activities, and progressive over months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Associated with ganglion cyst and compression at spinoglenoid or suprascapular notch.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weakness of infraspinatus (with ), possible supraspinatus involvement, and eMG abnormalities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows suprascapular nerve compression and associated paralabral ganglion cyst visible.

puzzles.explanation: Suprascapular neuropathy secondary to paralabral ganglion cyst compression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 75-year-old is 8 weeks post-aortic valve replacement presenting for cardiac rehab enrollment with multiple concerns.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Limited walking tolerance, getting winded easily. Swelling in saphenous vein harvest leg still present. Anxiety about returning to normal activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Aortic stenosis corrected with mechanical valve, on warfarin. Concurrent CABG performed. Hypertension, diabetes type 2.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sternal scar well-healed but somewhat hypertrophic. Left lower leg edema and skin changes from saphenectomy. Audible mechanical valve click.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows normal mechanical valve function, no regurgitation. Ejection fraction normal. Chest X-ray clear.

puzzles.explanation: Late post-cardiac surgery phase with emphasis on graduated return to activity and managing anticoagulation effects. Older age and comorbidities affect rehabilitation timeline.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports recurrent oral ulcers, genital ulcers, and skin lesions. Eye pain. Visual disturbances.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Oral ulcers limit eating and speaking. Genital ulcers cause with sitting. Activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recurrent aphthous ulcers since adolescence. Genital ulceration started 5 years ago. Recent ocular involvement with uveitis diagnosed.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Multiple oral ulcers at various stages of healing. Genital ulcers with scarring from previous lesions. Follicular pustules on neck and legs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ophthalmologic imaging shows anterior and posterior uveitis with vasculitis. Fundus photograph shows vascular involvement. Imaging supports inflammatory disease.

puzzles.explanation: Behçet's disease requires ophthalmologic monitoring and immunosuppressive therapy given risk of blindness, management of mucosal ulceration, control of vasculitis, and rehabilitation focusing on maintaining function during flare periods and preventing complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult reports severe burning pain in dermatomal distribution after shingles episode.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain constant, triggered by light touch (allodynia). Improves minimally with usual pain management.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain persists months after acute varicella zoster infection; severe in elderly or immunocompromised.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sensory changes in dermatomal distribution, allodynia to light touch, post-inflammatory skin changes visible.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: History and clinical findings diagnostic, may show nerve enhancement on MRI. Confirmatory viral serology available.

puzzles.explanation: Post-herpetic neuralgia shows neuropathic pain persisting after varicella zoster with dermatomal distribution and severe allodynia.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young patient with widespread joint pain and chronic pain complaints.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Joint with minimal provocation, dislocations common, and fatigue prominent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong history of joint hypermobility, frequent dislocations, and family history common.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, and tissue fragility poor wound healing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging normal, diagnosis based on clinical presentation, genetic testing, and connective tissue quality poor.

puzzles.explanation: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is the classic hypermobility presentation with widespread pain and systemic connective tissue involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old female at 8 weeks gestation with early onset pelvic pain. Anterior pubic pain and lateral hip pain bilaterally.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worse with asymmetric positions and weight-bearing. Sitting aggravates. Pain improves with pelvic support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: First pregnancy with very early PGP onset. History of sacroiliac joint dysfunction pre-pregnancy. Pelvic hypermobility syndrome.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive SIJ tests bilateral. Generalized ligamentous laxity. Pelvic asymmetry noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows early SIJ inflammation and increased capsular laxity. Pubic symphysis measurement normal. Assessment shows generalized pelvic hypermobility contributing.

puzzles.explanation: Pregnancy-Related Pelvic Girdle Pain shows early onset first trimester PGP in first pregnancy with underlying pelvic hypermobility contributing to increased instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Female patient reports chronic vulvar pain described as burning, rawness, and irritation without visible pathology. Pain constant but variable in intensity. Dyspareunia severe; sexual activity avoided.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant impact on intimate relationships and sexual function. Daily activities triggering symptoms; sitting prolonged periods problematic. Tight clothing avoided.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive vulvar pain over 3 years with no identified cause despite extensive gynecologic evaluation. Multiple treatments attempted without sustained relief. Pain affecting relationship and psychological well-being.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Normal vulvar skin appearance; no obvious dermatologic pathology. Pain reproduction with light touch to vulva (allodynia). No visible erythema, ulceration, or lesion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Vulvoscopy with biopsy shows non-specific inflammation if taken. Cultures negative for infectious organisms. Imaging workup unremarkable; no structural abnormality explaining symptoms.
