puzzles.explanation: Vulvodynia requires multidisciplinary approach including pelvic floor physical therapy, topical and systemic pain management, neuromodulating medications, cognitive behavioral therapy for pain and relationship impacts, and validation of often-dismissed symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Parent notices child walks with stiffness and one-sided weakness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Right hand function limited; child prefers left hand despite being right-handed family member.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Premature birth at 28 weeks; required prolonged NICU stay for respiratory support.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right-sided hemiparesis with increased tone; right foot in plantarflexion with tiptoe gait.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head shows asymmetric ventricular dilation with focal white matter loss on right side.

puzzles.explanation: Spastic hemiplegia is the most common form of CP, affecting one side of the body more than the other, often from unilateral perinatal brain injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Office worker reports low back or mid-back pain that develops during work hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting and poor posture; improves with movement breaks and stretching.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms correlate with workday; worse with busy periods requiring extended sitting.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Forward head posture, increased thoracic kyphosis, weak abdominal muscles, tight hip flexors.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal imaging; postural assessment shows abnormal spinal alignment and muscle imbalance pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Postural syndrome shows work-related positional pain from sustained postural stress in office setting.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old with recurrent pneumonia and chronic cough dating back to infancy presents for evaluation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot keep up with peers during play. Morning cough productive. Recurrent ear infections and sinusitis.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Neonatal intensive care admission for prematurity and respiratory distress. Three episodes of pneumonia by age 5. Immotile cilia suspect.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Clubbing developing. Chronic nasal congestion, sinus tenderness. Bilateral crackles in lower lobes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: HRCT shows bronchiectasis in lower lobes, bronchial wall thickening, and bronchus-to-artery ratio >1. Nasal mucosa ciliary dysfunction on testing.

puzzles.explanation: Pediatric bronchiectasis from primary ciliary dysfunction (PCD). Early-onset recurrent infections from infancy and associated features (chronic sinusitis, situs inversus possibility) are clues.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athletic male with anterior hip pain during high-demand activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with deep hip flexion and internal rotation; mechanical catching sensation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset during adolescence; often related to sports participation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive FADIR test, limited internal rotation in flexion. Posterior hip tightness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT or MRI shows increased femoral head-neck offset; alpha angle greater than 60 degrees.

puzzles.explanation: Cam-type FAI involves a focal femoral head-neck deformity causing labral impingement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old male retired painter presents with difficulty lifting his arm and a deep ache on the outside of his upper arm that worsens at night.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot reach overhead to change a light bulb. Reports a 'sudden worsening' event 3 months ago when lifting a heavy bucket, though he's had on-and-off problems for years.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decades of overhead work as a painter. Progressive weakness in the affected arm. Difficulty with hair washing and dressing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive drop arm test. Weakness in external rotation and abduction. Positive empty can test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows a full-thickness tear of the supraspinatus tendon (2cm retraction) with early fatty infiltration of the muscle belly (Goutallier grade 2). Associated superior labral fraying.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic degenerative rotator cuff tear with acute-on-chronic deterioration, positive lag and drop arm signs, and MRI showing full-thickness supraspinatus tear with muscle atrophy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Chronic construction worker reports progressive finger pain, numbness, and color changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation with cold; vascular and neurological symptoms both present.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decades of vibrating tool exposure; progressive worsening despite reducing vibration exposure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Multiple fingers affected, severe blanching with minimal cold exposure, significant sensory loss, atrophic changes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Advanced vascular imaging shows significantly reduced blood flow; marked sensorineural abnormalities on testing.

puzzles.explanation: Vibration white finger shows advanced hand-arm vibration syndrome with significant vascular and neurological damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old male presents with progressive inability to fully straighten his right ring and little fingers. He cannot lay his hand flat on a table.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty putting his hand in his pocket, pulling on gloves, and shaking hands. The contracture has gradually worsened over 2 years. No pain associated with the condition.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Northern European descent. History of heavy alcohol use (now sober 5 years). Type 2 diabetes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable firm nodule and cord in the palmar fascia extending to the ring finger. Fixed flexion contracture at the MCP joint (40 degrees) and PIP joint (25 degrees) of the ring finger. Positive Hueston tabletop test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Clinical diagnosis — imaging not typically required. Ultrasound shows hypoechoic nodule in the palmar aponeurosis with fibrous cord extension. No imaging features of tenosynovitis.

puzzles.explanation: Dupuytren's contracture with classic ring/little finger involvement, palmar nodules and cords, Hueston tabletop test, and risk factors of Northern European ancestry, alcohol, diabetes, and family history.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports minimal pain 1 week after arthroscopic knee surgery. Mild swelling and stiffness present. Protective reflex guarding limiting movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking improving; weight-bearing progressing well. Knee range of motion exercises beginning. Minimal limitations on functional activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diagnostic arthroscopy performed 1 week ago for knee pain. Procedure showed early degenerative changes; no major pathology. Straightforward post-operative course expected.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee portal sites healing well. Mild effusion present. Full active range of motion achieved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Arthroscopic findings showed minimal pathology. No meniscal or ligamentous injury. Articular cartilage generally intact with early wear changes in some areas.

puzzles.explanation: Simple diagnostic knee arthroscopy recovery is typically rapid with early range of motion restoration, minimal pain, and quick return to function when no significant intra-articular pathology is identified.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Chronic construction worker reports progressive hand and finger pain, numbness,. Color changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Persistent activity intolerance; functional capacity significantly reduced from baseline.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decades of vibrating tool exposure; progressive worsening despite reducing vibration exposure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced aerobic capacity, muscle weakness, poor exercise tolerance, emotional distress present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cardiac function assessment may show abnormalities, metabolic markers abnormal. Mental health screening positive.

puzzles.explanation: Cancer-related fatigue shows persistent post-treatment fatigue from late effects and deconditioning in cancer survivor.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 65-year-old man reports tremor in right hand that is worse at rest and improves with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Slow movements when picking up objects, difficulty with fine motor tasks, stooped posture when standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tremor began 2 years ago and is slowly progressive. Family member noticed loss of facial expression. Writing becoming smaller.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting tremor (pill-rolling), cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, masked facies, shuffling gait with reduced arm swing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain normal and clinical diagnosis based on motor findings. DaTscan shows reduced dopamine uptake in striatum.

puzzles.explanation: Classic idiopathic Parkinson's disease with rest tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and characteristic gait disturbance in typical age group.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old breast cancer survivor presents with progressive dyspnea and fatigue 18 months after completing anthracycline-based chemotherapy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dyspnea with stair climbing. Unable to exercise at previously tolerated intensity. Fatigue and reduced exercise capacity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diagnostic knee surgery performed 1 week ago for knee pain. Procedure showed early degenerative changes; no major pathology. Straightforward post-operative course expected.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild tachycardia, normal blood pressure. Baseline lung exam clear. Displaced apex beat.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows EF 38% (baseline 60% at end of treatment). Global hypokinesis without regional wall motion abnormality. Diastolic dysfunction present.

puzzles.explanation: Anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity with late-onset systemic symptoms and reduced ejection fraction. Cumulative dose near threshold for cardiotoxicity. Requires initiation of HF therapy and close monitoring.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athletic patient reports diffuse body pain triggered by minor injuries that don't resolve.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Previous training regimen now causes disproportionate pain response; activity avoidance has increased.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset following concussion or viral illness; accompanied by cognitive difficulties and persistent fatigue.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized hyperalgesia to light touch, allodynia present, heightened pain response to sensory stimuli.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal structural imaging; functional neuroimaging shows abnormal pain processing in central nervous system.

puzzles.explanation: Fibromyalgia shows post-traumatic or post-infectious onset in younger athlete with prominent central sensitization and neurocognitive symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with anterior knee pain that is difficult to localize, pain quality variable. Intermittent mechanical symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unpredictable symptom pattern, may be absent with similar activities on different days. Response to treatment variable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diagnosis of exclusion after other pathology ruled out, findings do not always correlate with symptoms. Chronic diagnostic challenge.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination findings non-specific, plica palpation has poor reliability. Reproduction of symptoms inconsistent, other structures appear normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows thickened plica but findings common in asymptomatic knees; correlating imaging with symptoms difficult; arthroscopy required if plica intervention considered.

puzzles.explanation: Diagnosis of plica syndrome requires careful exclusion of other pathology and should be made with caution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old new mother reports wrist pain that started 6 weeks after her baby was born. She finds it increasingly difficult to grip things.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: is worst when lifting her baby with an open-hand grip and when wringing out washcloths. Gripping. Twisting movements are particularly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She recently gave birth to her first child six weeks ago and is breastfeeding. She has no history of direct wrist trauma or inflammatory arthritis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness and swelling over the first dorsal compartment. Positive Finkelstein test reproducing the. Crepitus felt over the tendons with thumb movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickened and oedematous extensor pollicis brevis. Abductor pollicis longus tendons with fluid surrounding the tendon sheath in the first dorsal compartment.

puzzles.explanation: De Quervain's tenosynovitis is common in new mothers due to repetitive lifting and holding. The Finkelstein test is the hallmark clinical test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old female with episodic severe vertigo lasting 20 minutes to hours. Associated with unilateral ear fullness, tinnitus, and hearing loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo episodes completely incapacitate during attack. Nausea and vomiting frequent. Between episodes, normal function with residual hearing changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recurrent episodes over 6 months. Progressive unilateral hearing loss, particularly low frequencies. Fluctuating tinnitus (roaring quality).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: During attack: vertical and rotatory nystagmus. Between episodes: variable findings. Weber test shows conductive component in affected ear.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Audiometry shows low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss. Caloric may show reduced response. MRI rules out retrocochlear pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Ménière's Disease shows the classic tetrad of episodic vertigo, fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness in a middle-aged female with recurrent attacks.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with proximal ankle pain persisting beyond expected recovery period; pain with weight-bearing and pivot movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged symptoms, functional limitations persist despite conservative treatment. with dorsiflexion against resistance, sports performance limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Initial injury diagnosis may have been missed, slow recovery. Previous ankle sprain complications, recurrent episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Syndesmotic tenderness present, positive squeeze test. with dorsiflexion-external rotation, positive crossover test. Weight-bearing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows chronic syndesmotic inflammation, stress radiographs may demonstrate persistent widening. Assess for osteochondral injury.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic high ankle sprain symptoms may result from incomplete healing or missed associated injuries.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young adult (25 years old) with high arched feet and foot drop causing frequent tripping.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with ankle dorsiflexion, wears ankle-foot orthosis, and sensory loss in feet affecting proprioception.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Noticed foot deformities as teenager. Father has similar condition. Slowly progressive.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Distal leg atrophy (stork leg appearance), high arches (pes cavus), foot drop, absent ankle reflexes, distal sensory loss.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows demyelinating neuropathy with slowed conduction velocities and genetic testing shows PMP22 duplication.

puzzles.explanation: Type 1 CMT with demyelinating features, characteristic distal lower extremity involvement, and inherited pattern confirmed by genetics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Master's level athlete with patellar tendon pain, symptoms present despite reduced training load, and pain affects competitive performance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with all explosive movements, poor correlation between activity, symptoms, and with eccentric loading minimal with daily activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing issue and multiple treatment failures. Persistent symptoms despite rest periods possible tendon nodules palpable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Focal nodularity palpable in tendon, significant with resisted knee extension, and eccentric quadriceps loading highly provocative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows chronic tendinopathy with significant structural disorganization and possible tears or partial rupture. Considerable degeneration.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic patellar tendinopathy in mature athletes may show structural damage resistant to conservative treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Elderly person with progressive vision loss and with daily activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to read, difficulty recognizing faces, and reduced independence fall risk increased.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age over 75 and family history of macular degeneration. Smoking history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced central vision, scotoma in central field, and drusen present on macula.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: OCT shows macular drusen and atrophy consistent with age-related macular degeneration.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related macular degeneration causes central vision loss affecting reading and recognition, with significant functional impact.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with medial knee pain below joint line, pain localized to proximal medial tibia, and no giving way or locking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with stairs descending, running, symptoms worse with hip adduction, internal rotation, and morning stiffness present.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Often associated with hip adductor or hamstring tightness, may follow hip or knee surgery, and overuse activity tight hamstrings noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness at pes anserine insertion, with resisted hip internal rotation, adduction, and hip flexibility reduced effusion absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI shows bursal fluid, tendon changes, bursa may be thickened, and associated muscle strain visible.

puzzles.explanation: Pes anserine bursitis results from inflammation at the insertion of hamstring and adductor tendons.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset of localised chest wall swelling and pain at the second costochondral junction on the left side. The swelling appeared suddenly several days ago and has been progressively enlarging.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is significantly aggravated by deep inspiration, coughing, and reaching overhead. The patient reports that the affected area is tender to light touch and is concerned by the visible swelling, which he noticed while showering.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He had a mild upper respiratory tract infection with a productive cough two weeks prior. He denies any chest trauma or heavy manual work. Of note, there is no fever, weight loss, or systemic symptoms that would suggest a sinister aetiology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: A visible, firm, fusiform swelling is palpable at the second costochondral junction measuring approximately 3cm. The swelling is warm and tender to direct palpation. Respiratory examination is otherwise clear, and cardiovascular examination is unremarkable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray is normal with no cardiopulmonary pathology. CT of the chest wall confirms focal non-suppurative inflammatory swelling of the costal cartilage at the second costochondral junction, with no features of malignancy or infection. These findings, in combination with the characteristic clinical presentation, confirm the diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Tietze syndrome is a severe inflammatory presentation of costochondritis with visible swelling requiring imaging confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older person with progressive vision loss and difficulty with daily activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Snap reproducible with hip abduction and external rotation; occurring during sports.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Developed after increasing activity; asymptomatic initially then became painful.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable clicking over greater trochanter; positive dynamic hip rotational test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows tensor fasciae latae snapping over trochanter; may see underlying bursal inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Extra-articular snapping from the ITB can become symptomatic with associated bursitis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports chronic progressive arm swelling 2 years post-mastectomy with axillary dissection. Stage 2-3 lymphedema present. Recurrent infections and skin changes occurring.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Arm essentially non-functional for daily activities. Pain and heaviness severe. Repeated infections from skin breakdown interfering with quality of life.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Bilateral breast cancer; left-sided mastectomy with extensive lymph node dissection 2 years ago. Radiation therapy completed. Progressive lymphedema despite early conservative management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe non-pitting lymphedema of entire left arm. Skin thickened and fibrotic with hyperkeratosis. Evidence of previous infections with scarring.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy shows severely impaired lymphatic function in affected limb. Ultrasound shows significant subcutaneous fibrosis. Imaging findings consistent with stage 2-3 lymphedema with fibrotic changes.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic stage 2-3 lymphedema requires intensive compression therapy, potential surgical intervention (lymphaticovenous anastomosis or vascularized lymph node transfer consideration), infection prevention, skin care, and psychological support for functional limitation and appearance concerns.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Newborn holds head tilted to one side with chin rotated away from affected side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Limited neck rotation and lateral flexion; prefers to look toward affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Difficult breech extraction at delivery; birth weight 3.8 kg with large frame.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable mass in sternocleidomastoid muscle, restricted rotation to contralateral side. Limited flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows sternocleidomastoid muscle echogenicity increased with muscle thickening; fibrosis present.

puzzles.explanation: Congenital muscular torticollis results from sternocleidomastoid muscle injury during birth, causing contracture and head posture abnormalities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Active individual with lateral hip symptoms and mild associated discomfort.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms triggered by specific work tasks and functional hand use significantly reduced.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Years of heavy repetitive work, progressive functional decline, and poor ergonomic workplace setup.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hand weakness, reduced dexterity, multiple tender points in forearm, positive compression tests.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Advanced imaging shows nerve compression, muscle atrophy, possible multiple site involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational overuse syndrome shows chronic multi-site involvement from years of heavy manufacturing work.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old female with polyarticular joint pain and morning stiffness lasting 2 hours. Affects small joints of hands symmetrically, wrists,. Feet.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Morning stiffness and prevent normal hand function. Gripping. Fine motor tasks limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset 6 months ago. Progressive functional decline. Smoking history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symmetrical swelling and warmth of MCPs. PIPs. Morning with joint stiffness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays show uniform joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Lab work for rheumatoid factor. Anti-CCP antibodies.

puzzles.explanation: Rheumatoid Arthritis is the classic presentation of early RA in a middle-aged woman with symmetric polyarticular involvement, morning stiffness, and positive serology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult with groin or hip pain, pain worse with activity and improving with rest. Morning stiffness present.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with stairs and walking long distances, pain with hip flexion and internal rotation. Pain worse after activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset of pain, possible previous hip injury or surgery. BMI elevated, pain progressively worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Limited hip internal rotation and flexion, pain at end-range hip movements. Trendelenburg gait present, pain with log-roll test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays show joint space narrowing, osteophytes, and sclerosis characteristic of osteoarthritis.

puzzles.explanation: Hip osteoarthritis causes progressive cartilage loss and is common with aging and previous injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old with advanced lung cancer presents with acute dyspnea and syncope during chemotherapy treatment.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sudden onset severe dyspnea at rest, hemodynamic instability. Syncope episode noted. Chest pain present.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Stage IV lung cancer with metastases. Receiving active chemotherapy. No DVT history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severely hypoxemic (O2 sat 78%), hypotensive, tachycardic. Distended neck veins. Right heart strain signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT angiography shows massive PE in bilateral pulmonary arteries. Right ventricular dilation and dysfunction on echo. Troponin.

puzzles.explanation: Massive PE presentation in cancer patient with hemodynamic instability and right ventricular failure. Malignancy is significant thrombosis risk, and presentation requires urgent intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Athlete reports bilateral shin pain limiting training capacity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation affecting running performance and training compliance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic symptoms from high-volume training; poor recovery between sessions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral tenderness, weakness in foot inverters and evertors, lower extremity biomechanical dysfunction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bilateral periosteal changes on imaging, no fracture line; significant training-related stress evident.

puzzles.explanation: Shin splints shows bilateral medial tibial stress syndrome from sustained high training volume.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 70-year-old female is referred to physiotherapy day 1 post-operatively after elective surgery for severe right knee osteoarthritis. She reports pain and difficulty bending her knee.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to independently transfer from bed to chair. Cannot achieve 90 degrees of knee flexion. Quadriceps activation is poor — difficulty performing a straight leg raise without an extensor lag.

clues.label: History
clues.text: End-stage tricompartmental knee OA with failed conservative management over 4 years. Pre-operative range: 5-95 degrees. BMI 33.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wound clean and dry with staples in situ. Moderate swelling. Active ROM 0-70 degrees (day 1).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-ray shows well-aligned cemented total knee prosthesis with no evidence of periprosthetic fracture. Component alignment within acceptable parameters on AP and lateral views.

puzzles.explanation: Day 1 post-TKR rehabilitation focusing on early mobilisation, ROM recovery, quadriceps activation, and discharge planning — the standard acute post-surgical physiotherapy pathway.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old runner presents with progressive medial foot pain and visible arch flattening that has worsened over four months. She notes the arch appears lower when standing compared to sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worse with weight-bearing and walking; progressive functional limitation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Follows inversion injury or develops insidiously with flat foot deformity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Medial midfoot tenderness, positive spring ligament palpation. Arch flattening.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows ligament disruption; ultrasound demonstrates gap in ligament.

puzzles.explanation: Spring ligament injury compromises arch support and can lead to progressive flatfoot deformity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old female with sensation of bulging or heaviness in the vaginal area. Visible bulging when standing. Associated with difficulty evacuating bowel.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Heaviness worsens throughout day with activity. Improves when lying flat. Walking and standing limited by dragging sensation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Three vaginal deliveries with difficult labor. Chronic constipation and straining over 40 years. Post-menopausal for 20 years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Grade 3 cystocele with anterior wall prolapsing beyond introitus. Significant rectocele with posterior wall prolapse. Weak perineal body.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic MRI with straining shows extensive pelvic floor descent. Defecography demonstrates rectocele. Cystocele extends beyond introitus.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic Organ Prolapse shows severe multicompartment prolapse (anterior and posterior) in an elderly female with long history of straining and childbirth trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient presents with posterior knee swelling, calf symptoms mimicking deep vein thrombosis, and posterior knee tightness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms exacerbated by walking, calf may be present. Pain in lower leg, cyst may rupture causing acute symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Underlying knee osteoarthritis or meniscal pathology, cyst may rupture with sudden activity. increases progressively.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Popliteal fossa mass present, calf and erythema if ruptured. Positive signs for primary knee pathology, neurovascular exam normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows large cyst with possible rupture, fluid extravasation into calf. Underlying knee joint changes associated with OA or meniscal.

puzzles.explanation: Ruptured Baker's cyst can mimic serious conditions and produce calf symptoms requiring careful differential diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old football player experiences sudden burning pain down arm after tackle.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Immediate functional loss, burning along arm, and symptoms may resolve quickly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute impact during contact sport and neck distraction or shoulder compression mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Acute weakness and burning along nerve distribution. Neurapraxia findings may resolve during game.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI may show nerve edema, often normal in neurapraxia, and assess for avulsion in severe cases.

puzzles.explanation: Acute brachial plexus neurapraxia (stinger) from contact sport mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with chronic midfoot pain, dysfunction, previous ankle sprain with inadequate healing, and progressive midfoot symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with walking, weight-bearing, midfoot swelling chronic, and functional limitations persist with athletic activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Missed acute midfoot injury and inadequate initial treatment. Progressive degenerative changes chronic instability symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Midfoot swelling, tenderness, with abduction stress, and possible midfoot deformity gait abnormality evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows chronic Lisfranc changes and potential degenerative changes. Assess for post-traumatic oa demonstrate functional instability.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Lisfranc injury results from missed or inadequately treated acute injury causing long-term dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Elderly patient recovering from acute delirium now has functional decline.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: deconditioned, confusion persisting, and mobility severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent hospitalization with sepsis and delirium resolved but recovery slow.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Persistent cognitive impairment, severe weakness, and deconditioning evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows no structural cause and cognitive testing shows impairment.

puzzles.explanation: Post-delirium recovery requires intensive rehabilitation for deconditioning and persistent cognitive effects.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Athlete or active person with mid-back dysfunction limiting performance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Restriction affecting sport-specific movements and mobility improves with warm-up.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Activity-related, training demands exceed mobility, and responsive to intervention.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Movement restriction in functional patterns and good strength. Responsive to mobilization.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: No imaging needed, diagnosis functional, and mobility assessment key.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic spine dysfunction is activity-related functional limitation responsive to treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Child with previous normal neck development now has head tilt.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Recently limited neck rotation, prefers head in one position, and possible dystonia.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Missed acute midfoot injury and inadequate initial treatment. Progressive degenerative changes chronic instability symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Spastic or dystonic head tilt, possible dystonic features, and abnormal muscle tone.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI may show CNS pathology, metabolic screening needed, and genetic testing considered.

puzzles.explanation: Acquired later-onset torticollis may reflect dystonia or CNS pathology requiring metabolic and genetic investigation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old male athlete presents with intermittent knee pain that has been worsening over the past three months. He plays competitive basketball and notices the knee sometimes catches or locks during games.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are aggravated by running, jumping, and pivoting movements. He reports occasional episodes where the knee feels like it locks momentarily before releasing. There is mild swelling after intense activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No specific traumatic injury recalled. The symptoms began insidiously and have gradually progressed. He has increased his training load significantly over the past six months in preparation for the competitive season.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: There is tenderness on palpation of the medial femoral condyle. Wilson's test is positive, with pain on internal rotation of the tibia during extension that resolves with external rotation. A small effusion is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals a well-defined osteochondral lesion of the medial femoral condyle with evidence of partial fragment separation. The overlying cartilage shows signs of instability with fluid signal tracking beneath the fragment.

puzzles.explanation: Osteochondritis dissecans most commonly affects adolescent athletes, with the medial femoral condyle being the most frequent site. Wilson's test is a useful clinical screening tool, and MRI is essential for staging the lesion and guiding management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports knee swelling and pain 6 weeks after reconstructive surgery. Minimal instability sensation reported. Quadriceps weakness noted with difficulty walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking on flat surfaces improving but stairs remain challenging. Difficulty with pivoting and cutting movements. Unable to bear weight on affected leg for dynamic activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute ACL tear sustained during soccer with pivot-shift mechanism. Concomitant meniscal tear treated. ACLR performed using bone-patellar tendon-bone autograft 6 weeks ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Effusion present, Lachman test shows improved stability compared to acute injury. Anterior drawer test moderately positive. Quadriceps lag evident strength approximately 4-/5 and range of motion limited in extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows appropriate graft positioning and early integration into bone tunnels. No graft rupture or retear. Associated meniscal repair healing appropriately.

puzzles.explanation: ACLR rehabilitation requires progressive loading phases respecting graft healing timelines, aggressive quadriceps activation and strengthening, hamstring conditioning for dynamic stability, proprioceptive training, and sport-specific preparation for safe return to activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe right groin and hip pain following a low-energy fall at home. She is unable to bear weight and lies with her right leg shortened and externally rotated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to weight-bear, severe pain with any movement. Immobilized.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Osteoporosis, postmenopausal. Fall from standing, multiple prior fractures.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shortened externally rotated leg, severe pain with log roll. Unable to move.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows femoral neck fracture, DEXA confirms severe osteoporosis. High fracture risk.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporotic femoral neck fracture from minor trauma in elderly with severe bone loss requires surgical fixation and osteoporosis management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adolescent with chronic thoracic pain and postural concerns.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: variable, aggravated by certain sports, and prolonged postures.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive kyphosis noticed by parents, symptomatic since teen years, and adult residual symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Moderately increased kyphosis, some restriction in thoracic extension, and muscle tightness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show healing changes from old disease and mature thoracic kyphosis.

puzzles.explanation: Scheuermann's disease presents in an older adolescent with mature structural changes and chronic pain pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old recreational golfer presents with gradual onset inner arm pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with grasping, throwing, or flexing the wrist and relief with rest and immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute knee ligament tear sustained during soccer with pivot-shift mechanism. Concomitant meniscal tear treated. Ligament reconstruction performed using bone-patellar tendon-bone autograft 6 weeks ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness on palpation of inner bony prominence and pain with resisted wrist flexion and mild weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates tendon inflammation, thickening, and X-rays show no fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Classic medial epicondylitis from golf-related repetitive flexion and pronation activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old female 3 weeks after major knee surgery for severe osteoarthritis. Pain and swelling at surgical site. Limited knee range of motion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk normally; using walker for support. Knee extension and flexion very limited. Pain with weight-bearing and movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe osteoarthritis refractory to conservative treatment. Surgery 3 weeks ago. Standard surgical approach.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant effusion present. Knee flexion 60 degrees, extension lag 10 degrees. Quadriceps inhibition evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays show appropriate prosthetic alignment. Component position good. No instability.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Knee Replacement shows early post-operative period (3 weeks) with expected swelling, stiffness, and need for intensive rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Immobile patient develops significant skin breakdown and tissue loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Bedbound or chair-bound, unable to participate in mobility. High risk patient.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prolonged immobility, inadequate nutrition. Diabetes, infection risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Open wound with partial or full thickness loss, exposed dermis or deeper tissue. Purulent drainage.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI shows tissue depth and any undermining; wound culture positive if infected.

puzzles.explanation: Deep pressure injuries with tissue loss require aggressive wound care, offloading, and management of infection to promote healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old office worker presents with persistent mid-thoracic stiffness and aching that has worsened over six months. She describes a constant dull ache between the shoulder blades that worsens after prolonged sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worse with certain movements, postures, and improved with gentle mobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset, often related to posture, and may follow period of immobility.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced thoracic rotation, tenderness over mid-thoracic segments, and no neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging usually normal and functional restriction without structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic spine dysfunction is mechanical restriction from postural and movement factors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Elderly with persistent hip pain after surgery for fracture.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to achieve functional recovery and mechanical failure of fixation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Proximal femoral fracture repaired 12 weeks prior and now. Instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: with weight-bearing, mechanical instability, and functional decline.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows loss of reduction or screw penetration and osteoporosis limits options.

puzzles.explanation: Subtrochanteric fracture complications from osteoporosis may require revision surgery with different fixation strategy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged patient with progressive leg weakness and difficulty with breathing that worsens toward evening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot climb stairs, requiring mechanical ventilation at night, and weakness improves after rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Started with eye drooping 1 year ago, progressed to generalized weakness over months. Recently hospitalized for myasthenic crisis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: with fatigability, ptosis, and nasal speech positive tensilon test response Notably, normal sensation throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anti-acetylcholine receptor and anti-MuSK antibodies negative (seronegative MG). Repetitive stimulation shows >10% decremental response.

puzzles.explanation: Seronegative myasthenia gravis with generalized weakness, respiratory involvement, and characteristic decremental response on repetitive stimulation testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: School-age child with autism has coordination difficulties and sensory sensitivities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Clumsiness and poor balance. Difficulty with team sports avoids certain textures.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diagnosed with autism at age 3, ongoing speech, and occupational therapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Motor planning, poor proprioception, and unusual gait sensory defensiveness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain connectivity studies may show atypical patterns and developmental motor assessment shows delays.

puzzles.explanation: Autism with motor coordination deficits requires comprehensive evaluation for dyspraxia and sensory processing differences alongside social-communication support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 64-year-old female with recurrent episodes of room-spinning vertigo lasting seconds to minutes. Triggered by lying down or rolling in bed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo occurs with specific positional changes: head tilted back, rolling in bed, looking up. Can predict and avoid triggering positions. Between episodes, completely normal.

clues.label: History
clues.text: proximal femoral fracture repaired 12 weeks prior; now pain and instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Dix-Hallpike maneuver causing brief vertigo and rotatory nystagmus. Symptoms resolve within 60 seconds. Normal gait and balance between episodes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hallmark feature: brief (< 60 seconds) rotatory nystagmus with Dix-Hallpike maneuver. No imaging needed typically. Otoconia from utricle confirmed by characteristic nystagmus.

puzzles.explanation: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo shows recurrent BPPV in an older female after trauma with classic positional trigger and diagnostic Dix-Hallpike response.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged woman with hip pain related to acetabular anatomy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with hip flexion and adduction; limited external rotation in flexion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Often associated with increased acetabular coverage; symptom onset variable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive anterior hip pain provocation, tight adductors. Limited hip external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows over-coverage of acetabulum; coxa profunda or acetabular retroversion.

puzzles.explanation: Pincer-type FAI involves overcoverage of the acetabulum, often with retroversion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman in residential aged care presents with progressive unsteadiness and increasing reluctance to mobilise over the past several months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She has become increasingly dependent for transfers and walking. Staff report she frequently forgets to use her walking frame and becomes confused about where she is going mid-task.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diagnosed with a progressive neurodegenerative condition three years ago. Gradual cognitive decline with recent acceleration. Multiple falls over the past six months, now requiring two-person assist for mobilisation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Impaired balance with eyes open and closed. Shuffling gait pattern with reduced step length and arm swing. Difficulty following multi-step commands during assessment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT brain shows generalised cerebral atrophy with prominent ventricular enlargement. No acute intracranial pathology. Bone density scan reveals osteoporosis, increasing fracture risk with falls.

puzzles.explanation: Progressive cognitive decline leads to mobility deterioration through impaired motor planning, spatial awareness, and safety judgment. Physiotherapy focuses on maintaining function, falls prevention, and carer education.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with sudden loss of leg function and bowel/bladder control following lumbar spine trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe bilateral leg weakness with complete loss of sensation in the perineal area.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Crush injury from heavy equipment at work. Admitted emergently to trauma center.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe bilateral leg weakness (grade 1/5), saddle anesthesia, absent anal wink, absent bulbocavernosus reflex, and flaccid paralysis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows conus medullaris compression with hemorrhagic infarction and significant edema.

puzzles.explanation: Conus medullaris syndrome from lumbar spine crush injury with bilateral leg paralysis, saddle anesthesia, and complete sphincter loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged patient presenting with involuntary jerking movements and mood changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Uncontrolled arm movements preventing sitting still. Personality changes affecting work and relationships.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mother had similar symptoms and died at age 60. Behavioral issues began 2 years ago, followed by choreiform movements 1 year ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized choreiform movements, hypotonia, oculomotor abnormalities, and slow mentation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Genetic testing shows CAG repeat >39 in HTT gene. Brain MRI demonstrates caudate atrophy.

puzzles.explanation: Early-stage Huntington's disease with choreiform movements, behavioral disturbance, and positive family history confirmed by genetic testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old presents with sudden arm pain and visible deformity in the biceps.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weakness with elbow flexion and supination reduces arm strength.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He felt a sudden pop in the upper arm while lifting a heavy box overhead at work three days ago. No prior shoulder surgery or rotator cuff history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Loss of flexion power and supination strength with visible muscle retraction in upper arm.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows absent tendon at shoulder. MRI confirms rupture with retracted muscle.

puzzles.explanation: Long head biceps rupture with classic Popeye deformity from maximal effort.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports significant pain improvement 4 weeks after knee surgery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking improving with single-point cane support. Knee flexion progressing but extension lag persists; sit-to-stand transfers improving.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe osteoarthritis failed conservative management. Total knee arthroplasty performed 4 weeks ago via medial parapatellar approach.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Well-healed incision with mild effusion. Active knee flexion approximately 90 degrees with 15-degree extension lag. Quadriceps weakness evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show proper prosthesis positioning with neutral limb alignment. No evidence of loosening.

puzzles.explanation: TKA rehabilitation requires early extension lag correction, progressive range of motion gains, quadriceps strengthening, functional mobility training, and edema management to achieve optimal prosthetic function and patient satisfaction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 3-year-old child presents with high fever. A 3-year-old child presents with high fever, rapid breathing, and recurrent cough with no improvement over one week.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She is unable to care for her infant and avoids any exertion due to dyspnoea at rest. Breathing effort increases with minimal activity including walking to the bathroom.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She is a 3-month-old infant with no prior hospital admissions. Born at term with no perinatal complications. She has been unwell for four days with poor feeding.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, respiratory rate is 62 breaths per minute with subcostal and intercostal recession. Oxygen saturation is 91% on room air. Auscultation reveals coarse crepitations and reduced air entry in the left lower lobe.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates left lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms. Nasopharyngeal swab is positive for respiratory syncytial virus. Full blood count shows elevated white cell count at 18.2 × 10⁹/L.

puzzles.explanation: Pediatric pneumonia presentation with severe presentation and danger signs requiring hospitalization. Demonstrates how infection impacts children differently with higher respiratory rates and more dramatic clinical signs.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Progressive wound separation noted 10 days post-operatively with increasing drainage.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activity restrictions necessary with rehabilitation delayed. Complex wound care prolongs recovery.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Major surgical procedure 10 days prior with initially unremarkable course. Risk factors include obesity, diabetes, poor nutrition, and immunosuppression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Partial wound separation with serosanguineous drainage. Surrounding tissue induration with minimal erythema; patient systemically stable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows separation of fascial layers with fluid collection. Early abscess formation possible.

puzzles.explanation: Partial wound dehiscence without frank infection may be managed conservatively with meticulous wound care, negative pressure therapy, nutritional support, and delayed closure, or surgically with repeat closure depending on contamination risk and underlying structure exposure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young soccer player reports 'seeing stars' and brief confusion after heading the ball forcefully.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sat out game, reports feeling 'foggy' and having trouble remembering play sequences.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Moment of impact to temporal region. Momentary loss of awareness (estimated 30 seconds). No loss of consciousness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: GCS 15, normal neurological exam, normal pupil response, mild headache, no focal deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head normal; clinical diagnosis based on mechanism and symptom cluster.

puzzles.explanation: Grade 1-2 concussion with transient altered consciousness, post-concussive symptoms (fogginess, memory issues), and normal structural imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young professional with persistent groin discomfort interfering with gym workouts.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered by compound movements like squats and lunges; relieved with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradually worsening over months; no significant trauma or prior injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reproduction of pain with combined movement test; hip external rotation preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: 3D CT imaging demonstrates combined cam and pincer morphology.

puzzles.explanation: Combined FAI types are common and may present with non-specific complaints initially, requiring detailed imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient 34-year-old occupational worker develops chest tightness. A 34-year-old occupational worker develops chest tightness, cough, and wheezing specifically during work shifts and weekends off symptom-free.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen with exposure to workplace aerosols and dust.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Major surgical procedure 10 days ago. Initial post-operative course unremarkable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, diffuse expiratory wheeze is audible on auscultation bilaterally. Peak expiratory flow rate is 52% of predicted. Accessory muscle use is present with mild tachycardia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and no consolidation. Spirometry demonstrates FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.62 with 18% bronchodilator reversibility. Fractional exhaled nitric oxide is elevated at 42 ppb.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational asthma presentation with clear temporal relationship to workplace exposure and improvement with removal from exposure. Requires exposure history and documentation of work-relatedness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adult office worker with lower back pain and shooting pain down the leg.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worse with prolonged sitting and bending forward; improves when lying down or standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain started after lifting a heavy object; positive lower limb radiation below the knee.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive straight leg raise test, diminished ankle reflex. Weakness in foot dorsiflexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows disc material protruding posterolaterally compressing the nerve root.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar disc herniation presents as a classic occupational case with clear mechanical triggers and nerve root compression signs on examination and imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports profound fatigue unrelieved by rest, muscle pain, and impaired cognitive function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant post-exertional malaise with disproportionate symptom worsening after minor activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began gradually following a mild viral illness 14 months ago and have progressively worsened. She has no history of prior chronic fatigue, fibromyalgia, or mood disorder.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced exercise tolerance on testing, cognitive impairment on neuropsychological testing, tender lymph nodes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Low natural killer cell counts, elevated inflammatory markers, abnormal cytokine levels, brain imaging changes.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic fatigue syndrome shows post-viral onset with post-exertional malaise, cognitive dysfunction, and immunological abnormalities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old warehouse worker with sudden leg pain following heavy lifting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with sitting, forward bending, and coughing. Partially relieved by lying on side with knees bent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Two previous back pain episodes over 5 years without leg symptoms. Smoker with sedentary lifestyle outside work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive straight leg raise at 35 degrees reproducing leg pain. Reduced ankle jerk with weak great toe extension (L5 myotome) and sensory changes in L5 dermatome.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows posterolateral disc herniation at L4/L5 compressing the left L5 nerve root.

puzzles.explanation: Classic L4/L5 disc herniation with L5 radiculopathy: dermatomal pain and paraesthesia, weak toe extension, positive SLR, and aggravation with flexion-loading activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adult patient with knee pain after dashboard injury or forced flexion; minimal to moderate swelling reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with kneeling and deep squatting, symptoms worsen with stairs. Less instability sensation compared to ACL injury.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism (motor vehicle collision, fall), knee swells over hours. Patient may not recall complete injury mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive posterior sag sign at rest, posterior drawer test positive with knee at 90 degrees. Quadriceps strength assessment important.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows signal changes and disruption of PCL fibers; often accompanied by collateral ligament or meniscal injury.

puzzles.explanation: PCL injuries are often missed on initial assessment because they produce less obvious instability than ACL tears.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older patient with progressive difficulty reading small print.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Requires glasses for close work. Difficulty with near vision activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual-onset visual changes have been present for approximately 18 months, correlating with advancing age. She has no history of diabetes, hypertension, or prior ocular surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Accommodation reduced. No abnormal retinal findings observed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Refraction testing shows presbyopia. Eye imaging shows normal age-related changes.

puzzles.explanation: Presbyopia is age-related loss of accommodation affecting near vision, managed with reading glasses or bifocals.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of acute vertigo lasting 2-3 hours, accompanied by unilateral ear fullness and roaring tinnitus. The episodes began 18 months ago and have progressively worsened. Between attacks, she reports fluctuating low-frequency hearing loss and mild persistent dizziness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During acute episodes, she is unable to ambulate safely or focus visually due to severe spinning sensation and nausea with vomiting. She avoids crowded spaces and loud environments as these seem to exacerbate her symptoms. She has significantly reduced her work hours due to unpredictable symptom onset.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No recent head trauma or viral illness preceding symptom onset. She denies family history of similar vestibular disorder. Her past medical history includes hypertension managed with medication. She reports salt-sensitive migraines and occasional stress-related symptom clustering.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, during an acute episode, horizontal nystagmus is present with characteristic rotatory component. Romberg test shows significant instability. Dix-Hallpike maneuver does not reproduce vertigo. Caloric testing demonstrates reduced vestibular response on the affected side. Audiometry shows sensorineural hearing loss pattern in low frequencies.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the internal auditory canals reveals subtle endolymphatic hydrops on the affected side, demonstrating enlarged endolymphatic space within the cochlea and vestibule. No cerebellopontine angle mass or retrocochlear pathology is identified. Gadolinium-enhanced imaging confirms hydrops pattern consistent with endolymphatic dysfunction.

puzzles.explanation: Ménière's Disease shows bilateral disease (affecting 15-30% of cases) with progressive hearing loss and vestibular dysfunction on both sides requiring comprehensive management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive low back and hip pain over 2 years, which has significantly impacted his mobility and independence. He reports increasing bone pain in the lumbar spine and proximal femur, particularly with weight-bearing activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: He demonstrates a slightly widened gait and reports pain with prolonged standing. Walking distances have diminished from 2 miles to 200 meters. He experiences pain relief with rest but finds the pain reoccurs with minimal activity, restricting his previously active lifestyle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He has a family history of Paget's disease (father was affected). No recent trauma, but he notes gradual increase in shoe size and hat size over the past decade. Laboratory work shows markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase at 285 U/L (normal 30-120) and elevated urinary hydroxyproline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, the lumbar spine and proximal femurs demonstrate localized warmth indicating increased bone vascularity. Direct percussion over affected areas elicits pain. No neurological deficits currently present, though mild lower extremity weakness (4+/5) noted in hip flexors. Bone deformity palpable in lumbar region with mild anterior bowing of left femur.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the lumbar spine and pelvis show characteristic mixed lytic and sclerotic bone lesions with coarse trabecular pattern. The femoral necks and lumbar vertebrae demonstrate cortical thickening and increased bone density. Radionuclide bone scan reveals significantly increased uptake in multiple sites including lumbar spine, pelvis, and proximal femurs.

puzzles.explanation: Paget's disease of bone is a rare metabolic bone disease with progressive deformity and complication risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old software developer presents with gradually progressive eye strain, blurred vision, and dry eye sensation that worsens throughout the workday. Symptoms began 3 months ago coinciding with transition to full-time remote work. She describes general eye discomfort and occasional mild headaches in the frontal region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are directly correlated with screen time, typically worsening after 2-3 hours of continuous computer use. She reports relief with breaks and looking at distant objects (20-20-20 rule provides immediate improvement). Evening symptoms are minimal when she is not working. Weekend and vacation periods are essentially symptom-free.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She works 8 hours daily at a desk with dual monitors positioned suboptimally (too high and too close). She reports reduced blinking frequency while concentrating on screen work. Optometric examination 6 months ago was normal. She denies recent vision prescription changes. No history of dry eye or systemic autoimmune disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally at distance but becomes 20/40 at near distance (approximately 40 cm). Tear film break-up time is reduced at 4 seconds (normal >5 seconds), indicating aqueous tear deficiency. Ocular surface staining with lissamine green shows scattered punctate epithelial erosions. Accommodation amplitude measured at 10 diopters (expected 12-14 at age 32).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anterior segment optical coherence tomography demonstrates decreased tear meniscus height and thinning of the tear film layer. Meibography reveals mild gland dropout in the lower lid consistent with meibomian gland dysfunction from reduced blinking. No retinal pathology identified on dilated fundoscopy or OCT of optic disc and macula.

puzzles.explanation: Computer vision syndrome shows severe occupational eye strain with accommodation dysfunction and significant vision changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old office manager presents with chronic lower back pain and neck stiffness that has developed over 8 months of desk work. She describes gradual onset of central low back discomfort and progressive cervical tightness without any acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain intensifies significantly after prolonged sitting (>2 hours) and improves markedly with movement, stretching, and postural correction. She reports morning stiffness that resolves within 30 minutes of activity. Standing and walking provide symptomatic relief. Pain does not radiate distally and is not aggravated by specific movements or neural tension tests.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Her workstation has been recently assessed and found to have inadequate monitor height (too low), causing sustained forward head posture. She works 8-10 hours daily with minimal breaks. She denies history of significant spinal trauma or degenerative disc disease. No neurological symptoms, no bowel/bladder changes. General health is good with no systemic disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, postural assessment reveals prominent forward head posture (approximately 3 cm anterior to plumb line) with thoracic kyphosis exaggeration and anterior pelvic tilt. Cervical and lumbar range of motion are full but slightly limited by muscle tightness. Lumbar flexion shows reversal of normal curve but no radicular pain. Straight leg raise 85 degrees bilaterally. Slump test negative. Palpation reveals sustained muscle tension in cervical paraspinals and lumbar erector spinae.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the lumbar and cervical spine show preserved disc heights and normal alignment without degenerative changes. MRI of the lumbar spine demonstrates normal disc signal intensity, normal facet joints, and no central canal stenosis. Cervical MRI shows preserved lordotic curve with normal disc hydration. No neural compression or structural pathology identified.

puzzles.explanation: Postural syndrome shows work-related postural pain in manual worker with core dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 27-year-old overhead throwing athlete (baseball pitcher) presents with progressive medial elbow pain over 6 weeks. The pain is localized to the medial aspect of the elbow and has progressively worsened with continued pitching activity despite attempting rest periods.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specifically triggered by throwing, particularly during the late cocking phase and deceleration phase of the pitch. He reports ability to perform other upper extremity activities without pain. Throwing velocity has decreased noticeably, and he cannot maintain control of pitches. Non-throwing activities cause minimal discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He increased his pitching volume significantly 2 months ago without adequate progression in training load. He reports no specific acute traumatic event but rather insidious onset with progressive symptoms. He played through mild pain initially, thinking it would resolve. No previous elbow surgery or significant injury to this extremity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, point tenderness is elicited directly over the medial collateral ligament origin. The moving valgus stress test reproduces pain (also called dynamic valgus stress test). Milking maneuver is positive, reproducing sharp pain over the ligament. Manual valgus stress at 90 degrees elbow flexion demonstrates increased laxity compared to contralateral side with a soft end-feel. Grip strength is reduced on affected side. No nerve involvement signs on upper limb neurological testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the elbow reveals partial thickness tear of the anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament with associated edema at the ligament origin. High signal intensity within the ligament substance indicates ligamentous disruption. Small joint effusion present. Flexor-pronator origin shows mild edema consistent with associated overuse strain. No osteochondral lesion identified.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic UCL insufficiency from cumulative overhead throwing overuse.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman is 8 weeks post-total hip replacement for primary osteoarthritis. She reports persistent groin pain and lateral hip pain at the surgical site with difficulty with functional mobility including walking and stair climbing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She ambulates with an antalgic gait and a cane. Hip flexion beyond 90 degrees causes significant discomfort, limiting her ability to sit comfortably or use stairs independently. Hip abduction weakness prevents independent side-lying and transitions. She requires modified activities for dressing, bathing, and toileting. Pain is present with most functional activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She underwent total hip replacement via anterolateral approach 8 weeks ago for severe hip osteoarthritis. She completed standard post-operative protocol. She has well-controlled hypertension and type 2 diabetes. She denies episodes of mechanical catching or giving way. Her home environment includes stairs and limited accessibility modifications.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, she demonstrates significant hip flexion weakness (3/5) and hip abduction weakness (2+/5). Hip flexion range is 85 degrees limited by pain and stiffness. Hip abduction range is 25 degrees with weakness noted. Hip extension appears functional at 15 degrees. No apparent signs of hip instability with log-roll test or flexion-adduction test. Mild swelling present around anterior surgical site. Sensation intact to light touch throughout lower extremity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays show appropriate acetabular cup positioning (abduction 45 degrees, anteversion 20 degrees) and femoral stem alignment in neutral position without varus or valgus deformity. No signs of immediate aseptic loosening. Hip radiographs demonstrate maintained joint space with prosthetic articular surfaces appropriately seated. CT scan confirms no component malposition and intact bone stock.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Hip Replacement shows mid-term recovery (4 months) with good pain outcome but persistent hip weakness limiting functional progress.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man with a history of recurrent shoulder pain presents with acute worsening of his chronic shoulder discomfort following a fall onto his right shoulder 2 weeks ago. He reports constant aching pain in the lateral shoulder and difficulty with overhead activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: He has marked weakness with attempts to raise his arm overhead, which significantly limits reaching activities and self-care. Sleeping on the affected shoulder is impossible. Passive motion causes less pain than active motion. Pain is worse with lifting, pulling, and throwing activities. He avoids these activities due to weakness and sharp pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He has a 2-year history of intermittent right shoulder pain from overuse related to his work as a carpenter. He had not sought formal treatment, managing with over-the-counter analgesics. The acute trauma 2 weeks ago appears to have converted his chronic tendinopathy into a symptomatic tear. He reports no prior shoulder surgery or significant trauma to this shoulder.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, external rotation is significantly weakened (2/5) and internal rotation strength is preserved (4+/5). The supraspinatus strength test (empty can test) is positive and markedly weak (2/5). Abduction is weak at 3/5 with pain. Drop arm test is positive, indicating inability to lower arm smoothly from 90 degrees abduction. Infraspinatus atrophy is visible compared to contralateral shoulder. Apprehension and relocation tests are negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the right shoulder reveals a full-thickness rotator cuff tear of the supraspinatus measuring 1.2 cm in anteroposterior dimension with retraction. Fatty infiltration is present in the supraspinatus muscle at stage 2. The infraspinatus is intact. Acromion is hypertrophic (curved type) contributing to subacromial space narrowing. Acromioclavicular joint osteoarthritis is mild.

puzzles.explanation: Full-thickness rotator cuff tear with acute drop arm sign and weakness on examination.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 64-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 9.2%) presents with progressive bilateral foot pain and numbness over 18 months that has significantly impacted his mobility and quality of life. He describes the sensation as burning feet and loss of feeling, particularly in the toes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking distances are limited due to burning pain in his feet, and he reports difficulty with balance and proprioception, particularly in darkness. He has had episodes of stumbling. Nighttime pain significantly disrupts sleep quality. He avoids walking barefoot due to numbness and fear of injury. Standing tolerance is reduced to 15-20 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes 12 years ago but has had inconsistent glycemic control due to poor medication adherence. He also has hypertension and elevated cholesterol. No history of significant leg trauma or previous lower extremity nerve injury. He denies chemotherapy exposure. Vitamin B12 level is normal.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, ankle reflexes are bilaterally diminished (1+/4). Vibratory sense is markedly reduced at both ankles (perceives only 2/8 seconds of tuning fork). Proprioception is impaired in toes (unable to detect direction of passive toe movement). Two-point discrimination is diminished in feet (distance >1 cm). Monofilament testing shows decreased sensation to 5.07 monofilament in distal lower extremities bilaterally. Motor strength is 4+/5 in foot dorsiflexors with distal lower extremity weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies demonstrate reduced conduction velocities and decreased amplitudes in both peroneal and tibial nerves consistent with demyelination. Distal latencies are prolonged. F-waves are abnormal or absent. Electromyography shows denervation potentials (fibrillations and positive sharp waves) in intrinsic foot muscles, confirming axonal degeneration. MRI of lumbar spine is normal without disc herniation or stenosis contributing to symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexopathy with acute painful onset, proximal weakness, and focal denervation pattern atypical for distal neuropathy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old woman presents with profound and persistent fatigue lasting 4 years that is not relieved by rest and significantly limits her daily functioning. She reports that previously modest exertion leaves her exhausted for days afterward, preventing any form of work or hobbies.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities that previously required minimal effort (grocery shopping, light housework, brief walks) now precipitate severe fatigue and symptom exacerbation lasting days. She describes post-exertional malaise where activity triggers worsening of fatigue and other symptoms. She has become essentially housebound, spending most days in bed or on the couch.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Her symptoms began insidiously approximately 2 months after a presumed viral illness. Prior to illness, she was an active professional with full work capacity. She reports new-onset difficulties with concentration and short-term memory. She denies depression, major life stressors, or psychiatric illness. Comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid function, and vitamin levels are normal.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, mental status is clear and oriented, though she demonstrates slowed processing and occasional difficulty with recall. No signs of joint inflammation or objective muscle weakness (5/5 strength throughout). Neurological examination is otherwise normal. Vital signs show no significant orthostatic hypotension. Cognitive testing reveals mild impairment in attention and processing speed.
