clues.text: A 52-year-old office worker presents with sharp, radiating pain from the neck extending into the right shoulder, arm, and hand. The pain is described as burning with associated numbness and tingling in the fourth and fifth fingers, affecting daily function significantly.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged sitting and repetitive reaching overhead significantly aggravate symptoms. Cervical extension and ipsilateral rotation worsen the radiating pain acutely. Relief is obtained with gentle neck flexion and use of a supportive cervical pillow during sleep.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began gradually two months ago without clear trauma, though occupational posture has been poor for several years. The patient has had previous episodes of neck pain that resolved with conservative management. Recent increased keyboard work preceded the current exacerbation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the right upper limb tension test produces reproduction of arm pain and dysesthesia. Cervical spine palpation reveals restriction in extension and rotation toward the right. Grip strength is reduced on the affected side, and the C7 dermatome demonstrates diminished sensation to light touch.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the cervical spine demonstrates a left-sided herniated disc at C5-C6 with lateral recess stenosis compressing the C6 nerve root. The signal in the root itself shows evidence of irritation, and moderate foraminal narrowing is present on the affected side.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical radiculopathy has atypical features with inconsistent examination findings and unclear pathoanatomical correlation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old elite soccer player presents with insidious-onset pain at the pubic symphysis that has progressed over three weeks. The pain is felt in the midline lower abdomen and groin, radiating toward the medial thigh on both sides with variable intensity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The condition worsened significantly with high-impact activities, twisting movements, and kicking, forcing the athlete to reduce training volume by 50 percent. Walking with adduction against resistance reproduces sharp pain, whereas relative rest and gentle stretching provide temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient recently increased training frequency and intensity without adequate progression. A previous history of similar symptoms resolved with conservative management six months ago. Current symptoms emerged after a five-day intensive training camp with emphasis on explosive movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation of the pubic symphysis directly elicits tenderness and reproduces the patient's complaints. The adductor squeeze test performed at 45 degrees hip flexion reproduces pain, and crossed adductor palpation reveals muscle tenderness bilaterally. Hip abduction strength is reduced against resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray imaging of the pelvis shows irregularity and sclerosis of the pubic symphysis with marginal osteophyte formation bilaterally. Magnetic resonance imaging reveals bone marrow edema at the pubic rami and inflammatory changes surrounding the symphysis, consistent with degenerative and inflammatory pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Osteitis pubis results from chronic overload at the pubic symphysis, common in distance runners.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old professional baseball pitcher presents with acute onset of sharp medial elbow pain during the late cocking phase of pitching 3 weeks ago. The pain is severe enough to prevent continued pitching and has progressively limited his throwing ability.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Any throwing activity, especially high-velocity pitching, reproduces sharp pain over the medial elbow. Non-throwing upper extremity activities including strength training and daily tasks are pain-free. He is unable to return to competitive pitching. Resting the arm has provided some mild symptom improvement, but pain persists with attempted throwing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He is a high-level athlete with 20 years of throwing experience. He denies any specific traumatic event or fall on the elbow. He performed his normal pre-season training and conditioning program. No prior significant elbow injury or surgery. He was evaluated by team physicians who have suggested potential ligament injury and imaging confirmation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, point tenderness is localized directly over the medial collateral ligament. The moving valgus stress test is positive, reproducing sharp pain in the medial elbow with valgus stress applied at 90 degrees of elbow flexion during external rotation of the shoulder. Manual valgus stress reveals increased ligamentous laxity with a soft end-feel. Milking maneuver is positive. Upper extremity neurological examination is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the elbow demonstrates a complete full-thickness tear of the anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament with discontinuity of the ligament fibers and surrounding edema. The posterior bundle is intact. Significant joint effusion is present. No associated osteochondral lesions identified. No flexor-pronator tendinopathy noted. The tear is located at the proximal origin of the ligament.

puzzles.explanation: UCL injury in overhead athlete from valgus overload during throwing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old recreational basketball player reports posterior shoulder pain and apprehension that occurs with abduction and external rotation. The patient recalls a specific anterior shoulder dislocation incurred two weeks prior during competition that required emergency reduction.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Movements combining shoulder abduction with external rotation trigger both pain and a sensation of instability or 'slipping.' Overhead throwing and defensive positioning worsen symptoms acutely. Resting the shoulder and avoiding provocative movements provides significant relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The initial dislocation was the first major shoulder injury the patient sustained. Post-reduction imaging was not obtained, and the patient received brief immobilization before premature return to sport. Previous minor shoulder strains did not result in functional limitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive apprehension and relocation tests (Crank and Jobe) demonstrate anteroposterior instability. The posterior shoulder demonstrates loss of bone contour on palpation compared to the contralateral side. Resisted external rotation at 90 degrees abduction reproduces posterior shoulder apprehension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the shoulder joint reveals a compression fracture of the posterolateral humeral head consistent with a Hill-Sachs lesion. The lesion measures approximately 20 percent of the humeral head articular surface, and associated soft tissue damage to the anterior labrum is noted.

puzzles.explanation: Hill-Sachs lesion from recurrent anterior shoulder dislocations with posterolateral defect.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old female reports a sensation of heaviness or bulging in the posterior vaginal wall. The patient notes difficulty with bowel evacuation and describes manual splinting of the perineum during defecation to facilitate adequate emptying, a symptom that has worsened over two years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged standing and physical exertion increase the sensation of pelvic heaviness and bulging. The patient experiences incomplete evacuation despite prolonged straining, and symptoms improve with lying supine. Chronic straining during defecation has become necessary to achieve adequate bowel emptying.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient had three vaginal deliveries over her reproductive years with the largest baby weighing 4.2 kilograms. Chronic constipation has been present for over a decade, requiring regular use of stool softeners. Recent weight gain and poor abdominal muscle tone have coincided with symptom progression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On vaginal examination, a bulge of the posterior vaginal wall is observed with Valsalva maneuver, extending approximately 3 centimeters beyond the hymenal ring. Pelvic floor muscle strength is graded as 3/5 on digital assessment, and perineal descent measures 4 centimeters during straining.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Defecography demonstrates posterior displacement of the rectovaginal septum measuring 3.5 centimeters with retention of barium in the rectocele during evacuation. Dynamic magnetic resonance imaging confirms the anatomical abnormality with evidence of altered anorectal angle during straining and defecation.

puzzles.explanation: Rectocele management requires pelvic floor strengthening, dietary fiber increase, bowel habit modification, avoiding excessive straining, potentially pessary use, and surgical repair (posterior colporrhaphy) if conservative measures fail and defecatory dysfunction significant.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old female presents with widespread, non-specific pain affecting multiple body regions including neck, shoulders, lower back, and bilateral knees. The pain is variable in location and intensity, with associated fatigue, poor sleep quality, and cognitive difficulties that have persisted for over two years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient demonstrates inconsistent responses to physical activity, with some days tolerating light exercise while other days minimal activity provokes exacerbation. Pain appears disproportionate to tissue damage and can be triggered by minor physical stressors or emotional stress. Improvement plateaus despite escalating intervention intensity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began insidiously following a workplace injury with subsequent multiple investigations revealing minimal structural pathology. The patient has undergone numerous treatments with limited sustained benefit, leading to increasing disability. Significant stress at work preceded symptom onset by approximately six months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, widespread allodynia is demonstrated with pain response to light touch across multiple body regions. The 11-point tender point examination meets diagnostic criteria with significant pain provocation. Neurological examination is otherwise normal, and strength and reflexes are intact throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the affected joints reveals minimal degenerative change inconsistent with symptom severity. Functional magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates altered central pain processing with amplified responses in pain-sensitive brain regions. Laboratory investigations exclude systemic inflammatory or rheumatological conditions.

puzzles.explanation: Central sensitisation shows nociplastic pain mechanisms with amplified pain responses, sensory sensitivities, and evidence of central nervous system dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old male with a history of repetitive elbow contact presents with swelling and redness over the posterior elbow. The onset occurred acutely over two days with mild to moderate discomfort and restriction of elbow flexion, though overall arm function is relatively preserved.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities involving prolonged elbow flexion or direct pressure on the posterior elbow significantly worsen discomfort and swelling. Leaning on the elbow during work activities exacerbates both pain and inflammation. Rest with elbow extension and ice application temporarily reduce symptoms and swelling.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient's occupation involves frequent contact of the elbows with work surfaces throughout the day. Prior episodes of minor elbow inflammation occurred but resolved spontaneously with relative rest. Recent increased work demands have coincided with the current acute exacerbation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, significant swelling is palpable over the olecranon with localized warmth and mild erythema. The fluid collection is non-fluctuant at this stage, suggesting early serous accumulation. Elbow flexion range of motion is mildly restricted by swelling, but passive and active extension is pain-free.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging demonstrates fluid accumulation in the olecranon bursa measuring approximately 1.5 centimeters in anteroposterior dimension with echogenic particles suggestive of inflammatory material. Magnetic resonance imaging confirms serous bursitis without evidence of bursal infection, and surrounding soft tissue edema is minimal.

puzzles.explanation: Septic presentation of olecranon bursitis from traumatic inoculation, requiring drainage and antibiotics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 47-year-old office worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, middle, and medial aspect of the ring finger. The symptoms are worst at night and early morning, often waking the patient from sleep, and are occasionally accompanied by mild pain in the forearm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Keyboard and mouse work throughout the day significantly aggravates symptoms, with numbness intensifying as the workday progresses. Driving and gripping activities exacerbate paresthesias acutely. Symptoms improve markedly with wrist splinting at night and frequent breaks with wrist stretching during the day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has worked as a typist for 18 years with increasing repetitive strain on both wrists. Symptoms began three months ago and have progressively worsened despite ergonomic modifications. Recent weight gain and onset of hypothyroidism have coincided with symptom escalation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive Phalen test sustained for 30 seconds reproduces paresthesias in the median nerve distribution. Percussion of the median nerve at the wrist (Tinel sign) provokes tingling in the fingers. Grip strength is preserved, but two-point discrimination is impaired in the index finger.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies demonstrate significantly prolonged distal latency of the median nerve motor response (5.8 milliseconds) and reduced median sensory nerve conduction velocity. Ultrasound imaging reveals cross-sectional swelling of the median nerve with a cross-sectional area of 12 mm squared at the carpal tunnel inlet.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary CTS from rheumatoid arthritis with prominent nocturnal symptoms and bilateral presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old retired teacher presents with shoulder pain and stiffness in the glenohumeral region. Pain is described as dull ache at rest and sharp with movement attempts. Functional limitations include difficulty with overhead activities and hand-to-mouth movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports severe difficulty with activities of daily living including dressing, grooming, and reaching for objects above shoulder height. Pain worsens significantly with external rotation and abduction movements. Passive motion is more tolerable than active attempts.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient underwent surgical intervention 6 weeks ago for end-stage joint degeneration. Medical history includes osteoarthritis affecting multiple joints and controlled hypertension. Pre-operative imaging showed bone-on-bone articulation and severe cartilage loss.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, passive external rotation at 0 degrees abduction measures 35 degrees with guarding. Active assisted abduction is 65 degrees; active motion is markedly limited at 30 degrees. Rotator cuff muscles grade 2/5 due to pain inhibition. Forward flexion passive range is 110 degrees with significant end-feel restriction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs show appropriate prosthetic component positioning with good bony support. No signs of loosening, subluxation, or periprosthetic fracture. CT scan confirms stable component fixation and adequate cement mantle distribution.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Shoulder Replacement shows early post-operative period (6 weeks) with expected immobilization, passive-only motion phase, and pain management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old tradesperson presents with progressive pain localized to the base of the great toe. Pain is worse during weight-bearing activities and pushing off during walking. Patient reports noticeable stiffness in this area that worsens with activity throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient describes difficulty with propulsion activities including running, walking on inclines, and climbing stairs. Pain and loss of motion occur with plantarflexion and extension of the great toe. Flat shoes cause increased discomfort compared to those with a rocker design.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient reports a 2-year progressive course with intermittent swelling noticed after work shifts. No significant trauma, though patient recalls landing awkwardly on the foot during activity 3 years ago. Family history notable for osteoarthritis affecting other joints.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, dorsiflexion of the great toe measures only 15 degrees with firm end-feel; plantarflexion is preserved at 45 degrees. Palpation reveals osteophyte formation at the medial aspect of the first metatarsophalangeal joint. Passive motion elicits pain at end-range, and crepitus is noted during movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate marginal osteophytes at the first metatarsophalangeal joint with joint space narrowing. The articular surface shows characteristic flattening, and subchondral sclerosis is evident on both sides of the articulation.

puzzles.explanation: Hallux rigidus is osteoarthritis of the first metatarsophalangeal joint limiting dorsiflexion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old woman in her third trimester presents with unilateral pain in the lower back and buttock region. Pain onset occurred approximately 8 weeks ago and has progressively worsened with advancing pregnancy. Patient describes the discomfort as sharp and aching, affecting activities and sleep quality.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain with walking, climbing stairs, and rolling over in bed. Certain positions such as standing on one leg or crossing legs exacerbate symptoms significantly. Pain is notably worse at the end of the day and after prolonged standing or walking activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: This is the patient's second pregnancy; she had a complication-free first pregnancy 6 years prior. Current pregnancy is progressing normally without complications. Hormonal factors and weight gain associated with pregnancy are contributing factors. No prior history of low back or pelvic pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the long dorsal sacroiliac ligament test reproduces pain on the affected side. Patrick's test and FABER positioning elicit discomfort in the sacroiliac region. Palpation over the sacroiliac joint produces tenderness; midline sacral tenderness is absent. Hip abductor strength is reduced due to pain inhibition.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates normal fetal development with appropriate amniotic fluid volume. No structural abnormalities are noted in pelvic anatomy. MRI, if indicated, would show no degenerative changes or inflammatory findings in sacroiliac joints or supporting structures.

puzzles.explanation: Antenatal pelvic girdle pain results from hormonal effects on pelvic ligaments, weight gain, postural changes, and diastasis recti. Treatment requires pelvic stability exercises, pelvic support garments, activity modification, and reassurance regarding postpartum resolution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old manual laborer who underwent amputation 8 months ago presents with pain sensation in the absent limb. The patient describes burning, cramping, and shooting sensations in the amputated region. Pain occurs spontaneously and is not influenced by pressure on the residual limb.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain increases with prosthetic use and during weight-bearing activities. Pain is worse in the morning and evening hours. Emotional stress, weather changes, and changes in air pressure appear to exacerbate symptoms. Distraction and engaging activities provide temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Amputation was secondary to traumatic crush injury sustained in occupational accident with subsequent infection. Initial post-operative pain was well-controlled, but phantom pain emerged gradually over weeks following healing. No prior peripheral nerve injuries or chronic pain conditions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination of the residual limb, sensory testing shows altered sensation including hypersensitivity and allodynia in the medial aspect. No active trigger points identified in residual muscle mass. Palpation over the distal end elicits radiating sensations into the phantom region. Range of motion of adjacent joints is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the residual limb shows well-healed amputation site with normal bone formation and scar tissue maturation. No evidence of neuroma formation on high-resolution ultrasound or MRI. Stump shows normal vascularization without arterial insufficiency on vascular imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Phantom limb pain shows chronic refractory pain with residual limb contractures and extensive maladaptive cortical changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old patient admitted to intensive care 3 days ago following sepsis from pneumonia presents with severe dyspnea and hypoxemia. The patient requires mechanical ventilation and supplemental oxygen. Rapid onset of respiratory failure occurred over hours despite initial antibiotic therapy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is unable to perform any self-care activities due to critical illness and mechanical ventilation requirement. Respiratory effort is significantly compromised with minimal tolerance for any activity. Oxygen saturation drops rapidly with minimal exertion or positional changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient developed sepsis secondary to community-acquired pneumonia 5 days prior to ICU admission. Medical history includes chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and diabetes. Inflammatory markers are markedly elevated with worsening clinical trajectory despite antibiotic therapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, bilateral crackles are audible throughout all lung fields. Chest wall compliance is severely reduced. Arterial blood gas shows refractory hypoxemia with PaO2 of 65 mmHg despite FiO2 0.8. Bilateral infiltrates are confirmed on chest auscultation and consistent with diffuse alveolar damage.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates bilateral diffuse infiltrates consistent with pulmonary edema of non-cardiogenic origin. CT scan reveals extensive ground-glass opacities and consolidation throughout bilateral lung fields. Cardiac silhouette is normal on imaging, and pulmonary edema is not secondary to cardiac dysfunction.

puzzles.explanation: ARDS from sepsis with classic bilateral infiltrates, severe hypoxemia, and pulmonary edema. Requires aggressive respiratory management with lung-protective ventilation strategies and sepsis source control.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old recreational athlete presents with lateral knee pain and a sensation of the knee 'giving way' during activity. Pain is localized just below the lateral knee joint line. Episodes occur unpredictably with certain movements, causing the patient to feel unstable and apprehensive.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports symptoms with pivoting movements, lateral bounding, and activities requiring rapid direction changes. Pain and instability are worse with running on uneven surfaces. Single-leg standing and jumping activities trigger episodes of giving way or shifting sensation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient sustained a direct blow to the lateral knee during contact sport 2 years ago. Initial swelling resolved, but persistent lateral instability began after return to activity. No prior knee injuries or ligamentous laxity in other joints. Athlete reports lack of confidence when changing direction during play.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the anterior drawer test of the fibula is positive with subluxation noted. Palpation reveals prominence of the fibular head with reproduction of symptoms. The fibular head glides anteriorly with passive knee flexion. Lateral collateral ligament testing is intact; anterolateral rotatory instability is not demonstrated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates ligamentous disruption of the anterior and posterior tibiofibular ligaments with evidence of chronic instability. High-resolution ultrasound shows fibular head translation of 8mm compared to the contralateral side. CT imaging confirms anterior subluxation of the fibular head relative to tibial plateau.

puzzles.explanation: Proximal tibiofibular instability is often overlooked as a cause of lateral knee pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden sharp pain in the posterior ankle during a push-off movement while playing badminton. The patient immediately fell and was unable to bear weight. Swelling developed rapidly over the following hours, and ambulation is impossible without assistance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot walk without pain and mechanical inability to push off. Plantarflexion is not possible without assistance, and standing on the toes is impossible. Pain is severe with any attempt at weight-bearing or dorsiflexion stretch of the ankle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient had a 4-week period of increased training intensity prior to the acute injury. No prior ankle injuries or chronic tendon pain reported. Pain in the Achilles region had been absent before the acute event. Patient is generally fit and active in recreational sports.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, a palpable gap is felt in the Achilles tendon approximately 5 cm above the calcaneal insertion. The Thompson test is positive; squeezing the calf does not produce plantarflexion. Ankle plantarflexion strength is graded 0/5. Marked swelling and ecchymosis are visible in the posterior ankle region.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows complete discontinuity of the Achilles tendon with 2 cm separation between tendon ends. MRI demonstrates full-thickness tear at the musculotendinous junction with edema and hemorrhage in surrounding tissues. Sagittal imaging clearly delineates the gap between ruptured segments.

puzzles.explanation: Acute Achilles rupture presents with dramatic loss of function and requires urgent intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 56-year-old woman 2 months post-chemotherapy for breast cancer presents with generalized fatigue, weakness, and difficulty with functional mobility. She reports persistent pain in the surgical scar region and the ipsilateral upper extremity. Functional capacity has declined significantly since treatment onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot perform overhead activities or heavy lifting with the affected arm. Walking tolerance has decreased from 2 miles pre-treatment to 10 minutes currently. Stairs cause significant fatigue, and she requires rest periods throughout the day. Morning stiffness in the affected shoulder lasts 30-45 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and surgical breast reconstruction 8 weeks ago. Post-operative radiation therapy was completed 4 weeks prior. Medical history includes hypertension managed with medication. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy affects bilateral feet and lower legs.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, shoulder abduction is limited to 85 degrees due to pain and tightness. Grip strength is 18 kg on the affected side compared to 28 kg on the contralateral side. Palpation reveals axillary cording with tissue restriction and pain. Sensory testing demonstrates glove-like paresthesia in feet; ankle dorsiflexion strength is 4/5 bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative imaging shows appropriate surgical reconstruction with adequate tissue perfusion and no signs of recurrence. Chest CT imaging obtained during treatment follow-up shows no evidence of distant metastases. MRI of the upper extremity shows no nerve compression or lymphedema formation.

puzzles.explanation: Long-term cancer survivorship requires integrated approach addressing lymphedema management, chemotherapy-related peripheral neuropathy, cancer-related cognitive impairment, mood disorders, and structured rehabilitation for multi-system dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 18-year-old soccer player was struck in the head by another player's elbow during a match 5 days ago. The athlete reports headache, dizziness, and sensitivity to light and noise. Memory of the incident and the preceding 10 minutes is absent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient experiences worsening of symptoms with cognitive tasks and physical exertion. Reading, concentrating on schoolwork, and typing increase headache severity. Nausea occurs with rapid head movements. Balance appears impaired when eyes are closed or on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: This is the patient's first significant head injury; no prior concussions reported. No loss of consciousness documented, though witnessed impactor noted the athlete was dazed immediately after impact. Return-to-play protocols have not been initiated. Athlete is anxious about recovery timeline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the King-Devick test shows slowed reading speed and tracking difficulties. Tandem stance test demonstrates mild balance impairment with 1-2 foot touches required. Rapid alternating movements of hands are slightly uncoordinated bilaterally. Pupil reactivity is normal; cranial nerve examination is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT imaging of the head is normal with no acute intracranial abnormality, hemorrhage, or fracture identified. Baseline neuroimaging prior to injury is not available for comparison. Routine imaging does not show diffuse axonal injury on conventional sequences.

puzzles.explanation: Mild concussion in elderly with post-concussive vertigo and tinnitus secondary to labyrinthine irritation from head trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old competitive swimmer presents with insidious-onset lower back pain localized to one side of the midline. Pain worsens with backward arching or hyperextension movements. The pain is sharp and increases with high-intensity training or sprinting activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient experiences pain with butterfly stroke and backstroke more than freestyle. Pain intensifies with training progression and return to competitive intensity. Prolonged sitting and forward bending are tolerated without significant symptoms. Pain is worst at end-of-day after training.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has trained competitively for 5 years with high training volume and repetitive spinal extension loading. Pain onset occurred 8 weeks ago coinciding with increased training intensity. No traumatic event or acute injury. Family history is notable for low back pain in both parents.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, unilateral lumbar pain is reproduced with single-leg hyperextension test on the affected side. Lumbar extension combined with rotation toward the painful side increases symptoms. Palpation reveals paraspinal muscle tenderness at the level of pain. Lower extremity neurological examination is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan demonstrates a unilateral pars interarticularis defect at the L5 level with intact vertebral body and disc. SPECT imaging shows increased uptake at the symptomatic level, indicating active bone stress. Plain radiographs may appear normal; advanced imaging is required for definitive visualization.

puzzles.explanation: Spondylolysis presents in an adolescent with a growth-related stress fracture during increased physical demands.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old runner presents with plantar forefoot pain localized under the great toe ball of the foot. Pain has developed gradually over 6 weeks and is worsened by running and prolonged standing. The patient describes the discomfort as aching and tenderness in the area.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain with running, particularly with push-off during acceleration phases. Pain worsens with forefoot-strike running patterns and sprinting. Standing on the toes or performing rising-on-toes movements elicits significant discomfort. Minimal pain with walking in supportive footwear.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient recently increased running mileage from 15 to 30 miles per week over 4 weeks. No significant trauma reported, though patient notes minor heel blister 2 months ago that altered gait temporarily. Runs primarily on hard surfaces including concrete and asphalt roads.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation over the medial sesamoid bone reproduces pain and tenderness. Pain is specifically localized to the sesamoid region with surrounding plantar tissue examination unremarkable. Plantarflexion strength of the first metatarsophalangeal joint is reduced due to pain. Single-leg plantarflexion or rising on toes reproduces symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs show the sesamoid bones with normal alignment; no fracture or displacement is evident. MRI demonstrates bone marrow edema within the sesamoid with surrounding plantar soft tissue inflammation. Ultrasound shows increased echogenicity of the sesamoid bone consistent with inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Sesamoiditis involves inflammation of the sesamoid bones under the first metatarsal, common in weight-bearing athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old construction worker sustained a fall from scaffolding 2 weeks ago with immediate impact to the cervical spine region. The patient experienced immediate loss of sensation and motor function bilaterally below the shoulders. Acute hospitalization and spinal stabilization were performed emergently.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is unable to walk or stand independently and requires assistance with all activities of daily living. Upper extremity function is severely limited with absent hand grip bilaterally. Trunk control is absent, and the patient cannot sit upright without external support. Respiratory muscles are affected with breathing dependent on spinal position.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Traumatic injury resulted in spinal cord contusion and edema. Medical imaging at time of injury showed disruption of the spinal cord without complete transection. Pre-injury the patient was employed in physical labor with normal function. Current rehabilitation began immediately post-stabilization.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, motor testing demonstrates paralysis of bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensory examination shows complete sensory loss below the C6 dermatome. The patient retains some motor function in bilateral shoulder and elbow musculature graded 2-3/5. Reflexes are hyperreflexic in lower extremities indicating spasticity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows spinal cord contusion at C5-C6 levels with associated edema and hemorrhage within the cord. CT imaging demonstrates fracture-dislocation of the cervical vertebrae with posterior ligamentous disruption. Post-stabilization imaging confirms appropriate alignment and decompression of spinal canal.

puzzles.explanation: Incomplete C5 cervical spinal cord injury from motor vehicle trauma with tetraplegia and diaphragmatic breathing preserved.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 8-year-old child presents with parents reporting long-standing difficulty with social interactions at school. Teachers note unusual resistance to group activities and sensory sensitivities to loud noises and busy environments. The parents describe repetitive behaviors during unstructured free time.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child demonstrates atypical play patterns, preferring solitary activities and showing intense focus on specific interests. Physical education participation is inconsistent, with noticeable difficulty initiating peer interactions during group sports. Motor planning appears clumsy, though strength is normal.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Developmental milestones were generally met but with notable delays in social communication. Early childhood assessment identified concerns with language pragmatics and flexible thinking. Symptoms have been present since early childhood and are consistent across home and school settings.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, eye contact is avoided during conversation. The child demonstrates restricted interests in discussion topics. Gross motor coordination testing reveals mild dyspraxia during bilateral coordinated movements. Fine motor skills and strength testing are within normal limits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Neuropsychological testing via standardized assessment tools (ADOS-2) demonstrates criteria-consistent social communication deficits and restricted/repetitive behaviors. Brain MRI is structurally normal without white matter abnormalities. EEG shows normal background activity without seizure activity.

puzzles.explanation: Autism spectrum disorder involves social communication differences and restricted repetitive behaviors, affecting motor and functional skills.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old retired teacher reports recent onset of spinning sensation when changing positions in bed or bending to pick up objects from the floor. Episodes last seconds to minutes and are accompanied by mild nausea. No hearing loss or tinnitus is noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient avoids rapid head movements and reports difficulty with balance confidence when turning the head quickly. Morning routines are affected as dizziness occurs when lying down or rolling over. Walking is cautious but functional without assistive device.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent head trauma is denied. The patient reports mild diabetes and hypertension managed with medications. Multiple falls in the past year have increased safety concerns. No prior episodes of vertigo are documented.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The Dix-Hallpike maneuver reproduces brief rotatory nystagmus with a latency of 2-3 seconds. Gait testing shows mildly decreased weight transfer with narrow base. Head impulse test is normal bilaterally. Romberg test shows mild sway.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Diagnostic imaging via high-resolution CT of the temporal bones demonstrates normal semicircular canal anatomy. MRI of the internal acoustic meatus shows no retrocochlear pathology. Imaging confirms absence of other CNS abnormalities.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related vestibular dysfunction combines with proprioceptive loss causing balance impairment and dizziness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old with long-standing lower leg swelling affecting the left lower extremity. Reports progressive discomfort and functional decline with difficulty walking and standing for prolonged periods.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids prolonged standing or walking; symptoms worsen throughout the day. Difficulty with stair climbing and footwear selection due to ankle swelling. Night-time elevation partially relieves symptoms. Recent episode of severe swelling after long airplane flight.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of deep vein thrombosis in left lower extremity 8 years ago treated with anticoagulation. Post-thrombotic syndrome with chronic venous insufficiency. Cellulitis episodes managed with antibiotics. Obesity and immobility contributing factors.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left lower leg swelling extends from midthigh to ankle with circumferential enlargement. Skin brawny and indurated; palpable fibrosis in subcutaneous layers. Stemmer sign and Kaposi-Stumpe sign both positive. Mild erythema and warmth; no acute cellulitis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Duplex ultrasound reveals incompetent perforating veins and reflux in superficial venous system. Lymphoscintigraphy shows severely delayed transit time with collateral lymphatic patterns. No evidence of deep venous obstruction on repeat venography.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary lymphedema reactivation presentation with recurrent cancer compressing lymphatic vessels. Years after initial surgery, new malignancy reintroduces lymphatic obstruction requiring aggressive management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old office worker with a sedentary occupation reports generalized fatigue and difficulty with sustained activity. Breathing becomes labored after climbing a single flight of stairs. No acute illness precedes these symptoms, which developed gradually over several months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Previously independent with household tasks, the patient now requires frequent rest periods. Walking distance has progressively decreased to approximately two blocks. Cardiac symptoms are denied, but dyspnea is proportional to exertion intensity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient experienced prolonged illness requiring hospitalization and bed rest six months ago. Since discharge, activity levels have remained low despite medical clearance to resume normal function. No new cardiac or pulmonary diagnoses have been established.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate is elevated at 92 bpm, with blood pressure 118/76 mmHg. Six-minute walk test demonstrates distance of 280 meters with dyspnea rating of 4/10. Lower extremity strength is 4/5 throughout major muscle groups. Cardiopulmonary auscultation is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows no acute infiltrates or cardiac enlargement. Electrocardiogram demonstrates normal sinus rhythm without ischemic changes. Transthoracic echocardiography confirms normal ejection fraction and valvular function. Laboratory studies show no anemia or metabolic abnormalities.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic deconditioning from sedentary lifestyle progressively reduces functional capacity and increases disability in elderly without medical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old manual laborer reports progressive shoulder weakness and drooping appearance of the shoulder over four weeks. Shoulder elevation is effortful and shoulder shrugging is noticeably asymmetrical. No acute trauma or neck pain is reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty with overhead work tasks and carrying loads on the affected shoulder. Shrugging against resistance feels weak compared to the contralateral side. Neck lateral flexion and rotation are unaffected functionally.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset was insidious without clear precipitating event. The patient denies recent neck surgery, instrumentation, or sternocleidomastoid procedures. No family history of neuromuscular disorders is reported. Symptoms have been progressive without remission.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, shoulder shrug strength is 2/5 bilaterally asymmetrical with evident atrophy of the upper trapezius on the affected side. Scapular positioning demonstrates medial winging. Neck flexion and lateral flexion strength are 5/5 bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography shows denervation potentials in the trapezius muscle with reduced motor unit recruitment. MRI of the neck demonstrates normal spinal canal and nerve root foramina. No mass lesions are identified along the accessory nerve course.

puzzles.explanation: Post-surgical spinal accessory nerve injury with trapezius paralysis, shoulder droop, and inability to shrug shoulder.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old office manager presents with insidious onset of posterior neck and upper back pain that worsens during the workday. Headaches originating from the base of the skull are frequent. Symptoms are not associated with trauma or recent illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports significant limitation with sustained computer work and avoids overhead activities. Forward head posture is evident during daily tasks and contributes to symptom exacerbation. Postural correction temporarily eases discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a ten-year history of desk-based work without ergonomic modifications. Recent job transition to new workspace increased postural demands. No prior neurological symptoms or imaging are documented. Sleep quality has diminished due to nighttime neck stiffness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation reveals tight upper trapezius and levator scapulae muscles bilaterally. Cervical extension ROM is 35 degrees (normal 50 degrees) with reproduction of upper back discomfort. Scapular positioning demonstrates elevation and protraction. Neurological testing is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of cervical lordosis and forward head positioning. MRI shows no disc herniations or stenosis. Soft tissue imaging confirms bilateral upper trapezius and levator scapulae hypertrophy. Imaging findings are consistent with muscular compensation patterns.

puzzles.explanation: Upper crossed syndrome occurs in athletes with overuse and postural contribution to shoulder dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old carpenter reports progressive lateral forearm pain that is worse with gripping and sustained pinching activities. Pain is localized to the proximal forearm and does not extend into the hand. Symptom onset was gradual without documented injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty with sustained tool use and reports pain increases with repetitive supination and pronation movements. Gripping strength is significantly diminished compared to the contralateral side. Light activity such as writing causes mild discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occupational repetitive strain history is significant with decades of carpentry work requiring pronation and gripping. Symptoms began insidiously two months ago. No cervical radiculopathy history or neurological dysfunction is reported. No previous forearm injuries have occurred.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resisted supination reproduces lateral forearm pain. Resisted wrist extension is weak at 4/5 with lateral forearm reproduction. Palpation over the radial tunnel elicits point tenderness. Two-point discrimination and sensation are normal in the dorsal first web space.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the forearm demonstrates mild edema in the extensor carpi radialis muscle near the radial tunnel. High-resolution ultrasound shows compression of the posterior interosseous nerve with soft tissue impingement. No fractures or significant mass lesions are identified.

puzzles.explanation: Radial tunnel syndrome misdiagnosed as tennis elbow, with distinguishing features of thumb extension weakness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational swimmer reports acute onset of sharp posterior shoulder pain during a training session. Pain is located in the lower scapular region and radiates laterally toward the mid-arm. No arm numbness or tingling is experienced.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain increases significantly with arm pulling movements and rowing-like activities. Overhead activities provoke sharp symptoms, whereas resting the arm in the lap is comfortable. Swimming strokes that emphasize pull-through phase are most symptomatic.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during an intensive training session after several weeks of increased swimming volume. The patient has no prior shoulder injuries or surgery. Flexibility limitations in shoulder extension and horizontal adduction are noted on historical assessment.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, resisted shoulder adduction reproduces sharp posterior shoulder pain. Palpation over the latissimus dorsi insertion is tender. Shoulder flexion ROM is limited to 120 degrees due to pain. Manual muscle testing of shoulder adduction is 4/5 due to pain inhibition.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder MRI demonstrates acute strain injury of the latissimus dorsi muscle with edema at the musculotendinous junction near the humerus insertion. No complete muscle rupture is identified. Imaging confirms absence of rotator cuff pathology or labral involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Latissimus dorsi strain from pulling or rowing activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old with chronic cough and persistent respiratory symptoms since early childhood. Reports airway clearance difficulties and increasing shortness of breath with daily activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Participates in daily airway clearance treatments using percussion and oscillatory devices. Exercise tolerance severely limited; walks only short distances before requiring rest. Family reports progressive decline in functional capacity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known genetic pulmonary disorder diagnosed via newborn screening. Multiple hospitalizations for respiratory exacerbations in the past 2 years. Concurrent endocrine dysfunction with glucose intolerance. Recent sinus surgery for recurrent sinusitis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lungs demonstrate bilateral crackles and decreased air entry at lung bases. Six-minute walk test completed 280 meters with desaturation to 84%. Thoracic kyphosis with barrel chest appearance. Productive cough with thick, greenish sputum.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with increased bronchovascular markings and focal areas of bronchiectasis. CT confirms diffuse bronchial wall thickening and bronchus-to-artery ratio exceeding 1.5.

puzzles.explanation: CF pulmonary exacerbation presentation with acute clinical deterioration requiring hospitalization and intensification of therapy. Demonstrates the cyclical nature of CF management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old child presents with parental concerns about apparent clumsiness and difficulty with age-appropriate motor tasks. Teachers report the child drops objects frequently and moves awkwardly during class activities. Gross motor skills appear noticeably behind peers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child demonstrates difficulty with bilateral coordinated movement tasks such as skipping or riding a bicycle. Handwriting is immature for age with poor pencil grip and inconsistent letter formation. Ball-catching and throwing demonstrate poor timing and coordination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Developmental milestones were met but with noted awkwardness in acquisition of motor skills. Early childhood gross motor development appeared slower than siblings. No history of neurological disease, cerebral palsy, or intellectual disability is documented. Symptoms have been lifelong and persistent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, rapid alternating movements of the forearms are slow and dysrhythmic bilaterally. Balance testing with eyes closed demonstrates significant sway. Gait is wide-based with asymmetrical arm swing. Strength and reflexes are normal throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Standardized movement assessment using the Movement ABC-2 test demonstrates scores below the fifth percentile in motor proficiency. Brain MRI is structurally normal without white matter abnormalities or cerebellar pathology. Imaging confirms absence of organic neurological disease.

puzzles.explanation: Adolescent DCD persists into teenage years with significant functional limitations despite normal intelligence, impacting education and social participation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old cyclist reports acute onset of severe pain in the superior chest wall following a fall onto the shoulder during a cycling accident. Visible deformity is present in the clavicular region. Shoulder movement is severely limited and guarded.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is unable to bear weight through the upper extremity or support the arm without pain. Arm elevation and adduction movements reproduce sharp pain at the injury site. Even passive shoulder movements are limited by pain and muscle guarding.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct trauma to the anterior shoulder occurred when the patient fell from the bicycle and struck the clavicular region directly on a curb. Symptom onset was immediate with rapid swelling and bruising. No prior clavicular injuries or surgery have occurred.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visible deformity with superior displacement of the lateral fragment is apparent. Palpation elicits point tenderness over the fracture site. Shoulder ROM is severely limited in all planes. Neurovascular examination reveals normal radial pulse and intact sensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anteroposterior chest X-ray demonstrates a displaced fracture of the distal clavicle with superior angulation of the fragments. CT scan shows fracture through the lateral third of the clavicle with mild superior displacement. Associated soft tissue swelling is evident.

puzzles.explanation: Acute clavicle fracture from direct impact with typical presentation in young patient.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 68-year-old woman with osteoporosis reports acute onset of severe midline thoracic back pain without preceding trauma. Pain is constant and does not resolve with positional changes. Functional mobility is significantly restricted by pain severity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is unable to walk more than a few minutes without significant pain escalation. Bending forward reproduces acute pain at the injury site. Sitting tolerance is severely limited. All standing and walking activities require frequent breaks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 15-year history of diagnosed osteoporosis with multiple medication-treated risk factors. Recent history includes a minor fall from standing height that may have triggered onset. No direct significant trauma is reported, yet symptoms began acutely.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation over the mid-thoracic vertebrae elicits severe point tenderness. Thoracic flexion is markedly limited to approximately 20 degrees. Neurological examination is intact with normal lower extremity strength and sensation throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Thoracic spine X-rays demonstrate acute wedge-shaped compression deformity of T7 vertebral body with 30 percent height loss. MRI shows acute signal change on STIR imaging consistent with recent compression fracture. Posterior vertebral body line remains intact without retropulsion.

puzzles.explanation: Vertebral compression fracture is the classic osteoporotic fracture with acute severe pain and structural collapse.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old distance runner reports progressive deep buttock pain that worsens with running and sitting. Pain is located in the sacral region and does not radiate into the lower extremity. Symptoms developed insidiously over eight weeks of increased training volume.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports significant pain with running and cannot tolerate prolonged sitting without symptom exacerbation. Walking is tolerated but running produces sharp localized pain. Pain does not improve with typical stress fracture activity modification.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Training volume increased by 40 percent over the past three months to prepare for a marathon. The patient reports a female athlete triad history with irregular menses and low bone density screening concerns. Previous stress fractures in the femoral neck occurred two years ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation over the sacral ala reproduces localized pain. Single-leg stance on the contralateral leg reproduces symptoms on the affected side. Hip strength testing reveals weakness in hip abductors at 4/5. Neurological examination is completely normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Initial X-rays show no obvious fracture line. MRI demonstrates a linear fracture line through the sacral ala with surrounding bone marrow edema extending into the sacral body. CT imaging confirms fracture orientation consistent with stress mechanism. Imaging shows no displacement.

puzzles.explanation: Sacral stress fracture is an overuse injury in an athlete with occult fracture requiring advanced imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by parents who report noting asymmetry in shoulder positioning. The right shoulder appears elevated and displaced medially compared to the left. No complaints of pain are reported by the child.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child participates in sports with minimal limitation but demonstrates restricted overhead shoulder mobility on the affected side. Scapular positioning is visibly asymmetrical during functional tasks. Swimming and throwing activities demonstrate compensatory patterns.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The scapular asymmetry has been present since early childhood and has progressively worsened during growth spurts. The patient's mother reports similar shoulder positioning in paternal ancestry. No history of trauma, surgical instrumentation, or brachial plexus involvement is documented.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the affected scapula is positioned medially and superiorly relative to the contralateral side. The inferior angle is displaced upward and medially. ROM testing demonstrates limited shoulder abduction and external rotation. Strength testing is normal throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-rays demonstrate abnormal scapular positioning with underdevelopment of the scapular body. MRI shows abnormal embryological development with hypoplasia of the scapular wing and underdevelopment of the attached muscles. Imaging confirms the congenital developmental nature of the deformity.

puzzles.explanation: Sprengel's deformity is congenital failure of scapular descent, causing a high-riding scapula with restricted shoulder motion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old female presents with insidious onset of symmetrical hand and wrist pain, worse in the mornings. She reports significant stiffness affecting grip strength and daily activities, with swelling in multiple joints affecting function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning clothes and opening jars due to joint pain and reduced range of motion. Morning stiffness lasts over 60 minutes, improving only after activity and warm showers. Fatigue is a limiting factor in her functional capacity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-month progressive course with no recent trauma or infection preceding symptoms. Family history significant for autoimmune disease. Mild systemic symptoms including malaise and low-grade fevers accompanying the articular complaints.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, symmetrical swelling and warmth noted in metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Grip strength reduced at 15 kg bilaterally (normal >25 kg). Positive squeeze test of metacarpal heads elicits pain. Range of motion limited in wrist extension and flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays reveal periarticular soft tissue swelling, juxta-articular osteopenia, and early marginal erosions at metacarpophalangeal joints. Ultrasound confirms synovial hypertrophy with increased vascularity on power Doppler, consistent with active inflammatory synovitis.

puzzles.explanation: Rheumatoid Arthritis is a moderately aggressive presentation in a perimenopausal woman with prominent small joint involvement and systemic inflammatory markers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old male smoker presents with chronic productive cough and dyspnea, worse over the past 2 years. He reports recurrent respiratory infections requiring multiple courses of antibiotics and describes expectoration of purulent sputum daily.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Limited exercise tolerance; becomes breathless with moderate activity such as climbing stairs or brisk walking. Activities requiring sustained breathing lead to increased cough and sputum production. He tires easily during conversation and requires frequent rest periods.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Significant 30-year smoking history with recurrent lower respiratory infections since childhood. Previous pneumonia episodes with slow recovery. Currently manages symptoms with bronchodilators and mucolytics. No significant hemoptysis but chronic sputum production baseline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On auscultation, bilateral coarse crackles heard predominantly in basilar lung fields that do not clear with coughing. Prolonged expiration noted. Respiratory rate elevated at 24 breaths/min at rest. Oxygen saturation 92% on room air, improving only slightly with exertion tolerance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates bronchial wall thickening with bronchus-to-artery ratio >1, indicating bronchial dilation. Bronchial pooling of contrast and traction bronchiectasis visible in lower lobes bilaterally. Bronchial walls remain thick without normal bronchial tapering.

puzzles.explanation: Idiopathic bronchiectasis with significant disease burden. The characteristic bronchial dilation on CT with clinical correlation of recurrent infections and large sputum volumes is diagnostic.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old woman in her third trimester presents with unilateral pain in the lower back and buttock region. Pain onset occurred approximately 8 weeks ago and has progressively worsened with advancing pregnancy. Patient describes the discomfort as sharp and aching, affecting activities and sleep quality.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain with walking, climbing stairs, and rolling over in bed. Certain positions such as standing on one leg or crossing legs exacerbate symptoms significantly. Pain is notably worse at the end of the day and after prolonged standing or walking activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: This is the patient's second pregnancy; she had a complication-free first pregnancy 6 years prior. Current pregnancy is progressing normally without complications. Hormonal factors and weight gain associated with pregnancy are contributing factors. No prior history of low back or pelvic pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the long dorsal sacroiliac ligament test reproduces pain on the affected side. Patrick's test and FABER positioning elicit discomfort in the sacroiliac region. Palpation over the sacroiliac joint produces tenderness; midline sacral tenderness is absent. Hip abductor strength is reduced due to pain inhibition.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates normal fetal development with appropriate amniotic fluid volume. No structural abnormalities are noted in pelvic anatomy. MRI, if indicated, would show no degenerative changes or inflammatory findings in sacroiliac joints or supporting structures.

puzzles.explanation: Pregnancy-Related Pelvic Girdle Pain shows classic second trimester presentation with symphysis pubis dysfunction and SIJ involvement in first pregnancy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old collegiate soccer player sustained a direct blow to the anterior thigh during play. He reports immediate pain, significant swelling, and bruising over the impact site with functional loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to continue playing; pain with weight-bearing and hip/knee flexion prevents normal gait. He cannot perform knee strengthening exercises or walk distances without significant symptom exacerbation. Stair climbing and sit-to-stand transfers are currently limited by pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Single high-energy impact mechanism from opponent's knee strike. No previous thigh injuries or compartment syndrome history. Injury occurred 5 days ago; progressive swelling and bruising noted despite ice and compression application. No anticoagulation therapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable haematoma over anteromedial quadriceps with significant edema extending from mid-thigh proximally. Pain with passive knee flexion, particularly end-range. Quadriceps strength measured at 3/5 due to pain inhibition. Knee flexion ROM limited to 60 degrees secondary to tension in muscle.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound reveals well-defined hypoechoic collection within quadriceps musculature consistent with intramuscular haematoma, measuring 5 cm x 3 cm. Muscle fibers partially disrupted with surrounding edema. No evidence of compartment syndrome on clinical correlation, though compartments appear tense.

puzzles.explanation: Quadriceps contusions often worsen over first 48 hours due to continued hemorrhage and inflammation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old female is 6 months postpartum and reports involuntary leakage with coughing, sneezing, and exercise. She describes urinary symptoms that began after vaginal delivery and have not improved despite conservative measures.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids high-impact exercise and sneezing leads to observable urine loss. She reports difficulty with activities such as running, jumping, or playing with her child. Sexual function is affected, with pain and reduced enjoyment secondary to symptoms and self-consciousness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Uncomplicated vaginal delivery with episiotomy; labour duration 8 hours with instrumental assistance. No significant perineal trauma documented at delivery. Previous pregnancies without pelvic floor symptoms. Reports no pelvic pain but significant functional and psychological impact from symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On pelvic floor assessment, palpable weakness and reduced tone noted in pelvic floor musculature. Voluntary contraction strength graded at 3/5 on manual assessment. Positive cough stress test with demonstrable urine loss. Pelvic organ support appears normal on visual inspection without prolapse.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates normal bladder neck position at rest; however, dynamic ultrasound during cough shows insufficient urethral mobility and descent, with inadequate urethrovesical angle closure. No significant prolapse noted. Pelvic floor muscles show reduced echogenicity consistent with neuromuscular dysfunction.

puzzles.explanation: Postpartum pelvic floor dysfunction requires progressive pelvic floor muscle training, core reintegration, abdominal rehabilitation for diastasis correction, and education regarding activity progression to safely return to exercise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old male smoker with 40-year pack history reports progressive dyspnea and chronic cough productive of clear to whitish sputum. Symptoms have worsened over the past year, limiting his daily activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Becomes breathless with minimal exertion; walking flat ground at normal pace produces significant dyspnea. He is unable to climb stairs without stopping for rest. Household activities and self-care tasks are limited. Nocturnal symptoms requiring positional changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Forty-year smoking history continues despite multiple cessation attempts. Recurrent respiratory infections managed with courses of antibiotics. History of occupational exposure to dust. No significant cardiac history. Recently noted weight loss and reduced appetite.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On auscultation, bilateral coarse crackles and wheezes heard throughout lung fields. Prolonged expiration ratio noted with pursed-lip breathing observed. Resonance to percussion reduced bilaterally. Diaphragmatic excursion limited. Oxygen saturation 88% on room air, improving to 94% on supplemental oxygen.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and increased anteroposterior diameter. Peripheral bronchial wall thickening visible. HRCT demonstrates centrilobular emphysema predominantly in upper lobes with reduced lung parenchyma. Small airways disease pattern evident.

puzzles.explanation: Severe COPD (GOLD 3) with characteristic progressive dyspnoea, smoking history, hyperinflation, airflow limitation on spirometry, and significant exercise limitation requiring pulmonary rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old female reports unilateral headaches localized to the right frontal and occipital regions. Pain initiated after prolonged desk work posture and worsens with neck movement in certain directions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Headache reproduces with sustained neck flexion, rightward rotation, and extension. Relief occurs with neck rest and positional changes. Certain computer work positions consistently aggravate symptoms. Analgesic medications provide only partial relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-month history of recurrent episodes following increased screen time and poor ergonomics. No history of head trauma, though she notes chronic forward head posture. No photophobia or phonophobia. Pain began after sustained office work transitions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On cervical examination, restricted right cervical rotation (60 degrees; normal >80 degrees) and extension provokes familiar headache. Positive cervical flexion-rotation test reproducing unilateral frontal pain. Palpation reveals asymmetrical muscle tension in right upper trapezius and cervical paraspinals.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine MRI reveals mild degenerative changes at C3-C4 and C4-C5 levels with facet joint hypertrophy. No significant nerve root compression. Straightening of normal cervical lordosis noted. Multilevel spondylotic changes most prominent at C3-C5 corresponding to referred pain distribution.

puzzles.explanation: Cervicogenic headache is occupational in origin with clear postural and tension components.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 41-year-old female with recent whiplash injury reports upper chest and shoulder pain with associated upper extremity neurological symptoms. Pain is worse with certain neck postures and limits functional activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead activities and reaching aggravate symptoms dramatically. Sustained neck extension and contralateral side-bending reproducibly worsen pain and tingling in the arm. Symptoms improve with arm elevation and neck flexion positions. Sleeping on the affected side increases nocturnal symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Motor vehicle accident 3 months ago with hyperextension-hyperflexion mechanism. Gradual onset of vascular and neurological symptoms over weeks. Recent increase in symptoms correlating with return to work demands. No significant improvement with conservative treatment to date.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, restricted cervical extension and ipsilateral side-bending reproduce familiar symptoms. Elevated first rib palpable on the left side compared to right. Scalene muscles demonstrate increased tension bilaterally. Positive Roos test with upper extremity claudication and neurological symptoms within 2 minutes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical X-rays show elevated first rib position on the affected side. CT imaging demonstrates narrowing of the interscalene space with hypertrophic scalene muscle contributing to compression. MRI confirms no cervical spine pathology explaining symptoms, supporting mechanical compression of neurovascular structures.

puzzles.explanation: First rib dysfunction is a rare condition affecting thoracic outlet with serious vascular compromise risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old female presents with progressive bilateral upper extremity pain, numbness, and heaviness particularly affecting the hands and fingers. Symptoms began insidiously and are worsened by arm position and overhead activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms dramatically increase with overhead activities and sustained elevated arm positions such as typing or writing. Carrying objects with arms at side aggravates claudicatory pain. Sleep disturbance occurs secondary to positional symptoms. Symptoms improve significantly with arm rest and lowering activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-month progressive course without clear precipitant, though patient reports history of thoracic outlet symptoms with exercise. Recent workplace ergonomic changes and increased computer use. No prior surgical intervention. Symptoms initially postural but now present at rest in some positions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive Adson test, Roos test, and Wright maneuver reproducibly recreate familiar symptoms bilaterally. Palpable pulse diminishment noted when arms elevated. Sensory testing reveals diminished sensation in fingers 4-5 distribution. Positive Tinel sign over supraclavicular region bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MR angiography demonstrates reduced arterial caliber through thoracic outlet with compression of subclavian artery between anterior and middle scalene muscles. Color Doppler ultrasound confirms flow abnormality with positional changes. Cervical ribs absent but muscular hypertrophy contributes to narrowed interscalene space.

puzzles.explanation: Work-related neurogenic TOS from poor ergonomics and postural dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old overhead athlete reports insidious onset of deep shoulder pain with clicking and catching sensations. Pain is greatest with throwing and overhead activities and worsens progressively with continued participation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Throwing reproduces sharp deep pain with a catching sensation that forces cessation of activity. Late-cocking phase of throwing causes particular symptom exacerbation. Weakness and instability noted when performing overhead movements. Nighttime symptoms limit sleep comfort in certain positions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Eight-month history following increased training volume in throwing sport. No specific traumatic event preceding onset. Previous right shoulder impingement syndrome. Progressive worsening despite rest periods and anti-inflammatory medication management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive O'Brien's test producing deep shoulder pain consistent with patient's complaint. Crank test reproduces pain in abduction and external rotation. Positive anterior slide test noted. Glenohumeral translation demonstrating subtle increased humeral head excursion anteriorly. Positive apprehension test in 90-90 position.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MR arthrography reveals posterior superior labral tear extending from 10 o'clock to 2 o'clock position with associated irregular articular surface. Fluid tracking into the tear confirms disruption. Subtle associated undersurface rotator cuff fraying noted. Anteroinferior labrum appears intact.

puzzles.explanation: Bankart lesion from anteroinferior shoulder dislocation with labral detachment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 4-year-old child presents with developmental delay affecting gross motor milestones and cognitive function. Parents report hypotonia and reduced strength limiting activities compared to age-matched peers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain single-leg stance and demonstrates difficulty with jumping and running activities. Stair climbing requires railings and frequently uses hands for support. Fatigue noted with sustained play activities. Reduced fine motor dexterity affecting pencil grasp and feeding self.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known genetic condition diagnosed in infancy with characteristic facial features and musculoskeletal findings. Previous cardiac screening performed. Family history of same condition. Global developmental delay documented across motor, cognitive, and social domains.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, generalized hypotonia noted with decreased muscle tone throughout. Gross motor assessment reveals inability to maintain neutral head position without support, weak abdominal engagement, and pelvic floor weakness. Muscle strength assessed at 3+/5 throughout major muscle groups. Gait demonstrates wide base of support with knee hyperextension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Skeletal survey reveals findings characteristic of the diagnosis including atlantoaxial instability on cervical spine flexion-extension imaging. Cardiac ultrasound demonstrates no significant pathology. Spine imaging shows mild scoliotic curve with general ligamentous laxity evident on dynamic studies.

puzzles.explanation: Down syndrome frequently presents with atlantoaxial instability and global hypotonia affecting motor function and requiring careful cervical monitoring.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old runner reports insidious onset of deep anterior shin pain that began during training progression. Pain is activity-related and localized to the lower anterolateral leg, worse with running and relieving with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Onset of pain occurs during running, classically after 20 minutes of sustained activity. Sharp, deep pain in the shin prevents continued running and requires activity modification. Pain does not occur with walking at normal pace. Symptoms worsening with increased training volume despite attempts to rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Twelve-week progressive history correlating with increased distance running and addition of high-impact drills. Recent changes to training surface from track to asphalt. No previous lower extremity injuries. Training load increased 40% over 4 weeks preceding symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
