clues.text: A 67-year-old retired postal worker presents with 8-month progressive history of medial foot pain and arch discomfort. Symptoms began insidiously without injury recollection. Reports significant functional decline affecting independence with household activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking distances progressively shortened to less than one city block before discomfort forces rest. Difficulty with stairs, particularly descent. Shopping, gardening, and social activities increasingly limited. Patient notes feeling 'unstable' in the foot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong pes planus bilaterally. Type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) for 12 years, currently controlled on metformin. Mild peripheral neuropathy confirmed with monofilament testing. No prior foot surgery or acute injuries. History of obesity with BMI 31.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, marked midfoot collapse noted with severe valgus heel positioning. No swelling detected but severe point tenderness palpated along the entire posterior tibial tendon course. Single-leg stance impossible to achieve; single-leg heel raise completely absent bilaterally. Arch appears completely flattened.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates complete rupture of the posterior tibial tendon at the level of the medial malleolus with significant retraction of proximal tendon stump. Secondary changes include navicular bone edema, talonavicular joint space narrowing, and degenerative changes at the subtalar joint. Chronic pes planus configuration present.

puzzles.explanation: Significant posterior tibial tendon dysfunction produces marked foot deformity and functional impairment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner or athlete presents with acute calf pain that developed during activity. Pain is localised to the calf region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, jumping, or rapid weight shifts. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with walking and running.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset during or immediately after athletic activity. The patient may have inadequate warm-up or prior calf injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the calf muscles. Resisted plantarflexion or knee flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption, fluid collection, or hematoma within the calf. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Acute calf strains present with sudden onset and functional impairment during physical activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with acute knee trauma presents with acute knee pain and significant functional limitation. The injury involved a varus and external rotation mechanism.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation is present with inability to bear weight. The patient demonstrates knee instability with varus stress.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic injury with specific mechanism of varus and external rotation stress. The injury is often from sports or motor vehicle accident.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, varus instability is demonstrated with varus stress test. Positive posterolateral drawer sign and external rotation recurvatum test indicate posterolateral corner injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates injury to posterolateral corner structures including fibular collateral ligament, popliteus tendon, and biceps femoris. MRI characterises injury severity.

puzzles.explanation: Posterolateral corner injuries involve multiple structures and are often underdiagnosed due to complexity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with acute elbow trauma or sudden forceful elbow extension presents with sudden loss of elbow flexion strength. The patient reports acute pain and loss of function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation is present with inability to flex the elbow against resistance. The patient cannot perform lifting activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset during forceful activity or minor trauma. The patient may report sudden "pop" or tearing sensation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, loss of elbow flexion strength against resistance is evident. The biceps bulge sign demonstrates proximal retraction of the muscle belly.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates absence of the distal biceps tendon insertion and proximal retraction of the muscle-tendon unit. MRI shows complete rupture at the radial tuberosity.

puzzles.explanation: Acute distal biceps rupture from maximal effort in middle-aged male, classic presentation with hook test positive.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 3-year-old child presents with persistent leftward head tilt and neck rotation limitation noted by parents over the past 6 months. The head tilt is constant, and the child avoids turning the head to the right.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child has difficulty following objects that require rightward head rotation and shows reluctance to eat food presented on the right side. Attempts to manually straighten the neck cause significant distress. The abnormal posture does not improve with rest and is present throughout sleep and waking hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Parents recall a difficult breach delivery with forceps application; however, the child had normal head positioning at 1 month postpartum. The tilt gradually worsened over 4-6 months without clear precipitant. No history of trauma, fever, or recent upper respiratory infection. Developmental milestones were met appropriately.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation of the left sternocleidomastoid reveals a firm 1.5cm mass within the muscle belly, consistent with a fibromatosis. Passive cervical rotation to the right is limited to 35 degrees compared to 70 degrees to the left. No cervical lymphadenopathy, facial asymmetry, or neurological deficits are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates an echogenic nodule within the left sternocleidomastoid muscle measuring 1.2 by 0.8cm with minimal vascularity. MRI of the cervical spine and soft tissues confirms a well-defined mass consistent with fibromatosis within the sternocleidomastoid with no intracranial abnormality or spinal cord compression.

puzzles.explanation: Primary cervical dystonia with rotational head deviation, characteristic muscle hypertrophy, and abnormal sustained EMG activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient recovering from ankle fracture surgery presents with functional limitation in the post-operative period. The patient reports ankle pain, swelling, and restricted motion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient demonstrates functional limitation with weightbearing and walking. The patient requires assistive devices for mobility in the early phase.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Ankle fracture was repaired surgically with open reduction and internal fixation. The patient is in the early post-operative rehabilitation phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, ankle range of motion is restricted, particularly dorsiflexion and plantarflexion. Swelling is evident. Proprioceptive deficits may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate fracture fixation with appropriate hardware placement. Radiographs show fracture reduction achieved surgically.

puzzles.explanation: This delayed presentation requires intensive range of motion work, Achilles tendon mobilization, and gastrocnemius-soleus strengthening to overcome stiffness and restore normal ankle mechanics and gait.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An adolescent presents with progressive thoracic kyphosis and back pain. The pain is mechanical and activity-related.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with postural limitations and back pain with activity. Physical appearance shows rounded thoracic spine.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The condition develops during adolescence with progressive kyphotic deformity. Genetic predisposition may be present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, fixed thoracic kyphosis is evident that does not correct with forward bending. Back pain is elicited with extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate thoracic vertebral body wedging and Schmorl's nodes characteristic of Scheuermann's disease. Kyphosis is measured.

puzzles.explanation: Scheuermann's disease is the classic adolescent presentation with structural kyphosis and characteristic vertebral changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An older adult presents with severe joint pain and significant functional limitation. The patient has advanced degenerative joint disease.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with pain limiting all activities. The patient demonstrates significant gait abnormality.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing history of osteoarthritis with progressive worsening. Prior conservative treatment has been unsuccessful.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, significant joint deformity and motion loss is evident. Crepitus is pronounced. Muscle atrophy is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate severe degenerative changes with joint space obliteration, osteophytes, and bone sclerosis indicating advanced arthritis.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced osteoarthritis with complete cartilage loss and bone damage requires joint replacement for functional restoration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with history of ankle sprain presents with chronic ankle instability and recurrent ankle inversion injury. The ankle gives way with minimal provocation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional impairment is present with difficulty walking on uneven surfaces and participating in sports. The ankle is prone to repetitive inversion injury.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prior ankle sprain history with incomplete healing is documented. Recurrent ankle inversion injuries have occurred. No complete ankle ligament rupture at initial injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the anterior drawer test demonstrates anterior translation of the talus. The talar tilt test shows inversion laxity. Proprioceptive deficit is evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Stress radiographs demonstrate increased talar tilt and anterior translation compared to normal ankle. MRI may show ligament signal changes and chronic inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Functional chronic ankle instability involves proprioceptive and neuromuscular deficits beyond structural ligament damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old male with recent hip replacement surgery 8 days ago presents with acute right calf swelling and pain that developed over 4 hours. The calf is warm to touch, and discomfort increases with walking and when the leg is dependent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot ambulate independently due to pain and swelling; assisted gait is attempted but markedly limited. Leg elevation above heart level provides mild relief, but the swelling persists. The patient reports calf pain even at rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He is immediately postoperative from total hip arthroplasty with general anesthesia lasting 2 hours; immobilization time was prolonged during recovery. He has no personal or family history of blood clots but takes lisinopril for hypertension. He was not prescribed mechanical or pharmacological thromboprophylaxis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the right calf circumference exceeds the left by 3cm measured 10cm below the tibial tuberosity. Homan's sign (passive ankle dorsiflexion) reproduces sharp calf pain. The right leg is visibly erythematous and warmer than the left; palpable cord-like resistance is present in the posterior calf, and slight distal foot edema is noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bilateral lower extremity compression ultrasound demonstrates an echogenic thrombus within the right popliteal vein extending proximally into the superficial femoral vein; no compressibility is achieved with probe pressure. Doppler shows absent spontaneous flow; the contralateral left popliteal vein is fully compressible with normal augmentation.

puzzles.explanation: Acute DVT from travel-related immobilization with characteristic clinical findings confirmed by compression ultrasound. Requires anticoagulation and prevention of progression to PE.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old recreational soccer player reports sudden onset medial knee pain with a popping sensation during a game 1 week ago. The pain is localized to the medial joint line and worsens with weightbearing and twisting movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot return to sport due to intermittent episodes of knee locking where the knee becomes stuck in slight flexion for several seconds before suddenly extending with a pop. Squatting and cutting movements reliably trigger both locking episodes and sharp joint line pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during a rapid deceleration while pivoting on the planted foot. He has no prior knee injuries, previous meniscal pathology, or osteoarthritis. His current pain is out of proportion to prior minor knee sprains and has not improved with standard rest and ice protocols over 7 days.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, point tenderness is elicited directly over the medial joint line. The McMurray test—performed with the knee flexed 90 degrees, internally rotating the tibia while extending—reproduces the patient's locking sensation and sharp pain. The Thessaly test at 20 degrees flexion reproduces medial joint line discomfort. No effusion is appreciated, and Lachman and anterior drawer tests are negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee reveals a vertical longitudinal tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus extending to the free edge. The tear pattern is consistent with degenerative changes at the tear interface. No signal abnormality in the anterior cruciate ligament, posterior cruciate ligament, or medial collateral ligament is identified.

puzzles.explanation: Degenerative meniscal tears in older patients may cause pain without obvious mechanical symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An older adult with history of falls presents with significant anxiety and fear of falling. The patient demonstrates protective movement patterns.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient demonstrates functional limitation with reduced mobility due to fear. The patient avoids activities prone to falls.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prior fall history is documented. The fall resulted in injury or near injury causing significant anxiety.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, anxiety is evident when discussing movement. Movement patterns are guarded and protective. Balance confidence is reduced.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Neuropsychological assessment and balance testing document anxiety and reduced confidence. Imaging is performed to exclude structural causes of fall risk.

puzzles.explanation: Severe post-fall anxiety may create greater disability than the original injury, requiring cognitive-behavioral intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An older adult presents with dizziness and imbalance that developed insidiously. The patient reports difficulty with balance and increased fall risk.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient demonstrates functional limitation with walking and activities requiring balance. Fear of falling limits activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset is insidious without clear precipitant. Multiple age-related factors may be contributory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, balance abnormalities including tandem stance deficit are evident. Vestibular and proprioceptive deficits may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging of the head and vestibular system (MRI or CT) excludes acute central causes. Videonystagmography may demonstrate age-related vestibular decline.

puzzles.explanation: Multisystem balance dysfunction in advanced age creates severe dizziness and high fall risk requiring comprehensive management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient recovering from thoracic surgery presents with chest pain, limited arm movement, and reduced exercise tolerance. The patient is in the post-operative recovery phase.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation is present with difficulty with arm activities and respiratory function. The patient demonstrates limited shoulder motion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Thoracotomy was performed weeks prior for lung pathology. The patient is progressing through post-operative rehabilitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, chest wall tenderness is present. Shoulder range of motion is restricted. Breathing pattern is guarded. Scar tissue may limit movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs exclude pneumothorax or complications. Imaging shows appropriate healing of surgical incision.

puzzles.explanation: Complicated post-thoracotomy recovery requires pulmonary rehabilitation emphasis, management of cancer-related fatigue and anxiety, coordination with oncology for chemotherapy timing, and careful progression of aerobic conditioning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 67-year-old retired teacher reports insidious onset of pain at the base of the right thumb over the past 8 months, worsening gradually with increased hand use. The pain is described as aching with occasional sharp stabbing when grasping or pinching.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Fine motor activities—particularly opening jars, using tweezers, and writing—are significantly limited. The patient demonstrates thumb opposition weakness and avoids pinch grip; he substitutes lateral pinch for precision pinch. Pain intensifies progressively throughout the day with repeated pinching motions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient sustained a fall onto an abducted right thumb 14 months ago while hiking, causing acute pain and swelling that partially resolved over 6 weeks. He did not seek medical attention at the time. Subsequent attempts at recreational knitting and gardening triggered progressive worsening of baseline symptoms over the past year.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the radial collateral ligament stress test of the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint at 30 degrees flexion demonstrates excessive radial deviation (>30 degrees laxity compared to the left side). Pinch strength testing shows 40% reduction compared to the contralateral thumb. Mild thenar eminence atrophy is appreciated. No acute swelling or warmth is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate a small avulsion fracture fragment from the medial base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb with mild degenerative changes at the metacarpophalangeal joint. MRI shows disruption of the radial collateral ligament with chronic fibrosis and healing response; the ligament appears attenuated but intact.

puzzles.explanation: Acute gamekeeper's thumb from ski pole injury with UCL sprain or tear.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old warehouse worker reports chronic low back pain of 4 years' duration that fluctuates in severity. Pain is mechanical, reproducible, and worsens with certain movements and prolonged postures, particularly forward bending and sustained standing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitations include reduced tolerance for lifting (now limited to 15 pounds versus 50 pounds prior to symptom onset) and difficulty with sustained postures during work. The patient demonstrates guarding and splinting behavior during movement; shifting weight side-to-side improves tolerance. Pain is least with supported sitting and walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 4 years ago following a work-related lifting incident; imaging at that time was unremarkable. He has tried multiple conservative interventions with temporary benefits. Pain intensity ranges from 2/10 at best to 7/10 at worst, with exacerbations lasting 1-2 weeks. He works in a physically demanding job and is overweight (BMI 31).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, active lumbar flexion triggers pain reproduction and is accompanied by deviation (Schober test: 4cm excursion versus normal 5cm). Prone instability test produces sharp midline lumbar pain. Lumbar multifidus muscle tone is asymmetrically diminished on the left. Abdominal bracing partially relieves pain. Strength testing and straight leg raise are normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Flexion-extension lumbar radiographs demonstrate 4mm of anterior translation at L4-L5 on flexion compared to neutral, indicating segmental hypermobility. MRI shows loss of disc height at L4-L5 with facet hypertrophy; however, no significant canal stenosis or spondylolisthesis is present. Ligamentous insufficiency with facet effusions is noted.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar instability is segmental motion dysfunction with recurrent episodes improved by core stabilization.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with high-impact activities presents with progressive hip or thigh pain. Pain has developed insidiously during training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running and high-impact activities. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with weightbearing activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training volume or intensity coincides with symptom onset. Risk factors including female athlete triad or osteoporosis may be present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pain is elicited with single-leg stance. Focal tenderness may be present over the femoral neck. Pain is reproduced with hip flexion and internal rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show stress fracture line or callus formation. MRI demonstrates bone stress with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema indicating stress fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Femoral stress fracture shows tension-side fracture in male high-volume trainer with significant functional limitation and displacement risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset right shoulder and elbow pain following a fall onto an outstretched arm during a football match 72 hours ago. He reports inability to flex his elbow against gravity since the injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot perform elbow flexion movements; attempts at lifting objects or pulling motions are completely impossible. Additionally, he reports altered sensation over the lateral forearm—described as numbness and tingling. Fine motor control of the hand remains intact.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The mechanism involved forced shoulder abduction and external rotation combined with axial loading on the outstretched arm. He sustained a visible dislocation at the shoulder which was reduced in the emergency department. Radiographs excluded fractures. Prior shoulder history is unremarkable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, right elbow flexion strength is 0/5 throughout the full range; the patient cannot lift the forearm off the examination table against gravity. The biceps reflex is absent bilaterally when tested (injury may affect upper fibers only). Sensory loss to light touch is present over the lateral forearm and dorsal aspect of the thumb. Shoulder rotation and wrist/finger strength are intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography performed 5 days post-injury demonstrates acute denervation changes (positive sharp waves and fibrillations) in the biceps brachii and brachialis muscles; the brachioradialis is spared. MRI of the brachial plexus reveals stretching injury of the musculocutaneous nerve at the level of the shoulder with surrounding edema and partial discontinuity.

puzzles.explanation: Musculocutaneous nerve injury from traumatic stretch with selective elbow flexion weakness and lateral antebrachial sensory loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 51-year-old woman presents with a 3-year history of widespread musculoskeletal pain affecting all four body quadrants simultaneously. The pain is described as burning and aching, with no specific joint or structural origin, and demonstrates no clear diurnal pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Physical exertion—even light activities like walking or household chores—consistently triggers pain exacerbation lasting up to 48 hours. The patient reports severe morning stiffness lasting 2-3 hours, profound fatigue unrelieved by sleep, and cognitive difficulties ('brain fog') affecting work performance and memory.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain onset followed a severe respiratory viral infection 3 years ago. She reports a personal history of major depressive disorder (treated) and anxiety. Multiple investigations including rheumatologic workup (negative ANA, RF, and inflammatory markers), imaging, and specialist consultations have failed to identify structural pathology. Previous treatments with NSAIDs and corticosteroids provided minimal benefit.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, widespread tender points are reproducible with digital pressure at specific anatomic sites (cervical, trapezius, supraspinatus, second rib, lateral epicondyle, gluteus, greater trochanter, knee). Pain threshold to 4kg pressure is reduced at multiple sites. The general physical examination including joint assessment, strength testing, and neurological examination is entirely normal; no joint swelling, erythema, or synovitis is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Laboratory investigations including erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, rheumatoid factor, and antinuclear antibody are all within normal limits. Imaging of the spine, pelvis, and major joints on plain radiographs and MRI reveals normal bone architecture and disc spaces with no degenerative changes or inflammatory findings.

puzzles.explanation: Fibromyalgia diagnosed using the 2011 modified ACR criteria: widespread pain index ≥7 and symptom severity ≥5, or WPI 4-6 and SSS ≥9, with symptoms present ≥3 months and no alternative explanation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with high-energy ankle trauma presents with acute ankle pain and swelling. The injury is often from a fall from height or motor vehicle accident.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with inability to weightbear. The patient demonstrates ankle deformity and significant swelling.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy trauma is documented with direct axial load to the ankle. Associated injuries may be present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, ankle deformity is evident with severe swelling. The ankle is extremely painful with any movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate pilon fracture with comminution of the tibial plafond. CT imaging characterises the fracture pattern and is essential for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Pilon fracture sequelae often include chronic pain and reduced ankle mobility despite adequate fracture healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old amateur skier presents with acute onset sharp pain at the base of the left thumb immediately after falling on an outstretched hand 3 days ago. The thumb is swollen and painful, especially with any grasping attempts.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot perform pinch grip or fine motor activities; holding a pen or gripping a ski pole causes severe pain. Thumb opposition movements trigger sharp discomfort at the metacarpophalangeal joint. The patient is splinting the thumb and avoiding all use.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The mechanism involved forced abduction of the thumb during the fall when the patient tried to break the fall with an outstretched hand. He applied ice and kept the thumb immobilized at home but symptoms have not improved. No prior thumb injury, and he denies any sensation of instability before the fall.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, marked swelling and ecchymosis are evident over the radial aspect of the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint. The radial collateral ligament stress test performed with the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint in 30 degrees flexion demonstrates excessive radial deviation (>20 degrees) compared to the contralateral side. Thumbs-up test shows pain with resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs of the thumb in anteroposterior and lateral views are normal with no fractures or avulsion fragments visible. MRI demonstrates disruption of the radial collateral ligament with complete separation of the ligament from its proximal insertion; associated edema and effusion within the metacarpophalangeal joint capsule are present.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic gamekeeper's thumb with MCL insufficiency and functional loss from old untreated injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 46-year-old woman presents with anterior chest wall pain localized to the left parasternal region at the level of the 4th and 5th ribs. The pain is sharp and positional, worsening over 2 weeks with certain movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The pain is reproducibly triggered by deep inspiration, laughing, and upper extremity movements—particularly reaching across the body and pushing motions. Pressure to the affected region during activity intensifies pain significantly. Lying on the left side exacerbates symptoms, while anti-inflammatory medication and heat provide some relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset followed 3 weeks of a persistent dry cough from a recent viral upper respiratory infection. The patient reports working at a desk with suboptimal ergonomics and has been doing garden renovation involving repetitive overhead activities. She denies trauma, fever, or constitutional symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, well-localized tenderness is reproduced with direct palpation of the costochondral junction at the 4th rib on the left side. The Tietze test (reproduction of pain with passive vertical flexion of the thorax) produces sharp left parasternal pain. No joint swelling, warmth, or erythema is appreciated; no heart or lung findings on auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest radiographs are normal with no infiltrates, pleural effusion, or mediastinal widening. MRI of the chest wall demonstrates mild soft tissue edema at the left 4th costochondral junction with subtle enhancement on post-gadolinium imaging. No cardiac abnormality, pulmonary pathology, or mediastinal masses are identified.

puzzles.explanation: Costochondritis is a recurrent condition with post-viral trigger and variable inflammation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient following spinal cord injury presents with bilateral lower extremity paralysis and sensory loss below the level of injury. The injury resulted from trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Complete functional loss of lower extremity function is present with total loss of motor and sensory function below the level of injury.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Traumatic spinal cord injury from high-energy mechanism. The patient is in the post-acute spinal cord injury rehabilitation phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, bilateral lower extremity paralysis is complete. Sensory level is defined. Bowel and bladder function is altered.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates spinal cord transection or severe contusion. MRI shows extent of spinal cord injury.

puzzles.explanation: Complete T6 spinal cord injury (ASIA A) from diving accident, with intact upper limbs, paraplegia, and autonomic dysreflexia risk — requiring comprehensive multidisciplinary rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old man presents with acute polyarticular joint pain and swelling involving the knees, ankles, and wrists that developed 2 weeks after a diarrheal illness. The joints are painful, warm, and progressively more swollen over the past 10 days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitations are significant; the patient cannot walk without assistance due to bilateral knee and ankle pain, and gripping with both hands is severely limited. Morning stiffness lasts 45 minutes and improves slightly with movement. Symptoms are worst at rest and early morning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He recalls a 3-day episode of bloody diarrhea and cramping abdominal pain 3 weeks ago that resolved spontaneously without treatment. Stool culture subsequently confirmed Shigella flexneri infection. He also reports painful urination and mild urethral discharge beginning 1 week prior to joint symptoms. No prior inflammatory arthritis, and arthritis does not run in the family.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, symmetric swelling, erythema, and warmth are present over the bilateral knee joints and ankle joints; the right knee demonstrates an effusion (ballotable patella). Wrist joints are swollen with limited flexion-extension range. Systemic examination reveals no rash, mucosal ulcers, or conjunctivitis. Inflammatory markers are elevated, and the patient demonstrates no cardiac findings.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs of the knees and ankles show symmetric joint space widening from effusion with no erosions, narrowing, or bony proliferation. Ultrasound demonstrates bilateral knee synovitis with effusions and reduced echogenicity of synovial fluid. Synovial fluid aspiration from the knee shows elevated white blood cell count (4,200 cells/mm3 with lymphocytic predominance) with negative bacterial and crystal analysis.

puzzles.explanation: Reactive Arthritis presents as post-enteric reactive arthritis in a female with keratoderma without prominent urethritis/ocular involvement, demonstrating incomplete classic triad.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old man reports progressive abnormal head posture with involuntary leftward head tilt and rotation developing over the past 18 months. The symptoms worsen throughout the day and are worse during stress.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The abnormal posture causes neck pain that worsens with sustained head positioning in any direction. Reading and computer work trigger muscle fatigue and increased dystonic symptoms. Walking in crowded spaces exacerbates the involuntary movements, whereas lying down provides complete symptom relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset was insidious without preceding trauma or infection. A family history of essential tremor is noted in his mother. He has no psychiatric history, prior movement disorders, or medication exposure. Symptoms have not responded to NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, or physical therapy over 12 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the patient demonstrates involuntary sustained leftward head tilt with cervical rotation to the right; these movements are semi-voluntary and can be partially suppressed. Palpation reveals hypertonic sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles bilaterally, more pronounced on the left. No sensory level is appreciated, strength is 5/5 throughout, and reflexes are normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the cervical spine and brain shows normal spinal cord alignment, disc spaces, and neural foramina with no cord signal abnormality. Intracranial structures including basal ganglia are morphologically normal with no mass lesion or abnormal signal. EMG demonstrates burst-like muscle activity in sternocleidomastoid and trapezius consistent with dystonia.

puzzles.explanation: Post-traumatic secondary torticollis from cervical strain with acute muscle spasm and limited neck mobility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner or athlete presents with acute calf pain that developed during activity. Pain is localised to the calf region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, jumping, or rapid weight shifts. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with walking and running.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset during or immediately after athletic activity. The patient may have inadequate warm-up or prior calf injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the calf muscles. Resisted plantarflexion or knee flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption, fluid collection, or hematoma within the calf. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic calf strain develops gradually from overuse and flexibility deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young adolescent is found to have abnormal spinal curvature during routine screening. The patient is otherwise asymptomatic.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation may be minimal if curve is mild. More severe curves may cause functional limitation with activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The curve has developed during growth and typically progresses during adolescent growth spurt. Family history of scoliosis may be present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, asymmetric shoulder or hip height is evident. The Adam's forward bend test shows rib asymmetry. Curve magnitude is measured on radiographs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate the scoliotic curve with measurement of Cobb angle. Spine maturity is assessed. MRI may be obtained if atypical features present.

puzzles.explanation: Severe idiopathic scoliosis approaching skeletal maturity may require surgical fusion to prevent progression and cardiopulmonary compromise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An older adult is diagnosed with osteoporosis during bone density screening. The patient may be asymptomatic or report generalized bone pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation may develop with fragility fractures occurring from minimal trauma. Pain with movement may be present.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Risk factors including age, female gender, or medication use are present. Prior fracture history may be documented.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, height loss or kyphotic posture may be evident. Neurological examination documents associated fracture-related morbidity if present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) demonstrates bone mineral density in the osteoporotic range. Radiographs may show compression fractures.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporosis is asymptomatic bone loss discovered through fracture or screening in at-risk populations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with acute anterior thigh pain that developed during activity. Pain is localised to the anterior thigh.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or activities requiring hip flexion and knee extension. The patient demonstrates functional limitation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset during or immediately after athletic activity. The patient may have inadequate warm-up or prior thigh injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the rectus femoris. Resisted knee extension or hip flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption or hematoma within the rectus femoris. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Rectus femoris strains occur at the muscle belly from explosive hip flexion movements.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner or athlete presents with acute calf pain that developed during activity. Pain is localised to the calf region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, jumping, or rapid weight shifts. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with walking and running.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset during or immediately after athletic activity. The patient may have inadequate warm-up or prior calf injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the calf muscles. Resisted plantarflexion or knee flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption, fluid collection, or hematoma within the calf. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Calf muscle strains commonly involve the medial gastrocnemius at the musculotendinous junction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A pregnant woman presents with pelvic girdle pain localised to the pelvic region. Pain develops during pregnancy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with pain limiting walking, climbing stairs, and other activities. The patient demonstrates asymmetric gait.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain develops during pregnancy, typically in second or third trimester. Prior pelvic injury or multiple pregnancies increase risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, sacroiliac joint tenderness and pelvic mobility tests reproduce pain. Asymmetric pelvic movement is evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging is typically avoided during pregnancy. Assessment is clinical with pelvic stability examination.

puzzles.explanation: Pregnancy-Related Pelvic Girdle Pain shows severe third trimester presentation in multiparous woman with bilateral SIJ involvement and significant functional impairment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged to older adult presents with shoulder pain, often lateral shoulder pain. Pain develops with shoulder movements or activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by overhead movements, lifting activities, or activities requiring arm elevation. The patient demonstrates functional limitation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms have developed gradually with repetitive shoulder activities or overuse. Recent increase in activity may be contributory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive impingement signs including Neer's test and Hawkins' test are present. Pain with resisted shoulder abduction is demonstrated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates rotator cuff tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Rotator cuff tendinopathy exacerbated by sport-specific overhead activity with scapular dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An older adult with fall presents with acute shoulder pain and loss of arm function. The pain is severe and associated with swelling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with inability to move the arm. The patient requires significant pain control.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall mechanism in an older adult with osteoporosis. Immediate pain and disability onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, severe pain limits passive range of motion. Swelling and ecchymosis develop. Neurovascular examination documents integrity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate proximal humerus fracture with characterisation of displacement and pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Proximal humerus fracture in elderly from low-energy fall.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old female yoga instructor reports bilateral but asymmetric pelvic girdle pain that is worse on one side. The discomfort is localized to the sacroiliac region and occasionally refers to the medial hip and groin.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are triggered by deep hip flexion (forward folds), rotation movements, and side-lying on the symptomatic side. Pain resolves with prone lying, supine positions, and activities that stabilize the pelvis such as walking on level ground.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient completed a full marathon four weeks prior and has recently begun practicing more advanced yoga postures with hip rotation emphasis. They have a history of pelvic instability post-pregnancy (age 28) but have remained asymptomatic until now.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Gaenslen's test bilaterally, with greater pain on the involved side. Pelvic thrust test (standing hip extension on one leg) triggers ipsilateral posterior pelvic pain. Iliac crest height appears level with palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan of the pelvis demonstrates unilateral sacroiliac joint involvement with mild capsular inflammation and small osteophytes along the joint margins. No evidence of fusion or significant degenerative changes.

puzzles.explanation: Sacroiliac joint dysfunction presents acutely in an athletic setting with functional demands aggravating symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old graphic designer presents with a visible firm mass over the dorsal right wrist that has gradually enlarged over 6 months. The mass is generally painless but causes mild aching with repetitive wrist movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Gripping and forceful wrist extension trigger mild discomfort at the mass site. The patient reports that the mass becomes more prominent during active wrist extension and less noticeable after rest. Prolonged keyboard use causes intermittent soreness in the wrist region that may relate to mass compression.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The mass appeared without obvious precipitant, though the patient recalls a dull wrist ache 8 months prior to mass appearance. The cyst has remained stable in size for the past 2 months, though she notices subtle changes in prominence related to activity level. She works extensively with computers and denies any history of wrist trauma or osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, a firm 1.5cm mass is palpable over the dorsal wrist at the scapholunate interval. The mass is non-tender, transilluminates, and does not blanch with pressure. Wrist range of motion is full and pain-free in all directions except mild discomfort at the extremes of extension. No neurological deficit or vascular compromise is detected distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates a well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechoic fluid collection measuring 1.6 by 1.2cm at the dorsal scapholunate region with thin echogenic walls; the mass does not show internal vascularity on Doppler. MRI confirms a simple cyst containing fluid signal intensity with no internal hemorrhage, and demonstrates its direct communication with the dorsal wrist joint capsule.

puzzles.explanation: Symptomatic ganglion cyst with nerve compression causing functional deficit and pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient recovering from carpal tunnel release surgery reports ongoing hand symptoms or recurrent symptoms. The patient is in the post-operative phase.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation may persist with ongoing hand dysfunction. The patient may demonstrate improved or persistent symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Carpal tunnel release surgery was performed weeks to months prior. Recovery is ongoing with variable symptom improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, hand sensation and strength are assessed to document improvement from pre-operative baseline. Scar tenderness may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging may include electrophysiology studies to document improvement in median nerve conduction if symptoms persist.

puzzles.explanation: Post-CTR rehabilitation requires scar management, progressive grip strengthening, return to functional hand use, and close monitoring for recurrent symptoms while allowing adequate healing before resuming repetitive activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A parent presents a child with flat feet. The child may or may not have symptoms related to foot posture.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation may be minimal in flexible flatfoot. The child may report foot fatigue with prolonged activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Flat feet develop during childhood as normal arch development may be delayed. Family history of flat feet may be present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, medial arch is absent or minimal when standing. Weight-bearing foot posture shows eversion and valgus alignment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs of the foot demonstrate loss of medial arch and valgus hindfoot alignment. Weight-bearing radiographs document dynamic posture.

puzzles.explanation: Familial flexible flat feet in children are typically benign and rarely require intervention unless functionally limiting or symptomatic.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with acute knee injury and loss of knee stability. The injury occurred with a deceleration or pivoting mechanism.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient demonstrates functional limitation with inability to return to sport. The knee demonstrates giving way with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute injury during sport with specific mechanism of injury documented. The patient heard or felt a pop.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive Lachman test and anterior drawer test demonstrate anterior knee instability. Pivot shift test may be positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates complete anterior cruciate ligament tear. MRI characterises associated injuries including meniscal tears.

puzzles.explanation: This alternate presentation highlights female-specific risk factors (hormonal, hypermobility), a different mechanism (landing), and an associated meniscal injury — the 'unhappy triad' presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged to older adult presents with tremor and gradually progressive movement slowing. The patient reports progressive difficulty with movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation increases progressively with slowness of movement and difficulty initiating activity. Gait disturbance develops.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset is insidious with gradual progression. The patient reports tremor at rest that improves with movement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, resting tremor is evident. Rigidity with cogwheel quality is demonstrated. Bradykinesia affects movement speed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging of the brain (MRI or CT) excludes other causes of parkinsonism. Diagnosis is clinical based on motor features.

puzzles.explanation: Idiopathic Parkinson's disease with the classic triad of rest tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia, plus non-motor features (anosmia, REM sleep disorder, constipation) and asymmetric onset.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient recovering from total hip replacement presents with functional limitation in the early post-operative period. The patient reports hip pain and restricted motion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation is present with difficulty walking, ascending stairs, and returning to normal activities. The patient requires assistive devices.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Total hip replacement was performed weeks to months prior for osteoarthritis or post-traumatic arthritis. The patient is in early rehabilitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, hip abductor weakness is present. Hip motion is restricted. Gait pattern is abnormal with antalgic gait.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate appropriate prosthesis positioning and seating. Radiographs exclude mechanical complications.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Hip Replacement shows excellent long-term outcome at 18 months with nearly complete functional recovery and stable imaging findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with acute knee injury and loss of knee stability. The injury occurred with a deceleration or pivoting mechanism.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient demonstrates functional limitation with inability to return to sport. The knee demonstrates giving way with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute injury during sport with specific mechanism of injury documented. The patient heard or felt a pop.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive Lachman test and anterior drawer test demonstrate anterior knee instability. Pivot shift test may be positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates complete anterior cruciate ligament tear. MRI characterises associated injuries including meniscal tears.

puzzles.explanation: ACL Grade 2 shows partial ligament tear with positive Lachman and drawer tests, preserved but damaged fibers on MRI with classic mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient, often with diabetes or other systemic disease, presents with progressive sensory loss and weakness in the feet. Symptoms develop insidiously.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with difficulty walking and balance impairment. The patient demonstrates fall risk.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing diabetes, vitamin B12 deficiency, or other systemic disease is present. Symptom progression is gradual over months to years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, sensory loss in stocking-glove distribution is demonstrated. Reflexes are diminished or absent. Weakness may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography demonstrates axonal loss pattern consistent with peripheral neuropathy. MRI of brain and spinal cord excludes central causes if needed.

puzzles.explanation: Distal symmetric sensory diabetic polyneuropathy with typical distribution, functional impairment, and increased ulceration risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old warehouse worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the hand that worsens at night and affects sleep quality. The symptoms began insidiously 3 months ago without preceding trauma. Pain radiates from the elbow down to the ring and little fingers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty gripping tools at work and has dropped several items in the past month. Prolonged elbow flexion during computer work exacerbates symptoms, while extending the arm and shaking the hand provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works assembly lines requiring repetitive elbow flexion. Past medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis controlled on medication. The patient denies neck pain or shoulder involvement. Symptoms are worse in the morning after sleeping with the elbow bent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tinel's sign is positive at the medial elbow. Froment's sign demonstrates weakness of thumb adduction. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy is visible between thumb and index finger. Two-point discrimination is diminished in the ulnar distribution compared to the contralateral side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging shows enlargement and hypoechoic appearance of the ulnar nerve at the medial elbow, consistent with nerve swelling. The nerve measures 0.7 cm in cross-sectional area at the compression site, well above normal limits of 0.4 cm.

puzzles.explanation: Cubital tunnel syndrome secondary to osteoarthritis with prominent motor findings and muscle atrophy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An adolescent athlete presents with pain at the tibial tuberosity below the kneecap. Pain develops during or after physical activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specifically triggered by running, jumping, and activities involving knee extension. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The adolescent is typically 11-18 years old during growth spurt. Symptoms correlate with periods of rapid growth. Sport involvement is common.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, swelling and tenderness are present at the tibial tuberosity. Pain is reproduced with knee extension against resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show tibial tuberosity enlargement and fragmentation. MRI demonstrates tibial tuberosity inflammation and soft tissue involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a traction apophysitis of the tibial tuberosity common in adolescents during growth spurts with high sporting loads involving running and jumping.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient following extreme physical exertion presents with severe muscle pain, weakness, and dark urine. Symptoms develop during or immediately after intense exercise.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with inability to perform activities. The patient may develop acute kidney injury with fluid status abnormality.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Extreme physical exertion in an unaccustomed person or environmental conditions is documented. Heat exposure may be contributory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, severe muscle tenderness is evident. Myoglobinuria is present with dark-colored urine. Acute renal dysfunction may develop.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Laboratory testing demonstrates markedly elevated creatine kinase levels. Urinalysis shows myoglobin. Serum creatinine elevation indicates acute kidney injury.

puzzles.explanation: Exertional rhabdomyolysis shows muscle breakdown with severe CK elevation, myoglobinuria, and risk of acute kidney injury from intense exertion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with migraine headache history presents with episodes of vertigo that occur with or separate from headache. Vertigo episodes are recurrent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with balance impairment and nausea. The patient demonstrates reduced activity tolerance during episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of migraine headaches is present with associated visual or auditory symptoms. Vertigo may occur with headache or independently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positional nystagmus or gaze-evoked nystagmus may be present. Balance testing shows abnormalities. Neurological examination is otherwise normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain excludes other causes of vertigo. MRI is normal in vestibular migraine.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular Migraine presents as vertigo-predominant migraine without accompanying headache, showing the vestibular symptoms without the typical headache component.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with pain in the groin region or lower abdominal region. Pain develops with athletic activity and resolves with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or twisting movements. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. Sport involving rapid acceleration and deceleration is typical.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited in the groin region and lower abdomen. Pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction and internal rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates adductor muscle strain or rectus femoris tear near the pubic symphysis. MRI characterises muscle injury and inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Athletic pubalgia represents a functional defect in the posterior inguinal wall without overt hernia, difficult to diagnose.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old active recreational athlete presents with medial knee pain and functional limitation following arthroscopic partial meniscectomy 3 weeks ago. Recovering from surgical intervention with mechanical knee symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stairs, squatting, and kneeling due to joint line discomfort. Walking with an antalgic gait. Pain worsens with deep knee flexion and rotational activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Meniscal tear from twisting injury during football. Post-operative period with quadriceps inhibition noted. No prior significant knee pathology. Pain level moderate 5/10.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild joint effusion on examination. Quadriceps strength 4/5, VMO atrophy evident. Joint line tenderness medially reproduced. Knee flexion range 95 degrees with terminal extension lag 5 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs confirm meniscectomy status. MRI demonstrates meniscal resection with intact ligaments. No chondral damage noted on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Post-meniscectomy rehabilitation requires early quadriceps activation and strengthening, proprioceptive retraining, restoration of confidence in knee stability, and gradual progression to sport-specific activities while preventing secondary overload injuries.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged to older adult presents with unilateral low back pain and possible radiation into the leg. The pain is mechanical and activity-related.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by extension and ipsilateral rotation activities. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with certain movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms are chronic or recurrent with mechanical characteristics. Recent increase in activity may trigger exacerbation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pain is reproduced with extension and ipsilateral rotation. Unilateral tenderness over the facet joint is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates facet joint hypertrophy and degenerative changes. Imaging confirms facet joint involvement as pain source.

puzzles.explanation: Facet joint syndrome presents as a degenerative condition in an older patient with chronic pain and stiffness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An adolescent presents with progressive thoracic kyphosis and back pain. The pain is mechanical and activity-related.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with postural limitations and back pain with activity. Physical appearance shows rounded thoracic spine.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The condition develops during adolescence with progressive kyphotic deformity. Genetic predisposition may be present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, fixed thoracic kyphosis is evident that does not correct with forward bending. Back pain is elicited with extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate thoracic vertebral body wedging and Schmorl's nodes characteristic of Scheuermann's disease. Kyphosis is measured.

puzzles.explanation: Scheuermann's disease is a resolved condition with residual postural deformity and minimal ongoing pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with chronic lung disease presents with progressive dyspnoea on exertion and dry cough. Symptoms have developed insidiously over years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive functional limitation is present with reduced exercise tolerance. The patient demonstrates dyspnoea with minimal exertion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing history of exposure (environmental, occupational, or medication-related) may be documented. Progressive nature is characteristic.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, fine inspiratory crackles are heard on chest auscultation. Finger clubbing may be present. Cyanosis may develop in advanced disease.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT of the lungs demonstrates pulmonary fibrosis with ground glass infiltrates and reticular opacities. Pulmonary function tests show restrictive pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Acute exacerbation of IPF presentation, which carries poor prognosis. Respiratory failure triggered by viral infection, representing clinical deterioration despite antifibrotic therapy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old female presents with difficulty controlling bladder and bowel function that has progressed over 8 months. She reports urgency and occasional incontinence during exercise, coughing, or sneezing. There is also pain with sexual intercourse.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient has modified her routine to avoid jumping or running due to urine leakage. She performs Kegel exercises but reports poor muscle control. Prolonged sitting worsens pelvic discomfort, while lying down relieves symptoms. She avoids tampon use due to discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She had two vaginal deliveries 15 and 10 years ago with episiotomy noted during second delivery. She reports a history of chronic constipation. Menstrual cycles are regular without hormonal contraceptive use. She denies previous pelvic surgery beyond childbirth.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Internal palpation reveals weak muscle contraction during requested pelvic floor squeeze, graded 2/5. A cough stress test produces leakage before pelvic floor contraction occurs. Tender trigger points are noted in the pelvic floor muscles bilaterally during assessment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates descent of the bladder base beyond 5 cm on Valsalva maneuver, indicating hypermobility. Pelvic floor thickness measures 0.6 cm on endovaginal ultrasound, below the normal range of 0.8-1.2 cm, confirming atrophy and dysfunction.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction shows classic stress incontinence from weakness in a multiparous menopausal female with poor muscular support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient recovering from thermal injury presents with tissue damage requiring rehabilitation. The extent of burns affects functional rehabilitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation depends on burn severity and location, with limitation in range of motion and pain with movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Significant thermal injury is documented with degree of burn classified. The patient is in the post-acute rehabilitation phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, healing burn wounds are evident with scar tissue formation. Contractures may limit range of motion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging documents burn depth and characterises wound healing. Serial imaging documents scar tissue maturation.

puzzles.explanation: Full-thickness burn rehabilitation requires careful graft protection during early phases, progressive range of motion, hypertrophic scar management, pain control strategies, and cautious progression to weight-bearing and ambulation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with acute shoulder trauma presents with shoulder pain and visible deformity over the clavicle. Pain worsens with arm movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation is present with inability to perform shoulder activities. The patient demonstrates guarding of the arm.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute trauma with fall on the shoulder or direct blow to the clavicle. Immediate pain and disability onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visible deformity over the clavicle is present. Clavicular tenderness is elicited. Shoulder passive motion is limited by pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate clavicle fracture with characterisation of displacement and comminution. Imaging excludes neurovascular injury.

puzzles.explanation: Low-energy clavicle fracture in elderly patient with osteoporosis from minor trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old construction supervisor presents with severe lower back pain that began suddenly while lifting a heavy load 2 weeks ago. The pain radiates down the left leg into the foot with associated numbness in the foot. Sitting and bending forward intensely worsen the pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot sit for more than 15 minutes without severe radiating pain. Lying flat with the left leg elevated provides mild relief. Bending forward to pick up objects triggers sharp shooting pain down the left leg. Light walking is tolerated better than sitting or standing still.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 10-year history of intermittent lower back pain managed with NSAIDs and physical therapy. He reports smoking one pack daily and spends 8 hours per day in a vehicle for work. No prior episodes of leg pain. Family history includes degenerative disc disease in his father.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Straight leg raise test is positive on the left at 35 degrees with reproduction of radiating pain. Ankle dorsiflexion weakness is demonstrated as 4/5 on the left. Sensory testing shows decreased sensation in the L5 dermatome on the left foot. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are intact bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine reveals a large posterolateral disc herniation at L4-L5 with significant compression of the left L5 nerve root. The herniated disc material is noted to be sequestered, and there is associated left-sided foraminal stenosis at the level of involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar disc herniation is an atypical presentation in a young athlete with acute trauma during sport and initially misleading clinical presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old racquetball player presents with wrist pain on the ulnar side that began gradually during a tournament 6 weeks ago. Pain is located on the medial aspect of the wrist just above the wrist crease and worsens with gripping activities. There is associated clicking sensation in the wrist.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Racquet sports and activities requiring power grip aggravate the pain significantly. Twisting motions of the wrist, particularly combined with ulnar deviation, provoke sharp pain. The patient reports weakness when attempting to lift objects with the affected hand. Rest and ice provide temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has played racquetball competitively for 15 years with no prior wrist injuries. He reports recent increase in training intensity. No systemic inflammatory history. The patient is right-hand dominant with left wrist affected. He denies previous wrist fractures or dislocations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The ulnar fovea sign is positive with palpable laxity of the ulnocarpal ligaments. Positive ulnar fovea sign test reproduces the patient's clicking. The ulnar meniscus load test provokes pain with axial compression and ulnar deviation combined. Grip strength is reduced 25% on the affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the wrist shows a tear of the triangular fibrocartilage with associated fluid signal extending into the tear. There is mild signal change in the lunate bone consistent with early chondral injury. The ulnar styloid process is intact without fracture or edema.

puzzles.explanation: TFCC tear from acute twisting mechanism with ulnar-sided pain and instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with acute elbow trauma presents with elbow pain and swelling. The injury involved a fall on an outstretched hand.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with difficulty with elbow movement and gripping. The patient demonstrates pain with pronation and supination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute trauma mechanism documented with fall on outstretched hand and elbow compression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, elbow swelling and tenderness over the radial head are present. Pain with pronation and supination indicates radial head involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate radial head fracture. CT imaging characterises fracture pattern and displacement important for treatment planning.

puzzles.explanation: Radial head fracture from FOOSH injury with loss of forearm rotation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH) injury presents with acute wrist pain and visible deformity. The injury is dorsal wrist deformity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with inability to use the hand for functional activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall on outstretched hand mechanism documented. The elderly are most commonly affected.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, "dinner fork" deformity is visible over the dorsal wrist. Significant swelling and tenderness are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate distal radius fracture with dorsal angulation and displacement characteristic of Colles fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Classic Colles fracture in elderly osteoporotic patient from fall with typical 'fork' deformity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old male reports burning pain and numbness on the outer thigh that began 3 months ago and is progressively worsening. The symptoms are localized to the lateral thigh and do not extend below the knee. He describes a constant burning sensation with occasional electric shock sensations.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged standing and walking exacerbate the burning pain and numbness significantly. Wearing tight waistbands, belts, or sitting with legs crossed worsens the symptoms dramatically. Lying down with the hip flexed provides relief. The patient has reduced his walking distance from 30 minutes to 5 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 40-pound weight gain over the past 18 months and recently switched to wearing tighter fitting work pants due to his occupation. He denies lumbar pain or lower extremity radiculopathy. Recent history includes a fall onto his hip, though skin over the affected area is intact.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sensation is diminished to light touch and pinprick over the lateral thigh in the L2-L3 dermatome distribution. Straight leg raise test is negative. Hip flexion and extension strength are normal 5/5. Tapping over the inguinal ligament just medial to the anterior superior iliac spine reproduces the burning sensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the pelvis and hip shows no mass or structural lesion at the inguinal ligament. The appearance is normal without focal nerve enlargement. Imaging confirms the absence of lumbar radiculopathy or hip joint pathology, supporting a diagnosis of nerve entrapment rather than central or referred pain.

puzzles.explanation: Post-surgical meralgia paraesthetica from iatrogenic nerve compression during hip surgery with isolated sensory distribution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with acute chest trauma presents with chest wall pain that is sharp and localised to the rib region. Pain is worse with breathing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with difficulty with breathing due to pain. The patient demonstrates guarding and shallow breathing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute trauma with fall or direct blow to the chest. Pain onset is immediate.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, focal tenderness over the rib is elicited. Pain is reproduced with chest compression or rib palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may demonstrate fracture line, though many rib fractures are not visualized initially. Chest CT demonstrates fracture and excludes pulmonary injury.

puzzles.explanation: Rib fracture has chronic sequelae with persistent pain despite imaging healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with history of repetitive wrist activity presents with pain in the proximal dorsal forearm. Pain is localised to the area where extensor tendons cross.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by repetitive wrist extension or activities requiring wrist motion. Swelling is present in the forearm.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms develop from repetitive gripping or wrist extension activities. The patient has history of occupational or sport-related activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the proximal dorsal forearm where extensors carpi radialis longus and brevis cross over extensor carpi radialis and extensor digitorum communis. Crepitus may be appreciated with movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates tendon sheath inflammation and tenosynovitis at the intersection area. MRI shows inflammation and swelling.

puzzles.explanation: Intersection syndrome from intense gripping activities with characteristic location and crepitus finding.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An infant born with shoulder dystocia presents with arm weakness affecting the upper extremity. The arm demonstrates weakness and loss of sensation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation is present with inability to move the arm. The infant demonstrates absent Moro reflex on the affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Birth injury from shoulder dystocia during delivery. The infant demonstrates immediate weakness after birth.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, weakness of shoulder abduction and external rotation is evident, with characteristic "waiter's tip" position of the arm.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography documents denervation changes in affected muscles. MRI may show nerve root avulsion if severe.

puzzles.explanation: Erb's palsy is injury to the upper brachial plexus (C5-C6) from birth trauma, resulting in shoulder and arm weakness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with acute stroke involving anterior cerebral artery territory presents with acute neurological deficit. The patient has unilateral lower extremity weakness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with weakness in the contralateral lower extremity. The patient may demonstrate gait disturbance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute stroke event documented with vascular risk factors present. Time course indicates acute ischemia.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, lower extremity weakness is evident in the contralateral distribution. Sensory changes and cognitive changes may accompany.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates acute infarction in the anterior cerebral artery distribution. MRI characterises the extent of ischemia.
