clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays show mild degenerative disc disease at L4-L5 and L5-S1. MRI demonstrates small disc bulging without neural compromise. No significant pathology explaining symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Non-specific low back pain is the most common presentation: acute mechanical pain without serious structural disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man with hypertension and smoking history presents acute onset right-sided weakness and facial droop. Symptoms began 2 hours ago while at work. Admitted to acute care.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Speech dysarthria and receptive language difficulty noted. Right upper extremity strength 2/5, lower extremity 3/5. Gait unstable requiring assistance. Cognitive changes present.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of hypertension poorly controlled (BP 180/110 on admission). Recent smoking 20 cigarettes daily. No prior stroke history. Symptoms progressed rapidly from onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left facial droop evident. Right pronator drift. Right-sided hyperreflexia noted (3+). Babinski sign present on right. Homonymous right visual field cut noted on testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Acute CT brain shows hypodensity in left MCA territory involving motor cortex and corona radiata. Diffusion-weighted MRI confirms acute ischemic stroke. Angiography shows MCA occlusion.

puzzles.explanation: Right MCA stroke presenting with contralateral hemiparesis, UMN signs, and facial droop, secondary to atrial fibrillation with cardioembolic source.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman with varicose veins presents with a painful ulcer on the medial ankle area. Long history of lower leg edema and skin changes. Ulcer present 4 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worse with dependent leg position, eases with leg elevation. Exudate moderate amount. Difficulty walking due to pain and bulky dressing. Functional mobility impaired.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of varicose veins for 20 years. Previous DVT right leg 15 years ago. Mobility limited due to arthritis. Chronic lower leg edema despite compression attempts.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Medial malleolar region ulcer with depth 0.5 cm, irregular borders, beefy red base. Surrounding skin with lipodermatosclerosis and hemosiderin deposition. Pitting edema bilateral legs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Duplex ultrasound shows superficial and deep venous insufficiency with valve incompetence in great saphenous vein. No acute DVT. Ankle-brachial index 0.95, confirming adequate arterial perfusion.

puzzles.explanation: Classic venous ulcer with post-thrombotic sequelae, characteristic location above medial malleolus, and surrounding venous insufficiency skin changes. Hemodynamically significant venous disease confirmed by ultrasound.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old patient is evaluated 6 weeks following total hip replacement for osteoarthritis. The patient reports hip pain and restricted motion. Early post-operative rehabilitation is progressing gradually.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hip motion is limited in flexion, abduction, and rotation to avoid prosthetic dislocation. Weight-bearing is progressing gradually with assistive device use. Ambulation tolerance is improving with therapy.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Total hip arthroplasty was performed for advanced osteoarthritis. Hip precautions must be maintained during healing phase. Early post-operative period requires careful supervised rehabilitation to prevent prosthetic complications.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, hip range of motion is measured with precaution against forbidden positions. Hip abductor strength is diminished. Gait pattern demonstrates weight-bearing protection and abnormal mechanics.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs confirm appropriate prosthetic component positioning. No signs of instability, loosening, or impingement are visible. Imaging supports continued rehabilitation progression within precautionary guidelines.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Hip Replacement shows early post-operative period (6 weeks) with expected precautions, limited motion, and gradual weight-bearing progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man with shoulder calcification presents with recurrent episodes of acute shoulder pain. Previous episode 2 years ago, now recurring. Sudden exacerbation of symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain severe with abduction and elevation. Limited active range of motion. Functional limitation returning after period of relief. Pain at night limiting sleep.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prior calcific deposit managed conservatively. Symptoms resolved previously with therapy. Current exacerbation spontaneous without apparent trauma. No recent overuse.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tender supraspinous region on palpation. Pain reproduction with painful arc maneuver. Guarding with shoulder motion. Scapular dyskinesis evident on movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder radiographs demonstrate calcific deposit in supraspinatus region unchanged from prior films. Deposit size stable. CT imaging confirms deposit location. No fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic supraspinatus calcification with asymptomatic deposits causing chronic pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old soccer player presents with lateral midfoot pain after an inversion ankle sprain 2 weeks ago. Pain localized to lateral foot, not at ankle joint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty walking and running. Pain with weight-bearing on lateral foot edge. Unable to participate in training and competition. Worsens with plantarflexion and inversion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Inversion ankle injury during practice 2 weeks prior. Initial ankle swelling has resolved but midfoot pain persists. No previous foot/ankle injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness over cuboid bone palpated from lateral foot. Pain with pronation and plantarflexion loading. Resisted plantarflexion and inversion reproduces pain. Positive cuboid squeeze test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Foot X-rays show normal ankle and calcaneous alignment, but cuboid appears slightly subluxed relative to navicular. Talonavicular joint alignment assessed. No fractures identified.

puzzles.explanation: Cuboid syndrome involves subtle misalignment of the cuboid bone causing persistent lateral midfoot pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation develops acute left arm and leg weakness with speech difficulty. Sudden symptom onset during morning. Immediate hospital admission.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Speech slurred and difficult to understand. Left-sided weakness affecting arm and leg function. Gait impaired. Acute functional loss requiring assistance with mobility.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic atrial fibrillation not on anticoagulation. Hypertension and prior TIA history. Acute stroke symptoms after morning fall. Rapid progression of deficits.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left facial droop with asymmetric smile. Left upper extremity weakness 2/5, lower extremity 3/5. Pronator drift present. Hyperreflexia on left side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows acute ischemic stroke in left MCA distribution. Diffusion-weighted imaging confirms infarction. Atrial fibrillation confirmed on cardiac monitoring.

puzzles.explanation: Acute left MCA stroke with large territory involvement causing contralateral motor deficit, speech disturbance, and visual field loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman, 4 weeks post-myocardial infarction (inferior wall), presents to begin supervised cardiac rehabilitation. Anterior wall territory preserved. Moderate left ventricular dysfunction.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Exercise tolerance 2 METs at baseline. Dyspnea and chest discomfort with greater exertion. Anxiety about cardiac status limiting activity participation. Fatigue prominent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Inferior STEMI treated with primary angioplasty and stent 4 weeks prior. Ejection fraction 40% measured on echocardiogram. On appropriate medical therapy. Risk factor modification ongoing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate elevated at 78 bpm. Blood pressure normal at current status. No arrhythmias on telemetry monitoring. Cardiac murmurs absent. Peripheral edema absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Stress echo shows improvement in wall motion from initial. Ejection fraction remains at 40%. Ischemia not provoked. Imaging supports safe progression of exercise.

puzzles.explanation: Elderly post-MI rehab patient with multiple comorbidities and reduced exercise capacity. Requires modified rehabilitation approach addressing orthostatic symptoms and arthritic limitations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old typist reports catching sensation in right index finger with frequent triggering during work. Clicking sensation during finger flexion and extension. Progressive symptoms over 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with repetitive gripping and typing activities. Morning stiffness and locking worse before activity. Functional limitation in keyboard work and writing tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occupational repetitive strain from data entry work 8 hours daily. Gradual progressive onset. No trauma. Previous history of mild wrist pain from RSI.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nodule palpable at MCP level of index finger. A1 pulley tenderness on palpation. Triggering demonstrated with active finger motion. Mild swelling over pulley.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows flexor tendon nodule and pulley thickening at A1 level index finger. Mild effusion around tendon. No partial tear. Dynamic ultrasound confirms tendon nodule catching.

puzzles.explanation: Multi-digit trigger finger in diabetic patient, more severe and difficult to treat than typical single-finger presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old cyclist with chronic lateral ankle pain and lower leg aching. Pain present over 4 weeks, worsening with lateral foot-loading activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with cycling and hill training. Functional impact on training schedule and race participation. Lateral ankle instability sensation with uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous inversion ankle sprain 18 months prior that resulted in chronic lateral ankle instability. Cycling training frequency increased 2 months ago. Weak ankle stabilizers noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Peroneal tendon tenderness palpated behind lateral malleolus with thickening noted. Pain with resistance eversion testing. Ankle inversion stress test positive for functional instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows peroneal tendon thickening with increased echo texture heterogeneity. Mild subcutaneous fluid. No tendon subluxation. MRI demonstrates tendinopathy without tear.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic peroneal tendinopathy often results from chronic ankle instability and compensatory overuse.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old mechanic presents with inability to flex the thumb IP joint and index finger DIP joint. Noticed sudden weakness with loss of pinch function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional loss of thumb pinch strength affecting ability to grip small tools. Cannot make OK sign with thumb and index finger. Weakness causes work limitations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset of weakness with overhead reaching activity at work 3 days ago. No trauma but possible nerve compression from muscle hypertrophy or pronator teres tightness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Inability to flex thumb IP joint (positive weak thumb sign) and index FDP weakness. Forearm pronation strength normal. Sensation intact in first web space. Motor deficit specific to AIN.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowed motor conduction in anterior interosseous branch of median nerve. EMG demonstrates acute denervation changes in flexor pollicis longus and flexor digitorum profundus.

puzzles.explanation: Anterior interosseous syndrome with motor-only deficits and preserved sensation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old woman presents 3 weeks after a fall from standing height with anxiety and fear-avoidance behaviors. Physical injuries minor, but psychological impact significant.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Reduced mobility and activity due to fear of falling again. Avoids walking independently. Functional independence decreased significantly. Sleep disturbance due to anxiety.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall occurred while reaching for object, fell onto carpeted floor. Minor bruising but no fractures or serious injury sustained. Lived independently prior, now isolated due to fear.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gait cautious and slower, avoids turning. Functional reach test limited due to fear rather than physical limitation. No demonstrable neurological deficit. Cognitive assessment normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Physical examination and imaging (falls risk screen, DEXA scan) show no injury or bone loss to explain avoidance. Anxiety scores elevated (GAD-7). Psychological assessment confirms post-trauma anxiety.

puzzles.explanation: Post-fall anxiety syndrome causes activity avoidance and deconditioning, increasing actual fall risk and disability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old woman develops acute onset severe neck pain after turning to reach something while seated. Neck rigidity and inability to move head. Pain sudden and severe.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to lift head from pillow without assistance. Arm and hand functional (no neurological deficit). Pain radiates across shoulders. Significant functional impairment.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden unexpected neck movement caused acute spasm. No trauma or pre-existing neck issues. Associated with acute facet joint involvement. Symptoms severe and acute.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant cervical muscle spasm and rigidity palpable. Neck rotation severely limited in both directions. Cervical flexion-extension significantly restricted. No neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical X-rays normal, no structural abnormality. Muscle spasm confirmed on imaging. Likely acute facet-mediated wry neck. No nerve root compromise demonstrated clinically.

puzzles.explanation: Acute wry neck occurs in a young person with activity-related acute onset and rapid symptom progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old soccer player sustains direct blow to lateral leg during match. Acute onset severe lateral leg pain at mid-fibular level. Immediate swelling and ecchymosis.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Rapid swelling around lateral leg. Pain with weight-bearing and ankle motion. Unable to continue playing. Significant functional limitation with gait disturbance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct trauma from opposing player's knee to lateral leg during contact play. Immediate pain and swelling. No prior leg injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant swelling and ecchymosis over lateral leg, mid-fibular region. Fibular head palpable bone tenderness. Pain with ankle plantarflexion and eversion loading. Ankle dorsiflexion intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Leg X-rays show simple transverse fracture of fibula at mid-shaft level. No associated tibia fracture. No proximal fibula injury. No ankle syndesmotic involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Fibular fractures may be isolated or associated with ankle fractures requiring appropriate management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old man presents with progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness over 5 days following viral illness. Ascending paralysis pattern noted. Presented to emergency department.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weakness progresses proximally over days. Hip flexion and knee extension weakening. Able to ambulate with assistance but fatigue-prone. Progressive loss of motor function.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Upper respiratory tract infection 1 week prior with cough and congestion. Complete recovery from respiratory symptoms. 3 days post-infection, leg weakness began acutely.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral lower extremity weakness greater distally, ascending pattern. Areflexia in legs (absent patellar reflexes). Hip flexor weakness 4/5, knee extensors 4/5. Sensory exam relatively preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography shows acute demyelinating pattern with prolonged latencies and conduction blocks. Cerebrospinal fluid albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, normal cells). Nerve conduction slowing distally.

puzzles.explanation: GBS presenting as ascending paralysis with areflexia following Campylobacter infection, with albuminocytologic dissociation on LP and declining FVC requiring close respiratory monitoring.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational athlete presents with acute quadriceps contusion from direct blow during contact sport. Immediate pain and swelling over anterior thigh.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant pain with knee motion. Difficulty with walking and stair climbing. Swelling and ecchymosis developing. Functional limitation in lower extremity activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct blow from opponent's knee to anterior thigh during competition 2 hours ago. Immediate injury with pain and rapid swelling onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant ecchymosis and swelling over quadriceps region. Knee flexion limited to 75 degrees due to pain and swelling. Quadriceps strength 4/5 due to pain inhibition. Palpable muscle firmness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows muscle hematoma within quadriceps. Muscle fiber disruption visible with heterogeneous echotexture. No fracture seen on adjacent X-ray imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Muscle contusion is classic 'dead leg' presentation with direct trauma history, localized swelling, and hematoma formation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 41-year-old woman presents with winging of the scapula, noticeable when reaching forward or pushing. Gradual onset over 3 weeks with progressive functional loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with overhead reaching and pushing activities. Scapula protrudes from chest wall when pushing against wall. Functional limitation with upper extremity strength.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No acute trauma identified. Possible nerve injury from heavy lifting or overhead activity. Gradual progressive onset suggesting nerve inflammation or injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive scapular winging when patient pushes arms against wall or performs wall push-up. Serratus anterior strength significantly weakened. Scapula borders prominent. Shoulder ROM preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography shows denervation of serratus anterior muscle. Motor unit action potentials reduced and reinnervation changes variable. Nerve conduction normal for other upper extremity nerves.

puzzles.explanation: Long thoracic nerve palsy causing serratus anterior paralysis with characteristic winged scapula and weakness of scapular protraction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old racquet sport player presents with lateral elbow pain and functional limitation. The patient reports pain with gripping and upper extremity use. Onset followed an increase in training intensity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is consistently exacerbated by gripping, lifting, and repetitive forearm supination during racquet sports. Functional activities including writing and carrying are limited. Complete rest provides relief, whereas activity provokes pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Repetitive microtrauma from racquet sports and increased training intensity precipitated symptoms. Chronic overuse of forearm extensor muscles has led to tendinopathy at the lateral epicondyle. No acute traumatic injury preceded the gradual symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, lateral epicondyle palpation elicits focal tenderness. Grip strength testing reproduces lateral elbow pain. The Cozen test (resisted wrist extension) is positive. Range of motion is preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasonography demonstrates tendon thickening and hyperechoic changes in the extensor carpi radialis brevis tendon. Fluid is not typically seen. Imaging findings confirm lateral epicondylitis.

puzzles.explanation: Atypical presentation in a younger athlete with rapid onset, likely from biomechanical errors and sudden increase in activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 13-year-old obese boy presents with groin pain and limping gait. Pain began 2 weeks ago with insidious onset. Hip pain worse with internal rotation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation with walking and sports participation. Hip internal rotation painful and limited. Gait antalgic with external rotation preference. Pain radiates to knee.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rapid growth during puberty. Obesity (BMI 32 at age 13). No preceding trauma. Pain with physical activity but worsens with continued loading.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip internal rotation severely limited and painful. Positive Lachman-like sign (external rotation with hip flexion). Knee and hip examination otherwise benign. Gait externally rotated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip X-rays (frog-leg view) show widened and irregular epiphyseal plate with femoral head slippage relative to neck. Posterior displacement of capital femoral epiphysis visible. MRI confirms SCFE.

puzzles.explanation: SCFE is a pediatric emergency requiring urgent orthopedic management to prevent complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man recovering from septic shock requiring 2-week mechanical ventilation presents with profound muscle weakness. Multiple ICU complications during hospitalization. Muscle loss evident.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty transitioning from bed to chair. Requires assistance with all mobility. Unable to stand independently. Profound functional dependence present.

clues.label: History
clues.text: ARDS from severe sepsis requiring intubation 2 weeks. Prolonged sedation with paralytic agents. Minimal mobilization during acute illness. Nutritional support inadequate.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked generalized weakness in all muscle groups. Hip strength 2/5, knee extensors 1+/5. Hand grip minimal bilaterally. Tone decreased. Reflexes intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows myopathic changes consistent with critical illness myopathy. CK normal. No denervation pattern. Muscle biopsy would show fiber atrophy predominantly Type 2.

puzzles.explanation: Post-COVID ICU-acquired weakness presentation with motor-predominant deficits requiring intensive rehabilitation. Long-term recovery potential given younger age and improving inflammatory markers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-week-old female infant presents with positive hip examination findings detected at birth screening. Ortolani maneuver positive. Hip ultrasound ordered for confirmation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Asymmetric skin folds noted. Asymmetric hip abduction when hips flexed and externally rotated. No pain noted as infant non-verbal but shows discomfort with maneuvers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Born full-term vaginally, no complications. Female sex, first-born, positive family history of hip dysplasia in paternal grandmother. Breech presentation during labor.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ortolani maneuver elicits clunking sensation with hip reduction. Barlow maneuver positive with hip dislocation. Limited hip abduction bilaterally. Asymmetric gluteal folds.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip ultrasound shows dysplastic acetabulum with inadequate coverage. Femoral head position lateralized relative to acetabulum. Graf classification system used for assessment.

puzzles.explanation: DDH involves abnormal development of the hip joint where the femoral head is not properly seated in the acetabulum, detected early through clinical tests.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman reports stress incontinence with functional limitations from loss of bladder control with exertion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation with physical activities. Avoidance of exercise due to incontinence. Social impact from symptoms. Loss of confidence in physical abilities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple vaginal deliveries with difficult obstetric history. Menopause 8 years prior. Relatively recent symptom onset 1 year ago. Progressive worsening with activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor muscles on examination. Positive cough stress test. Normal detrusor function on preliminary assessment. Sphincter laxity evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Urodynamics documents urethral sphincter incompetence and bladder neck hypermobility on cough stress test. Detrusor function normal. Leak point pressure reduced at 45 cm H2O.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction shows severe weakness with prolapse in an elderly multiparous female with chronic straining and long-standing pelvic floor damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man, hospitalized for 5 days post-surgical abdominal procedure, develops acute onset dyspnea and chest pain. Tachypnea and tachycardia noted. Acute presentation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe dyspnea with minimal exertion. Pleuritic chest pain. Hypoxemia noted (SpO2 90%). Acute functional limitation with significant distress.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Post-operative day 5 from abdominal surgery for small bowel obstruction. Immobility during recovery period. Hypercoagulable post-operative state. No anticoagulation prophylaxis given.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tachycardia (HR 115), tachypnea (RR 28), hypoxemia (SpO2 89% on room air). Unilateral leg swelling absent. JVD not prominent. Lung auscultation with scattered crackles. Positive Wells score.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT pulmonary angiography shows filling defect in right lower lobar pulmonary artery confirming embolus. Bilateral subsegmental emboli visible. Echocardiography shows right ventricular strain.

puzzles.explanation: Classic acute PE presentation with risk factors (immobilization, recent surgery), acute onset dyspnea, and confirmatory imaging showing thrombus.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old man presents with progressive weakness and slowing of function over 1 year. Unintentional weight loss of 4 kg over 6 months despite adequate nutrition.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe reduction in physical activity and endurance. Fatigues easily with minimal exertion. Difficulty with stairs and walking distance decreased significantly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive decline without specific illness. Lives alone in apartment. Minimal social engagement. Chronic conditions include hypertension and diabetes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized weakness in lower extremities (hip extensors 3+/5). Slow gait velocity approximately 0.6 m/s. Unintentional weight loss noted (10% from baseline). Meets criteria for frailty phenotype.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Comprehensive geriatric assessment shows low physical activity, grip strength reduced, gait slowed, spontaneous weight loss. Complete blood count shows mild anemia. Albumin levels low at 3.2 g/dL.

puzzles.explanation: Frailty syndrome is a geriatric syndrome characterized by reduced physiologic reserve, weakness, and vulnerability to adverse outcomes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old assembly line worker with repetitive hand grasping tasks develops radial wrist pain. Thumb pain with gripping and pinching activities affecting work. Symptoms over 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with fine gripping motions in assembly work. Inability to perform repetitive tasks. Swelling visible at radial wrist. Functional limitation in work and ADL.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occupational injury from repetitive assembly line work. 20 years in current position. Gradual progressive symptom onset. No acute trauma. Poor ergonomics at workstation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Finkelstein test positive reproducing pain. Tenderness at radial styloid process. Thumb abduction strength reduced secondary to pain. First dorsal compartment swelling palpable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows APL and EPB tendon thickening with synovial effusion. First dorsal compartment tendinitis evident. No tendon subluxation. Mild surrounding edema.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic refractory De Quervain's in elderly patient, atypical in duration and resistance to treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old manual laborer presents with lower back pain radiating into the lower extremity. The patient reports pain, numbness, and weakness in the leg. Functional limitation affects work capacity and mobility.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain radiates from the low back into the lower leg following a dermatome pattern. Symptoms are exacerbated by forward bending and sitting. Lying down with legs elevated provides relief. Weakness is noted in foot movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset followed a heavy lifting episode with acute low back strain. Chronic degenerative disc disease predisposes to nerve root compression. Progressive neurological symptoms indicate ongoing nerve compression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, straight leg raise reproduces lower extremity pain. Dermatome-specific sensory changes are present. Motor weakness is evident in muscles corresponding to the affected nerve root. Deep tendon reflexes may be diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates intervertebral disc herniation at the appropriate level with nerve root compression. The specific level correlates with the patient's clinical presentation. Imaging confirms mechanical nerve root compression.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar radiculopathy occurs in an occupational context with mechanical trauma and clear radicular pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old man presents with progressive loss of muscle mass and strength over 2 years. Difficulty with stairs and arising from chair. Generalized weakness noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation with activities of daily living. Difficulty with household tasks requiring strength. Increased risk of falls. Reduced independence with mobility.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sedentary lifestyle due to knee osteoarthritis. Retirement and reduced physical activity. Poor nutritional intake with reduced protein consumption. Progressive age-related changes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized muscle atrophy visible in thighs and upper arms. Leg strength testing shows hip flexors 3+/5, knee extensors 3+/5. Slow gait velocity. Poor sit-stand performance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry shows low appendicular lean mass index (ALM/height2 = 5.8 kg/m2). Muscle biopsies would show Type 2 fiber predominance and atrophy.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related sarcopenia involves progressive loss of muscle mass and strength from aging and reduced physical activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old male hockey player sustains acute groin injury during game. Acute onset medial thigh pain with immediate functional loss. Unable to continue playing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with hip adduction and skating motions. Difficulty walking with antalgic gait. Immediate functional limitation in sport. Significant pain with exertion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden acceleration and direction change during hockey play. High-speed sport mechanism. Acute onset with immediate pain. No prior groin injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Adductor tenderness at insertion. Pain with resisted hip adduction testing. Muscle guarding present. Swelling developing at acute phase. Antalgic gait evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows adductor muscle fiber disruption with hematoma formation. Fluid collection at injury site. Partial muscle tear confirmed. No complete rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Groin strain shows chronic recurrent adductor strain with muscle weakness and biomechanical dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old distance runner with previous history of medial tibial stress syndrome develops recurrent shin pain. Pain along medial tibia worse with running. Returning to training after recovery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with distance running and tempo training. Difficulty with intense training sessions. Previous episode had full recovery. Current recurrence with increased mileage.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous MTSS 2 years ago that resolved with conservative treatment. Returned to running progression. Current flare with recent training volume increase. Similar pain pattern to prior episode.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness reproduced along medial tibial border. Pain with resisted plantarflexion. Palpable periosteal thickening. Similar exam to previous episode.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays normal without stress fracture. Bone scan shows uptake along medial tibia consistent with stress response. Similar pattern to previous episode of MTSS.

puzzles.explanation: Bilateral medial tibial stress syndrome suggests systemic biomechanical or training factors rather than isolated pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old falls from height (3 meters) sustaining direct blow to leg with severe trauma. Acute severe pain in lower leg with immediate functional loss and swelling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight. Severe pain with any movement. Rapid swelling and deformity of lower leg. Acute functional loss of mobility.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy trauma from fall onto concrete surface with direct tibial impact. Significant mechanism of injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible deformity of lower leg with angulation at fracture site. Significant swelling and ecchymosis. Pain with palpation over fractured area. Distal neurovascular status intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Leg X-rays show transverse fracture of mid-tibial shaft with displacement. Fibula fracture also present (associated injury). No vascular compromise. Open fracture assessment performed.

puzzles.explanation: Tibial shaft fractures require careful management due to common complications like compartment syndrome.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 14-year-old male basketball player presents with pain at inferior patellar pole and proximal patellar ligament insertion. Pain begins with jump training 2 weeks ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with jumping, running, and kneeling activities. Exacerbated by basketball practice. Functional limitation with sport participation. Pain with palpation of patellar ligament insertion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Adolescent growth spurt with rapid skeletal maturation. Intensive basketball training with frequent jumping. No prior knee pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness at inferior patellar pole and patellar ligament origin. Pain with resisted quadriceps contraction and patellar ligament loading. Minimal effusion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Knee X-rays show small ossification center irregularity at patellar ligament insertion. MRI demonstrates inflammation at patellar ligament enthesis. Growth plate changes evident.

puzzles.explanation: Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease is traction apophysitis of the inferior patellar pole from growth plate stress during sports.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old woman presents with diffuse anterior knee pain with functional limitation. Pain with squatting, climbing stairs, and prolonged sitting. Gradual onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stairs, squatting, and standing from sitting. Functional limitation in activities of daily living and exercise tolerance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sedentary occupation with prolonged sitting. Recent home exercise program initiation. Possible hip weakness and flexibility issues contributing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Clarks test reproduces anterior knee pain. Vastus medialis strength reduced. Patellar tracking questionable. Pain with single-leg squat. Tight hip flexors and IT band.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging normal without structural pathology. Patellofemoral cartilage intact on MRI. Q-angle and patellar tilt measured normal. Diagnosis based on clinical examination.

puzzles.explanation: Patellofemoral pain is common in runners and results from patellar maltracking and muscular imbalances.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old woman with orthostatic tachycardia and exercise intolerance reports functional limitation with standing and exertion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation from tachycardia episodes. Difficulty with work activities requiring standing. Fatigue limiting daily activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young woman with sudden onset symptoms post-viral. POTS diagnosis suspected from orthostatic response. Exercise previously tolerated but now causing symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked heart rate increase with minimal BP change on orthostatic challenge. Tachycardia sustained during upright posture. Symptoms reproducible.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Tilt table test confirms POTS with sustained tachycardia >30 bpm increase. Norepinephrine inappropriately high supine relative to upright. Cardiovascular imaging normal.

puzzles.explanation: Severe POTS presentation with autonomic hyperactivity and possible mast cell activation. Marked disability despite therapeutic interventions indicates complex autonomic dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old athlete reports recurrent slipping sensation of tendons behind lateral ankle. Palpable subluxation of peroneal tendons with ankle inversion and plantarflexion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain and instability with ankle motion. Functional limitation with sport and activity. Recurrent episodes of tendon slipping causing ankle giving way sensation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous inversion ankle sprain 8 months ago with associated peroneal tendon dislocation briefly noted. Chronic lateral ankle instability. Recurrent subluxation with activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable subluxation of peroneal tendons posterior to lateral malleolus with ankle position changes. Positive peroneal tendon subluxation test. Pain with resisted eversion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound and dynamic MRI show peroneal tendons subluxing posterior to lateral malleolus with ankle plantarflexion and inversion. Superior peroneal retinaculum laxity noted.

puzzles.explanation: Peroneal tendon subluxation causes dynamic instability from loss of retinacular support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 75-year-old man with severe COPD presents with progressively worsening dyspnea despite therapy. Continuous cough with minimal sputum. Severe functional decline. Minimal exercise tolerance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dyspnea at rest and minimal activity. Difficulty with stairs and walking. Complete functional dependence. Requiring continuous oxygen therapy. Isolated socially.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Very long smoking history with continued smoking recently. COPD for 12+ years. Multiple exacerbations yearly. Significant decline in past year. Multiple hospitalizations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe cough. Minimal air movement throughout lungs. Prolonged expiration. Accessory muscle use prominent. SpO2 low even on oxygen. Barrel chest prominent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spirometry shows severe obstruction, FEV1 25% predicted. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation and emphysema. ABG shows chronic hypoxemia and hypercapnia. Cor pulmonale suspected.

puzzles.explanation: Non-smoking COPD presentation from biomass exposure (common in developing countries). Demonstrates that smoking is not the only etiology of airway obstruction and emphysema.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman with stress urinary incontinence from pelvic floor laxity reports functional limitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation from incontinence episodes. Difficulty with exercise and social activities. Leakage with lifting and physical exertion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple childbirths with difficult deliveries. Menopause 5 years prior. Progressive incontinence worsening with activity. Previous pelvic surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Stress test positive. Bladder neck hypermobile. No urgency incontinence component.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Urodynamics confirms stress incontinence. Sphincter incompetence documented. Normal detrusor function without overactivity.

puzzles.explanation: Stress Urinary Incontinence shows mixed incontinence (stress + urge) with overactive bladder component and elevated post-void residual in an older multiparous woman.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old presents 2 weeks post-motor vehicle accident with significant head trauma. Loss of consciousness for 3 hours at scene. Hospitalized for 5 days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cognitive difficulties including memory problems and concentration issues. Headaches daily with light and sound sensitivity. Dizziness and balance difficulty. Functional limitation with school work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Motor vehicle accident 2 weeks prior with high-impact head trauma. Loss of consciousness and post-traumatic amnesia for 4 hours. ICU admission for monitoring.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild cognitive deficits noted on basic testing. Photophobia and phonophobia present. Balance testing shows mild deficit. Headache reproducible with movement. Mood irritability noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Initial CT brain showed small epidural hematoma (managed conservatively). Follow-up CT normal. MRI shows diffuse axonal injury. Neuropsych testing shows mild cognitive deficits.

puzzles.explanation: Moderate traumatic brain injury with loss of consciousness, subdural hematoma, and diffuse axonal injury secondary to high-velocity motor vehicle collision.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man, 4 weeks post-rotator cuff repair (supraspinatus and infraspinatus), begins physiotherapy. Arm immobilized with sling. Pain controlled on medication.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation with arm mobility and use. Protected ROM being gradually restored. Pain present with attempted movement beyond protocol limits.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Large rotator cuff tear repaired surgically 4 weeks ago with open repair and anchor fixation. Post-operative protocol requires gradual ROM progression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder abduction passive ROM 65 degrees (protocol limit). External rotation 30 degrees passive. Shoulder flexion 70 degrees passive. Scapulohumeral rhythm guarding. Pain 3/10.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs confirm anchor positioning appropriate for healing. Ultrasound assessment shows intact repair at 4 weeks. No complications such as stiffness evident.

puzzles.explanation: Post-rotator cuff repair requires strict immobilization and protection during early healing phases, followed by cautious progression of passive then active-assisted movements, scapular stabilization training, and eventual strengthening to restore function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete presents with acute knee instability and pain following a twisting injury. The patient reports knee giving way during activity. The patella was visibly displaced laterally before spontaneous reduction.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The knee gives way with certain movement patterns, particularly with deceleration and pivoting. The patient demonstrates apprehension with weight-bearing and lateral knee movement. Activities requiring directional changes trigger symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute patellar dislocation occurred during athletic activity with a pivot-and-plant mechanism. Underlying anatomical predisposition such as increased Q-angle or ligamentous laxity may be present. This is the initial episode of dislocation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive apprehension test indicates fear of redislocation. Medial restraint testing reveals ligamentous insufficiency. Quadriceps strength is diminished. Swelling and joint effusion are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate a patella that is reduced in anatomical position with possible osteochondral findings. MRI shows acute ligamentous disruption of the medial patellofemoral ligament. Imaging confirms acute patellar dislocation.

puzzles.explanation: Acute patellar dislocation is traumatic and usually reduces spontaneously but leaves significant effusion and apprehension.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman living independently presents to the emergency department following a fall from standing height at home, reporting severe right hip and groin pain with inability to weight-bear.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She was unable to stand or take any steps after the fall and required an ambulance. Prior to the fall she was ambulatory with a walking stick indoors, and her mobility had been declining over the preceding months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She has a history of osteoporosis and takes calcium and vitamin D supplementation. She has experienced two previous falls without fracture in the last year. Of note, she is on warfarin for atrial fibrillation, which will require management prior to surgical intervention.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The right lower limb is shortened and externally rotated at rest. There is marked tenderness over the greater trochanteric and proximal thigh region. Any attempt at active hip movement produces severe pain, and a positive log-roll test is noted with axial loading.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic AP X-ray demonstrates a fracture line below the femoral neck in the region between the greater and lesser trochanters. The fracture is classified as an unstable 4-part intertrochanteric pattern, and orthopaedic referral for dynamic hip screw fixation is arranged.

puzzles.explanation: Intertrochanteric fracture complications from severe osteoporosis may lead to loss of fixation or delayed healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old man with diagnosed polymyalgia rheumatica presents for monitoring. On low-dose prednisone 10 mg daily. Symptoms well-controlled.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Mild residual morning stiffness but significantly improved from baseline. Functional improvement with shoulders and hips. Activity tolerance improved.

clues.label: History
clues.text: PMR diagnosed 6 months prior with good corticosteroid response. Maintained on stable low-dose prednisone. Regular follow-up for ESR trending. No systemic complications.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Morning stiffness mild now with prednisone. Shoulder and hip ROM largely restored. Strength improved. Minor discomfort with full active ROM.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR decreased to 28 mm/hr on current therapy. Inflammatory markers improved. No evidence of giant cell arteritis. Continued monitoring planned.

puzzles.explanation: Polymyalgia Rheumatica is complicated by Giant Cell Arteritis (temporal arteritis) with vascular manifestations including headache, jaw claudication, and visual symptoms requiring urgent intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational runner with chronic posterior ankle pain reports worsening with running. Pain posterior ankle with plantarflexion loading.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with running and running-specific activities. Difficulty with downhill running. Functional limitation with training. Persistent symptoms over 3 months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progressive symptoms with running. No specific traumatic event. Previous ankle sprain history. Tried relative rest with minimal improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Posterior ankle tenderness with palpation. Pain with passive plantarflexion and inversion positioning. Flexor hallucis longus palpable and tender. Pain reproduced with plantarflexion resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates posterior ankle impingement with flexor hallucis longus involvement. Soft tissue inflammation in posterior compartment. No bony pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Symptomatic os trigonum syndrome causes chronic posterior ankle pain unresponsive to conservative treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old woman, 3 years post-breast cancer surgery with axillary node dissection, presents with progressive arm swelling. Gradual onset of edema in arm and hand.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant arm volume increase limiting activities. Difficulty with certain clothing fit. Heaviness and reduced range of motion. Functional limitation with upper extremity activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Right breast cancer with axillary dissection 3 years prior. No recurrent cancer. Gradual progressive swelling over 2-year period.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right arm swelling with pitting edema initially, now non-pitting and indurated. Circumference difference 4 cm at midarm. Skin thickened and fibrosed. Reduced shoulder ROM secondary to edema.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates impaired lymphatic drainage in right upper extremity. Ultrasound shows increased soft tissue thickness in affected arm. Diameter discrepancy documented.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary lymphedema post-cancer surgery, most common cause in developed countries. Arm circumference difference and fibrotic changes confirm diagnosis. Management focuses on compression and exercise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old competitive gymnast presents with distal radius pain at growth plate region. Pain with weight-bearing on hands and wrist extension.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain limits training and competition participation. Difficulty with apparatus work requiring hand weight-bearing. Functional limitation in sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Repetitive loading on wrists through gymnastics training. High-impact landings and weight-bearing through hands. Progressive symptoms over 4 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness over distal radial growth plate on palpation. Pain with wrist extension loading. Swelling over distal radius minimal. Functional wrist ROM preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Wrist X-rays show widened distal radial growth plate with possible irregularity. MRI shows inflammatory changes at growth plate. Physis intact without displacement.

puzzles.explanation: Growth plate fractures require careful management to prevent premature closure and subsequent limb length or alignment complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old woman in nursing home after hip fracture surgery presents with sacral erythema. Non-blanchable redness noted on routine skin check. Early pressure injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Limited mobility post-surgery restricting repositioning ability. Risk of progressing to deeper tissue involvement without intervention. High-risk patient for complications.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Post-operative hip fracture recovery with restricted mobility during healing phase. Nursing home resident with care assistance for turning. 1 week post-op.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Erythema visible over sacral area. Non-blanchable on blanching test. Skin intact without breakdown. Edema present under erythematous area. Minimal pain response.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Stage 1 pressure injury confirmed. Risk factors multiple (immobility, age, post-operative state). Aggressive prevention protocol initiated.

puzzles.explanation: Early pressure injury recognition allows intervention with repositioning, pressure relief, and skin care to prevent wound progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete presents with acute knee instability and pain following a twisting injury. The patient reports knee giving way during activity. The patella was visibly displaced laterally before spontaneous reduction.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The knee gives way with certain movement patterns, particularly with deceleration and pivoting. The patient demonstrates apprehension with weight-bearing and lateral knee movement. Activities requiring directional changes trigger symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute patellar dislocation occurred during athletic activity with a pivot-and-plant mechanism. Underlying anatomical predisposition such as increased Q-angle or ligamentous laxity may be present. This is the initial episode of dislocation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive apprehension test indicates fear of redislocation. Medial restraint testing reveals ligamentous insufficiency. Quadriceps strength is diminished. Swelling and joint effusion are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate a patella that is reduced in anatomical position with possible osteochondral findings. MRI shows acute ligamentous disruption of the medial patellofemoral ligament. Imaging confirms acute patellar dislocation.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic recurrent patellar dislocation often involves underlying anatomical factors and systemic hypermobility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old female ballet dancer presents with an audible and palpable snapping sensation around the hip that has been present for over a year and has recently become painful.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The snapping consistently occurs when moving the hip from flexion into extension or during lateral movements in her dance routine. It was initially painless and a curiosity, but now causes a sharp brief pain that is limiting her rehearsals.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She has no history of hip trauma or prior hip pathology. The onset coincided with a significant increase in her dance training hours twelve months ago. She notes the sound is audible to others in the room during warm-up exercises.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dynamic assessment reveals a reproducible snap palpable over the anterior hip with passive hip flexion and extension. Ober's test is positive indicating iliotibial band tightness. There is mild tenderness over the greater trochanteric region. Hip range of motion is full but the snap is reproduced with resisted hip flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the hip are normal with no bony abnormality. Dynamic ultrasound imaging confirms snapping of the iliotibial band over the greater trochanter during the provocative movement, with no underlying bursitis or labral pathology identified.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior snapping from ligamentum teres is less common but may coexist with hip instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with chronic dry eyes and dry mouth for 8 months. Ocular symptoms significant with constant dry eye sensation. Xerophthalmia noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with reading and computer work due to eye dryness. Reduced tear production affecting vision quality. Functional limitation from visual symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious progressive onset over several months. Associated fatigue and joint aching. No prior autoimmune history. Symptoms progressive and worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced tear production on Schirmer test (<5 mm in 5 minutes bilaterally). Xerostomia evident on examination. No focal parotid swelling. Ocular surface drying evident on slit lamp.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La antibodies positive confirming Sjögren syndrome. Labial salivary gland biopsy shows lymphocytic infiltration. Rheumatoid factor positive.

puzzles.explanation: Sjögren's syndrome requires multidisciplinary approach including symptom management, salivary and lacrimal gland support, management of systemic manifestations, monitoring for complications like lymphoma, and fatigue management strategies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old painter with occupational repetitive wrist extension reports lateral elbow pain. Pain with painting brush work and gripping activities over 6 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with work activities involving wrist extension. Functional limitation affecting work capacity and income. Difficulty with repetitive gripping and lifting tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occupation requires repetitive gripping and wrist extension motions. Progressive symptom onset over 2 months. No acute injury. Continued work aggravating symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lateral elbow tenderness at epicondyle. Painful resisted wrist extension. Mill's test positive with pain reproduction. Grip strength reduced bilaterally more on affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound common extensor origin shows tendinopathic changes with heterogeneity. Mild surrounding edema. No complete rupture. Lateral collateral ligament intact on assessment.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral epicondylitis in an occupational context (not tennis-related), highlighting that repetitive gripping and wrist extension in manual workers is the most common cause.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old construction worker sustains blow to arm during work accident causing elbow injury. Acute severe pain and deformity. Swelling developing rapidly. Emergency care indicated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional loss of arm. Unable to bear weight or move elbow. Pain severe limiting all activities. Significant swelling and deformity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct trauma to elbow from work accident. High-force impact. Acute severe injury with immediate onset of symptoms. Emergency department presentation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elbow held in deformed position. Significant swelling and ecchymosis developing. Tenderness severe on palpation. Crepitus palpable. Neurovascular compromise possible.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show elbow dislocation with possible associated fracture. CT performed for further characterization. Neurovascular assessment. Post-reduction imaging planned.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent/chronic elbow instability after initial dislocation in young athlete with functional deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 81-year-old woman with progressive weakness and functional decline from age-related muscle loss. Difficulty with mobility and self-care activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional impairment from weakness. Increased dependence on others for basic activities. Fall risk markedly elevated from muscle weakness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong relatively low physical activity. Post-menopausal with inadequate nutrition. Recent decline in function over 1-2 years. Multiple fall history from weakness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked generalized weakness bilaterally. Slow gait with small steps. Hip and knee strength severely reduced 2-3/5. Grip strength markedly diminished. Visible wasting.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Sarcopenia confirmed by imaging with low lean muscle mass and functional testing. Nutritional status poor with low protein intake. Exercise capacity markedly limited.

puzzles.explanation: Severe sarcopenia in advanced age creates profound functional loss and vulnerability to complications from minimal stress.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman with progressive groin and medial thigh pain reports numbness and weakness. Pain radiates along medial thigh. Progressive symptoms over 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with hip adduction. Pain with groin activities. Medial thigh weakness noted. Functional limitation with walking. Numbness in medial thigh distribution.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progressive onset without specific injury. Possible nerve compression from anatomic variant or mass. Pain progressive. No trauma. Associated with certain movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Medial thigh numbness in obturator nerve distribution. Hip adduction weakness present. Pain reproduced with flexion and internal rotation. Positive nerve stretch test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows possible nerve compression at obturator foramen. Obturator nerve pathway visualized with compression noted. No obvious mass. Possible anatomic variation.

puzzles.explanation: Post-surgical obturator nerve injury with selective hip adductor weakness and medial thigh sensory loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old falls on outstretched hand with acute wrist pain and swelling. Wrist held in guarded position. Immediate functional loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with wrist motion limiting hand use. Swelling and ecchymosis developing. Difficulty gripping and manipulating objects.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall landing on outstretched hand with wrist in extension. Immediate pain and swelling onset. No loss of consciousness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist swelling and ecchymosis present. Tenderness over scaphoid region and throughout wrist. Wrist motion limited by pain. Neurovascular intact distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Wrist X-rays normal without fracture identified. MRI shows scapholunate ligament sprain with edema. No scaphoid or other carpal fractures.

puzzles.explanation: Acute wrist sprain with scapholunate ligament injury from typical FOOSH mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old soccer player sustains groin strain during match play. Acute pain in medial thigh region. Unable to continue playing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant pain with hip adduction and kicking activities. Difficulty walking and weight-bearing. Acute functional limitation in sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden directional change and rapid acceleration caused acute adductor strain. Immediate pain with functional loss. First episode of groin injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Adductor palpable tenderness. Pain with hip adduction resistance. Muscle guarding present. Minimal swelling at acute presentation. Antalgic gait.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows muscle fiber disruption with surrounding inflammation. Hematoma forming. Adductor muscle involvement confirmed. No complete rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic adductor strain develops gradually from overuse or inadequate flexibility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old man with chronic dizziness for 10 months reports constant swaying sensation. Balance difficulty with movement. Anxiety about symptoms prominent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Persistent dizziness limiting activities and work productivity. Difficulty with normal head movements due to dizziness exacerbation. Anxiety and panic accompanying symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mild head injury with brief dizziness 10 months prior. Dizziness persisted after physical recovery. Progressive anxiety worsened symptoms. Multiple specialist evaluations unrevealing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild postural imbalance evident. Dizziness reproducible with head movements. Significant psychological component evident. Hyperventilation contributing to symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Vestibular testing largely normal. MRI head normal. High anxiety scores. Diagnosis PPPD based on symptom pattern and persistent nature.

puzzles.explanation: Persistent Postural-Perceptual Dizziness shows primary PPPD without documented prior vestibular event, with prominent anxiety and visual/postural triggers with completely normal objective testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 15-year-old basketball player with epiphyseal plate injury at knee from overuse training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation from growth plate injury. Pain with training activities. Difficulty with jumping and explosive movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive training program with frequent jumping and running. Rapid growth during adolescent spurt. Inadequate recovery between training sessions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness at growth plate region on knee. Pain with jumping and explosive activities. Swelling possibly present. ROM intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Growth plate widening on radiographs. Inflammatory signal on MRI at physeal region. No fracture line. Immature skeleton noted.

puzzles.explanation: Growth plate injury complications include premature closure causing limb length and angular deformities requiring corrective surgery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 29-year-old rugby player sustains injury to ankle syndesmotic region during tackle. External rotation stress to ankle with pain above ankle joint. High ankle sprain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain well above ankle joint at syndesmotic area. Difficulty with weight-bearing and pivoting activities. Significant functional limitation in sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy rugby tackle with ankle external rotation stress. Syndesmotic injury mechanism. Significant pain and functional limitation acute.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Syndesmotic tenderness above ankle joint, between tibia and fibula. Pain with external rotation stress test positive. Squeeze test positive (pain with compression of tibia/fibula).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ankle X-rays with weight-bearing show widened tibiofibular space suggesting syndesmotic disruption. Stress radiographs demonstrate increased talar rotation. CT further characterizes injury.

puzzles.explanation: High ankle sprains involve the syndesmotic ligaments and carry risk of chronic pain and instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old male presents with hand pain after punching during boxing match. Fifth metacarpal head swelling and deformity. Visible angulation at fracture site.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with gripping and fist clenching. Swelling significant over metacarpal head. Functional limitation with hand use.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct impact to fifth metacarpal head during punch. High-force mechanism from boxing strike.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fifth metacarpal head swelling, ecchymosis, and angulation. Pain with palpation over fracture site. Prominence of knuckle reduced (depressed fracture appearance).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays show fifth metacarpal neck fracture with apex dorsal angulation (classic 'boxer's fracture'). Displacement and angulation measured on X-rays.

puzzles.explanation: Boxer's fracture shows fifth metacarpal neck fracture from punching mechanism with typical angulation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old tennis player presents with elbow pain at triceps insertion. Pain with elbow extension against resistance. Posterior elbow pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with overhead tennis serving and pushing activities. Pain limits training intensity. Functional limitation in sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Increased tennis training volume focusing on serving technique. Repetitive overhead serving causing triceps overload. Progressive symptoms over 4 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Posterior elbow tenderness at triceps insertion. Pain with resisted elbow extension. Triceps strength slightly reduced secondary to pain. Palpable tendon thickening possible.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows triceps tendon thickening at insertion with heterogeneous echotexture. Possible small tear or chronic inflammation. Olecrannon bursa may show fluid.

puzzles.explanation: Triceps tendinopathy from repetitive extension and throwing activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old woman, 6 weeks post-lumbar spinal fusion (L4-L5) for degenerative disc disease, begins rehabilitation. Surgical incision healing appropriately. Pain controlled.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Early post-operative phase with protected ROM and activity restrictions. Pain present but manageable. Functional limitations from post-operative restrictions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lumbar spinal fusion L4-L5 performed 6 weeks prior for degenerative disc disease and spinal instability. Post-operative protocol followed. Fusion hardware in place.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar extension limited to 10 degrees (post-operative restriction). Flexion limited to 20 degrees. Minimal muscle guarding. Incision well-healed. Pain 2-3/10.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays confirm appropriate fusion hardware positioning. Lateral films show preservation of disc space height above and below fusion level. CT fusion status assessed.

puzzles.explanation: Post-lumbar fusion rehabilitation requires long-term graduated progression, emphasis on core stabilization without spinal motion, modification of activity patterns, and realistic expectations for return to function given the extensive surgical trauma and fusion site healing requirements.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old assembly line worker presents with onset of dry cough and shortness of breath that worsens during work shifts. Symptoms began 2 months ago and have progressively increased in frequency. History of allergic rhinitis but no prior asthma diagnosis.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cough and dyspnea are exacerbated by exposure to chemical fumes and dust in the manufacturing environment. Symptoms improve during weekends and vacation periods. Patient reports wheezing and chest tightness that improves with distance from work exposure.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive exposure to isocyanates and epoxy resins over 3 years without adequate respiratory protection. Initial sensitization period of 2 weeks to 5 years typical. Recent change in ventilation system at workplace coincided with symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Spirometry shows obstructive pattern with FEV1/FVC ratio 65% and reversibility with bronchodilators. Peak flow variability exceeds 20% during work weeks versus non-work days. Bronchial hyperresponsiveness confirmed on methacholine challenge test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest normal excluding other pathology. Specific IgE testing positive to isocyanate conjugates. Workplace exposure assessment confirms elevated airborne levels of offending agent.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational asthma shows work-triggered bronchoconstriction from occupational sensitization or irritant exposure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset following viral prodrome typical for vestibular neuritis. No prior episodes of vertigo. Hearing preserved, ruling out audiovestibular involvement. No recent head trauma or otologic surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI inner ear structures normal excluding neuroma or structural lesion. No enhancement of CN VII or VIII. Audiometry normal. Electrography confirms vestibular asymmetry consistent with peripheral vestibulitis.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular Neuritis shows subacute recovery phase with persistent unilateral vestibular deficits, oscillopsia, and ongoing rehabilitation needs.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old athlete reports acute groin pain with resisted adduction during sport. Medial thigh pain with swelling. Acute functional limitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with kicking and adduction activities. Pain with walking and running. Functional limitation in sport and daily activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset with sudden rapid acceleration movement during sport. Direct muscle strain of adductor longus. Immediate pain and functional loss.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Adductor tenderness at insertion near pubis. Pain with resisted adduction. Possible small myofascial defect palpable. Adductor weakness secondary to pain (4-4+/5).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows partial muscle fiber disruption of adductor longus with surrounding edema and fluid. No complete muscle rupture. Strain injury with inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Acute adductor strains occur with explosive side-to-side or rotational movements, particularly in sports.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old motorcyclist presents 3 weeks post-traumatic shoulder dislocation. Reports pain in upper arm and progressive weakness. Mechanism involved traction injury during shoulder depression during fall.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weakness predominantly affects shoulder abduction, external rotation, and elbow flexion. Patient unable to lift arm against gravity. No sensory loss in hand noted but altered sensation in lateral shoulder and thumb/index finger region.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Traumatic mechanism with shoulder depression and neck lateral flexion away from injured side consistent with upper trunk injury pattern (Erb's palsy pattern). Post-injury edema and hemorrhage in plexus region. No prior neurological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows Erb's palsy pattern: waiter's tip position of arm with internal rotation, adduction, and elbow extension. Supraspinatus/infraspinatus atrophy visible. Upper limb reflexes (biceps, brachioradialis) diminished or absent on affected side. Horner syndrome absent (excluding lower trunk).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG/NCS shows acute denervation potentials in biceps, supraspinatus, and deltoid muscles with slowed conduction velocities. Motor amplitudes reduced in axillary and suprascapular nerves. MRI demonstrates brachial plexus edema and hemorrhage at level of upper trunk with partial continuity.

puzzles.explanation: Severe brachial plexus injury from high-energy trauma with persistent deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old office worker presents with progressive hand clumsiness and unsteady gait over 6 months. Reports difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning. Wife noted increased stumbling and careful walking pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dexterity impaired more than strength in hands. Gait described as wide-based and cautious with reduced arm swing. Patient reports difficulty with stairs and uneven surfaces. Symptom progression noted with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of cervical spine stiffness. Previous minor car accident 5 years ago. Gradual symptom progression over months suggesting chronic cord compression. No acute trauma. Smoking and age as risk factors.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lhermitte sign positive on forward neck flexion. Bilateral hyperreflexia with Hoffman's reflex present and pathologic. Lower extremity hyperreflexia with spasticity. Gait shows bilateral stiffness pattern. Weakness mild to moderate in hand intrinsics with loss of fine coordination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine shows multilevel cervical spondylosis with cord compression at C5-6 and C6-7 levels. T2-weighted imaging demonstrates myelopathic changes with central cord signal hyperintensity. Sagittal diameter reduced to 10mm at most compressed level. No acute fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical myelopathy is chronic and insidious with subtle early signs that progress to significant dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old woman presents with progressive ascending paralysis starting in lower extremities. History of gastroenteritis 1 week prior. Worsening weakness over 4 days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive paralysis affecting mobility. Facial muscle involvement developing. Risk of respiratory compromise. Dysphagia noted with disease progression.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent gastroenteritis with diarrhea and abdominal symptoms. Complete recovery seemed normal then weakness began. Rapid bilateral ascending pattern. No prior neurological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral lower extremity weakness symmetric. Absent reflexes throughout. Ascending pattern with hip and knee involvement. Upper extremity strength testing begins to show weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CSF shows elevated protein with minimal cells (albuminocytologic dissociation). EMG shows demyelination pattern. Nerve conduction studies show conduction blocks. Respiratory function monitored.

puzzles.explanation: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with classic ascending paralysis, areflexia, and respiratory compromise in post-infectious setting.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 65-year-old man of Scandinavian descent presents with progressive loss of hand extension. Palmar nodule first noticed 2 years ago in ring finger region. Increasing difficulty with flat hand positioning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive flexion contracture of ring and little fingers preventing full hand extension. Difficulty with gripping and manipulating objects. Palmar skin thickening and cord-like structures palpable. Pain absent or minimal.

clues.label: History
