clues.text: Family history of similar hand contractures in father and uncle. Northern European ancestry (Swedish descent). Age-related presentation typical for Dupuytren disease. No significant trauma or repetitive hand use occupation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination reveals firm palmar nodules along flexor surface. Palpable longitudinal cords extending from palm into ring and little finger. Ring finger MCP joint flexion contracture 45 degrees, PIP involvement minimal. Table top test fails with inability to place palm flat on surface. Skin dimpling and pit formation noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging demonstrates hypoechoic cords in palmar fascia with MCP joint involvement. No neurological or vascular compromise on imaging. Plain radiographs exclude bony involvement. Dupuytren's severity graded as moderate with contracture limiting function.

puzzles.explanation: Progressive Dupuytren's contracture in middle-aged male with family history and progressive functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain for 3 weeks. Pain began during overhead serve motion. Reports occasional clicking sensation in shoulder during reaching.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp anterior shoulder pain with overhead activities, serving, and throwing motions. Pain exacerbated by resisted elbow flexion and supination. Discomfort improves with rest and ice. Patient experiences pain at night when lying on affected shoulder.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Repetitive overhead sports participation (tennis 4x/week) for past year. Gradual onset suggesting tendinopathy rather than acute tear. No prior shoulder surgery. Underlying rotator cuff dysfunction noted on screening examination.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Speed's test (supinated elbow flexion against resistance) reproduces anterior shoulder pain. Yergason test (supinated forearm with elbow flexion) also positive. Palpable tenderness over bicipital groove. Apprehension test negative. Rotator cuff strength 4+/5 suggesting dynamic instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening (5mm) with hypoechoic signal and increased vascularity on Doppler imaging consistent with tendinopathy. Subacromial space narrowed suggesting associated subacromial impingement. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration of proximal biceps tendon without full thickness tear.

puzzles.explanation: Biceps tendinopathy with underlying instability as dynamic stabilizer is compromised.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged office worker presents with hand paresthesias and numbness affecting the lateral fingers. The patient reports functional difficulty with fine motor tasks and grip strength. Nocturnal symptoms disturb sleep quality.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are exacerbated by repetitive gripping, prolonged wrist flexion, and sustained keyboard use. Paresthesias are predominantly nocturnal, affecting sleep. Shaking the hand provides transient symptomatic relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prolonged occupational exposure to repetitive wrist flexion and gripping activities has contributed to nerve compression. Recent increase in workload and keyboard hours has exacerbated symptoms. No significant trauma or acute injury preceded the insidious onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, Tinel's sign is positive over the median nerve at the wrist. Phalen's test reproduces symptoms after 60 seconds. Two-point discrimination is reduced in median-innervated fingers. Grip strength is diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyographic testing demonstrates median nerve conduction slowing across the wrist with fibrillation potentials. Nerve conduction studies confirm focal demyelination at the carpal tunnel. The diagnosis is established by neurophysiological testing.

puzzles.explanation: Work-related carpal tunnel syndrome with clear occupational trigger and ergonomic factor.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old man with known metastatic lung cancer presents with progressive dyspnea and chest discomfort over 2 weeks. Exercise tolerance markedly decreased. History of 40 pack-year smoking and COPD.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Shortness of breath worse with exertion and when lying flat on left side. Dull chest pain that increases with deep breathing. Patient reports mild orthopnea. No fever or productive cough noted. Symptoms progressively worsening.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known metastatic adenocarcinoma with recent chemotherapy 4 weeks ago. Risk factors include malignancy, pulmonary disease, and recent pleural manipulation. Progressive symptom development typical for malignant effusion. Oncology follow-up ongoing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dullness to percussion at left lung base with decreased tactile fremitus. Breath sounds markedly reduced or absent at bases bilaterally. Egophony positive over area of effusion. Hepatomegaly noted on abdominal examination suggesting liver involvement. Oxygen saturation 88% on room air.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows blunting of left costophrenic angle with meniscus sign consistent with moderate effusion. CT chest confirms left-sided pleural effusion measuring 4cm at widest point with associated right-sided smaller effusion. Pleural fluid analysis shows exudative properties with adenocarcinoma cells present on cytology.

puzzles.explanation: Malignant pleural effusion from primary lung cancer. Often rapid accumulation, high suspicion given cancer history, and positive cytology confirms malignancy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old woman awakens with sudden onset facial droop on right side and cannot close right eye properly. No prior neurological history. Onset occurred overnight without prodrome.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Right lower facial weakness greater than upper face. Complete eye closure impossible on right. Water drips from affected side during drinking. Difficulty with oral intake and speech distortion. Pain in right ear preceding facial weakness by 24 hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset within 72 hours typical for Bell's palsy. Associated ear pain (herpes zoster oticus variant possible). No preceding trauma or surgery. Hypertension and diabetes present as risk factors. Recent upper respiratory infection not present this time.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Complete right-sided facial weakness with inability to wrinkle forehead, close eye, or smile symmetrically. Intact sensation in all trigeminal branches. Hearing intact bilaterally, no vesicles visualized in ear. Weber test midline, Rinne normal. Smile asymmetric with right facial droop.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromiography shows denervation pattern in orbicularis oris and orbicularis oculi muscles on right. Conduction velocity slightly reduced in facial nerve. MRI brain and internal auditory canal normal excluding tumor or stroke. No enhancement of CN VII on contrast study. EMG prognostication shows moderate denervation.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary facial nerve palsy from mastoiditis and otitis media with gradual onset, severe otalgia, and imaging evidence of bone involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old recreational athlete presents following acute knee injury during sports. The patient reports sudden-onset knee pain and immediate functional loss. The knee gave way and could not bear weight.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The knee demonstrates instability with a sensation of giving way during pivoting movements. Weight-bearing is severely limited acutely. Range of motion is restricted by pain and swelling. Rehabilitation is required for functional recovery.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute injury occurred during a deceleration-plant-and-cut maneuver in sports. Mechanism involved combined flexion, external tibial rotation, and valgus stress. This acute ACL injury requires surgical consultation and management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Lachman test indicates anterior tibial translation. Positive pivot shift test demonstrates rotational instability. Mild effusion is present. Pain limits examination, but clinical signs are consistent with ACL rupture.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates disruption of the anterior cruciate ligament with discontinuity of fibers. Bone bruising is visualized at the expected locations. Associated meniscal or collateral ligament injuries may be present. Imaging confirms complete ACL tear.

puzzles.explanation: ACL Grade 2 presents as chronic post-injury instability with associated muscle weakness and secondary degenerative changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old postmenopausal woman with 15-year history of estrogen deficiency presents for bone health screening. Reports progressive height loss of 2 inches and chronic thoracic spine pain. Recent fall with wrist fracture prompted evaluation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Wrist pain from recent fracture with minimal trauma. Thoracic spine pain described as dull and constant, worse with prolonged sitting and forward bending. Increasing kyphosis limiting functional activities. Fracture from ground-level fall concerning for fragility.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Menopause at age 57 without hormone replacement. Long-standing low calcium and vitamin D intake. Sedentary lifestyle with minimal weight-bearing exercise. Family history of osteoporosis in mother. Limited sunlight exposure and dietary restrictions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Height measurement reveals 2-inch loss compared to previous records. Thoracic kyphosis pronounced on physical examination. Wrist flexor strength and grip strength both reduced (3/5). Spinal palpation reveals tenderness at T7-8 level. TUEP positive (increased time up and go).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan shows T-score of -2.8 at lumbar spine and -2.5 at femoral neck (osteoporotic range). Vertebral fracture assessment shows compression fracture at T7 (15% height loss). Biochemical markers show elevated P1NP and CTX-1 indicating high bone turnover.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporosis presents with symptomatic multiple fractures and progressive deformity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old man sustains buttock laceration from glass during fall. Acute severe pain noted with immediate weakness in lower leg. Wound bleeding controlled but patient unable to dorsiflex foot.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Inability to lift foot or flex ankle (foot drop). Weakness extends to hip flexion and knee extension. Severe pain in buttock radiating down posterior and lateral leg. Sensory loss in lateral lower leg and dorsum of foot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Traumatic injury with direct laceration to gluteal region where sciatic nerve courses superficially. Acute mechanism with immediate neurological deficit. Injury at level of sciatic nerve in proximal thigh region. No prior neurological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Foot drop evident with characteristic high-stepping gait. Unable to dorsiflex ankle against resistance (0/5). Hip flexion weakness 1/5, knee extension weakness 2/5. Sensation decreased in common peroneal and tibial nerve distributions. Tinel sign positive at gluteal wound site. Patellar and Achilles reflexes diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI pelvis and thigh shows disruption of sciatic nerve at mid-gluteal level with hemorrhage surrounding nerve. Partial transection visualized with retracted nerve ends separated by 2cm. Muscle denervation changes evident in tibialis anterior and gastrocnemius. EMG confirms acute denervation potentials in relevant myotomes.

puzzles.explanation: Iatrogenic sciatic nerve injury from hip injection with acute onset, motor loss in sciatic distribution, and imaging evidence of nerve trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man returns 6 weeks post-fasciectomy for ring finger Dupuytren contracture. Reports excellent initial improvement with good range of motion but now developing tightness and restricted movement returning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Ring finger MCP extension improving but PIP extension becoming limited again (contracture tightening). Palmar scar healing well without infection. Some residual swelling in palm. Hand increasingly difficult to extend flat.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Surgical fasciectomy performed 6 weeks ago for ring finger contracture of 50 degrees. Pre-operative plan included full fascial excision. Recent increased activity with physical therapy and return to work. Post-operative protocol followed initially.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Range of motion measurement shows MCP extension improved to 10 degrees flexion contracture (from pre-op 45 degrees) but PIP extension limitation developing. Scar tissue palpable along surgical site with firmness increasing. Minimal swelling and erythema. No signs of infection or wound complications.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound at 6 weeks post-op shows intact fasciectomy with resected tissue planes visualized. No fluid collection or hematoma. However, early recurrent disease process evident with hypoechoic nodules reforming at operative margins and PIP joint region. Scar tissue shows intermediate echogenicity consistent with early proliferative phase.

puzzles.explanation: Post-fasciotomy rehabilitation requires intensive range of motion work, night-time splinting to prevent recurrence, scar management, edema control, and progressive strengthening while protecting surgical sites and skin grafts.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old office worker presents with acute onset severe shoulder pain yesterday during routine desk work. Sharp anterior shoulder pain unrelated to specific trauma. Pain awakens patient at night with inability to rest arm comfortably.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with all shoulder movements, particularly abduction and internal rotation. Pain exacerbated by lying on affected shoulder or reaching across body. Limited active range of motion due to pain (guarding). Discomfort described as sharp and well-localized anteriorly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset without preceding injury or repetitive activities. Office worker without strenuous overhead activities. Pain intensity disproportionate to functional demands suggesting inflammatory process. Sudden presentation typical for calcific tendinitis acute phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe tenderness over supraspinatus tendon insertion. Passive range of motion better preserved than active motion. Supraspinatus strength testing limited by pain rather than true weakness. Dugas test negative (shoulder can still adduct). Hawkins test markedly positive reproducing sharp pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-ray (AP and scapular Y views) demonstrates dense calcium deposit within supraspinatus tendon measuring 8x6mm. Calcium appears radiodense with well-defined borders. Associated soft tissue swelling visible around deposit. MRI shows T2 hypointense deposit with surrounding fluid signal indicating active inflammation and resorption phase.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic calcific tendinopathy with asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic calcium deposits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man with 12-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with progressive numbness and tingling in feet for past 2 years. Recently developed burning pain in lower legs, affecting sleep and daily function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Bilateral symmetric symptoms in feet and lower legs with progressive ascent pattern. Numbness makes patient trip over uneven surfaces. Burning pain worse at night and with cold exposure. Difficulty feeling temperature with feet (reports difficulty perceiving hot water in shower).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Poor glycemic control with HbA1c 8.5% throughout disease course. No prior neuropathy screening or intervention. Sedentary lifestyle and obesity (BMI 32). Recent addition of statin medication 6 months ago. Multiple vascular risk factors including hypertension and smoking history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Diminished vibratory sensation at great toe (tuning fork 128Hz lost after 3 seconds versus normal 10+ seconds). Monofilament testing shows loss of protective sensation in both feet using 10g monofilament. Ankle reflexes absent bilaterally. Mild weakness in ankle dorsiflexion 4/5. Proprioception decreased in lower extremities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction study shows reduced amplitudes in sural sensory response bilaterally with prolonged distal latencies. Motor conduction of peroneal nerve shows reduced velocities (38 m/s). EMG demonstrates chronic denervation changes in foot intrinsic muscles. MRI excludes other spinal causes. Fasting glucose 185 mg/dL.

puzzles.explanation: Painful diabetic neuropathy with prominent neuropathic pain symptoms (burning, allodynia) and confirmed small-fiber involvement on biopsy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 5-month postpartum woman reports persistent midline abdominal wall separation and weakness. Functional limitation with daily activities. Concerned about long-term cosmetic outcome.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with returning to exercise. Pain with coughing and Valsalva. Core stability deficits noted during functional activities and posture maintenance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Twin pregnancy with significant weight gain. Vaginal delivery 5 months prior. Currently breastfeeding. No formal postpartum core rehabilitation undertaken. High parity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible abdominal bulging with contraction. Palpable separation 3.5 fingers wide at umbilicus. Reduced abdominal wall tension. Rectus contraction shows lateral bulging. No signs of hernia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates diastasis rectus measuring 4.2 cm interrectal distance with thin linea alba. No fascial dehiscence. Cross-sectional imaging confirms bilateral muscle separation.

puzzles.explanation: Diastasis Rectus Abdominis shows persistent separation in multiparous woman one year postpartum with associated postural dysfunction and core weakness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational runner presents with buttock pain for 8 weeks that worsens during runs over 5km. Pain localized to lower buttock region near ischial tuberosity. Recent increase in training volume and intensity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worse during running, especially hills and sprinting. Sitting on hard surfaces increases discomfort (describing difficulty with office chair use). Pain radiates to posterior thigh but does not extend below knee. Morning stiffness in buttock lasting 10-15 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 8 weeks correlating with increased running mileage (40 to 55 miles per week). No acute traumatic event. Prior history of lower back tightness and hip flexor tightness. Weak gluteal activation noted. Running on cambered surfaces recently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness over ischial tuberosity region with pain on hamstring contraction against resistance. Positive modified Puranen-Orava test (buttock pain with hip flexion and knee extension). Pain with straight leg raise 70 degrees. Slump test negative (radicular component excluded). Hip internal rotation limited compared to external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows increased T2 signal in proximal hamstring tendons at ischial attachment with tendon thickening (8mm versus normal 5mm). No full-thickness tear visualized. Associated inflammation around ischial tuberosity. Ultrasound confirms hypoechoic tendon with loss of normal fibrillar pattern and increased vascularity on Doppler imaging at attachment point.

puzzles.explanation: High hamstring tendinopathy can involve the musculotendinous junction, often related to sprint training.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old man with history of myocardial infarction 3 years ago presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion. Recently unable to climb one flight of stairs without shortness of breath. Increasing fatigue limiting work and hobbies.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Orthopnea present requiring 3 pillows for sleep. Bilateral ankle swelling noted by patient's wife. Reduced exercise tolerance with dyspnea at minimal exertion (NYHA Class III). Reports nocturnal cough and weight gain of 5 pounds over 2 weeks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous anterolateral myocardial infarction treated with percutaneous coronary intervention 3 years ago. Multiple cardiac risk factors present: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and smoking history. Recent non-compliance with medications noted. No recent chest pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vital signs show blood pressure 150/95 and heart rate 102 bpm. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral crackles at bases. Elevated jugular venous pressure (JVP) at 8cm. Peripheral pitting edema bilaterally to mid-shin. Hepatomegaly palpable 4cm below costal margin. S3 gallop present on cardiac auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiography demonstrates ejection fraction 35% (HFrEF). Left ventricular end-diastolic dimension 60mm (dilated). Anterior wall motion abnormality consistent with prior MI. Mitral regurgitation moderate degree. Diastolic dysfunction also evident with restrictive filling pattern. BNP elevated at 850 pg/mL.

puzzles.explanation: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), a distinct entity from systolic HF. Normal or near-normal EF with diastolic dysfunction is key finding.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 75-year-old osteoporotic woman falls from standing height onto outstretched arm. Immediate severe shoulder pain and inability to move arm. Right shoulder swelling and deformity noted at scene.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with any attempted shoulder movement. Unable to lift arm or rotate shoulder. Pain out of proportion suggests neurovascular compromise or severe fracture. Swelling rapidly progressing around shoulder and upper arm.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 75 with known osteoporosis (prior DEXA T-score -3.2). Fall from standing height mechanism. History of previous wrist fracture. Long-standing corticosteroid use for COPD increasing fracture risk. Right arm dominant but injury on right side.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder deformity with loss of normal contour. Crepitus palpable at fracture site. Axillary nerve testing shows sensory loss to lateral shoulder and difficulty with deltoid contraction (0/5). Radial pulse intact and capillary refill normal. Sensation intact in radial and ulnar distributions but diminished axillary.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows 4-part proximal humerus fracture with displacement. Greater tuberosity fragment displaced 1.2cm. Humeral head retropulsion present. CT scan confirms comminution with axillary nerve involvement. Angiography normal excluding vascular injury but confirms soft tissue trauma with hematoma.

puzzles.explanation: Complex proximal humerus fracture from high-energy trauma requiring surgical planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old woman presents with widespread musculoskeletal pain for 5 years involving neck, shoulders, back, hips, and knees. Pain described as constant burning and aching throughout body. Multiple prior investigations normal.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain disproportionate to physical examination findings and imaging results. Allodynia present with light touch causing pain. Symptoms worsened by stress and weather changes. Sleep severely disrupted with non-restorative sleep pattern. Cognitive complaints (brain fog) accompanying pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual insidious onset 5 years ago following minor neck strain. No significant trauma or incident. Multiple negative investigations over years. History of anxiety and depression. Recent major life stressors and significant emotional distress. Pain catastrophizing evident in interview.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination remarkably benign relative to severity of pain complaints. No objective neurological deficits. Widespread tender points throughout body (11+ of 18 tender points positive on fibromyalgia examination). Allodynia demonstrated to light touch. No synovitis, no muscle atrophy. Cognitive testing shows poor concentration and memory complaints.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Laboratory studies all normal including inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, and thyroid function. MRI cervical and lumbar spine normal without disc herniation or stenosis. Sleep study shows non-restorative sleep pattern (reduced deep sleep stages). Quantitative sensory testing shows lowered pain thresholds throughout body (wind-up amplification confirmed).

puzzles.explanation: Central sensitisation presents as post-viral central sensitization with post-exertional malaise, systemic symptoms, and neuroinflammatory findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old man with type 2 diabetes undergoes left transtibial amputation for severe diabetic foot ulcer with osteomyelitis. Amputation performed 8 weeks ago. Currently in rehabilitation phase with prosthetic fitting planned.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Stump pain and discomfort with active range of motion. Reports phantom limb pain described as cramping sensation in absent foot. Early mobilization limited by pain and deconditioning. Psychological adjustment to amputation progressing with denial and frustration present.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c 9.2%) leading to peripheral neuropathy and compromised wound healing. Sepsis from foot infection requiring aggressive antibiotics. Sedentary pre-morbid lifestyle. Limited social support system. Recent housing change affecting rehabilitation compliance.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Stump edema present requiring compression wrapping. Incision healing well without significant drainage or erythema. Hip abductor and flexor strength 3+/5 bilaterally. Trunk balance fair requiring upper extremity support for stability. Transfer ability requires moderate assistance. Psychological assessment shows depression (PHQ-9 score 18).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Stump radiograph shows consolidated bone at amputation level without prominent bony protrusions. Soft tissue imaging shows mature scar formation with appropriate tissue planes. Vascular ultrasound confirms adequate blood flow to stump tissues (ankle-brachial index 0.7 in residual limb). Prosthetic prescription ready: SACH foot with microprocessor knee recommended for safety.

puzzles.explanation: This complex presentation requires interdisciplinary management including pain modulation strategies, contracture prevention and treatment, psychological support for depression and anxiety, and collaborative prosthetic rehabilitation planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old collegiate soccer player reports acute anterior knee pain during game when planting foot during pivot maneuver. Severe pain with sensation of something tearing in anterior knee. Immediate swelling and loss of function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe anterior knee pain exacerbated by any attempt at knee extension. Inability to perform straight leg raise. Swelling rapidly developed over 30 minutes. Pain with palpation of tibial tubercle region. Loss of functional activity with guarding of knee.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young athletic male with high-demand sport (soccer). Acute traumatic mechanism during cutting maneuver. No prior knee surgery or injury. Recent growth spurt (18 years old) with some residual cartilage sensitivity. Full participation in sport up to injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gross swelling of anterior knee with visible hematoma formation over tibial tubercle. Severe tenderness directly over tibial tuberosity. Unable to perform active knee extension against any resistance (0/5 strength). Patellar tendon taut and tender. Quadriceps muscle palpable but non-contractile. Knee effusion present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP, lateral, and Merchant views) shows avulsion of tibial tuberosity fragment with 1.5cm displacement. CT confirms fracture extends into articular surface of tibial plateau. MRI shows complete disruption of patellar tendon at bone insertion with severe edema. Associated bone marrow edema in proximal tibia. Soft tissue trauma with hematoma extensive.

puzzles.explanation: Postoperative tuberosity avulsion management focuses on restoring knee extension strength.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old man presents with deep buttock pain with walking for 3 months. Pain described as sharp and localized deep in buttock. Difficulty with prolonged ambulation (limited walking distance to 500 meters).

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Deep buttock pain worse with walking, particularly during hip adduction phase of gait. Pain relieved by sitting and lying down. Difficulty transitioning from sitting to standing. Pain not radiating down leg (no sciatica pattern). Groin pain sometimes accompanying buttock discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual insidious onset over 3 months. No significant trauma reported. Occupational sitting 8 hours daily. Hip tightness with limitation in internal rotation. History of groin strain 2 years prior to current symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pain with hip adduction across midline against resistance. Ischiofemoral stretch test reproduces deep buttock discomfort. Limited hip internal rotation (25 degrees versus 45 degrees opposite side). Tenderness with deep palpation in infrapiriformis region. Hamstring muscle tenderness present. Gait antalgic with reduced weight bearing phase.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI pelvis shows narrowing of ischiofemoral space measuring 18mm (normal >24mm) with adjacent quadratus femoris muscle edema. Hamstring origin tendinopathy visualized at ischial tuberosity. Gadolinium-enhanced imaging confirms inflammation in deep hip musculature. No femoroacetabular impingement or labral pathology identified.

puzzles.explanation: Ischiofemoral impingement is a rare but significant cause of deep posterior hip pain, often overlooked initially.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 7-year-old boy referred for poor school performance and motor coordination concerns. Mother reports difficulty with pencil grip, unable to ride bicycle, and poor balance. Clumsiness noted since preschool years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Struggles with fine motor tasks including writing, drawing, and buttoning clothes. Poor gross motor coordination with difficulty running smoothly and jumping. Falls frequently and appears clumsy compared to peers. Writing illegible with poor letter formation and spacing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Developmental motor milestones significantly delayed compared to siblings and peers. No significant prenatal complications or prematurity. No hearing or vision problems identified. No seizure history. Cognitive development normal by parent report and screening tests.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows poor pencil grasp and weak finger dexterity. Nine-hole peg test significantly slower than age norms (taking 2.5 minutes versus 1 minute expected). Difficulty with tandem stance (falls after 5 seconds). Hopping on single leg shows poor balance and coordination. Gait analysis reveals asymmetric arm swing and poor dynamic balance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Standardized motor assessment (Movement Assessment Battery for Children) shows scores below 5th percentile for age across gross and fine motor domains. Neurological examination normal with normal strength, reflexes, and sensation. Brain MRI normal excluding structural pathology. Audiological testing normal. Cognitive testing normal.

puzzles.explanation: DCD is a neurodevelopmental disorder of motor planning and coordination without neurological lesion, affecting academic and social activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman on long-term corticosteroid therapy for rheumatoid arthritis falls from standing height. Acute severe left hip pain with inability to bear weight. Deformity noted with external rotation of left leg.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain in hip region worse with any movement or attempted weight bearing. Inability to transfer independently from bed to chair. Guarding with hip flexion and external rotation posturing. Swelling present in upper thigh region.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prolonged corticosteroid use (15 years) for autoimmune disease causing osteoporosis. Low-energy mechanism (standing fall) concerning for pathological fracture. History of previous right hip fracture 8 years ago. Female postmenopausal age 62. History of insufficient vitamin D.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip shortened 2cm compared to contralateral side. Leg externally rotated and flexed. Severe tenderness over greater trochanteric region and proximal femur. Hip abduction strength impossible to test due to pain. Groin pain with passive internal rotation. Neurovascular examination intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows transverse subtrochanteric fracture with lateral comminution. Fracture line extends from medial aspect at junction of femoral neck to lateral cortex. Medial cortex disruption with 30-degree valgus angulation. CT confirms comminuted fracture pattern with significant soft tissue trauma. DEXA T-score -3.5 confirming osteoporosis.

puzzles.explanation: Subtrochanteric fractures are challenging to fix due to high stress and poor bone quality in osteoporotic bone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 60-year-old patient presents with chronic neck pain and progressive upper extremity symptoms. The patient reports stiffness and functional limitation. Neurological symptoms have gradually progressed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are exacerbated by prolonged sitting and neck extension. Functional activities requiring sustained neck positioning are limited. Pain radiates into the upper extremities. Neurological symptoms progressively worsen.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing cervical degenerative disc disease has progressed over decades. Chronic neck strain and age-related joint degeneration have contributed. Spinal cord compression may be developing gradually.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, neck range of motion is limited in multiple planes. Upper extremity strength testing reveals deficits consistent with myelopathy. Pathological reflexes (hyperreflexia, Babinski sign) may be elicited.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates multiple level disc degeneration with osteophytes and spinal cord compression. The spinal canal is narrowed. Cord signal changes may indicate myelopathic compromise. Imaging confirms cervical spondylosis with myelopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical spondylosis has progressed to myelopathy with serious neurological involvement requiring urgent intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset following viral prodrome typical for vestibular neuritis. No prior episodes of vertigo. Hearing preserved, ruling out audiovestibular involvement. No recent head trauma or otologic surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI inner ear structures normal excluding neuroma or structural lesion. No enhancement of CN VII or VIII. Audiometry normal. Electrography confirms vestibular asymmetry consistent with peripheral vestibulitis.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular Neuritis shows chronic incomplete compensation with persistent vestibular hypofunction and functional limitation requiring long-term rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old man catches ring finger in door resulting in hyperflexion injury. Immediate pain over distal phalanx with inability to extend distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint. Minimal swelling and no gross deformity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to actively extend distal phalanx at DIP joint (fixed flexion position). Pain with passive extension. No sensory loss in finger. Mild swelling over DIP joint. Distal phalanx held in flexed position at rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism with direct hyperflexion injury to finger. Injury occurred 1 day ago. No prior finger injuries or arthritis history. Full-time office worker without heavy labor demands. No underlying connective tissue disorder.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows DIP joint in 30-degree flexion with inability to actively extend. Passive extension possible with resistance and pain. Isolated DIP flexor tendon (flexor digitorum superficialis) intact. No lacerations to extensor surface. Sensation intact to light touch and two-point discrimination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray lateral view shows small avulsion fracture at base of distal phalanx involving dorsal lip (approximately 15% of articular surface). Minimal displacement with fragment size 4mm. No intra-articular air or soft tissue emphysema. Dorsal lip avulsion consistent with mallet finger pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Acute mallet finger from sports injury with characteristic DIP flexion deformity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old man develops painful swelling in knees and ankles 2 weeks after acute diarrheal illness. Systemic symptoms include fever (101°F), fatigue, and malaise. Morning stiffness lasting 2 hours in lower extremity joints.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Acute polyarthritis affecting knees, ankles, and wrists symmetrically. Joint pain worse in morning and with activity, improving somewhat with rest. Marked swelling and warmth in affected joints. Associated urethritis with dysuria and penile discharge. Conjunctivitis with eye redness and discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute diarrhea 2 weeks prior to joint symptoms (self-limited 3-day illness with bloody diarrhea). Recent unprotected sexual contact 4 weeks ago with new partner. Family history negative for autoimmune disease. No prior joint problems. Previously healthy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Polyarthritis of knees, ankles with warmth and effusion. Urethritis evident with urethral discharge noted. Conjunctivitis bilateral with mild mucoid discharge. Keratoderma blennorrhagicum lesions on palms and soles (psoriasiform papules). Oral ulceration observed on palate. No cardiac murmurs or lung findings. Arthritis asymmetric.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR elevated at 68 mm/hr, CRP 12 mg/dL (elevated). Rheumatoid factor negative. Anti-CCP negative. HLA-B27 positive. Stool culture positive for Shigella flexneri. Serology positive for chlamydial antibodies (microimmunofluorescence). Synovial fluid shows inflammatory non-septic pattern (WBC 8,000, PMN-predominant).

puzzles.explanation: Reactive Arthritis (Reiter's Syndrome) shows the classic triad of arthritis, urethritis/conjunctivitis, and mucocutaneous lesions following enteric or STI infection in a young HLA-B27 positive male.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man with Type 2 diabetes develops triggering in index and middle fingers bilaterally. Sudden onset locking with difficulty extending fingers in morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional impact on gripping and fine motor tasks. Finger locking causing pain with forced extension. Nocturnal symptoms less prominent than daytime.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diabetes diagnosed 8 years ago, poorly controlled (HbA1c 8.5%). No prior hand pathology. Symptoms began 1 month ago with rapid bilateral involvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nodular thickening palpable at A1 pulley level bilaterally. Triggering phenomenon reproducible with grasping. Pain with triggering event. Reduced grip strength secondary to pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound bilateral index and middle fingers shows flexor tendon nodular thickening and pulley hypertrophy. Inflammatory changes around tendon sheath. No tendon tearing.

puzzles.explanation: Pediatric congenital trigger thumb, painless with contracture presentation distinct from adult acquired form.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old rugby player's ring finger caught in opposing player's jersey during tackle. Sharp pain and immediate loss of finger flexion. Swelling over proximal forearm developed within minutes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to flex distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint of ring finger (flexor digitorum profundus avulsion). Mild proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint flexion intact (flexor digitorum superficialis functioning). Pain with attempted flexion. Swelling in volar forearm region.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism with finger caught in jersey with sudden traction injury. Injury occurred 2 hours ago. Sport involves high-contact mechanism. Young athletic male with full mobility demands. No prior finger trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ring finger held in extension at DIP joint with inability to actively flex (0/5 DIP flexion). PIP flexion intact at 4/5 strength. Mild swelling noted in volar forearm proximal to wrist. Tender palm with fullness along flexor digitorum profundus tendon region. No sensory loss or vascular compromise. No wound or skin disruption.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows no bone avulsion at distal phalanx base (distinguishes from bony mallet). MRI demonstrates complete rupture of flexor digitorum profundus tendon at level of wrist crease. Tendon retracted proximally with gap of 3cm. Associated edema in forearm soft tissues. Ultrasound confirms tendon discontinuity with fluid collection (hematoma) tracking proximally in forearm.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic jersey finger presenting late, beyond optimal surgical repair window.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old patient presents with neck pain radiating into the upper extremity. The patient reports pain, numbness, and weakness following a position-change injury. Functional limitation affects work capacity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms radiate from the neck into the arm following a dermatome pattern. Neck flexion and rotation toward the side of pain exacerbate symptoms. Supportive positioning and traction provide partial relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute cervical strain with nerve root compression occurred following a position change or lifting activity. Underlying cervical spondylosis or degenerative disc disease predisposes to nerve compression. Progressive symptoms indicate ongoing nerve irritation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, neck range of motion is limited in flexion and rotation. Dermatome-specific sensory changes are present. Motor weakness corresponds to the affected nerve root. Reflexes may be diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates intervertebral disc herniation or spondylotic changes compressing the nerve root at the appropriate spinal level. The compression level corresponds to clinical findings. Imaging confirms mechanical nerve root compression.

puzzles.explanation: C6 radiculopathy with classic dermatomal referral pattern, positive Spurling's test, shoulder abduction relief sign, and corresponding imaging findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old woman underwent anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) at C5-6 level 6 weeks ago for cervical myelopathy. Reports persistent neck stiffness and mild residual arm numbness. Pain well-controlled on current medications.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Neck mobility limited compared to pre-operative baseline but improving. Early stage post-operative dysphonia present (voice hoarseness) from surgical approach. Right arm paresthesias reduced by 50% from pre-operative level. Functional capacity increasing with compliance to therapy.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pre-operative cervical myelopathy with progressive hand dysfunction and gait disturbance. Surgical intervention for cord compression at C5-6 from degenerative disc disease. Anterior approach chosen for decompression and fusion. Patient motivated for rehabilitation with realistic expectations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Neck active range of motion limited: flexion 30 degrees (normal 45), extension 20 degrees (normal 45), lateral flexion 20 degrees bilaterally (normal 40). Incision healed without complications. Upper extremity strength 4+/5 (improved from 3+/5 pre-operatively). Dysphonia present but voice improving. Gait improved with better balance and coordination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-ray shows adequate positioning of ACDF allograft with normal alignment. Anterior plate and screw fixation appropriately positioned. Early fusion incorporation visible on lateral radiograph. MRI shows decompression of cord at C5-6 with resolution of cord signal abnormality. No recurrent disc herniation or new compression.

puzzles.explanation: Post-cervical fusion rehabilitation requires careful progression given myelopathy recovery potential, protection of fusion sites while gradually restoring neck stability, upper extremity strengthening, and close monitoring for incomplete cord recovery signs.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with lateral hip pain for 4 weeks exacerbated by walking and lying on affected side. Pain localized to outer hip region. Affects sleep and daily walking tolerance limited to 1 block.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp lateral hip pain worse with walking, especially on stairs and uneven surfaces. Pain severe when lying on affected side at night. Difficulty crossing legs and internal rotation of hip increases discomfort. Pain improves with rest and ice.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset without clear traumatic event. Recent increase in walking activity (new exercise program 6 weeks ago). History of lower back pain. Sedentary occupation with prolonged sitting. Mild hip abduction weakness bilaterally.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over greater trochanter. Positive Trendelenburg test showing gluteal dysfunction. Limited hip abduction strength 3+/5 on affected side. Noble compression test reproduces lateral hip pain. Single-leg stance balance reduced. Lumbar spine examination normal (no radiation).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray hip shows no osteoarthritis or structural abnormality. MRI reveals gluteus medius tendinopathy with intrasubstance signal changes. Modest edema in greater trochanteric bursa. No labral pathology. Ultrasound demonstrates tendon thickening and hypoechoic degeneration with increased Doppler vascularity. Bursal thickening confirmed.

puzzles.explanation: GTPS is the most common cause of lateral hip pain, often involving bursitis and tendinopathy together.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old man reports progressive hearing difficulty for past 5 years affecting both ears symmetrically. Difficulty hearing high-pitched sounds and female voices. Wife notes television volume excessively loud.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty hearing conversations in noisy environments. Speech discrimination affected with words sounding unclear. Tinnitus present as high-pitched ringing. Vertigo not reported. Social withdrawal due to communication difficulty. Significant impact on quality of life.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 68 with expected age-related hearing changes. 45-year occupational noise exposure as machinist without ear protection. No ototoxic medications. No prior ear surgery or trauma. Family history of hearing loss in father. Smoking history 40 pack-years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Otoscopy normal with clear tympanic membranes bilaterally. Weber test shows conductive component (lateralizes to better ear). Rinne test shows air-bone gap in both ears. Speech discrimination score 68% at conversational levels. Cerumen impaction noted and removed bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Audiometry confirms bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss with air-bone gaps suggesting mixed component. Bone conduction thresholds elevated at frequencies >2000 Hz. Speech discrimination reduced. Tympanometry normal type A indicating normal middle ear function. Acoustic reflex present.

puzzles.explanation: Significant presbycusis requires hearing aids or assistive devices to maintain social engagement and quality of life in elderly.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old woman presents with recent onset blurred vision in right eye and progressive leg weakness over 4 weeks. Recent viral illness-like symptoms 2 months ago. Fatigue and cognitive fog affecting work performance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vision loss in right eye with pain on eye movement (optic neuritis). Progressive weakness in both legs affecting gait and stair climbing. Difficulty with balance and coordination. Bladder urgency and frequency present. Numbness in feet following stocking-glove distribution.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Relapsing symptom pattern with previous episodes of unexplained transient neurological symptoms. Recent upper respiratory infection. No significant prior medical history. Family history of autoimmune disease. Symptoms wax and wane suggesting multiple lesions at different times.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Eye examination shows reduced visual acuity 20/40 right eye with afferent pupillary defect. Visual field testing shows central scotoma on right. Lower extremity strength 4/5 with increased tone and hyperreflexia. Positive Babinski signs bilaterally. Gait shows spasticity and ataxia. Finger-to-nose dysmetria present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain shows multiple periventricular T2/FLAIR hyperintensities consistent with demyelination. Spinal cord MRI reveals T2 hyperintense lesion at C4-5 level. Gadolinium enhancement noted in multiple lesions indicating acute inflammation. CSF analysis shows oligoclonal bands positive. Visual evoked potentials show prolonged latency in right eye (optic nerve demyelination).

puzzles.explanation: Relapsing-remitting MS with dissemination in time (optic neuritis 2 years ago, current relapse) and space (brain and spine lesions), Uhthoff's phenomenon, and Lhermitte's sign.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old office worker presents with progressive hand clumsiness and unsteady gait over 6 months. Reports difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning. Wife noted increased stumbling and careful walking pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dexterity impaired more than strength in hands. Gait described as wide-based and cautious with reduced arm swing. Patient reports difficulty with stairs and uneven surfaces. Symptom progression noted with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of cervical spine stiffness. Previous minor car accident 5 years ago. Gradual symptom progression over months suggesting chronic cord compression. No acute trauma. Smoking and age as risk factors.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lhermitte sign positive on forward neck flexion. Bilateral hyperreflexia with Hoffman's reflex present and pathologic. Lower extremity hyperreflexia with spasticity. Gait shows bilateral stiffness pattern. Weakness mild to moderate in hand intrinsics with loss of fine coordination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine shows multilevel cervical spondylosis with cord compression at C5-6 and C6-7 levels. T2-weighted imaging demonstrates myelopathic changes with central cord signal hyperintensity. Sagittal diameter reduced to 10mm at most compressed level. No acute fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical myelopathy is the classic progressive upper motor neuron syndrome with cord imaging confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old female with anterior knee pain limiting running activities. Pain worsens with running volume.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation with running activities. Pain with stairs and squatting. Activity avoidance due to pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Increased running frequency without adequate recovery. Previous episode of knee pain 2 years prior resolved with training modification. Recurrent with current training increase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive patellar mobilization test. VMO weakness evident. Gait analysis shows knee valgus pattern. Pain reproducible with functional activities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays normal. MRI normal. Diagnosis clinical based on pain pattern and functional limitations. VMO dyskinesis noted on dynamic analysis.

puzzles.explanation: Runner's knee shows typical patellofemoral pain from biomechanical imbalance and training error.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged woman presents with intermittent hip pain following return to recreational dancing. The patient reports functional limitations with lower extremity mobility. Lifelong history of hip tightness and restricted mobility predates current symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hip adduction combined with internal rotation movements consistently provoke pain, particularly during repetitive flexion patterns. Functional limitations restrict combined movement planes. Symptom reproduction occurs predictably with provocative movement combinations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Anatomical predisposition likely involves proximal femoral or acetabular morphology. Return to activity after a period of relative inactivity has triggered acute symptoms. Long-standing hip mobility restriction contributes to impingement mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion combined with adduction and internal rotation (impingement sign) reproduces pain. Range of motion is restricted in the combined flexion-adduction-internal rotation plane. Hip flexor tightness is evident on examination. Positive impingement tests confirm mechanical origin.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Advanced imaging with MRI or CT demonstrates morphological abnormalities of the proximal femur or acetabulum. Labral pathology may be visualized. Imaging findings explain the mechanical basis of the patient's symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: FAI results from abnormal bone morphology causing impingement of the labrum during movement. Common in athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old recreational volleyball player reports anterior knee pain for 6 weeks localized to superior patellar pole. Pain worsens with jumping and repetitive ball hitting. Swelling palpable at tendon insertion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp anterior knee pain with explosive movements (jumping, spiking ball) and eccentric loading during deceleration. Pain improved with rest and modified activity but returns with sport participation. Stiffness after activity settling within hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset correlating with increased training volume (volleyball 4x per week). Repetitive jumping and landing mechanics in sport. No acute traumatic event. History of patellar tendon soreness 2 years ago. Adequate flexibility initially but recently decreased.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over proximal patellar tendon at superior patellar pole. Quadriceps contraction against resistance elicits pain. Single-leg squat shows valgus knee position (femoral internal rotation) suggesting biomechanical dysfunction. Knee flexion 0-120 degrees pain-free but 120-140 degrees aggravating. Palpable swelling at insertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic thickening of proximal quadriceps tendon (8mm versus normal 5-6mm) with loss of fibrillar echotexture. Increased Doppler signal indicating inflammation and neovascularity. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration without full-thickness tear. T2 signal abnormality within proximal third of tendon.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic quadriceps tendinopathy in older athletes reflects cumulative loading and age-related tendon changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old female overhead volleyball player develops scapular winging and shoulder dysfunction during season. Onset gradual over 4 weeks with difficulty with throwing mechanics. Associated shoulder weakness with overhead activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Medial border of scapula prominent during push-up or reaching forward (scapular winging). Pain in shoulder with overhead throwing activities. Weakness with overhead pressing motions. Difficulty with bench press and push-up exercises.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Overhead athlete with repetitive throwing and pressing movements. Gradual overuse syndrome from increased training volume without adequate rest. No acute nerve trauma noted. Long thoracic nerve injury suspected from overuse rather than acute injury. Previous shoulder soreness reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visibly prominent medial scapular border evident during push-up test (scapular winging present). Positive serratus wall push-up test with inability to maintain scapular control. Weakness with forward reaching and arm elevation. Shoulder abduction and internal rotation strength 3+/5. Thoracic kyphosis noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows active denervation in serratus anterior muscle with fibrillations and positive sharp waves. Motor conduction of long thoracic nerve demonstrates reduced amplitude. Nerve conduction velocity normal but amplitude reduced 50% compared to contralateral side. Chest X-ray normal excluding bony pathology. MRI shoulder shows mild scapular dyskinesis pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Long thoracic nerve palsy causing acute serratus anterior paralysis and scapular winging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old man sustains thoracic spine injury from motor vehicle collision with loss of consciousness. Acute spinal cord contusion at T6-7 level. Acute paralysis below level of injury with sensory loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Bilateral lower extremity paralysis (0/5 strength below T7 level). Complete loss of sensation to pinprick and light touch below T8 dermatome. Anal tone absent with loss of perianal sensation (cauda equina component at T12-L1). Urinary retention requiring catheterization. Incomplete presentation with asymmetry.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-velocity motor vehicle collision with significant thoracic trauma. Acute spinal cord injury with contusion and edema. Loss of consciousness indicating significant head trauma. Unstable fracture-dislocation pattern. Admission to ICU with critical injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Complete lower extremity paralysis with flaccid tone acutely. Deep tendon reflexes absent below level (areflexic phase). Spinal shock evident with no reflex activity. Upper extremity strength normal 5/5. Thoracic dermatome sensation loss at T8. Saddle anesthesia present. Severe trunk instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows thoracic cord contusion extending from T5-8 levels with T2 hyperintense signal indicating spinal cord edema. Hemorrhage visible as T1 hyperintense signal within cord at maximum injury. Associated fracture dislocation with retropulsion of vertebral body. Ligamentous disruption posterior elements. Canal narrowing 50% at maximum compression point.

puzzles.explanation: Incomplete thoracic spinal cord injury with lower extremity paralysis, preserved upper extremity function, and developing spasticity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old girl presents with knee pain and morning stiffness in both knees lasting 2 hours daily. Mother notes decreased activity compared to peers. Swelling in knees apparent after child wakes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Bilateral knee swelling and warmth limiting activity. Morning stiffness and limping pattern worst after sleep. Decreased running and jumping compared to siblings. Pain with range of motion exercises. Limp resolves partially after warming up and activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset of symptoms 2 months ago insidious without trauma. Negative for recent infections or illness triggers. Family history negative for autoimmune disease. Otherwise healthy child with normal development. No rash or systemic symptoms reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows bilateral knee effusions with warmth and swelling. Knee flexion contractures bilateral measuring 15 degrees. Decreased knee active range of motion. Mild pain with passive range of motion. No joint asymmetry. Gait shows antalgic pattern with shortened step. Absence of rash and hepatosplenomegaly.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR elevated at 32 mm/hr, CRP 8 mg/dL. Antinuclear antibody positive (homogeneous pattern). Rheumatoid factor negative. Anti-CCP antibody negative. Knee X-rays show no structural damage early on. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with joint effusions. Complete blood count shows mild anemia (Hb 10.2).

puzzles.explanation: Polyarticular JIA involves multiple joints with inflammation and potential for progressive damage requiring intensive immunosuppressive treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old man with acute wrist drop after sleeping with arm draped over a chair edge. Weakness in hand extension noted immediately upon waking. Mild paresthesias in dorsal hand reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Inability to extend wrist and fingers (wrist drop) requiring hand support. Inability to extend thumb interphalangeal joint. Hand clenching limited by inability to extend metacarpophalangeal joints. Sensory loss in first dorsal web space between thumb and index finger.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset from sleeping position with excessive pressure on lateral upper arm (compression neuropathy). No trauma or blow to arm. Spontaneous onset classic for sleep-related nerve compression. No prior nerve injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist held in flexion with inability to dorsiflex (0/5 wrist extension). Thumb interphalangeal joint extension weak (1/5). Finger metacarpophalangeal extension weak (2/5) for all digits. Sensory loss in first dorsal web space only (posterior interosseous nerve sparing). Triceps and elbow extension strength intact. Sensation in remaining hand normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG/NCS shows reduced motor amplitude in posterior interosseous nerve branch with slowed conduction velocity. Sensory conduction normal in superficial radial sensory branch. Denervation pattern in extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis muscles. MRI shows nerve compression at level of spiral groove with surrounding edema. No fracture or other structural lesion.

puzzles.explanation: Post-surgical radial nerve injury from hardware placement causing complete wrist drop and denervation in radial distribution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old female distance runner complains of lateral knee pain starting 3 miles into long runs. Pain localized to lateral knee near tibiofemoral joint line. Pain resolves with rest between workouts.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp lateral knee pain elicited during running, particularly with downhill running and repetitive knee flexion-extension cycles. Pain increases as running distances increase. Resolves quickly with rest. Pain not present with walking or static stretching.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 3 weeks correlating with increased running mileage (increased from 30 to 50 miles per week). Recent change to hillier running routes. History of weak hip abductors. Running on cambered surfaces. Tight hip abductors and lateral thigh noted on examination.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Ober test indicating ITB tightness and hip abduction contracture. Noble compression test reproduces lateral knee pain over ITB insertion. Iliotibial band palpable tenderness at knee level. Hip abduction strength weak (3/5). Glute med weakness with positive Trendelenburg sign. Gait shows excessive knee valgus on single leg stance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows thickened ITB with edema and signal changes at distal insertion point at knee. Inflammation at ITB-knee lateral compartment interface. No meniscal or ligamentous injury. Associated hip abductor muscle edema (gluteus medius and minimus). Ultrasound confirms ITB thickening with hypoechoic signal change and Doppler hyperemia.

puzzles.explanation: ITBFS presents with classic lateral knee pain from friction syndrome in distance runner with hip biomechanical deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old male golfer presents with groin pain worsening over past 8 weeks coinciding with increased golf practice. Pain localized to anterior hip. Difficulty with golf swing mechanics particularly on follow-through.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Groin pain worse with hip adduction activities and during golf swing. Pain with hip flexion and internal rotation movements required in golf. Painful arc during adduction against resistance. Pain improves with rest between golf rounds but returns with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 8 weeks with increased practice frequency (3 rounds per week). Swing change 2 months ago changing weight transfer mechanics. Long-term golfer without prior hip issues. Age-related tissue changes contributing. Limited hip flexibility particularly adductors.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness at hip adductor origin on inferior pubis. Pain reproduction with hip adduction against resistance. Reduced hip abduction strength secondary to pain inhibition. Limited hip internal rotation compared to external rotation. Positive adductor squeeze test (pain with squeeze between knees). Gait shows antalgic pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows adductor longus tendinopathy at proximal insertion with T2 signal hyperintensity. Pubic bone edema present at adductor origin. Associated labral fraying at anteroinferior labrum. Iliopsoas myotendinopathy present as secondary finding. Ultrasound confirms thickened adductor tendon with mixed echotexture and Doppler neovascularization.

puzzles.explanation: Golfer's hip shows posterior hip impingement from repetitive golf swing mechanics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old sedentary retired man with chronic low back pain reports persistent symptoms despite various treatments. Pain 4-5/10 baseline with periodic increases. Limited benefit from interventions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain limits functional activities. Difficulty with prolonged sitting and standing. Walking provides some relief. Previous treatments provided minimal benefit. Functional decline progressive.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset 3 years ago without specific injury. Extensive prior workup. Multiple therapeutic interventions attempted. Psychological factors may contribute. Pain behavior prominent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar segmental dysfunction palpable. Hip flexor tightness significant. Lumbar ROMMildly restricted. Core strength weak 3+/5. Neurological exam normal. Palpable muscle tension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar MRI shows degenerative changes. Disc bulging noted but no cord compression. Facet arthrosis present. Hyperostosis at multiple levels. Vascular insufficiency possible contributor.

puzzles.explanation: Non-specific low back pain is a recurrent pattern in an older adult with multiple contributing lifestyle factors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old trail runner presents with insidious onset lateral ankle and lower leg pain. Pain localized behind the lateral malleolus and along lateral foot border.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with trail running and inversion activities. Subsides with rest. Unable to continue training volume. Tightness along lateral ankle and foot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in running mileage by 30% over 3 weeks. Previous history of inversion ankle sprain 6 months ago with incomplete rehabilitation. Peroneal tendon subluxation briefly noted post-sprain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness over peroneal groove behind lateral malleolus. Pain with resisted foot eversion. Swelling along lateral ankle. Pain reproduced with single-leg stance on uneven surface.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates peroneal tendon thickening and hypoechogenicity over 1.5 cm length behind lateral malleolus. No subluxation noted. Mild fluid surrounding tendon.

puzzles.explanation: Peroneal tendinopathy results from overuse or ankle instability causing inflammation of the evertors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete presents following acute ankle inversion injury during sports. The patient reports lateral ankle pain, swelling, and functional limitation. Weight-bearing is compromised.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking and weight-bearing activities exacerbate pain. Directional changes and uneven surfaces provoke symptom exacerbation. Swelling limits ankle motion. Supportive footwear provides symptomatic relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute ankle inversion occurs during sports activity with rapid directional change. The anterior talofibular ligament is the most commonly injured structure. This is the initial ankle sprain episode.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, lateral ankle swelling and tenderness are evident. Anterior drawer test demonstrates increased translation. Inversion stress test reproduces pain. Ankle range of motion is limited by pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs are obtained to exclude ankle fracture. MRI demonstrates lateral ligament disruption with fluid signal indicating acute ligament tear. Imaging severity correlates with clinical presentation.

puzzles.explanation: Ankle inversion sprain shows typical lateral ankle ligament injury from inversion mechanism during sport.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old woman post-hysterectomy reports urine loss with coughing, exercise, and straining. Small volume leakage with physical activities. Significant social limitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Involuntary urine loss with Valsalva. Difficulty with exercise and social situations. Avoids activities due to incontinence fear. Quality of life impacted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple vaginal deliveries prior. Hysterectomy 5 years prior. Menopause 3 years ago. Progressive incontinence worsening with activity. Previous bladder symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Positive cough stress test. Bladder neck hypermobile on straining. No involuntary contractions. Positive stress incontinence sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Urodynamics shows stress incontinence with sphincter incompetence. Normal detrusor function. Bladder neck descent on cough. Urethral pressure profile weak.

puzzles.explanation: Stress Urinary Incontinence shows activity-triggered stress incontinence in an athletic middle-aged woman, related to pelvic floor weakness and increased training demands.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man presents with burning pain and numbness on lateral thigh surface for 6 months. Symptoms unilateral on right side. Burning dysesthesia constant but variably intensified. No lower extremity weakness reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Burning pain and paresthesias (pins-and-needles) affecting lateral thigh from hip to knee region. Pain worse with standing and walking, improved by sitting and lying down. Sensation described as burning and hypersensitive to touch. No pain in lower leg or foot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual insidious onset over 6 months without clear inciting event. Occupational sitting with tight waistband pressure (construction supervisor). Obesity present with BMI 31. Recent weight gain of 15 pounds over past year. No recent trauma or nerve compression episode.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sensory loss to light touch in lateral femoral cutaneous nerve distribution (lateral thigh). Dysesthesia and allodynia present in same distribution. No motor weakness noted. Lower extremity strength intact (5/5 throughout). Straight leg raise normal and painless. Palpable tenderness at inguinal ligament where nerve may be compressed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG/NCS shows reduced sensory amplitude in lateral femoral cutaneous sensory response. Motor conduction studies normal (femoral, peroneal, tibial). MRI shows no structural mass or pathology compressing nerve at groin level. Ultrasound demonstrates nerve thickening at inguinal ligament with hypoechoic changes. CT confirms mild nerve compression at inguinal canal.

puzzles.explanation: Meralgia paraesthetica from lateral femoral cutaneous nerve entrapment with pure sensory symptoms, positional aggravation, and ultrasound evidence of nerve compression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness and functional decline over 2 years. Difficulty rising from chair without armrest support. Walking slower and shorter distances than previously. Recent fall from trip on uneven pavement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Reduced grip strength (12 kg versus 22 kg in contralateral hand). Difficulty with stairs and walking long distances. Loss of independence with heavy household tasks. Slow gait velocity (0.7 m/sec versus normal 1.4 m/sec). Difficulty getting out of bed and toilet independently.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 76 with sedentary lifestyle and reduced physical activity. Inadequate protein intake (40g daily versus recommended 1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight). Loss of appetite with reduced food intake. Multiple comorbidities including osteoarthritis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease limiting activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible muscle wasting in upper extremities and thighs. Reduced grip strength 12 kg (abnormal cutoff <16 kg for women). Slow gait speed 0.7 m/sec (abnormal <1.0 m/sec). Reduced lower extremity strength particularly hip flexors and knee extensors (4/5). Difficulty rising from chair in <12 seconds (requires 18 seconds).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan confirms low fat-free mass index (5.8 kg/m2, cutoff <5.75). MRI thigh shows reduced muscle mass with increased intramuscular fat infiltration. Protein markers: prealbumin 18 mg/dL (low, <20), albumin 3.2 g/dL (borderline). Inflammatory markers: CRP 5.2 mg/L (mildly elevated). Vitamin D level 22 ng/mL (insufficient).

puzzles.explanation: Severe sarcopenia with deconditioning results in loss of independence and high risk for adverse outcomes in elderly patients.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old woman 8 months post-motor vehicle collision presents with chronic pain and movement dysfunction. Associated anxiety and sleep disturbance. Functional capacity significantly reduced with avoidance of driving.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Chronic widespread musculoskeletal pain exacerbated by reminders of accident (loud noises trigger pain). Guarded movement patterns with excessive protective muscle tension. Hypervigilance noted during physical examination. Startle response exaggerated with sudden movements or loud sounds.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe motor vehicle collision 8 months prior with loss of consciousness and hospitalization. Extensive soft tissue injuries with whiplash mechanism. Subsequent nightmares and flashbacks with avoidance of vehicles. Anxiety and depression symptoms prominent. Poor sleep quality.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Examination shows widespread muscle guarding and protective splinting. Forward head posture with upper trapezius muscle tension and myofascial trigger points. Limited cervical and thoracic range of motion (protected movement). Increased pain with specific movements resembling injury mechanism. Functional limitations in reaching and loading.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine shows no structural abnormality but demonstrates muscle edema in cervical paraspinals suggesting chronic tension. Psychiatric evaluation confirms PTSD diagnosis with trauma-related avoidance and arousal symptoms. Pain catastrophizing scale elevated (42/52). Fear-Avoidance Beliefs Questionnaire elevated indicating pain-related anxiety.

puzzles.explanation: PTSD in rehabilitation shows complex PTSD with severe symptoms significantly limiting rehabilitation despite physical recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete presents with acute knee instability and pain following a twisting injury. The patient reports knee giving way during activity. The patella was visibly displaced laterally before spontaneous reduction.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The knee gives way with certain movement patterns, particularly with deceleration and pivoting. The patient demonstrates apprehension with weight-bearing and lateral knee movement. Activities requiring directional changes trigger symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute patellar dislocation occurred during athletic activity with a pivot-and-plant mechanism. Underlying anatomical predisposition such as increased Q-angle or ligamentous laxity may be present. This is the initial episode of dislocation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive apprehension test indicates fear of redislocation. Medial restraint testing reveals ligamentous insufficiency. Quadriceps strength is diminished. Swelling and joint effusion are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate a patella that is reduced in anatomical position with possible osteochondral findings. MRI shows acute ligamentous disruption of the medial patellofemoral ligament. Imaging confirms acute patellar dislocation.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent patellar subluxation indicates structural or neuromuscular predisposition requiring stabilization.

clues.label: Complaint
