clues.text: A 68-year-old retired teacher reports dizzy spells upon standing from a seated or supine position, occurring several times daily. Experiencing presyncope without loss of consciousness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms resolve within seconds to minutes of remaining still. Patient restricts activities due to fall risk concern. Slower positional changes help reduce episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of hypertension managed with antihypertensive medications including ACE inhibitor and calcium channel blocker. Recent dose increase 2 weeks prior. No cardiac arrhythmias noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Systolic blood pressure drops 25 mmHg when rising from supine (sitting BP 142/88, standing BP 117/82). Heart rate increases appropriately from 62 to 80 bpm. Cardiac auscultation normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Supine and standing blood pressure measurements show orthostatic blood pressure change. ECG normal rate and rhythm. No vasovagal reflex identified on tilt table testing.

puzzles.explanation: Postural hypotension from medications or autonomic dysfunction causes blood pressure drops with position change, increasing fall risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 61-year-old man 6 weeks post-coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) x3 presents for cardiac rehabilitation. Sternotomy incision healing well. Reports progressive dyspnea and chest discomfort with activity. Exercise tolerance severely limited.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dyspnea with light exertion (walking 100 meters on flat surface). Chest incisional pain limiting arm and shoulder mobility. Significant deconditioning with rapid heart rate response to minimal activity (HR 110 at rest, 140 with walking). Fatigue severe affecting all functional activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Post-operative status 6 weeks s/p CABG with saphenous vein grafts. Ejection fraction improved from 35% pre-operative to 50% post-operative. Pre-operative sedentary lifestyle. Multiple cardiac risk factors: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes. Age 61.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sternotomy incision well-healed without dehiscence. Reduced upper extremity active range of motion secondary to pain and precautions (no reaching overhead). Shoulder abduction limited to 100 degrees. Chest wall splinting evident with guarded breathing. Gait slow with reduced activity tolerance. Orthostatic vital signs normal. Resting HR 85, BP 138/82.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative echocardiography shows improved ejection fraction 50% with anterior wall motion improving. ECG shows normal sinus rhythm without ischemic changes. Cardiac biomarkers (troponin, BNP) normalized. Stress testing at 6 weeks shows no ischemic changes with target heart rate achieved. Exercise tolerance limited by deconditioning rather than ischemia.

puzzles.explanation: Early post-CABG rehabilitation phase focusing on sternotomy healing and deconditioning. Pain management and graduated activity progression are primary concerns.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old hockey player sustains medial clavicular injury during check into boards. Direct impact to anterior chest wall. Acute pain at sternoclavicular joint with swelling and deformity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Anterior chest wall pain localized to sternoclavicular joint region. Pain with arm movement particularly across body adduction. Visible prominence of medial clavicle suggesting anterior dislocation. Difficulty with overhead reaching.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism with direct anterior blow during collision. High-velocity sport-related injury (hockey). Young athlete with anticipated good healing potential. No prior upper extremity injury or instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness at sternoclavicular joint with prominence of medial clavicle superiorly. Pain with cross-body adduction test. Upper extremity strength grossly intact. Negative neurovascular examination. Possible retrosternal involvement on deep palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP and 40-degree cephalic tilt views) shows anterior displacement of medial clavicle with joint space widening. CT chest demonstrates clavicle positioned anterior to sternum. No vascular compromise on imaging. Soft tissue swelling around joint evident on advanced imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Sternoclavicular joint dislocation with anterior deformity and risk of mediastinal complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old man with history of rotator cuff syndrome presents with worsening anterior shoulder pain and weakness in internal rotation. Unable to perform activities of daily living requiring internal rotation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe weakness with internal rotation activities (lift-off test impossible to perform). Sharp anterior shoulder pain aggravated by internal rotation against resistance. Difficulty with personal hygiene and dressing tasks. Pain worse at night affecting sleep.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic rotator cuff pathology with degenerative changes. Progressive symptoms over 6 months despite physical therapy. Age-related tissue degeneration contributing. History of overhead work stress. Poor proprioceptive control and scapular dyskinesis noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive lift-off test (Gerber test) with inability to lift hand away from back (0/5 power). Positive bear-hug test showing weakness with internal rotation (3/5). Subscapularis palpable tenderness. Positive anterior slide test. Scapular dyskinesis evident. Subacromial space narrowed with impingement signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows subscapularis tendon tear with full-thickness component at musculotendinous junction. Associated rotator cuff pathology with supraspinatus and infraspinatus involvement. Subscapularis muscle atrophy evident. Bone marrow edema at lesser tuberosity insertion site. Ultrasound confirms tear with loss of normal tendon architecture.

puzzles.explanation: Subscapularis tear with loss of internal rotation strength and positive lift-off test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman presents with history of 2 falls in past month from standing to ground level. Reports dizziness before falls. Loss of confidence with walking affecting mobility and independence.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unsteady gait with reduced walking speed (0.5 m/sec). Balance impaired requiring contact guard for safety. Reports lightheadedness with position changes. Fear of falling evident with cautious movement patterns and activity avoidance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 82 with multiple fall risk factors: osteoporosis, hypertension on multiple medications causing orthostatic hypotension, mild cognitive impairment, vision reduced from cataracts. Lives alone in cluttered home environment. History of vitamin D insufficiency.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Timed up and go test 28 seconds (abnormal >12 seconds). Single leg stance 3 seconds each leg (abnormal <10 seconds). Berg Balance Scale score 38/56 (high fall risk <45). Orthostatic vital signs positive (systolic BP drop 20 mmHg on standing). Gait assessment shows reduced stride length, shuffle pattern, and reduced arm swing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bone density DEXA scan shows T-score -2.9 (osteoporosis). Brain MRI normal excluding stroke or structural lesion. Vestibular testing normal excluding balance organ pathology. Home safety assessment identifies multiple environmental hazards including throw rugs and poor lighting. Medication review identifies orthostatic-inducing antihypertensives.

puzzles.explanation: High-risk recurrent falls in frail elderly with multiple comorbidities require comprehensive assessment and preventive interventions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old man catches ring finger in door resulting in hyperflexion injury. Immediate pain over distal phalanx with inability to extend distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint. Minimal swelling and no gross deformity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to actively extend distal phalanx at DIP joint (fixed flexion position). Pain with passive extension. No sensory loss in finger. Mild swelling over DIP joint. Distal phalanx held in flexed position at rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism with direct hyperflexion injury to finger. Injury occurred 1 day ago. No prior finger injuries or arthritis history. Full-time office worker without heavy labor demands. No underlying connective tissue disorder.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows DIP joint in 30-degree flexion with inability to actively extend. Passive extension possible with resistance and pain. Isolated DIP flexor tendon (flexor digitorum superficialis) intact. No lacerations to extensor surface. Sensation intact to light touch and two-point discrimination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray lateral view shows small avulsion fracture at base of distal phalanx involving dorsal lip (approximately 15% of articular surface). Minimal displacement with fragment size 4mm. No intra-articular air or soft tissue emphysema. Dorsal lip avulsion consistent with mallet finger pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic untreated mallet finger with secondary deformities and functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old woman presents with palpitations and tachycardia upon standing. Heart rate increases rapidly from sitting. Symptoms of palpitations, lightheadedness, and tremor.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation from orthostatic tachycardia. Difficulty with prolonged standing. Fatigue and exercise intolerance. Avoids activities requiring sustained standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms present for 3 years with gradual onset post-viral illness. No cardiac history. Symptoms worsen with heat, dehydration, and menstrual cycle.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Supine heart rate normal (62 bpm), standing heart rate 115 bpm within 5 minutes. Blood pressure change minimal. Symptoms reproduce with standing. Positive tilt table test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Tilt table testing shows sustained heart rate elevation of >30 bpm with minimal blood pressure change, confirming POTS criteria. Plasma norepinephrine elevated when upright. Cardiac imaging normal.

puzzles.explanation: Classic POTS presentation with sustained excessive heart rate increase on postural change, post-viral onset, and autonomic dysfunction confirmed by tilt testing. High prevalence in young women.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete presents with recurrent shoulder instability during sports activities. The patient reports sensation of shoulder slipping or apprehension with certain movement patterns. Functional limitation affects upper extremity performance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The shoulder feels unstable with activities requiring abduction and external rotation in overhead positions. The patient demonstrates apprehension with provocative positioning. Protective guarding limits shoulder mobility during functional activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous shoulder dislocation or subluxation episodes establish the diagnosis. Underlying glenohumeral ligamentous laxity predisposes to ongoing instability. Repetitive sports participation with overhead activities perpetuates the instability pattern.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive apprehension test indicates instability fear. Relocation test (Jobe's) relieves apprehension. Shoulder range of motion in external rotation is increased. Generalized capsular laxity is evident on examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates labral pathology with potential glenohumeral ligamentous disruption. The capsule demonstrates stretching or attenuation. Imaging establishes the anatomical basis for instability.

puzzles.explanation: First-time anterior shoulder dislocation in young athlete with typical mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old man with long-standing psoriasis (20 years) presents with insidious onset joint pain affecting fingers, wrists, and knees over 6 months. Psoriasiform rash present. Morning stiffness lasting 1.5 hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Asymmetric polyarthritis with fingers and wrists predominantly affected. Nail changes present (pitting, onycholysis, nail bed destruction). Morning stiffness limiting function. Dactylitis (sausage digits) present in ring fingers bilaterally. Enthesitis with heel pain and Achilles tenderness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing psoriasis with progressive joint involvement suggesting psoriatic arthritis. Recent skin exacerbation preceding joint symptoms by 2 months. Family history of psoriasis and arthritis. Age 45 consistent with typical presentation window. No prior joint disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Asymmetric polyarthritis with dactylitis (sausage appearance of fingers). Nail dystrophy with pitting and onycholysis. Psoriatic plaques on elbows, knees, and scalp. Tenderness at Achilles insertions bilaterally. Wrist and finger joint swelling with warmth. Reduced grip strength (28 kg). Joint flexion contractures developing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR elevated at 42 mm/hr, CRP 8.2 mg/dL. Rheumatoid factor negative, anti-CCP negative (seronegative pattern). HLA-B27 positive (positive in 70% of PsA). Hand and wrist X-rays show periosteal new bone formation (fluffy periostitis) and pencil-in-cup deformity early stages. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with power Doppler activity indicating active inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Psoriatic Arthritis shows a predominantly axial/spinal presentation with minimal skin manifestations, demonstrating spondyloarthropathy features with late skin diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old sprinter presents with acute posterior thigh pain during 400-meter race. Pain onset abrupt during acceleration phase. Immediate functional loss with difficulty continuing sprint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Acute sharp pain in posterior mid-thigh during running with inability to continue activity. Pain with hip flexion and knee extension movements. Difficulty walking with protective antalgic gait. Visible swelling and ecchymosis developing over 24 hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism during sprinting with forceful hip flexion and knee extension (acceleration phase of running). Young athletic male with high-demand sport. No prior hamstring injury. Pre-existing hip flexor tightness noted. Inadequate warm-up prior to race.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness at mid-belly of hamstring. Swelling and warmth at mid-thigh location. Positive straight leg raise test with pain (limited to 40 degrees). Weak hip extension against resistance (3/5) due to pain. Gait antalgic with reduced stride length. Visible ecchymosis beginning to appear.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI confirms Grade II partial thickness hamstring strain at musculotendinous junction of biceps femoris. Hematoma present at injury site (4cm x 3cm). Surrounding muscle edema. Intact fascial integrity (not retracted). Ultrasound shows hypoechoic fluid collection at strain site with intact muscle architecture proximally and distally.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic hamstring symptoms often follow previous injury and reflect ongoing weakness and flexibility deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old woman presents with catching and locking sensation in the right ring finger at the MCP joint. Clicking noise with finger flexion and extension. Worsens in morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor tasks including writing and typing. Painful with full finger extension. Functional limitation in hand dexterity and grip activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 3 months. No acute trauma. Repetitive gripping activities in workplace (packing work). Symptoms progressive with worsening locking episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nodular thickening palpable over A1 pulley at MCP joint. Triggering reproduced with active finger flexion and extension. Triggering relieved with passive extension by opposite hand. Swelling at pulley level.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows nodular thickening of flexor digitorum superficialis tendon at A1 pulley. Synovial fluid surrounding tendon slightly increased. MRI confirms nodule location and synovitis.

puzzles.explanation: Classic trigger finger with morning stiffness and clicking, typical middle-aged presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old sprinter presents with acute posterior thigh pain during 400-meter race. Pain onset abrupt during acceleration phase. Immediate functional loss with difficulty continuing sprint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Acute sharp pain in posterior mid-thigh during running with inability to continue activity. Pain with hip flexion and knee extension movements. Difficulty walking with protective antalgic gait. Visible swelling and ecchymosis developing over 24 hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism during sprinting with forceful hip flexion and knee extension (acceleration phase of running). Young athletic male with high-demand sport. No prior hamstring injury. Pre-existing hip flexor tightness noted. Inadequate warm-up prior to race.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness at mid-belly of hamstring. Swelling and warmth at mid-thigh location. Positive straight leg raise test with pain (limited to 40 degrees). Weak hip extension against resistance (3/5) due to pain. Gait antalgic with reduced stride length. Visible ecchymosis beginning to appear.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI confirms Grade II partial thickness hamstring strain at musculotendinous junction of biceps femoris. Hematoma present at injury site (4cm x 3cm). Surrounding muscle edema. Intact fascial integrity (not retracted). Ultrasound shows hypoechoic fluid collection at strain site with intact muscle architecture proximally and distally.

puzzles.explanation: Partial hamstring strains can present more insidiously, often related to inadequate warm-up or prior tightness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old man with 5-year history of lower back pain now presenting with worsening lumbar pain and bilateral lower extremity radiculopathy. Progressive neurological symptoms and claudication affecting walking tolerance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Lumbar pain worse with extension and standing, improved by flexion and lying down. Bilateral lower extremity pain radiating down legs. Neurogenic claudication with walking tolerance limited to 50 meters before pain and heaviness in legs. Symptoms improve with rest and flexion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progressive course over 5 years with spinal degenerative changes and spondylolisthesis. Chronic mechanical back pain history. Occupational risk factors with heavy lifting and twisting. Age 54 with age-related disc degeneration. Sedentary with limited core stability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Extension lumbar spine reproduction pain and neurological symptoms. Limited forward flexion due to pain (hands to knees level versus knees). Lower extremity strength 4/5 in ankle dorsiflexion (L5 distribution). Diminished ankle reflex on right. Positive straight leg raise bilateral but limited by pain. Lumbar stenosis signs present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows L4-5 spondylolisthesis with L4 vertebra slipped anteriorly 3mm relative to L5. Degenerative disc changes at L4-5 and L5-S1. MRI confirms spondylolisthesis with significant central spinal stenosis at L4-5 level (50% canal narrowing). Ligamentum flavum hypertrophy and disc bulging contributing. Foraminal stenosis on left side.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spondylolisthesis presents as an acute traumatic event in a young patient with significant inflammation and functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old man 12 weeks post-traumatic hip dislocation reduction presents with progressive loss of hip motion and functional decline. Hip stiffness limiting activities of daily living. Swelling and pain in hip region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive loss of hip internal and external rotation. Hip flexion contracture developing (unable to fully extend hip). Abduction and adduction limited. Pain with attempted motion. Functional decline with difficulty dressing and toileting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy trauma 12 weeks prior with hip posterior dislocation and associated femoral head fracture. Complex fracture pattern with soft tissue injury. Delayed presentation to rehabilitation after acute care. Age 42 and male sex associated with higher HO risk. Extended immobilization period.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion contracture present (unable to achieve full extension). External and internal rotation severely limited (<15 degrees each direction). Abduction limited to 10 degrees. Palpable stiffness with firm end-feel on passive motion. Tenderness over hip region. Functional limitation with gait and transfers.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray hip shows extensive heterotopic ossification Brooker Grade III-IV with bridging bone formation across hip joint. CT hip demonstrates mature bone formation in soft tissues surrounding joint capsule. MRI shows ossified bridges with mature bone marrow signal. Radiographs demonstrate hip internal architecture with associated degeneration.

puzzles.explanation: Heterotopic ossification is extensive and disabling with multiple sites of ectopic bone formation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old warehouse worker presents with chronic lower back pain for 3 years exacerbated by work activities. Pain worse at end of work week. Associated work-related absence and functional limitations.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Mechanical lower back pain with activity exacerbation. Pain worse with bending and lifting activities at work. Relief with rest and position changes. Episodic exacerbations coinciding with busy work periods. Morning stiffness improving with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Work-related mechanical low back pain from heavy lifting and manual material handling duties. Sedentary pre-injury history with deconditioning. Poor body mechanics with bending and lifting. Lack of ergonomic modifications at workplace. Psychosocial factors with job dissatisfaction and limited support.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Limited forward flexion to fingertips 15cm from toes due to pain. Straight leg raise limited to 60 degrees by hamstring tightness and pain. One-legged stance provocative on both sides with pain reproduction. Lumbar paraspinal tenderness with guarding. Gait antalgic. Neurologically intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar X-rays show mild degenerative disc disease at L4-5 and L5-S1 levels without significant stenosis. MRI confirms modest disc bulging and minimal stenosis not explaining symptoms. Functional Capacity Evaluation shows inconsistent effort with high pain levels during testing. Psychological distress screening positive (PHQ-9 score 14).

puzzles.explanation: Lower back pain shows chronic occupational low back pain with significant functional limitation and psychological overlay.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old man presents with progressive morning stiffness in lumbar spine lasting 2 hours daily for 8 months. Pain improves with activity and worsens with rest. Nocturnal pain awakening him from sleep.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Spinal stiffness particularly in morning and with inactivity. Buttock and lower back pain described as deep ache. Pain improves with activity and exercise. Progressive loss of spinal mobility with difficulty bending forward. Associated fatigue and malaise.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young male age 28 (typical for AS presentation). Insidious onset with progressive spinal involvement. Morning stiffness >1 hour typical of inflammatory pattern. No significant trauma or inciting event. Family history negative but HLA-B27 status unknown. Improved with activity suggesting inflammatory process.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Markedly limited lumbar forward flexion (Schober test: 2cm movement versus normal 5cm indicating rigidity). Reduced cervical spine motion with limited neck extension. Spinal extension limited. Reduced chest wall expansion (3cm versus normal 7cm) from costovertebral involvement. Straight leg raise normal. Neurologically intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI sacroiliac joints shows bilateral sacroiliitis with erosions and inflammatory signal changes. Lumbar spine shows syndesmophyte formation connecting vertebral bodies (bamboo spine early phase). HLA-B27 positive. ESR elevated at 48 mm/hr, CRP 9.2 mg/dL. Complete blood count and metabolic panel normal.

puzzles.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis presents in young males with inflammatory back pain pattern (morning stiffness >30 min, improved by exercise), extra-articular features, and bilateral sacroiliitis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old man presents 3 days post-abdominal surgery with worsening dyspnea and hypoxemia. Oxygen saturation dropped to 90% on room air. Fever present at 38.5°C. Post-operative pain limiting respiratory effort.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Shortness of breath with minimal exertion post-operatively. Reduced oxygen saturation despite supplemental oxygen requirement. Rapid shallow breathing pattern. Reduced exercise tolerance post-operatively. Pain with deep breathing limiting respiratory mechanics.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Post-operative status 3 days s/p major abdominal surgery. Significant post-operative pain limiting deep breathing and coughing. Immobility and inadequate pain control increasing atelectasis risk. Weak respiratory effort from anesthesia effects. Secretion retention from reduced cough.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tachypnea with respiratory rate 24/min. Reduced breath sounds at lung bases bilaterally. Dullness to percussion at bases. Fever 38.5°C. Rales present on auscultation. Reduced oxygen saturation 90% on room air, improving to 95% with oxygen. Shallow rapid breathing pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows opacification and collapse of left lower lobe fields consistent with atelectasis. Right lower lobe shows similar but less severe changes. Elevated hemidiaphragm on left. CT chest confirms left lower lobe consolidation and volume loss. No pneumonia (no peripheral consolidation pattern initially).

puzzles.explanation: Atelectasis from splinting due to rib fracture pain. Demonstrates importance of pain control and respiratory efforts post-trauma, particularly in patients with underlying lung disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman presents with chronic lower back pain for 8 years with gradual onset without specific inciting event. Pain mechanical in nature worse with activity and sitting. Progressive loss of spinal flexibility.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dull aching lower back pain worse at end of day and with sustained activities. Relief with position changes and rest. Morning stiffness lasting 30 minutes improving with movement. Loss of mobility with difficulty bending forward. Standing tolerance gradually declining.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 62 with degenerative changes expected with aging. Occupational prolonged standing and repetitive bending for 35 years. History of heavy lifting in younger years. Sedentary lifestyle contributing to deconditioning. Smoking history 30 pack-years with known effects on disc health.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar forward flexion limited to fingertips 20cm from toes. Straight leg raise normal (70 degrees) without radiculopathy. Lumbar paraspinal muscles tight with guarding. One-legged stance normal without pain. Neurologically completely intact. Gait normal without antalgic pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays show multilevel degenerative disc disease with loss of disc height at L4-5 and L5-S1. Osteophyte formation present (bone spurs). Facet joint osteoarthritis. MRI shows disc desiccation with decreased T2 signal in multiple levels. Modest disc bulging at L4-5 without stenosis. No neural compromise.

puzzles.explanation: Degenerative disc disease is a chronic condition in an older patient with imaging findings exceeding clinical symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old elite endurance athlete presents with persistent fatigue and declining athletic performance despite increased training volume over 3 months. Resting heart rate elevated 15 beats above baseline. Multiple minor illnesses over past 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Fatigue out of proportion to training load. Difficulty maintaining usual training intensity with early fatigue. Persistent elevated resting heart rate (75 bpm versus baseline 60 bpm). Delayed recovery between training sessions. Frequent upper respiratory infections and minor illnesses.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive endurance training program with progressively increased volume without adequate recovery. Mileage increased 50% over 3 months. Inadequate sleep averaging 6 hours nightly. Poor nutritional recovery with insufficient caloric intake. Psychological stress from competition and team dynamics.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate elevated at 75 bpm (personal baseline 60 bpm). Heart rate recovery after exercise test shows delayed normalization. VO2 max testing shows 10% reduction compared to previous testing. Psychological assessment shows mood disturbance and depression symptoms. Musculoskeletal examination normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Blood work shows normal thyroid function excluding thyroiditis. Normal complete blood count excluding anemia. Serum cortisol elevated with abnormal circadian rhythm. Ferritin at lower limit of normal suggesting iron depletion. CPK mildly elevated at 380 IU/L suggesting muscle stress. No viral serology positive.

puzzles.explanation: Overtraining syndrome shows classic presentation with performance plateau, hormonal dysfunction, and energy deficit despite high training load.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 79-year-old woman with progressive functional decline and multiple comorbidities presents with weakness and fatigue.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with basic activities of daily living. Fall risk significantly elevated. Social isolation developing. Reduced life expectancy noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple chronic conditions including diabetes, arthritis, heart disease. Progressive decline over past year. No specific acute illness. Dependence on family support increasing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Frail appearance with obvious muscle wasting. Slow movement and delayed responses. Weakness throughout examination. Gait slow and uncertain. Cognitive decline noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Labs show anemia with low hemoglobin. Albumin reduced indicating malnutrition. Strength testing confirms weakness. Functional measures all impaired. Prognosis guarded.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced frailty with multiple deficits creates vulnerability to cascading complications and poor prognosis requiring comprehensive geriatric care.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman with Alzheimer's dementia presents with progressive gait deterioration and functional decline over 18 months. Increased fall risk requiring increased supervision. Unable to ambulate independently with stairs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Slow shuffling gait with reduced stride length (15cm versus normal 60cm). Loss of arm swing with stiff posture. Balance impaired requiring contact guard. Difficulty navigating stairs with cognitive processing delays affecting movement. Unable to perform dual tasks (walking while talking difficult).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Advanced Alzheimer's dementia (MMSE score 8/30) with 6-year disease duration. Multiple comorbidities including hypertension and osteoporosis. Sedentary lifestyle in care facility. Nutritional decline with weight loss of 8 kg over past year. Limited cognitive ability to follow movement commands.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gait examination shows slow shuffling pattern (0.3 m/sec, normal >1.4 m/sec). Reduced stride length with feet not lifting properly (shuffling pattern). Postural instability evident during stance transitions. Diminished hip and knee flexion during swing phase. Stooped posture with forward head position. Unable to correct gait errors by instruction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI shows advanced cortical atrophy and ventricular enlargement consistent with Alzheimer's stage disease. Generalized cortical atrophy pattern affecting motor and executive areas. White matter changes present. No acute stroke or subdural hematoma. Gait analysis confirms subcortical movement pattern with basal ganglia involvement pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Dementia-related mobility decline reflects progressive brain atrophy and neurodegeneration affecting motor control and balance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old patient presents with chronic knee pain and stiffness affecting functional mobility. The patient reports pain with weight-bearing activities and morning stiffness. Functional limitation affects gait and stairs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is exacerbated by weight-bearing, stairs, and prolonged standing. Morning stiffness improves with activity. Functional activities are limited. Symptom relief occurs with rest and anti-inflammatory medication.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing degenerative joint disease of the knee has progressed over years. Previous injuries or meniscal pathology may have contributed. Age-related joint cartilage loss is evident on imaging.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, joint crepitus is palpable. Range of motion is limited, particularly in full flexion. Mild effusion may be present. Muscle weakness is evident secondary to disuse.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs demonstrate joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis. Cartilage loss is evident. Imaging confirms osteoarthritic changes.

puzzles.explanation: Knee osteoarthritis results from progressive cartilage loss and is common with aging and prior injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old construction worker presents with lateral lower back pain radiating around trunk at level of L4-5. Pain localized to thoracolumbar fascia region. Occupational repetitive twisting and bending exacerbating symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp lateral lower back pain worse with trunk rotation and twisting movements. Pain localized to flanks and lateral trunk. Difficulty with reaching across body and rotational activities. Pain improves with rest and position changes. Functional limitation with occupational tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occupational mechanical strain from repetitive twisting and bending in construction work. Gradual onset over 6 weeks correlating with demanding project. Poor core stability and ergonomics. History of lower back strain 2 years prior. Sedentary leisure time with limited exercise.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness along thoracolumbar fascia region bilaterally (flanks at L4-5 level). Pain with trunk rotation against resistance. Reduced lumbar rotation in both directions (limited by pain). Palpable muscle guarding in quadratus lumborum region. Single leg stance provokes lateral back pain. Neurologically intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays normal excluding fracture or structural abnormality. MRI shows fascial thickening and edema in thoracolumbar fascia region. Quadratus lumborum muscle shows edema and signal changes consistent with myofascial injury. No nerve compression or disc pathology. Ultrasound confirms fascial thickening with echo changes.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracolumbar fascia pain is a complex regional pain from fascia and muscular structures.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old woman presents with 6-week history of symmetric polyarthritis affecting multiple small joints. Morning stiffness lasting 2 hours severely limiting function. Systemic symptoms of fatigue and malaise.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symmetric inflammation of metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Morning stiffness lasting >2 hours (diagnostic criterion). Joint pain and swelling worse in morning, improving gradually with activity. Associated fatigue affecting work and home function. Low-grade fever present.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset of symmetric polyarticular inflammation over 6 weeks. Functional limitation with difficulty gripping, dressing, and other activities. Systemic symptoms of fatigue, malaise, and low-grade fever. No recent viral illness. Family history of autoimmune disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symmetric swelling and warmth of metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Grip strength bilaterally reduced (14 kg versus baseline 30 kg). Rheumatoid nodules palpable on elbows. Positive squeeze test of metacarpophalangeal joints reproducing pain. Reduced hand function with difficulty making fist.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Rheumatoid factor positive (titer 1:1280). Anti-CCP positive (256 IU/mL). ESR elevated at 52 mm/hr, CRP 18 mg/dL. Hand X-rays show early periarticular osteopenia with marginal erosions. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with power Doppler activity in affected joints. Complete blood count shows mild anemia (Hb 10.8).

puzzles.explanation: Rheumatoid Arthritis shows more aggressive disease with extra-articular manifestations in an older male, including nodules and pulmonary involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old woman presents with painful intercourse for 8 months with significant impact on relationship and quality of life. Pain localized to vaginal entry region. Partner supportive but sexual function severely limited.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp pain at introitus during penetration preventing normal intercourse. Pain onset immediately with penetration attempt. Associated muscle guarding and tension. Dyspareunia progressive over 8 months with increasing severity. Pain affects mood and relationship.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset 8 months ago without clear inciting event. Post-menopausal status 1 year prior contributing to tissue changes. No prior pelvic trauma or surgery. Stress and relationship factors contributing. History of anxiety and depression. Recent hormonal changes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor examination shows hypertonic pelvic floor muscles with trigger points palpable. Tenderness on introital palpation. Pelvic floor muscle spasm evident during examination. Levator ani muscles tight bilaterally. Vulvar erythema present in introital region. Speculum examination normal excluding other causes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Transvaginal ultrasound shows hypoechoic pelvic floor muscles consistent with myofascial dysfunction. No endometriosis, cysts, or structural abnormality identified. MRI pelvis normal excluding neuropathic or structural causes. Pelvic floor electromyography shows increased resting muscle tension and guarding pattern abnormal.

puzzles.explanation: Dyspareunia without obvious structural cause likely involves pelvic floor dysfunction, myofascial restrictions, or central sensitization. Treatment requires pelvic floor physical therapy, trigger point release, education, and pain neuroscience approaches.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old woman with 4-year history of persistent widespread pain following low back injury. Extensive prior investigations normal without clear structural cause. Functional capacity significantly reduced with mood disturbance and social withdrawal.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Widespread pain affecting multiple body regions including back, neck, shoulders, and hips. Pain described as constant with variable intensity fluctuating throughout day. Disability and functional loss disproportionate to structural findings. Sleep severely disrupted affecting daytime function.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progression over 4 years since initial low back strain injury. Unsuccessful multiple treatments including surgeries (two prior back surgeries with persistent pain). Significant psychosocial stressors contributing (divorce, job loss). Pain catastrophizing evident. Multiple failed medication trials.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows non-anatomic pain patterns and inconsistent findings. Pain reproducible with non-organic examination maneuvers. Widespread tender points throughout body. Psychological distress evident with mood changes during examination. Functional guarding with inconsistent effort during testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Multiple prior imaging studies normal including MRI spine, pelvis, and brain. Extensive lab work unrevealing (normal inflammatory markers, metabolic panel, thyroid). Psychological evaluation shows depression (PHQ-9 score 22), anxiety (GAD-7 score 18), and pain catastrophizing. Pain amplification scale elevated suggesting central sensitization component.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic pain syndrome shows persistent pain beyond tissue healing with psychological factors and complex pain behaviors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old girl presents with pain in the ball of foot under second metatarsal head. Pain with walking and weight-bearing activities. Insidious onset over 2 months with progressive functional limitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain localized to metatarsal head region (second MTP joint). Pain worse with weight bearing and walking. Swelling over metatarsal head region. Difficulty with running and sports. Pain improves with rest and off-loading.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 16 with developmental lesion typical for adolescent presentation. Insidious onset without acute trauma. Sports participation (track athlete) with repetitive impact loading. Gradual progressive course over 2 months. Pain affecting activity tolerance.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over second metatarsal head (dorsal aspect). Swelling and prominence of metatarsal head region. Pain with passive dorsiflexion of second metatarsophalangeal joint. Limited metatarsophalangeal joint motion secondary to pain. Gait antalgic with reduced stance phase on affected foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows flattening and fragmentation of second metatarsal head consistent with Freiberg's infraction (osteonecrosis). Dorsal hypertrophic changes present. MRI shows osteochondral lesion with bone marrow edema. T1 signal hypointensity within metatarsal head indicating necrosis. Associated cartilage irregularity.

puzzles.explanation: Freiberg's disease involves avascular necrosis of a metatarsal head, typically the second.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old male athlete sustains anterior shoulder dislocation during contact sport. Violent arm positioning with forced external rotation and abduction. Immediate severe pain with arm held in protective position.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe shoulder pain with complete loss of function. Arm held in adduction and internal rotation position. Visible deformity with prominence of humeral head anteriorly. Unable to move shoulder voluntarily.

clues.label: History
clues.text: First-time anterior shoulder dislocation from contact sport. Young athletic male with full-contact participation. Acute traumatic mechanism with forced external rotation positioning. No prior shoulder problems or instability reported. High-energy injury mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder held in protective adduction and internal rotation position. Visible anterior prominence of humeral head below clavicle. Severe pain with any attempted motion. Palpable emptiness in glenoid fossa. Inability to perform active motion (0/5 across all planes). Neurovascular examination intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP, scapular Y, and axillary views) confirms anterior humeral head dislocation. Associated Bankart lesion (glenoid rim fracture) visible on specialized imaging. CT scan shows intra-articular loose body and osseous Bankart fragment. MRI post-reduction shows rotator cuff integrity with possible labral involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Shoulder dislocation presents as acute anterior glenohumeral dislocation from traumatic mechanism with structural damage evident on imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old overhead thrower (baseball pitcher) presents with vague shoulder pain and feeling of instability during throwing. Apprehension and episodes of shoulder slipping sensation. Functional impact on throwing mechanics.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Shoulder apprehension with loaded position in cocking phase of throw. Feeling of instability or slipping sensation in shoulder. Difficulty with end-range external rotation activities. Pain secondary to apprehension in weight-bearing positions. Functional limitation with overhead sports.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Overhead athlete with repetitive throwing mechanism. Gradual onset of instability symptoms over season (chronic overhead loading). Associated capsular laxity (ligamentous laxity) noted on examination. Scapular dyskinesis evident. Poor proprioceptive control and dynamic stabilization.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive sulcus sign (inferior shoulder subluxation with traction). Positive apprehension and relocation tests. Laxity demonstrated with inferior translation in dependent position (drawer sign). Scapular dyskinesis evident during active motion. Reduced internal rotation relative to external rotation (reverse position). Strength 4+/5 indicating dynamic stabilizer weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows enlarged rotator cuff interval with increased capsular volume. Posterior labral fraying (SLAP pathology) associated lesion. Excessive glenohumeral translation documented on imaging. No discrete labral detachment (rules out Bankart). Ultrasound demonstrates increased capsular volume and laxity on dynamic maneuvers.

puzzles.explanation: Multidirectional instability with generalized ligamentous laxity in young patient.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A neonate presents with upper limb weakness and loss of function following a complicated delivery. The presentation involves shoulder and arm dysfunction noted shortly after birth. Shoulder dystocia during delivery is the preceding event of concern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The infant demonstrates inability to abduct and externally rotate the shoulder. Waiter's tip position is observed with the arm held in adduction and internal rotation. Movement of the upper extremity is severely limited with protective positioning evident.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Birth history reveals shoulder dystocia or forceful traction during delivery as the causative mechanism. Brachial plexus injury occurred during the perinatal period. The C5-C6 nerve roots are the anatomical level affected in this classic upper trunk injury pattern.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, biceps and deltoid muscles demonstrate marked weakness and atrophy. The Moro reflex is absent on the affected side while the lower limb response remains intact. Sensory testing along the lateral arm and forearm shows impairment corresponding to C5-C6 dermatomes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brachial plexus reveals avulsion of the C5-C6 nerve roots from the spinal cord. The upper trunk demonstrates signal abnormality and discontinuity. Nerve root avulsion is confirmed with imaging findings consistent with preganglionic injury.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Erb's palsy with incomplete recovery requires assessment for nerve regeneration potential and consideration of surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 30-year-old athlete presents with recurrent ankle instability episodes during activities. The patient reports ankle giving way with inversion movements. Functional limitation affects sports participation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Lateral ankle instability occurs with inversion movements, particularly on uneven surfaces. The ankle intermittently gives way during walking. Proprioceptive deficits lead to sudden functional instability.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic ligamentous laxity follows previous ankle sprains and incomplete rehabilitation. Proprioceptive deficits persist despite symptom resolution. Multiple recurrent inversion episodes have established chronic instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive anterior drawer test indicates significant tibiotalar translation. Inversion stress testing demonstrates hypermobility. Proprioceptive testing reveals impaired balance. Ankle proprioception is diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates chronic lateral ligament attenuation and scarring. Syndesmotic stability should be confirmed. Imaging reveals chronic ligamentous changes supporting functional instability.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic ankle instability results from incomplete healing and proprioceptive deficits after previous sprains.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young child presents with developmental delay in motor milestones and abnormal movement patterns. Muscle tone abnormalities and stiffness restrict normal motor function. The condition is nonprogressive but becomes more apparent with age and development.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Spastic diplegia is the movement pattern observed with increased tone predominantly affecting the lower limbs. Scissor gait is characteristic with hip adduction and knee flexion during walking. Equinus positioning of the feet and toe-walking are common compensatory patterns.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Birth history reveals perinatal hypoxia or asphyxia as the causative event. The timing of injury occurred around the time of delivery. Brain injury resulted in permanent motor dysfunction that remains static from that point onward.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination reveals increased muscle tone with hyperreflexia throughout the lower limbs. Clonus is elicited at the ankle bilaterally. The Babinski sign is extensor bilaterally indicating upper motor neuron involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain demonstrates periventricular leukomalacia with signal changes in white matter around the ventricles. Ventricular dilation may be present secondary to white matter loss. The imaging findings correlate with the pattern of spastic diplegia observed clinically.

puzzles.explanation: Cerebral palsy is a non-progressive motor disorder from perinatal brain injury, typically presenting with spasticity and developmental delays.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports improved mood and mental clarity following regular exercise sessions. Depression and anxiety symptoms improve with consistent physical activity. Psychological well-being is enhanced post-exercise.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Mood enhancement occurs during and after moderate to vigorous aerobic exercise. The patient reports feeling energized and psychologically better following workouts. Regular exercise participation is maintained due to positive mood effects.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Exercise tolerance and motivation improve with consistent training. History of depression or anxiety may improve with exercise programming. Neurotransmitter changes facilitate mood regulation through physical activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On assessment, psychological mood scales demonstrate improvement post-exercise. Cardiovascular fitness improvements correlate with mood enhancement. Endorphin release and monoamine neurotransmitter changes are proposed mechanisms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Functional MRI studies demonstrate enhanced activation in reward centers of the brain post-exercise. Endorphin and serotonin levels increase measurably following physical activity. Neuroimaging confirms the biological basis for exercise-induced mood improvement.

puzzles.explanation: Exercise-induced mood improvement shows positive psychological response to regular physical activity in depressed patient.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged woman with diabetes presents with shoulder pain and profound loss of motion. Shoulder function is markedly restricted in all planes of movement. Pain is present both at rest and with activity, limiting sleep and daily function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: All shoulder movements demonstrate significant restriction in both active and passive ranges. External rotation is most severely limited followed by abduction and flexion. The restriction occurs in a capsular pattern indicating joint involvement rather than muscular dysfunction.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Risk factors include middle age, female gender, and poorly controlled diabetes mellitus. The condition often follows minor shoulder trauma or immobilization. Onset is typically gradual with progressive loss of motion over weeks to months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, passive motion reveals the characteristic capsular pattern of restriction. External rotation at 0 degrees abduction is severely limited. Scapulohumeral rhythm is disrupted with compensatory scapular elevation during attempted flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the shoulder are typically normal without bony abnormality. MR arthrography demonstrates thickening and enhancement of the joint capsule. The rotator cuff interval is tightened with loss of the normal axillary pouch distensibility.

puzzles.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis presents with insidious onset of global shoulder stiffness in a capsular pattern, strong association with diabetes and thyroid disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of urinary urgency and frequency that significantly impacts her quality of life. She experiences a persistent urge to urinate 12-14 times daily and 3-4 times nightly, even when the bladder contains minimal urine.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient has modified her activities extensively to stay near bathroom facilities and has experienced occasional urgency incontinence when unable to reach the toilet immediately. Social activities and travel are limited by her symptoms, and sleep is disrupted by nocturia.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began after menopause approximately 2 years ago without urinary tract infection or medication changes. The patient denies dysuria or hematuria. She reports no significant fluid intake changes. No prior episodes of similar urgency; general health is good aside from mild seasonal allergies.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, vital signs are normal and abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness. Pelvic floor muscle assessment shows normal strength but evidence of increased tone. Neurological examination of lower extremities and perineal sensation are intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bladder ultrasound performed post-void demonstrates normal post-void residual volume of 25 mL. Renal ultrasound is unremarkable with no hydronephrosis. Urinalysis is completely normal with negative culture. Cystoscopy was performed and revealed no structural abnormalities or malignancy.

puzzles.explanation: Overactive Bladder shows neurogenic OAB secondary to CNS disease (stroke, neurological decline) with impaired detrusor function and elevated post-void residual.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete reports anterior knee pain specifically at the patellar tendon. Pain is worse with jumping and pivoting activities. Functional limitation occurs with sport-specific movements requiring explosive power.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are exacerbated by jumping, running, and rapid direction changes. Pain occurs at the onset of activity and may improve as tissues warm but return post-activity. Loading activities that stress the patellar tendon reproduce pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset follows increased training volume or intensity, particularly jumping-based sports. Weakness in hip or core muscles may contribute to abnormal loading patterns. Previous patellar or knee pathology may predispose to tendinopathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is localized to the patellar tendon insertion at the tibial tubercle. Single leg squats demonstrate poor motor control and knee valgus. Pain occurs with resisted knee extension testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging reveals thickened patellar tendon with mixed echogenicity and increased vascularity on Doppler. MRI demonstrates signal changes within the tendon substance. Imaging findings correlate with symptom duration and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic patellar tendinopathy results from cumulative microtrauma and incomplete healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old male presents with acute-onset shoulder weakness and inability to initiate arm abduction following a severe fall with direct trauma to the shoulder region. He reports numbness over the lateral shoulder and upper arm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot perform overhead activities or lift the arm away from the body due to profound weakness. He is unable to do any pushing or reaching movements requiring shoulder abduction. Pain is primarily from the associated shoulder injury rather than nerve pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred when the patient fell directly onto his shoulder from a standing height and struck a hard surface. No prior neurological symptoms or shoulder pathology existed. The patient seeks immediate evaluation within 48 hours of the injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, deltoid muscle is atrophic and significantly weakened with shoulder abduction strength 2/5. Sensation is diminished over the lateral shoulder in the regimental badge distribution. Axillary nerve traction test is positive. Rotator cuff strength is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-rays rule out fracture or dislocation. MRI shows no rotator cuff tear but reveals signal changes in the axillary nerve course with surrounding soft tissue edema. Electromyography demonstrates denervation potentials in deltoid and axillary nerve conduction slowing.

puzzles.explanation: Post-dislocation axillary nerve injury with deltoid paralysis and sensory loss in axillary nerve distribution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient in early post-operative period following total shoulder arthroplasty reports pain at surgical site. Shoulder motion is limited by protective guarding and pain. Functional use of the arm is significantly restricted immediately post-surgery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain and stiffness limit active range of motion in all planes. Passive motion is protected in early stages to allow tissue healing. Immobilization is maintained according to surgical protocol.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Total shoulder replacement was performed for severe osteoarthritis or rotator cuff damage. Pre-operative shoulder function was markedly limited by arthropathy. Post-operative rehabilitation follows a predictable timeline with progressive loading.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pain is elicited with passive motion beyond protected range. Incision appears well-healed without signs of infection. Arm position is supported in protective sling during early post-operative phase.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs confirm prosthetic component positioning and alignment. Serial imaging monitors for component loosening or complications. CT may be used if complications are suspected.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Shoulder Replacement shows mid-term recovery (4 months) with good pain control but persistent stiffness and weakness requiring intensive strengthening.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A woman reports pelvic heaviness and pressure sensation in the vagina. A bulging sensation is felt with prolonged standing or activity. Urinary symptoms including frequency and urgency accompany the pressure complaint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are exacerbated by prolonged standing, walking, or heavy lifting. Symptom severity worsens throughout the day with improved sensation when lying supine. Heavy lifting and straining increase the pressure sensation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Parity and vaginal deliveries are significant risk factors for pelvic floor dysfunction. Chronic straining from constipation or chronic cough may contribute. Advancing age and menopause worsen pelvic floor laxity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On pelvic examination, anterior vaginal wall bulging is evident with Valsalva maneuver. The bladder prolapse may be visualized or palpated during straining. Grade of prolapse is classified from 1-4 based on extent of descent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dynamic pelvic MRI demonstrates bladder descent during straining with measurement of prolapse grade. Ultrasound imaging can assess pelvic floor support and measure descent. Video urodynamics confirms bladder position and function during filling and straining.

puzzles.explanation: Cystocele management requires pelvic floor strengthening, pessary fitting for conservative management, weight reduction, smoking cessation, and consideration of surgical repair (anterior colporrhaphy) if conservative measures fail and functional limitation significant.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient in the immediate post-operative period following total hip arthroplasty reports pain at the surgical site. Hip motion is restricted by protective positioning and precautions. Functional mobility is severely limited in early post-operative phase.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weight bearing is progressed according to surgeon protocol with crutches or walker. Hip flexion is restricted to prevent prosthetic dislocation. Abduction pillows or positioning devices maintain protective alignment.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Total hip replacement was performed for advanced osteoarthritis or femoral neck fracture. Pre-operative hip pain and dysfunction significantly impaired mobility. Post-operative rehabilitation involves progressive weight bearing and motion over weeks to months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, surgical site shows appropriate wound healing without excessive drainage. Hip motion is limited per post-operative precautions. Muscle guarding is present with pain on end-range motion testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs confirm prosthetic component positioning with appropriate hip center reconstruction. Component alignment is assessed to ensure optimal biomechanics. Repeat imaging monitors for subsidence or component migration.

puzzles.explanation: THA rehabilitation requires strict hip precautions in the acute phase, progressive hip abductor strengthening to prevent Trendelenburg gait, hip flexor stretching, and functional mobility training to optimize long-term prosthetic function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-week postpartum woman presents with visible midline abdominal bulging and separation of rectus abdominis muscles. Concerned about functional abdominal weakness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with core-dependent activities including rolling, sitting up, and carrying infant. Worsens with Valsalva maneuver. Improves with splinting support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Uncomplicated vaginal delivery 6 weeks prior. Weight gained 16 kg during pregnancy. Return to exercise initiated without postpartum rehabilitation guidance. No diastasis history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable gap of 3 fingers width between rectus muscles at midline above umbilicus, reducing to 2 fingers below umbilicus. Rectus muscles separate further with abdominal contraction. Intact fascia, no hernia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound confirms diastasis rectus abdominis with interrectal distance 3.5 cm. Linea alba thickness normal. No ventral hernia present. Dynamic ultrasound shows muscle separation increase with contraction.

puzzles.explanation: Diastasis Rectus Abdominis shows postpartum separation in early recovery period following single uncomplicated vaginal delivery with visible bulging and palpable gap.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A weightlifter reports acute sharp pain in the anterior chest with sudden loss of function. Chest deformity with loss of pectoral contour is immediately apparent. Strength in horizontal adduction and internal rotation is lost.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Horizontal adduction of the arm is severely limited or absent. Bench pressing and pushing activities are impossible. Arm positioning demonstrates loss of anterior chest muscular bulk.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rupture typically occurs during heavy weightlifting with eccentric loading during bench press. The distal tendon attachment at the humerus is the most common rupture site. Steroids or chronic tendon pathology may predispose to rupture.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, a gap may be palpable in the pectoral region where the muscle has retracted. Loss of muscle bulk is visible with asymmetry compared to the contralateral side. Strength testing demonstrates severe weakness in horizontal adduction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the chest reveals discontinuity of the pectoralis major tendon near its humeral insertion. Muscle retraction and edema are evident at the rupture site. Imaging confirms the site and extent of rupture for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Pectoralis major rupture from maximal weight lifting effort with acute onset.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports mechanical symptoms in the knee including catching and locking. Pain is localized to the joint line. Swelling develops in the knee, worse with prolonged standing or walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Twisting movements and cutting activities trigger catching sensations. Mechanical locking may occur with weight bearing requiring manipulation to unlock. Squatting and kneeling activities are particularly provocative.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tear may follow a specific injury with twisting mechanism or develop insidiously in degenerative knees. Previous meniscal pathology or knee injuries increase risk. Degenerative tears are common in middle-aged and older patients.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: McMurray test and Thessaly test reproduce the mechanical symptoms. Joint line tenderness is localized to the meniscal location. Effusion may be present with ballottement demonstrating fluid in the joint.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates meniscal tear in the medial or lateral meniscus with classification of tear pattern. T2 signal intensity within the meniscal substance indicates tear. Imaging localizes the tear and guides treatment decisions.

puzzles.explanation: A medial meniscal bucket-handle tear presents with mechanical locking, joint line tenderness, delayed effusion (distinguishing from ACL), and positive McMurray's test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old man reports progressive hearing difficulty for past 5 years affecting both ears symmetrically. Difficulty hearing high-pitched sounds and female voices. Wife notes television volume excessively loud.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty hearing conversations in noisy environments. Speech discrimination affected with words sounding unclear. Tinnitus present as high-pitched ringing. Vertigo not reported. Social withdrawal due to communication difficulty. Significant impact on quality of life.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 68 with expected age-related hearing changes. 45-year occupational noise exposure as machinist without ear protection. No ototoxic medications. No prior ear surgery or trauma. Family history of hearing loss in father. Smoking history 40 pack-years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Otoscopy normal with clear tympanic membranes bilaterally. Weber test shows conductive component (lateralizes to better ear). Rinne test shows air-bone gap in both ears. Speech discrimination score 68% at conversational levels. Cerumen impaction noted and removed bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Audiometry confirms bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss with air-bone gaps suggesting mixed component. Bone conduction thresholds elevated at frequencies >2000 Hz. Speech discrimination reduced. Tympanometry normal type A indicating normal middle ear function. Acoustic reflex present.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related hearing loss (presbycusis) involves progressive sensorineural hearing loss, particularly in high frequencies, affecting communication.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete reports pain in the lower abdomen and inner groin region. Pain is specific to the pubic symphysis area and radiates to inner thigh. Functional limitation occurs with running, jumping, and kicking sports activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is exacerbated by hip adduction activities and crossing the legs. Running and rapid changes of direction reproduce symptoms. Abdominal and adductor muscle exertion increases pain at the pubic bone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Condition occurs in athletes with repetitive hip adduction and flexion activities. Asymmetric hip strength and flexibility contribute to excessive shear at the symphysis. Previous hip or groin injuries increase risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited directly over the pubic symphysis. Pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction and flexion maneuvers. Palpable tenderness occurs over the symphysis during suprapubic palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate symphyseal joint changes including widening, sclerosis, and bone resorption. Bone scintigraphy shows increased uptake at the pubic symphysis. MRI reveals edema and inflammatory changes at the symphyseal joint.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic osteitis pubis often involves pelvic instability and may be associated with lumbar or hip dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness and functional decline over 2 years. Difficulty rising from chair without armrest support. Walking slower and shorter distances than previously. Recent fall from trip on uneven pavement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Reduced grip strength (12 kg versus 22 kg in contralateral hand). Difficulty with stairs and walking long distances. Loss of independence with heavy household tasks. Slow gait velocity (0.7 m/sec versus normal 1.4 m/sec). Difficulty getting out of bed and toilet independently.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 76 with sedentary lifestyle and reduced physical activity. Inadequate protein intake (40g daily versus recommended 1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight). Loss of appetite with reduced food intake. Multiple comorbidities including osteoarthritis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease limiting activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible muscle wasting in upper extremities and thighs. Reduced grip strength 12 kg (abnormal cutoff <16 kg for women). Slow gait speed 0.7 m/sec (abnormal <1.0 m/sec). Reduced lower extremity strength particularly hip flexors and knee extensors (4/5). Difficulty rising from chair in <12 seconds (requires 18 seconds).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan confirms low fat-free mass index (5.8 kg/m2, cutoff <5.75). MRI thigh shows reduced muscle mass with increased intramuscular fat infiltration. Protein markers: prealbumin 18 mg/dL (low, <20), albumin 3.2 g/dL (borderline). Inflammatory markers: CRP 5.2 mg/L (mildly elevated). Vitamin D level 22 ng/mL (insufficient).

puzzles.explanation: Sarcopenic obesity combines muscle loss with excess adiposity, masking underlying sarcopenia and significantly impairing function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports progressive dyspnea on exertion with gradual loss of exercise capacity. Dry cough that does not improve persists for months. Fatigue and general weakness accompany respiratory symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Exertional dyspnea is disproportionate to the activity level performed. Walking short distances produces significant breathlessness. Climbing stairs or inclines causes substantial respiratory distress.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progressive onset over months to years characterizes pulmonary fibrosis. Occupational or environmental exposure may be identified. Smoking history may contribute to disease severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On auscultation, fine end-inspiratory crackles are heard bilaterally at lung bases. Digital clubbing may be evident with nail bed changes. Cyanosis may appear with advanced disease and hypoxemia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates reticular opacities and traction bronchiectasis in a usual interstitial pneumonia pattern. Progressive fibrotic changes are visible with serial imaging. Pulmonary function testing shows restrictive pattern with reduced diffusion capacity.

puzzles.explanation: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis with classic presentation of progressive dyspnea, dry cough, clubbing, and UIP pattern on HRCT. Reduced DLCO is characteristic.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old female cyclist with chronic anterior knee pain reports increasing difficulty with cycling distances. Pain worsens with climbing hills and longer rides. Progressive functional decline.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with longer cycling sessions. Pain increases with climbing. Reduced training tolerance. Difficulty with squatting activities. Functional limitation progressive.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cycling hobby with recent increase in distance and intensity. No acute injury. Gradual progressive onset. Multiple attempted conservative interventions with limited benefit.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive J-sign patellar tracking. VMO strength weak. Patellar irritation with movement. Pain with loaded knee extension. Tight hip muscles evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cycling biomechanics analysis shows patellar tracking abnormality. MRI patellofemoral cartilage appears normal. Mild patellofemoral effusion. Imaging supports functional etiology.

puzzles.explanation: Runner's knee shows chronic patellofemoral pain with significant biomechanical dysfunction and functional limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports sharp chest wall pain that worsens with breathing. The pain is located along the intercostal space between the ribs. Movement and breathing are limited by pain severity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Deep breathing exacerbates the chest wall pain significantly. Coughing and sneezing trigger sharp pain in the intercostal region. Twisting movements and reaching activities reproduce symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Strain typically follows direct trauma, coughing, or sudden twisting movement. Heavy lifting or forceful physical exertion may precipitate the injury. Previous chest wall strain increases recurrence risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, tenderness is elicited along the intercostal muscles. Reproducing pain with intercostal space compression confirms muscular origin. Restricted breathing pattern is evident with protective guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging is typically not needed as diagnosis is clinical. Ultrasound may demonstrate intercostal muscle edema or hematoma if performed. CT rules out rib fracture or pneumothorax if there is concern for more serious pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Intercostal muscle strain is acute mechanical injury from repetitive or forceful activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with Down syndrome presents with neck pain exacerbated by certain movements. Instability is present between the atlas and axis vertebrae. Myelopathic symptoms may develop if compression becomes severe.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specifically triggered by rotation and flexion of the cervical spine. Neck stiffness occurs with restricted active range of motion. The patient may demonstrate protective guarding to limit potentially harmful movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Down syndrome is the primary risk factor with ligamentous laxity predisposing to instability. Rheumatoid arthritis can also cause atlantoaxial subluxation through odontoid erosion. The condition requires monitoring for progressive neurological deficit.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The Sharp-Purser test is performed by supporting the occiput and gently translating C1 anteriorly; reproduction of neurological symptoms is concerning. Upper extremity weakness and hyperreflexia may indicate spinal cord compression. Lower limb signs include hyperreflexia and positive Babinski response.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the cervical spine demonstrates increased atlantodental interval measuring greater than 5mm. Cervical myelopathy is evident with T2 signal changes in the spinal cord. The odontoid process position and cord compression severity are critical findings.

puzzles.explanation: Atlantoaxial instability is a serious condition with risk of serious neurological or vascular compromise requiring specialist care.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports a palpable lump on the wrist or hand that may be painless. The cyst may enlarge and shrink over time. Some patients report aching pain or functional limitation depending on cyst location and size.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms may be exacerbated by wrist activity or certain movements depending on cyst location. Pressure from gripping or leaning on the hand worsens discomfort. Rest may provide relief with decreased activity-related swelling.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Ganglion cysts arise from joint capsules or tendon sheaths with no clear precipitant. They are most common on the dorsum of the wrist. Cysts may develop after wrist trauma or degeneration but often appear spontaneously.

clues.label: Examination
