clues.text: On examination, a firm, movable mass is palpable typically on the dorsum of the wrist. The cyst is transilluminated indicating fluid-filled contents. Associated joint tenderness or range of motion limitation may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates a fluid-filled cyst with no internal echoes. MRI shows homogeneous T2 hyperintense lesion consistent with fluid. Imaging confirms the cystic nature and origin from joint or tendon sheath.

puzzles.explanation: Classic ganglion cyst with painless presentation and typical dorsal location.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old female rugby player sustains direct blow to left thigh during match. Acute severe pain and rapid swelling over lateral thigh. Unable to continue playing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with thigh motion. Significant swelling with functional limitation. Difficulty walking and weight-bearing. Rapid development of ecchymosis.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-impact contact during rugby tackle to lateral thigh. Immediate pain onset with rapid swelling. No prior thigh injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant swelling and bruising visible over lateral thigh. Hip abduction painful. Knee ROM limited by thigh pain. Strength testing limited by guarding. Visible muscle deformation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows muscle hematoma with fiber disruption. Vastus lateralis and biceps femoris involvement noted. Hematoma measures 6x8 cm. No fracture or compartment syndrome signs.

puzzles.explanation: Muscle contusion shows severe response with risk of myositis ossificans and extensive hematoma formation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient in the immediate post-operative period following total hip arthroplasty reports pain at the surgical site. Hip motion is restricted by protective positioning and precautions. Functional mobility is severely limited in early post-operative phase.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weight bearing is progressed according to surgeon protocol with crutches or walker. Hip flexion is restricted to prevent prosthetic dislocation. Abduction pillows or positioning devices maintain protective alignment.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Total hip replacement was performed for advanced osteoarthritis or femoral neck fracture. Pre-operative hip pain and dysfunction significantly impaired mobility. Post-operative rehabilitation involves progressive weight bearing and motion over weeks to months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, surgical site shows appropriate wound healing without excessive drainage. Hip motion is limited per post-operative precautions. Muscle guarding is present with pain on end-range motion testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs confirm prosthetic component positioning with appropriate hip center reconstruction. Component alignment is assessed to ensure optimal biomechanics. Repeat imaging monitors for subsidence or component migration.

puzzles.explanation: Suboptimal THA outcomes with high acetabular version require modified precautions, hip flexor stretching emphasis, careful activity modification, and consideration of potential surgical revision if symptoms persist despite comprehensive rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman, 8 weeks post-rotator cuff repair (massive tear involving supraspinatus and infraspinatus). Progressing through intermediate rehabilitation phase.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Active-assisted ROM exercises beginning. Pain managed well. Gradual strength work initiation. Functional recovery progressing appropriately.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Large rotator cuff tear repair 8 weeks ago. Repair integrity confirmed on post-operative imaging. Following standard post-operative rehabilitation protocol.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive ROM improving toward normal. Active-assisted ROM initiated. Pain controlled with activity. Repair integrity confirmed on ultrasound. Scapular control improving.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-op imaging confirms healing repair. No retear on reassessment. Progressing rehabilitation with improved ROM. Strength work beginning cautiously.

puzzles.explanation: Repairs of massive or fatty infiltrated rotator cuff tears often have prolonged recovery with residual weakness. This requires extended strengthening progression, scapular stabilization emphasis, and realistic functional goal setting.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old athlete presents with severe leg pain disproportionate to clinical findings following an acute crush injury to the anterior lower leg during a contact sport. Pain is out of proportion to the apparent soft tissue injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports progressive pain with passive stretching of the foot, particularly dorsiflexion and plantarflexion, which produces severe discomfort. Active foot movements are increasingly limited. The pain is not controlled by standard analgesics and worsens over hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 4-6 hours prior to presentation from direct blunt trauma during athletic competition. No fracture was evident on initial clinical assessment. The patient reported swelling beginning soon after injury and steadily worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the anterior leg demonstrates marked swelling and tenseness of the compartment. Passive ankle plantarflexion produces severe pain. Foot sensation is diminished in a stocking distribution. Peripheral pulses remain palpable, but pale appearance is noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Compartment pressure measurement via needle manometer shows anterior compartment pressure of 65 mmHg at rest, exceeding the critical threshold of 60 mmHg. CT imaging of the leg demonstrates swollen muscles with no fracture. MRI shows edema within the anterior compartment muscles.

puzzles.explanation: Compartment syndrome presents as surgical emergency with severe pain, swelling, and positive passive stretch test requiring immediate intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports mechanical symptoms in the knee including catching and locking. Pain is localized to the joint line. Swelling develops in the knee, worse with prolonged standing or walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Twisting movements and cutting activities trigger catching sensations. Mechanical locking may occur with weight bearing requiring manipulation to unlock. Squatting and kneeling activities are particularly provocative.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tear may follow a specific injury with twisting mechanism or develop insidiously in degenerative knees. Previous meniscal pathology or knee injuries increase risk. Degenerative tears are common in middle-aged and older patients.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: McMurray test and Thessaly test reproduce the mechanical symptoms. Joint line tenderness is localized to the meniscal location. Effusion may be present with ballottement demonstrating fluid in the joint.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates meniscal tear in the medial or lateral meniscus with classification of tear pattern. T2 signal intensity within the meniscal substance indicates tear. Imaging localizes the tear and guides treatment decisions.

puzzles.explanation: Meniscal tears produce mechanical symptoms such as catching and locking due to displaced fragments.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports wrist pain with gripping or twisting motions. Pain is localized to the wrist region depending on affected tendon. Functional loss of grip strength or dexterity occurs with progression.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities requiring wrist flexion, extension, or rotation exacerbate the pain. Repetitive gripping or pinching activities are particularly provocative. Pain may improve slightly with activity warm-up but worsens as activity continues.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tendinopathy develops from repetitive wrist motion or overuse activities. Poor ergonomics during work or sports activities contribute to injury. Degenerative changes may predispose middle-aged patients to tendinopathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pain is elicited with resisted wrist movements in the plane of the affected tendon. Palpation over the tendon reproduces pain. Crepitus may be palpated with tendon movement if chronic inflammation is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound reveals thickened tendon with mixed echogenicity and increased vascularity on Doppler. MRI demonstrates signal changes within the tendon substance. Imaging confirms the presence and extent of tendon pathology.

puzzles.explanation: ECU tendinopathy from repetitive wrist extension and deviation activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports pain in the dorsal forearm with grip weakness. The pain is located in the proximal forearm rather than lateral elbow. Functional loss includes difficulty with gripping and fine motor control.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is exacerbated by gripping and twisting activities of the forearm. Resisted finger extension reproduces discomfort in the dorsal forearm. Repetitive forearm pronation and supination worsen symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Radial nerve compression occurs at the radial tunnel in the proximal forearm. Repetitive gripping activities and racquet sports predispose to the condition. Anatomical variations may contribute to nerve compression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited in the proximal dorsal forearm. Resisted finger extension with wrist extension reproduces pain. Posterior interosseous nerve motor testing may reveal weakness of finger extensors.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the forearm shows signal changes in the nerve or evidence of mass compressing the radial nerve. Ultrasound demonstrates focal nerve enlargement or compression at the radial tunnel. Nerve conduction studies may show slowing across the region of compression.

puzzles.explanation: Radial tunnel syndrome with motor predominance and preserved sensation, distinguished from lateral epicondylitis by exam findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports shoulder pain specifically at the acromioclavicular joint following direct trauma. Pain is localized to the superior aspect of the shoulder. Functional limitation occurs with overhead activities and crossing the arm over the body.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is exacerbated by adduction of the arm across the body and horizontal adduction. Overhead activities trigger discomfort at the AC joint. Push-up type activities and horizontal pressing movements reproduce pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sprain typically follows direct blow to the shoulder or fall onto the outstretched arm. The injury grades from 1-6 based on ligament disruption and clavicular displacement. Higher grade sprains demonstrate clavicular subluxation or complete dislocation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited directly over the AC joint. Crossover adduction test reproduces pain at the AC joint. The O'Brien test may also elicit AC joint pain with positive result.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate clavicular displacement and AC joint space abnormalities based on sprain grade. Weighted views show degree of clavicular elevation if AC ligament is disrupted. MRI reveals ligament injury and associated findings.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic AC joint arthropathy and instability from old high-grade separation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old woman with history of varicose veins and prolonged standing occupation presents with chronic venous leg ulcer. Recurrent ulcer with healed scar from prior episode.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Chronic leg edema with clothing marks. Pain with weight-bearing. Functional limitation in mobility and standing duration. Difficulty with wound care.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of varicose veins managed conservatively. Occupation requires prolonged standing and walking. Previous venous leg ulcer healed 2 years prior. Current ulcer recurring in same location.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Medial leg ulceration with surrounding hemosiderin staining and lipodermatosclerosis. Edema pitting on pressure. Calf circumference increased bilaterally. Venous stasis signs present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Venous duplex ultrasound demonstrates valvular incompetence in superficial and deep veins. Reflux pattern documented. Ankle-brachial index normal excluding arterial disease.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced venous ulcer disease with bilateral involvement and significant complications. Chronic venous insufficiency with poor healing despite treatment, complicated by superinfection and extensive tissue damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An infant or young child presents with head tilted toward one shoulder. Restricted rotation of the head occurs with preference to keep head in abnormal position. Facial asymmetry may develop secondary to chronic positioning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Active rotation of the head away from the affected side is limited. Passive range of motion is restricted with guarding. Infant may demonstrate difficulty tracking objects across midline in restricted direction.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Congenital torticollis results from shortening or fibrosis of the sternocleidomastoid muscle. Intrauterine positioning or birth trauma may precipitate muscle injury. The condition is present from birth or develops in early infancy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, a tight mass or rope-like sternocleidomastoid muscle is palpable on the affected side. Head tilt toward the affected side with chin rotation away is evident. Facial asymmetry may be present with flattening on the affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the sternocleidomastoid muscle demonstrates focal enlargement and echogenic appearance consistent with fibrosis. MRI may show muscle pathology and structural changes. Imaging rules out spinal or skeletal abnormalities.

puzzles.explanation: Progressive muscular torticollis from birth trauma can develop secondary facial and cranial deformities without early intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports acute posterior elbow pain and swelling following direct trauma. The elbow cannot be extended actively. Severe pain limits any arm movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Active elbow extension is severely limited or impossible. Passive extension may be restricted by swelling and severe pain. Arm positioning is guarded with elbow flexed for comfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fracture typically follows direct blow to the olecranon or fall on flexed elbow. High energy trauma results in displaced fractures. Low energy injuries may result in undisplaced fractures.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, significant swelling and ecchymosis are present over the olecranon. A palpable step-off may be felt at the fracture site. Crepitus may be elicited with movement of fracture fragments.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate olecranon fracture with classification of displacement and comminution. Fracture line is visible with relationship to the joint surface assessed. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding operative intervention.

puzzles.explanation: Olecranon fracture with loss of triceps function from direct trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset following viral prodrome typical for vestibular neuritis. No prior episodes of vertigo. Hearing preserved, ruling out audiovestibular involvement. No recent head trauma or otologic surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI inner ear structures normal excluding neuroma or structural lesion. No enhancement of CN VII or VIII. Audiometry normal. Electrography confirms vestibular asymmetry consistent with peripheral vestibulitis.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular Neuritis is acute post-viral vestibular dysfunction with sudden severe vertigo, no hearing loss, and unilateral vestibular deficits in acute phase.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient in the post-operative period following spinal fusion reports back or neck pain at surgical site. Spinal mobility is restricted per post-operative precautions. Neurological function is monitored for improvement or complications.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Bending and twisting movements are restricted per surgical protocol to protect fusion site. Weight bearing progression occurs gradually depending on construct. Bed mobility and transfer training are critical early post-operative activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Spinal fusion was performed for stenosis, spondylolisthesis, or instability. Pre-operative symptoms included pain and/or neurological deficit. Post-operative recovery involves bone fusion at surgical levels over 3-6 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, post-operative incision appears well-healed without infection signs. Spinal motion is restricted per precautions. Neurological examination monitors for nerve root recovery.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays confirm instrumentation position and alignment. Serial imaging monitors for fusion progression and hardware integrity. CT or MRI assesses neural element decompression and hardware position.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Spinal Fusion shows early post-operative period (8 weeks) with expected limited mobility, activity restriction, and early fusion integration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports progressive tremor, stiffness, and slowness of movement. Motor function gradually declines affecting gait and fine motor control. Postural instability develops with disease progression increasing fall risk.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Movement is slow with reduced amplitude (bradykinesia). Tremor is worse at rest and improves with purposeful movement. Stiffness limits range of motion and comfortable positioning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Parkinson's disease typically develops in older adults with gradual progressive course. Family history may be positive. Dopaminergic neuron loss in substantia nigra is the pathological basis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, resting tremor is evident at 4-6 Hz frequency. Rigidity is present with cogwheel quality on passive motion testing. Gait demonstrates shuffling pattern with reduced arm swing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain may show midbrain atrophy. PET imaging demonstrates reduced dopamine uptake in striatum confirming diagnosis. DaTscan shows reduced dopaminergic activity in the putamen.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced idiopathic Parkinson's disease with prominent rigidity, postural instability, freezing gait, and significant dopamine depletion.

aliases: Benign Nocturnal Limb Pains of Childhood

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A school-age child reports intermittent leg pain typically in the evening or night. Pain occurs in the lower extremities without specific localization. Symptoms occur during periods of rapid growth.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is typically worse in evening and night with resolution by morning. Activity-related pain may occur but typically pain is at rest. Symptoms may affect sleep quality.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Growing pains occur during growth spurts in children typically between ages 3-10. Symptoms are benign and self-limited. Family history of growing pains is common.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, no objective findings are present. Lower extremity range of motion is normal. Gait is normal during examination with no limp observed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: No imaging is indicated unless red flags are present. X-rays are normal without evidence of fracture or pathology. The diagnosis is clinical based on characteristic history.

puzzles.explanation: Growing pains are benign nocturnal limb pains in children during growth periods, resolving with reassurance and symptomatic treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports sudden brief episodes of severe facial pain. Pain is electric shock-like in quality affecting one side of the face. Pain episodes are unpredictable but may be triggered by touch or chewing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by specific activities such as chewing, talking, or touching the face. Light touch to the face may precipitate sudden pain episode. Eating and dental hygiene become difficult due to pain episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Trigeminal neuralgia typically presents in older adults with increased prevalence after age 50. Multiple sclerosis increases risk particularly in younger patients. Vascular compression of the trigeminal nerve root is proposed mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, facial sensation may be normal or demonstrate subtle changes. Triggering the pain by light touch to specific areas confirms diagnosis. Detailed neurological exam rules out other cranial nerve involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain and brainstem assesses for vascular loop compressing the trigeminal nerve. T1 weighted imaging demonstrates anatomical relationships. High-resolution CISS sequences show nerve-vessel interface.

puzzles.explanation: Classic trigeminal neuralgia with sudden severe lancinating pain, clear trigger areas, unilateral distribution, and MRI-confirmed vascular compression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young baseball pitcher reports medial elbow pain during throwing. Pain is exacerbated by pitching with progressive worsening over season. Functional limitation occurs with inability to throw without pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is worst during the acceleration phase of throwing. Medial elbow stress increases with throwing velocity and frequency. Rest from throwing typically relieves symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Little League elbow occurs in young pitchers typically before skeletal maturity. Excessive throwing volume and poor mechanics increase risk. Rapid growth during adolescence predisposes to apophyseal injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited over the medial epicondyle. Valgus stress testing reproduces medial-sided elbow pain. Forearm pronation and wrist flexion against resistance may reproduce symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show medial epicondyle apophysitis with fragmentation or widening of the apophysis. MRI demonstrates edema and inflammatory changes at the medial epicondyle. Advanced imaging confirms extent of injury.

puzzles.explanation: Little Leaguer's elbow is medial epicondyle apophysitis from repetitive throwing, requiring activity modification and rest.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An elderly patient reports acute severe hip pain following a fall. Weight bearing is impossible due to pain severity. The hip and thigh are swollen with visible deformity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weight bearing is severely limited or impossible. Passive and active motion is severely restricted by pain. Bed mobility is impaired with inability to turn in bed.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hip fracture typically follows a fall from standing height in elderly patients. Osteoporosis is a significant risk factor. Femoral neck fractures have higher risk of complications than intertrochanteric fractures.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the hip is often held in external rotation and flexion. Tenderness over the hip and groin is severe. Crepitus may be palpated with leg movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate femoral neck or intertrochanteric fracture with displacement. Fracture classification guides treatment decisions. CT provides additional detail if radiographs are inconclusive.

puzzles.explanation: Intertrochanteric hip fractures in very elderly with dementia have poor prognosis and require careful surgical decision-making.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A woman reports severe menstrual pain and pelvic discomfort. Pain is often worse than typical dysmenorrhea and progressively worsens. Associated symptoms include painful intercourse and bowel dysfunction.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is exacerbated during menses with peak symptoms mid-cycle. Physical activity may be limited during menses due to pain severity. Pelvic floor muscle tension is often present affecting function.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Endometriosis typically presents in reproductive-age women with peak incidence in 30s-40s. Symptom severity does not correlate with lesion extent. Infertility may be associated with endometriosis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On pelvic examination, tenderness is elicited with palpation of posterior cul-de-sac and uterine ligaments. Pelvic floor muscle tension and hypertonicity are common. Fixed uterine retroversion may be noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound shows endometriotic lesions and ovarian endometriomas. MRI demonstrates endometrial implants and adenomyosis. Diagnostic laparoscopy provides definitive diagnosis through visualization and biopsy.

puzzles.explanation: Endometriosis-related pain requires multidisciplinary management including gynecologic treatment, pelvic floor therapy for associated myofascial dysfunction, pain management strategies, and psychological support given chronic nature of condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old recreational volleyball player reports anterior knee pain for 6 weeks localized to superior patellar pole. Pain worsens with jumping and repetitive ball hitting. Swelling palpable at tendon insertion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp anterior knee pain with explosive movements (jumping, spiking ball) and eccentric loading during deceleration. Pain improved with rest and modified activity but returns with sport participation. Stiffness after activity settling within hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset correlating with increased training volume (volleyball 4x per week). Repetitive jumping and landing mechanics in sport. No acute traumatic event. History of patellar tendon soreness 2 years ago. Adequate flexibility initially but recently decreased.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over proximal patellar tendon at superior patellar pole. Quadriceps contraction against resistance elicits pain. Single-leg squat shows valgus knee position (femoral internal rotation) suggesting biomechanical dysfunction. Knee flexion 0-120 degrees pain-free but 120-140 degrees aggravating. Palpable swelling at insertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic thickening of proximal quadriceps tendon (8mm versus normal 5-6mm) with loss of fibrillar echotexture. Increased Doppler signal indicating inflammation and neovascularity. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration without full-thickness tear. T2 signal abnormality within proximal third of tendon.

puzzles.explanation: Quadriceps tendinopathy typically presents at the insertion above the patella, often activity-related.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient post-cancer treatment reports pervasive fatigue and deconditioning. Exercise capacity is markedly reduced compared to pre-treatment baseline. Functional limitations impact ability to return to work and normal activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Submaximal exertion rapidly produces dyspnea and fatigue limiting exercise duration. Tasks requiring sustained effort are poorly tolerated. Cardiac dysrhythmias or dyspnea may occur during activity requiring cardiac monitoring.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History includes recent completion of chemotherapy, radiation, or surgical cancer treatment. The timing and intensity of cancer therapy contributed to deconditioning. Prior fitness level before diagnosis should be documented for rehabilitation goals.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On physical examination, generalized weakness is evident with reduced muscle strength. Cardiovascular deconditioning is present with elevated heart rate response to minimal activity. Pulmonary function may be compromised secondary to chemotherapy toxicity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: The 6-minute walk test demonstrates reduced exercise capacity with distance significantly below predicted values. Cardiopulmonary exercise testing reveals reduced VO2 max and abnormal cardiorespiratory response. ECOG performance status scores document baseline functional limitations requiring rehabilitation intervention.

puzzles.explanation: Cancer-related fatigue requires structured exercise program, psychological support for anxiety and depression, symptom management including sleep optimization, energy conservation strategies, and gradual return-to-work planning with occupational rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports pain in the midfoot specifically at the base of the fifth metatarsal. Pain onset may be sudden with specific inciting activity. Functional limitation occurs with weight bearing and running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is exacerbated by running and weight bearing activities. Sudden twisting or push-off movements reproduce pain. Pain worsens with continued activity on fractured bone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Jones fracture occurs at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal. Repetitive stress or acute inversion injury during running is the typical mechanism. The location has poor blood supply increasing healing difficulty.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is specifically localized at the base of the fifth metatarsal. Swelling may be present in the lateral midfoot. Pain occurs with weight bearing and resisted plantar flexion of the foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate transverse fracture at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal. CT imaging assesses fracture displacement and healing. Advanced imaging guides timing of weight bearing progression.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent or non-union Jones fractures require careful management due to poor healing potential.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports anterior chest and shoulder pain located in the pectoralis minor region. Pain is exacerbated with arm elevation and reaching activities. Functional limitation affects overhead and pressing movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead activities and horizontal adduction movements reproduce symptoms. Push-up type activities and throwing motions exacerbate pain. Pain improves with rest and reduced upper extremity activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Strain develops from overuse of the muscle in overhead athletes or following direct trauma. Tight pectoralis minor muscles contribute to anterior shoulder pain. History of shoulder pathology may predispose to strain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited in the pectoralis minor muscle in the anterior axilla. Resisted scapular protraction reproduces pain. Palpable tension or spasm may be noted in the muscle.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound may demonstrate muscle edema or strain pattern in the pectoralis minor. MRI shows signal changes within the muscle consistent with strain. Imaging confirms muscle pathology if clinical diagnosis is uncertain.

puzzles.explanation: Pectoralis minor strain from repetitive or forceful activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease presents with a 5-day history of productive cough with purulent sputum, fever, and dyspnea. The patient reports worsening respiratory symptoms with difficulty sleeping and fatigue.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient experiences significant dyspnea with minimal exertion, such as walking to the bathroom or standing briefly. Activity tolerance has declined markedly over the past 2 days. Fatigue is profound, limiting functional mobility and self-care activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient initially had an upper respiratory infection 1 week ago that progressively worsened. Smoking history of 40 pack-years and underlying COPD are significant risk factors. Recent hospitalization 2 months ago for COPD exacerbation. Lives in a crowded home environment.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, respiratory rate is 28 breaths per minute with oxygen saturation 88% on room air. Auscultation reveals consolidative crackles and bronchial breath sounds in the right lower lobe. Fremitus is increased over affected area. Vital signs show fever of 38.5°C and tachycardia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates a segmental consolidation in the right lower lobe with air bronchograms, consistent with bacterial pneumonia. Blood cultures are positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae. Sputum culture confirms the same organism.

puzzles.explanation: Typical community-acquired pneumonia with acute onset, focal findings, and imaging confirmation of infiltrate. Likely bacterial etiology given clinical presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A worker exposed to vibrating tools reports episodic finger blanching and color changes. Fingers become white then red and painful during rewarming. Sensory loss and numbness accompany the color changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are triggered by exposure to vibrating equipment and cold exposure. Finger blanching occurs during or shortly after tool use. Rewarming causes pain and restoration of normal color and sensation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Vibration white finger develops from chronic exposure to vibrating power tools. Jackhammers, chainsaws, and pneumatic tools are common culprits. Duration and intensity of tool exposure correlate with symptom severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination during an episode, finger blanching is visible affecting primarily the peripheral digits. Numb and cold sensation is present in affected fingers. After rewarming, erythema and pain occur with return of sensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nailfold capillaroscopy demonstrates abnormal capillary patterns characteristic of vascular dysfunction. Thermography shows abnormal temperature distribution in affected fingers. Doppler ultrasound assesses digital artery flow and perfusion.

puzzles.explanation: Vibration white finger shows hand-arm vibration syndrome with Raynaud-like episodes and sensory dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old woman presents with recent onset blurred vision in right eye and progressive leg weakness over 4 weeks. Recent viral illness-like symptoms 2 months ago. Fatigue and cognitive fog affecting work performance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vision loss in right eye with pain on eye movement (optic neuritis). Progressive weakness in both legs affecting gait and stair climbing. Difficulty with balance and coordination. Bladder urgency and frequency present. Numbness in feet following stocking-glove distribution.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Relapsing symptom pattern with previous episodes of unexplained transient neurological symptoms. Recent upper respiratory infection. No significant prior medical history. Family history of autoimmune disease. Symptoms wax and wane suggesting multiple lesions at different times.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Eye examination shows reduced visual acuity 20/40 right eye with afferent pupillary defect. Visual field testing shows central scotoma on right. Lower extremity strength 4/5 with increased tone and hyperreflexia. Positive Babinski signs bilaterally. Gait shows spasticity and ataxia. Finger-to-nose dysmetria present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain shows multiple periventricular T2/FLAIR hyperintensities consistent with demyelination. Spinal cord MRI reveals T2 hyperintense lesion at C4-5 level. Gadolinium enhancement noted in multiple lesions indicating acute inflammation. CSF analysis shows oligoclonal bands positive. Visual evoked potentials show prolonged latency in right eye (optic nerve demyelination).

puzzles.explanation: Relapsing-remitting MS with optic neuritis history, progressive motor symptoms, and characteristic demyelinating lesions on MRI with positive CSF markers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports sudden swelling and pain in the lower extremity. The leg is warm and edematous with possible pitting edema. Pain is constant and worse with weight bearing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weight bearing exacerbates leg pain and swelling. Foot elevation and rest provide some symptom relief. Leg mobility is limited by pain and swelling.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Risk factors include recent surgery, immobilization, or prolonged travel. Malignancy, hypercoagulable states, and previous DVT increase risk. Oral contraceptive use and pregnancy are important risk factors in women.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, unilateral leg edema is present with increased calf circumference compared to opposite side. Homan's sign and calf pain with dorsiflexion may be present. Skin changes including warmth and erythema may be evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Venous duplex ultrasound demonstrates absence of venous compressibility and thrombus visualization. Compression ultrasound shows no flow in the involved vein. CT or MR venography confirms diagnosis and extent of thrombosis.

puzzles.explanation: DVT in setting of active malignancy with high thrombotic risk. Bilateral presentation and central catheter both increase risk. Cancer-induced hypercoagulability is significant thrombogenic factor.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports pain in the nipple area occurring during or after running. Chafing from clothing causes irritation and bleeding. The nipple becomes tender with possible callus formation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running activity with friction from chest wall clothing. Distance running worsens symptoms with longer running duration. Pain improves with rest and adequate wound care.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Runner's nipple occurs from repetitive friction between clothing and chest wall. Male runners are more commonly affected than females. Increased running volume and intensity increase symptom risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, nipple irritation, scabbing, or bleeding is visible. Callus formation and thickening of the areola may be present. Palpation elicits tenderness at the affected nipple.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging is not typically needed for diagnosis as clinical examination is sufficient. Ultrasound may identify callus formation or skin thickening if complications are suspected. The diagnosis is clinical based on history and examination.

puzzles.explanation: Runner's nipple shows severe chafing with secondary infection from inadequate protection in endurance event.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old ballet dancer presents with posterior ankle pain with plantarflexion and inversion activities. Pain localized to posterior ankle compartment.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant pain with pointe work and plantarflexion maneuvers. Functional limitation in dance training. Unable to continue high-level dance activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive ballet training with excessive plantarflexion demands. Gradual progressive symptoms over 6 weeks with increased training intensity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pain reproduced with passive plantarflexion and inversion (Hawkins test). Palpable tenderness in posterior ankle compartment behind lateral malleolus. Pain with resisted plantarflexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ankle MRI shows impingement of posterior structures (flexor hallucis longus and soft tissues) with plantarflexion. Possible posterior talus talar chondral lesion. Achilles intact.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior ankle impingement results from os trigonum or posterior process causing symptoms with plantarflexion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports pain radiating from the low back down one leg. Numbness and tingling accompany the pain in a dermatomal distribution. Functional limitation occurs with bending and walking activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Forward bending and sitting exacerbate the radiating pain. Walking may cause symptom progression with increased nerve root tension. Lying supine with hips and knees flexed provides pain relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Radiculopathy may follow a specific lifting injury or develop insidiously from degenerative disc disease. Disc herniation is the most common mechanical cause. Spinal stenosis and spondylolisthesis contribute in older patients.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, a positive straight leg raise test reproduces the radiating pain below the knee. Sensory testing demonstrates dermatomal sensory loss. Motor testing reveals weakness in muscles innervated by the affected nerve root.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine demonstrates disc herniation with nerve root compression. The specific nerve root level and side are identified. Imaging correlates with clinical examination findings and symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar radiculopathy is atypical with confounding factors, inconsistent examination findings, and unclear pathoanatomical basis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports anterior ankle pain with plantarflexion and inversion movements. Pain is localized to the anterior ankle. Functional limitation occurs with movements that pinch structures in the anterior ankle.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Plantarflexion and inversion movements reproduce anterior ankle pain. Descending stairs and uneven surfaces exacerbate symptoms. Activities requiring ankle mobility worsen discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Impingement syndrome develops from repetitive ankle plantarflexion inversion. Previous ankle sprain or trauma may initiate the process. Osteophyte formation or soft tissue hypertrophy narrows the anterior ankle space.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, anterior ankle tenderness is elicited between the talus and tibia. Plantarflexion and inversion loading reproduces pain. Swelling may be present in the anterior ankle region.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show anterior talar osteophytes causing impingement. MRI demonstrates soft tissue impingement with inflammatory changes. Ultrasound visualizes synovitis and soft tissue involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic anterior ankle impingement may develop from cumulative osteophyte formation and synovitis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports weakness in hand grip and pinch strength with progressive loss of hand function. Pain or paresthesias may occur in the hypothenar region and medial hand. Functional impairment affects fine motor control and gripping.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Gripping and pinching activities are limited by weakness. Sustained hand use worsens symptoms and weakness. Pain may increase with repetitive hand activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Guyon's canal syndrome results from compression of the ulnar nerve in the canal at the wrist. Ganglion cysts, lipomas, or mass lesions compress the nerve. Occupational activities involving hand vibration or compression increase risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, hypothenar muscle weakness and atrophy are evident. Froment sign is positive indicating thumb interphalangeal flexion with key pinch. Sensation is intact in the medial palm as the dorsal sensory branch is spared.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the wrist demonstrates mass lesion or cyst compressing the ulnar nerve in Guyon's canal. Ultrasound visualizes nerve compression and causative lesion. Nerve conduction studies confirm ulnar nerve motor involvement distal to the wrist.

puzzles.explanation: Guyon's canal syndrome from chronic handlebar pressure with motor predominance and preserved sensation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports midfoot pain with progressive flat foot deformity. Pain is localized behind and below the medial ankle. Functional limitation occurs with prolonged standing and walking activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged standing and walking exacerbate midfoot pain and deformity progression. Running and jumping activities are poorly tolerated. Swelling behind the medial ankle worsens with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Stage 2 dysfunction involves progressive tendon damage with starting of rigid deformity. Painful dysfunction occurs with loss of arch height. The tibialis posterior tendon is increasingly incompetent to support the arch.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visible flatfoot deformity is evident with loss of medial arch height. Medial ankle tenderness and swelling are present. The single leg heel rise test demonstrates weakness or inability to rise on the medial forefoot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates thickened and abnormal tibialis posterior tendon. Tendon signal changes indicate inflammation and partial tears. The degree of arch collapse and talonavicular subluxation is assessed on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Stage 2 PTTD involves architectural foot deformity with functional decline, requiring more aggressive intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports back pain localized to the facet joint region with radiation into the buttock or upper thigh. Pain is worse with extension and rotation movements. Functional limitation occurs with standing and walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Extension and rotation movements exacerbate facet joint pain. Standing and walking activities worsen symptoms. Forward bending and sitting provide pain relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Facet joint syndrome develops from osteoarthritic changes or facet joint inflammation. Repetitive extension loading contributes to degeneration. Aging and degenerative disc disease predispose to facet joint pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited over the facet joints. Extension and ipsilateral rotation movements reproduce pain at the facet region. Palpable tenderness localizes to the affected segment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates facet joint hypertrophy and osteoarthritic changes. Signal changes indicate inflammation within the joint. Degree of facet-mediated stenosis is assessed if neurological symptoms are present.

puzzles.explanation: Facet joint syndrome is the classic mechanical pattern with extension provocation and localized findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports systemic symptoms including fever, malaise, and fatigue. Joint and muscle pain are accompanied by skin lesions and rash. Multi-organ involvement may be apparent with renal or pulmonary symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Constitutional symptoms limit activity tolerance with generalized weakness and fatigue. Joint pain limits mobility and exercise capacity. Systemic inflammation affects overall functional status.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Vasculitis may be primary or secondary to underlying autoimmune disease. Infection can trigger vasculitic inflammation. Recent upper respiratory infection or sinusitis may precede symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpable purpura is visible particularly on lower extremities. Joint swelling and tenderness accompany the rash. Systemic examination reveals findings consistent with multi-system inflammation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Laboratory findings including elevated ESR and CRP indicate systemic inflammation. Antigen testing for ANCA (ANCA-associated vasculitis) or ANA may be positive. Imaging and tissue biopsy confirm the diagnosis and extent of organ involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Vasculitis requires diagnosis of specific type, immunosuppressive therapy initiation, monitoring for systemic organ involvement particularly renal, and rehabilitation focused on functional restoration while managing acute inflammatory phase and preventing complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports deep groin pain with activities involving hip flexion and internal rotation. Mechanical symptoms including catching or clicking may occur. Functional limitation occurs with running and pivoting activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hip flexion combined with internal rotation activities reproduce groin pain. Running, cutting, and pivoting sports movements are limited. Pain may worsen with prolonged sitting or standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Labral tear may follow a specific trauma or develop insidiously from repetitive microtrauma. Hip impingement and femoroacetabular impingement increase tear risk. Athletes in rotation-based sports have increased incidence.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the FABER test and anterior impingement test may reproduce symptoms. Internal rotation of the hip in flexion triggers pain. Hip flexibility deficits are often noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI arthrography demonstrates labral tear with visualization of contrast leaking through the tear. MRI shows the tear morphology and associated findings. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding surgical intervention.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic labral pathology from overuse may present with subtle signs and requires provocation testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old squash player presents with radial wrist pain affecting athletic performance. Pain on thumb side of wrist with gripping racquet. Symptoms over 3 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with gripping and racquet handling in sport. Pain worsens with forceful thumb pinch and wrist deviation. Functional limitation in sport and work activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in squash training frequency with 4 matches per week. Repetitive gripping and dorsiflexion movements during sport.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Radial-sided wrist pain reproduced with Finkelstein test. Thumb abduction strength testing painful. Swelling visible over radial styloid region. Pain with thumb opposition against resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates first dorsal compartment tenosynovitis with fluid around APL and EPB tendons. Tendons thickened with hypoechoic appearance. No subluxation evident.

puzzles.explanation: De Quervain's from postpartum repetitive strain in new parent, classic demographic and activity trigger.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports neck pain with radiating arm pain in a dermatomal distribution. Numbness and tingling occur in the hand and fingers. Functional limitation affects arm use and fine motor control.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Neck extension and lateral bending away from the lesion exacerbate radiating pain. Overhead activities worsen symptoms with increased nerve root tension. Rest and cervical support provide pain relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Radiculopathy develops from cervical disc herniation or foraminal stenosis. Repetitive neck extension activities may precipitate symptoms. Degenerative changes increase risk in middle-aged and older patients.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, upper limb strength testing reveals dermatomal weakness corresponding to nerve root involved. Sensory testing demonstrates dermatomal sensory loss. Spurling test reproduces the radiating arm pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the cervical spine demonstrates disc herniation with nerve root compression. Foraminal stenosis and nerve root involvement are assessed. Imaging correlates specific level and side of compression with clinical findings.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical radiculopathy occurs in an occupational context with clear mechanical aggravation and positive neural tension findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old woman presents with symmetrical muscle pain and stiffness in shoulders and hips. Pain and morning stiffness worse upon waking, gradually improving with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation with shoulder and hip mobility. Difficulty getting out of bed, dressing, and lifting arms. Morning stiffness lasting 1+ hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden onset 2 weeks ago with symmetrical pattern. No prior rheumatologic history. Associated low-grade fever and malaise. Rapid functional decline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Painful shoulder abduction and hip flexion. Generalized muscle tenderness in shoulders, buttocks. Morning stiffness evident. Temporal artery firm and slightly tender on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR 92 mm/hr, CRP 8.5 mg/dL). Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP negative. Rapid response to low-dose corticosteroids confirms diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Polymyalgia Rheumatica is the typical presentation in an elderly female with acute symmetric proximal muscle pain, morning stiffness, and markedly elevated inflammatory markers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports bilateral anterior shin pain that occurs predictably during running activity. Pain develops after consistent running distance and resolves completely with rest. The condition recurs with return to running at similar intensity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducible and occurs during running or intense lower leg activity. The pain is bilateral and symmetric in the anterior tibial compartments. Complete pain relief occurs within 15-30 minutes of ceasing activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset correlates with increased running volume or intensity. The athlete may have history of shin splints or tibial stress. Repetitive high-impact activities such as running on hard surfaces exacerbate symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pre-exercise examination may be normal with no resting compartment tenderness. Post-exercise examination reveals tight and tender anterior compartments. Compartment pressure measurement at rest is normal but elevated post-exercise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Compartment pressure measurement obtained immediately post-exercise (within 1-5 minutes) demonstrates elevated pressures in anterior compartment. Post-exercise pressure exceeds 30 mmHg with slow return to normal. Serial measurements during standardized exercise protocol establish diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Confirmed chronic exertional compartment syndrome may require fasciotomy when conservative treatment fails.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 72-year-old bedridden man hospitalized post-stroke with limited mobility presents with non-blanchable erythema over sacrum. Skin integrity intact but inflammation present.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Immobility from neurological deficit limits pressure relief. Risk of progression without intervention. Area of erythema approximately 5 cm diameter.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Post-stroke with right-sided hemiplegia limiting mobility and repositioning ability. Hospitalized for 2 weeks. Limited ability to change positions independently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Non-blanchable erythema over sacral prominence. Skin warm and edematous. Surrounding skin normal. No skin breakdown or ulceration. Area of redness approximately 4x5 cm.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Clinical staging confirms Stage 1 (skin intact with non-blanchable erythema). Sacral area at high pressure risk. Protective measures and frequent repositioning initiated.

puzzles.explanation: Stage 1 pressure injury shows intact skin with non-blanching erythema requiring pressure relief and preventive measures to avoid progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young female presents with joint pain and swelling in multiple joints. Generalized fatigue and malaise are prominent features. Multi-system involvement is apparent with constitutional symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Joint pain is aggravated by activity with morning stiffness lasting greater than one hour. Fatigue is disproportionate to activity level and limits exercise tolerance. Photosensitivity causes skin rash exacerbation with sun exposure.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young female gender is the primary demographic risk factor. Symptoms wax and wane with periods of flare and remission. Medication history may include oral contraceptives which can trigger or exacerbate SLE.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the characteristic malar or butterfly rash is visible across the cheeks and nose. Symmetric polyarthritis is evident with swelling of multiple joints. Oral ulcers may be present on palate or buccal mucosa.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serology testing reveals positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) with high titers. Anti-double stranded DNA antibody and anti-Smith antibody are positive indicating specific SLE diagnosis. Complement levels (C3, C4) are low reflecting disease activity.

puzzles.explanation: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus shows severe organ involvement with polyserositis and renal disease in an atypical male patient with prominent systemic manifestations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A worker reports upper extremity pain and dysfunction related to repetitive job tasks. Pain affects the shoulder, arm, or hand depending on workstation demands. Functional limitation restricts job performance and productivity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are exacerbated by the specific repetitive activity required by job demands. Sustained postures and forceful gripping increase pain. Rest breaks and activity modification provide symptom relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Work-related upper limb disorder develops from repetitive overuse in occupational settings. Prolonged computer use, assembly line work, or manual labor are common causes. Poor ergonomics and inadequate breaks contribute to development.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, regional pain and tenderness are found in muscles or tendons involved. Functional testing reveals pain with specific work-related movements. Range of motion may be limited depending on tissues involved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI demonstrates tendon thickening and inflammation at sites of repetitive strain. Imaging identifies specific tissue pathology. Nerve conduction studies rule out compression neuropathy if symptoms suggest neural involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Work-related upper limb disorder shows multi-site nerve compression from years of assembly line work.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports acute knee pain with severe swelling following high-energy trauma. Weight bearing is severely limited by pain. The knee is unstable with possible ligamentous injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weight bearing is severely limited or impossible. Active knee motion is restricted by pain and instability. Passive motion testing is limited by swelling and guarding.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tibial plateau fracture typically follows high-energy trauma such as motor vehicle accident or fall from height. Associated ligamentous and meniscal injuries are common. The fracture may be compound with skin and soft tissue damage.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, severe knee swelling and ecchymosis are present. Effusion is large with ballottement demonstrating fluid. Ligamentous testing suggests instability with positive anterior drawer or Lachman test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate tibial plateau fracture with depression and comminution severity. CT imaging shows three-dimensional fracture anatomy guiding surgical planning. Associated ligamentous and meniscal injuries are identified.

puzzles.explanation: Tibial plateau fractures are complex injuries with associated soft tissue damage requiring careful management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with Marfan syndrome reports progressive connective tissue manifestations affecting multiple systems. Musculoskeletal complaints include skeletal deformities and joint hypermobility. Systemic symptoms may include cardiac and ocular concerns.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Skeletal deformities limit normal movement patterns and functional activities. Hypermobility in joints may cause instability and pain with certain movements. Activity restriction is often necessary for cardiac safety.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Marfan syndrome is an autosomal dominant connective tissue disorder affecting fibrillin-1. Positive family history is often present. Diagnosis requires systemic features affecting skeletal, ocular, and cardiovascular systems.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, skeletal features include tall stature, arachnodactyly, and chest wall deformities. Joint hypermobility is evident with increased range of motion. Lens dislocation and cardiac murmur may be detected.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Genetic testing confirms FBN1 gene mutation characteristic of Marfan syndrome. Echocardiography assesses aortic root dilation and cardiac valve abnormalities. Skeletal imaging documents chest wall deformities and spinal involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Marfan syndrome is a genetic fibrillin disorder with serious cardiovascular implications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports chronic productive cough with copious sputum production. Recurrent pulmonary infections occur with fever and systemic symptoms. Progressive dyspnea develops with disease progression.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Exercise tolerance is limited by dyspnea and fatigue. Activity exertion triggers coughing episodes with sputum production. Airway clearance requires frequent positioning and mobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Bronchiectasis develops from chronic infection, congenital abnormality, or immunodeficiency. Previous severe pneumonia may lead to bronchiectasis. Progressive airway dilation occurs with chronic infection and inflammation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On auscultation, coarse crackles are heard predominantly at lung bases. Finger clubbing may be evident with chronic hypoxemia. Cough is productive with purulent sputum.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates bronchial dilation with bronchus to artery ratio greater than 1. Bronchial wall thickening and traction bronchiectasis are visible. Extent and distribution of bronchiectasis are documented.

puzzles.explanation: Post-TB bronchiectasis presentation. TB is a leading cause of secondary bronchiectasis, particularly in upper lobes. Chronic inflammation and airway destruction persist long after infection control.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports chronic knee pain with morning stiffness that improves with activity. Pain worsens with prolonged standing and walking. Functional limitation occurs with stairs and uneven ground.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged walking and standing exacerbate knee pain with increased swelling. Stairs, particularly descending, trigger pain. Activity modification and rest provide symptom relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Knee osteoarthritis develops gradually with progressive cartilage loss. Previous knee injury or meniscal pathology increases risk. Obesity and repetitive impact activities accelerate degeneration.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, knee effusion may be present with positive ballottement test. Crepitus is palpated with knee motion. Range of motion is limited with pain at end-range flexion and extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis. Imaging severity correlates with symptom severity. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding conservative versus surgical management.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced osteoarthritis produces significant structural changes and substantial functional impairment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient post-breast cancer surgery reports arm swelling and heaviness. The limb is grossly enlarged with skin thickening. Functional limitation affects arm use and daily activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Arm elevation and compression provide temporary swelling relief. Upper extremity exercise must be carefully progressed to avoid worsening edema. Repetitive gripping and heavy lifting are restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lymphedema develops from lymph node removal or radiation during breast cancer treatment. Onset may be immediate or develop years post-treatment. Progressive swelling occurs without appropriate management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, significant limb volume difference is evident compared to opposite side. Stemmer sign is positive with skin thickening at base of toes/fingers. Pitting edema may be present in early stages progressing to non-pitting in chronic stages.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Limb circumference measurements document lymphedema severity. Bioimpedance spectroscopy quantifies fluid accumulation. Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates lymphatic system dysfunction and obstruction.

puzzles.explanation: Breast cancer lymphedema requires compression therapy, manual lymphatic drainage, exercise and activity modification, patient education on precautions, and monitoring for progression or complications including infection risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman presents with history of 2 falls in past month from standing to ground level. Reports dizziness before falls. Loss of confidence with walking affecting mobility and independence.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unsteady gait with reduced walking speed (0.5 m/sec). Balance impaired requiring contact guard for safety. Reports lightheadedness with position changes. Fear of falling evident with cautious movement patterns and activity avoidance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 82 with multiple fall risk factors: osteoporosis, hypertension on multiple medications causing orthostatic hypotension, mild cognitive impairment, vision reduced from cataracts. Lives alone in cluttered home environment. History of vitamin D insufficiency.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Timed up and go test 28 seconds (abnormal >12 seconds). Single leg stance 3 seconds each leg (abnormal <10 seconds). Berg Balance Scale score 38/56 (high fall risk <45). Orthostatic vital signs positive (systolic BP drop 20 mmHg on standing). Gait assessment shows reduced stride length, shuffle pattern, and reduced arm swing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bone density DEXA scan shows T-score -2.9 (osteoporosis). Brain MRI normal excluding stroke or structural lesion. Vestibular testing normal excluding balance organ pathology. Home safety assessment identifies multiple environmental hazards including throw rugs and poor lighting. Medication review identifies orthostatic-inducing antihypertensives.

puzzles.explanation: Acute falls from specific medical causes like spinal stenosis or cardiac events differ from multifactorial falls requiring targeted intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old distance runner presents with medial knee pain located just below the joint line, accompanied by mild swelling in the same region. Pain is reproducible with weight-bearing activities and has developed over the past six weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running, particularly on cambered surfaces or hills, triggers sharp anteromedial knee pain within 10-15 minutes. Walking on level ground is minimally symptomatic. Pain relief occurs with ice, elevation, and anti-inflammatory medications.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient increased training volume by 40% over the past two months in preparation for a marathon. They recently switched running shoes and report tightness in the hamstring muscles bilaterally. No prior knee pathology or previous running-related injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness over the pes anserine insertion with palpable swelling. Noble test is negative. Single-leg squat triggers medial knee discomfort. Hamstring tightness confirmed with straight-leg raise limited to 60 degrees bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Knee MRI reveals fluid collection within the pes anserine bursa (12mm in transverse diameter) with surrounding soft tissue edema. No meniscal tears, ligamentous injuries, or articular cartilage damage identified. Tibia and femur intact.

puzzles.explanation: Pes anserine bursitis in overweight patients results from biomechanical stress and hip weakness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient in the post-operative period following carpal tunnel release reports surgical site pain and swelling. Hand symptoms begin to improve as compression is relieved. Scar tenderness and sensory changes may be present.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hand function improves gradually post-operatively as swelling decreases. Grip strength recovery is progressive over weeks to months. Fine motor activities are gradually reintroduced as healing progresses.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Carpal tunnel release was performed to decompress the median nerve. Pre-operative symptoms included numbness, tingling, and weakness. Post-operative recovery involves scar healing and nerve regeneration.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, incision appears healed without significant infection signs. Swelling gradually decreases post-operatively. Hand strength testing reveals progressive improvement in motor function.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI may assess carpal tunnel decompression success and rule out recurrent compression. Nerve conduction studies show improving median nerve function post-operatively. Imaging documents healing of surgical site and adjacent structures.

puzzles.explanation: Severe or chronic carpal tunnel cases with significant preoperative nerve damage often have prolonged recovery requiring intensive scar mobilization, sensory reeducation, extended strengthening program, and realistic expectations for incomplete neurologic recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An amputee reports pain in the absent limb that feels as if the limb is still present. Pain quality varies from cramping to burning to stabbing sensations. Pain persists despite the absence of the limb creating distressing symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain may be triggered or exacerbated by emotional stress or changes in weather. Residual limb activity and prosthetic use may affect phantom pain intensity. Pain may be worse at night or during periods of inactivity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Phantom limb pain develops post-amputation from altered sensory input to the brain. Pre-operative pain in the limb increases risk of phantom pain post-amputation. The pain typically decreases over time but may persist chronically.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, physical findings are limited to the residual limb with no pathology in the missing limb. Stump pain must be distinguished from phantom pain. Assessment includes trigger identification and pain quality description.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Neuroimaging studies have shown abnormal cortical reorganization in individuals with phantom limb pain. fMRI demonstrates altered sensory cortex representation. PET imaging shows metabolic changes in pain processing regions.

puzzles.explanation: Phantom limb pain presents with typical onset early after amputation, burning quality, and cortical reorganization evident on imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports progressive finger deformity with PIP joint flexion and DIP joint extension. The finger assumes a characteristic button-hole appearance. Functional limitation occurs with gripping and fine motor control.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities requiring finger extension are limited by the fixed flexion posture. Gripping strength is compromised by abnormal finger position. Fine pinch and precision activities are functionally limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Boutonniere deformity develops from disruption of the central slip of the extensor tendon mechanism. Traumatic injury from laceration or blunt trauma causes the initial injury. Progressive deformity occurs if the deformity is not corrected early.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the PIP joint is fixed in flexion with DIP joint hyperextended. Central slip function is lost with inability to extend the PIP joint. Swan neck deformity develops if untreated with progressive loss of finger function.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays show joint positioning and may reveal underlying fracture. MRI demonstrates tendon injury and severity of extensor mechanism disruption. Imaging guides surgical planning for correction of the deformity.

puzzles.explanation: Acute boutonniere deformity from crush injury with central slip extensor disruption.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A swimmer reports shoulder pain during and after training sessions. Pain is located anteriorly in the shoulder. Functional limitation occurs with overhead swimming strokes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is exacerbated by freestyle and butterfly strokes with repetitive shoulder motion. Training volume increases pain with excessive training load. Rest and activity modification provide relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Swimmer's shoulder develops from repetitive overhead swimming motion and muscle imbalance. Rapid increase in training volume precipitates symptoms. Weakness in rotator cuff and scapular muscles contributes to pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, shoulder weakness is evident particularly with external rotation. Scapular dyskinesis is noted with altered scapulohumeral rhythm. Positive impingement signs are elicited on examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates rotator cuff tendinopathy with thickening and signal changes. MRI shows supraspinatus tendon involvement and inflammatory changes. Imaging correlates with clinical examination findings.

puzzles.explanation: Swimmer's shoulder shows chronic impingement with rotator cuff pathology from swimming demands.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports sudden acute joint pain, swelling, and erythema. The big toe is most commonly affected (podagra). Pain is severe limiting weight bearing and mobility.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weight bearing on the affected joint exacerbates pain significantly. Any movement of the inflamed joint triggers severe discomfort. Rest and elevation provide some relief during acute flare.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gout develops from monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints from hyperuricemia. Male gender, alcohol consumption, and rich diet increase risk. Chronic kidney disease and diuretic use contribute to elevated uric acid.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, joint erythema and swelling are marked with warmth to palpation. The joint is extremely tender with severe pain on minimal motion. Systemic signs including fever may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serum uric acid level is elevated confirming hyperuricemia. Joint aspiration demonstrates monosodium urate crystals confirming diagnosis. Radiographs may show tophaceous changes in chronic gout.

puzzles.explanation: Gout is the classic acute monoarticular presentation of the first metatarsophalangeal joint with sudden severe onset, typically nocturnal, following dietary/alcohol triggers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports mild foot pain behind and below the medial ankle. Arch support is maintained but pain increases with activity. Functional limitation is minimal with mostly pain with prolonged standing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged walking and standing increase mild arch pain. Running activities may exacerbate symptoms minimally. Pain improves significantly with rest and arch support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Stage 1 dysfunction involves early tibialis posterior tendon inflammation and overuse. The arch structure remains largely intact but pain develops. Progressive dysfunction occurs if activity is not modified appropriately.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, mild tenderness is present behind the medial ankle. Single leg heel rise is possible but may produce mild discomfort. Arch height is maintained with intact support mechanism.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates tibialis posterior tendon inflammation with mild thickening. Signal changes within the tendon indicate inflammation. Arch alignment remains normal without structural changes at this stage.

puzzles.explanation: Early PTTD can be managed conservatively with proper arch support and strengthening.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old presents with persistent pain six months following a work-related low back strain, despite imaging showing only minor disc bulge. The pain intensity has remained moderate despite appropriate tissue healing timelines. Differential diagnosis includes organic pain generators versus pain-related disability syndromes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient avoids bending, lifting, and prolonged sitting due to fear that these movements will cause further injury or re-injury. Family members report increasing avoidance behaviours even for light activities. Pain catastrophizing beliefs have progressively limited functional participation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Significant anxiety history predates the initial injury by several years. The injury occurred in a high-stress work environment with ongoing job dissatisfaction. Previous pain episodes resolved fully with activity resumption.

clues.label: Examination
