clues.text: Physical examination reveals pain responses disproportionate to tissue pathology, with excessive guarding during movement screening. The patient demonstrates inconsistent strength testing and difficulty tolerating repeated movements. Psychometric screening using the Tampa Scale of Kinesiophobia yields elevated fear-avoidance scores.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows resolved inflammatory changes from the initial injury with normal neural structures. Imaging findings are inconsistent with the degree of reported disability and functional limitation. Diagnosis is supported by the combination of disproportionate fear, avoidance behaviours, and Tampa Scale elevation.

puzzles.explanation: Kinesiophobia shows fear-avoidance behavior with pain catastrophizing beliefs limiting function despite tissue healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 10-year-old competitive soccer player presents with heel pain that worsens during and after training sessions. Pain is localized to the posterior heel with occasional radiation to the plantar surface. Growth plate pathology should be considered in this age group.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is significantly aggravated by running, jumping, and change-of-direction activities. Rest and ice provide temporary relief lasting several hours. Morning stiffness in the heel is particularly notable after rest periods.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient is in a rapid growth phase with a recent height increase noted over the past 3-4 months. High-volume training and competitive pressure have increased in the past 6 weeks. No significant trauma or fall is reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Midline calcaneal tenderness is elicited with direct palpation and medial-lateral compression of the calcaneus reproduces pain. Ankle dorsiflexion is limited, and gastrocnemius tightness is evident on assessment. The heel appears mildly swollen relative to the contralateral side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show fragmentation and sclerosis of the calcaneal apophysis consistent with apophysitis. No frank fracture or epiphyseal displacement is present. Imaging findings, combined with age, activity level, and clinical presentation, confirm the diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Persistent Sever's disease may require more intensive management of calf flexibility and activity progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old postmenopausal woman with 15-year history of estrogen deficiency presents for bone health screening. Reports progressive height loss of 2 inches and chronic thoracic spine pain. Recent fall with wrist fracture prompted evaluation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Wrist pain from recent fracture with minimal trauma. Thoracic spine pain described as dull and constant, worse with prolonged sitting and forward bending. Increasing kyphosis limiting functional activities. Fracture from ground-level fall concerning for fragility.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Menopause at age 57 without hormone replacement. Long-standing low calcium and vitamin D intake. Sedentary lifestyle with minimal weight-bearing exercise. Family history of osteoporosis in mother. Limited sunlight exposure and dietary restrictions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Height measurement reveals 2-inch loss compared to previous records. Thoracic kyphosis pronounced on physical examination. Wrist flexor strength and grip strength both reduced (3/5). Spinal palpation reveals tenderness at T7-8 level. TUEP positive (increased time up and go).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan shows T-score of -2.8 at lumbar spine and -2.5 at femoral neck (osteoporotic range). Vertebral fracture assessment shows compression fracture at T7 (15% height loss). Biochemical markers show elevated P1NP and CTX-1 indicating high bone turnover.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporosis is secondary in a younger patient with identifiable systemic cause.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old with history of mild osteoporosis presents with sudden-onset sharp chest wall pain following a minor fall from a standing height. Pain is strictly localized to the lateral chest wall on one side. Differential includes musculoskeletal versus cardiac or pulmonary pathology.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is markedly aggravated by deep inspiration, coughing, and any trunk flexion or rotation movements. The patient splints the injured side when coughing. Lying on the affected side is intolerable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has been on corticosteroids for 2 years due to polymyalgia rheumatica, increasing fracture risk. No previous rib injuries are reported. Smoking history is significant with 30 pack-years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Precise point tenderness over the posterolateral rib at one specific level is elicited. Direct palpation reproduces the sharp pain. Intercostal muscle guarding is evident, and breathing pattern is visibly shallow and splinted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest reveals a linear lucency through the cortical bone of one rib with minimal displacement. No flail segment is identified. Imaging confirms the fracture location and rules out pulmonary complications.

puzzles.explanation: Rib fracture is acute post-traumatic with clear mechanism and localized findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports chronic knee pain with morning stiffness that improves with activity. Pain worsens with prolonged standing and walking. Functional limitation occurs with stairs and uneven ground.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged walking and standing exacerbate knee pain with increased swelling. Stairs, particularly descending, trigger pain. Activity modification and rest provide symptom relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Knee osteoarthritis develops gradually with progressive cartilage loss. Previous knee injury or meniscal pathology increases risk. Obesity and repetitive impact activities accelerate degeneration.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, knee effusion may be present with positive ballottement test. Crepitus is palpated with knee motion. Range of motion is limited with pain at end-range flexion and extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis. Imaging severity correlates with symptom severity. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding conservative versus surgical management.

puzzles.explanation: Early osteoarthritis may present with inflammatory symptoms and variable pain patterns.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old desk worker presents with chronic upper back and neck tension with associated cervical headaches. Symptoms have developed gradually over 3 years of sedentary work. Multiple musculoskeletal patterns may contribute to this presentation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen significantly with prolonged computer work and improve with movement breaks and stretching. Forward shoulder posture increases pain within 15-20 minutes. Thoracic extension and scapular retraction relieve symptoms temporarily.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works 9-10 hours daily at a desk with a poorly positioned monitor and keyboard. Previous physiotherapy provided only temporary relief. No significant trauma history, though postural habits have been poor since adolescence.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Forward head posture and rounded shoulders are evident on observation. Significant tightness of the pectoralis major and upper trapezius is noted with limited scapular retraction. Weakness of the serratus anterior and lower trapezius is confirmed on testing. Cervical extension is limited.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine imaging is negative for structural pathology. The clinical diagnosis is based on postural assessment, manual muscle testing, and flexibility findings. No imaging is necessary to confirm this postural dysfunction pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Upper crossed syndrome is postural dysfunction from modern sedentary work with predictable muscle imbalances.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old office worker presents with bilateral neck stiffness and posterior cervical muscle tension concentrated in the upper trapezius region. Symptoms emerged 4 weeks into a remote work transition. Cervical pathology is initially broad in differential.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Neck tension worsens progressively through the workday and is worst by late afternoon. Taking frequent breaks and performing neck stretches provides 30-45 minutes of relief. Symptoms are minimal on weekends and during vacations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient transitioned to a home office setup with suboptimal ergonomics (laptop screen positioned too low). No history of neck trauma, whiplash, or previous neck symptoms. Stress levels have increased due to workload.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral upper trapezius muscle tightness is palpable with trigger points elicited. Cervical range of motion is full but produces muscular discomfort. No neurological deficits or radicular symptoms are present. Posture assessment reveals forward head position.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays are normal with no degenerative changes, subluxation, or fracture. MRI is not indicated given the clear occupational trigger and benign clinical presentation. Diagnosis is confirmed through the temporal relationship to work setup change.

puzzles.explanation: Neck tension from desk work shows occupational strain from computer work and forward head posture.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 71-year-old smoker with long-standing COPD presents with acute exacerbation. Increased dyspnea, productive cough with yellow sputum, and wheezing. Acute on chronic symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Marked dyspnea at rest and minimal activity tolerance. Difficulty with activities of daily living. Acute functional decline from baseline.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of COPD for 8 years, current smoker. Multiple prior exacerbations. Recent URI symptoms 1 week prior. Possible respiratory infection trigger.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tachypnea (RR 26), accessory muscle use marked. Diminished breath sounds with wheezes. Productive cough. Cyanosis noted in nail beds. Mental status alert.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with possible infiltrate suggesting infection. Sputum culture growing bacteria. Spirometry consistent with obstructive pattern. ABG shows hypoxemia and hypercapnia.

puzzles.explanation: Classic COPD presentation with significant smoking history, progressive airflow obstruction confirmed by spirometry, and characteristic clinical findings. represents the post-smoking-cessation patient with established disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old with advanced knee osteoarthritis presents 6 weeks post-operative from total knee replacement for severe degenerative joint disease. Pain and stiffness persist despite appropriate surgical technique. Post-surgical complications must be ruled out.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Knee extension remains limited with a 10-degree extension lag noted during ambulation. Stairs, particularly descending, remain significantly limited. Walking tolerance exceeds 30 minutes but is associated with increased swelling.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient had severe pre-operative knee flexion contracture of 15 degrees. A continuous passive motion machine was used post-operatively for 10 days. Compliance with home exercises has been suboptimal.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee extension is -10 degrees (extension lag present during straight leg raise). Knee flexion reaches 85 degrees actively. Quadriceps strength is 4/5. Significant anterior knee swelling is noted with a positive joint effusion sign. No signs of infection or deep vein thrombosis are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs show appropriate component position and alignment with no signs of loosening or varus/valgus malalignment. No fracture, dislocation, or hardware complications are identified. Imaging is reassuring for mechanical factors.

puzzles.explanation: Suboptimal TKA outcomes require intensified extension lag correction through electrical stimulation and biofeedback, aggressive flexion stretching, patellar mobilization, and neuromuscular retraining to restore functional mobility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old female presents 8 weeks post-operative from left total mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection for invasive ductal carcinoma. Restricted shoulder function and chest wall tightness are limiting daily activities. Post-surgical complications including seroma and lymphedema must be assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead reaching and lifting are significantly restricted. Shoulder internal rotation and adduction are limited. Putting on a bra and reaching for back pockets are particularly difficult. Pain with arm elevation above 90 degrees is reported.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The surgical incision healed well without dehiscence. No chemotherapy or radiation therapy has been initiated yet. Lymph nodes were removed from levels 1 and 2. The patient is right-hand dominant.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder internal rotation is limited to 30 degrees compared to 65 degrees on the right. Forward flexion reaches 110 degrees with restriction in end-range. Axillary scar tissue is palpable with significant restriction to tissue mobility. No signs of active infection, seroma recurrence, or marked lymphedema present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the axilla shows no evidence of seroma recurrence or lymph collection. Soft tissue imaging confirms scar tissue adhesions in the axillary region. Imaging does not identify mechanical complications requiring surgical intervention.

puzzles.explanation: Post-mastectomy rehabilitation requires early scar mobilization, gradual shoulder range of motion restoration, lymphedema prevention through compression and elevation, and preparation for adjuvant therapies that may affect rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old overhead athlete (volleyball) presents with progressive anterolateral shoulder pain during throwing and spiking activities. Pain has worsened over the past 8 weeks despite activity modification. Rotator cuff pathology versus scapular dysfunction should both be considered.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specific to overhead activities performed explosively and is worst during the late cocking and acceleration phases of throwing. Rest provides partial relief but symptoms recur immediately upon return to sport. Repetitive activities reproduce pain reliably.

clues.label: History
clues.text: A rapid increase in training volume occurred 2 months prior to symptom onset. No significant trauma or fall occurred. Previous episodes of shoulder pain resolved completely with rest. The athlete reports a history of lower crossed syndrome.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Scapular dyskinesis is evident on visual observation with winging of the scapula during arm elevation and functional testing. Scapular stabilizer muscles (serratus anterior and lower trapezius) are weak on manual muscle testing. Posterior shoulder tightness and pectoral tightness are noted. Positive supraspinatus sign with pain in the 80-120 degree range.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows mild supraspinatus tendinopathy with no full-thickness tear and normal rotator cuff otherwise. No labral pathology is identified. Imaging confirms soft tissue pathology consistent with subacromial impingement from scapular dyskinesis.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic scapular dyskinesis with significant functional loss and muscle weakness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old presents 12 weeks post-partum from vaginal delivery with two small perineal tears (first degree). She reports persistent pain with intercourse and difficulty with continence of flatus. Pelvic floor assessment is necessary to guide rehabilitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with intercourse has prevented sexual activity resumption. Occasional stress urinary incontinence occurs with coughing, sneezing, or exercise. Activities requiring pelvic floor stability (running, jumping) are avoided due to discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The labor lasted 16 hours with instrumental assistance in the second stage. Perineal tears occurred but were repaired immediately post-delivery. The patient is breastfeeding exclusively, which affects hormonal status. No episiotomy was performed.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor muscle assessment reveals pain with palpation of the perineal scar tissue. Voluntary pelvic floor contraction is weak at 2/5. Pelvic floor endurance is severely limited with rapid fatigue after 2-3 contractions. Palpable tension and restrictions in the perineal body are noted. Vulvovaginal appearance shows healed scar tissue without obvious dehiscence.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging of the perineum shows intact external anal sphincter with preserved symmetry. No significant defects in the perineal body are identified. Pelvic floor muscle thickness is decreased compared to published norms. Imaging rules out significant structural complications.

puzzles.explanation: Postpartum pelvic floor dysfunction from sphincter injury requires specialist management potentially including colorectal and gynecologic consultation, scar tissue release and mobilization, biofeedback therapy, and careful progression recognizing complexity of sphincter healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old recreational tennis player presents with insidious-onset anterolateral shoulder pain that has gradually worsened over 3 months. Pain is activity-related and occurs with overhead reaching. Rotator cuff pathology is a primary consideration in this age group.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by overhead activities, particularly serving in tennis and reaching across the body. Resting the shoulder provides gradual improvement over days to weeks. Sleeping on the affected side reproduces pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient plays competitive tennis 3 times weekly and has recently increased intensity. No acute trauma or fall occurred. The dominant (right) shoulder is affected. Previous shoulder problems are denied.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Hawkins test (forced internal rotation and horizontal adduction) reproduces anterolateral pain. The Neer impingement sign is also positive. Supraspinatus weakness is present but no full strength loss. Suprascapular notch tenderness is elicited with palpation. Scapular mobility is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickening and heterogeneity of the supraspinatus tendon with intact fiber continuity confirming tendinopathy. No full-thickness tear is present. Subacromial subdeltoid bursa shows mild fluid consistent with secondary bursitis.

puzzles.explanation: Classic rotator cuff tendinopathy from occupational overhead activities in middle-aged individual.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old presents with chronic ankle pain and stiffness, particularly affecting the anteromedial joint space. Symptoms have progressed over 5 years with recent acceleration. A 30-year history of recurrent ankle sprains is noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens significantly with ambulation on uneven ground and with pivoting movements. Walking tolerance has decreased from 45 minutes to 20 minutes due to progressive ankle pain. Morning stiffness lasting 30 minutes is reported upon waking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple ankle sprains over 30 years have progressively damaged the joint surfaces and ligamentous support. No significant trauma in the past year, but cumulative joint loading has been high from walking/hiking activities. Previous physiotherapy for ankle stability was completed 10 years ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ankle dorsiflexion is limited to 5 degrees (normal 15 degrees) and plantarflexion to 35 degrees (normal 50 degrees). Significant anteromedial ankle joint line tenderness is elicited. The gait demonstrates a stiff ankle pattern with circumduction. Weight-bearing calf raise produces pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs show marked joint space narrowing at the tibiotalar joint with marginal osteophytes and subchondral sclerosis. No significant subluxation or instability is evident. Imaging confirms advanced ankle osteoarthritis with degenerative changes consistent with post-traumatic disease.

puzzles.explanation: Ankle OA typically develops after prior injury or chronic instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports deep groin pain with activities involving hip flexion and internal rotation. Mechanical symptoms including catching or clicking may occur. Functional limitation occurs with running and pivoting activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hip flexion combined with internal rotation activities reproduce groin pain. Running, cutting, and pivoting sports movements are limited. Pain may worsen with prolonged sitting or standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Labral tear may follow a specific trauma or develop insidiously from repetitive microtrauma. Hip impingement and femoroacetabular impingement increase tear risk. Athletes in rotation-based sports have increased incidence.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the FABER test and anterior impingement test may reproduce symptoms. Internal rotation of the hip in flexion triggers pain. Hip flexibility deficits are often noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI arthrography demonstrates labral tear with visualization of contrast leaking through the tear. MRI shows the tear morphology and associated findings. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding surgical intervention.

puzzles.explanation: Labral tears often occur with underlying impingement or instability. Mechanical symptoms like clicking are characteristic.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden-onset severe calf pain during a tennis match while plantarflexing the ankle. A sharp, tearing sensation occurred, and immediate functional loss was noted. This midsubstance calf tear is a distinct injury pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking is severely limited with inability to plantarflex the ankle against resistance. Standing on the affected leg produces pain and instability. The patient cannot climb stairs or return to running.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred acutely during the sport without prior calf symptoms. Warm-up was adequate. A previous episode of similar acute calf pain occurred 10 years prior, which resolved. Achilles tendon assessment is important given the plantarflexion pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant calf swelling is noted with bruising extending toward the ankle over days post-injury. Plantarflexion strength is severely weakened (1-2/5) with pain limiting effort. The Thompson test is negative (indicating intact Achilles tendon). A midcalf muscle defect may be palpable on careful examination. Considerable ecchymosis is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic disruption in the gastrocnemius-soleus muscle complex with edema and hemorrhage consistent with a partial muscle tear (grade 2-3 strain). The tear is localized to the medial gastrocnemius. No Achilles tendon discontinuity is identified.

puzzles.explanation: Tennis leg shows post-injury gastrocnemius weakness with healing muscle tear and functional deficit.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old desk worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the medial hand (ring and little fingers) over 4 months. Symptoms are worse at night and have begun affecting grip strength. Ulnar nerve compression at the elbow must be confirmed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged typing exacerbates symptoms significantly. Sleeping with the elbow flexed triggers symptoms that wake the patient during the night. Activities requiring sustained elbow flexion (holding phone to ear) reproduce paresthesias within 5-10 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has poor ergonomics with the desk work station (armrest too low, keyboard too high). A previous medial elbow fracture occurred 15 years ago but healed uneventfully. No recent trauma to the elbow region.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's sign is elicited by percussing the ulnar nerve at the elbow (just posterior to the medial epicondyle). The Froment test is positive with inability to adduct the thumb without flexing the interphalangeal joint. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy is subtle but present in the interossei. Two-point discrimination is diminished in the little finger.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of conduction velocity across the elbow segment consistent with cubital tunnel syndrome. EMG demonstrates denervation activity in intrinsic hand muscles and mild motor involvement. MRI shows compression of the ulnar nerve at the level of the cubital tunnel.

puzzles.explanation: Classic cubital tunnel syndrome with nocturnal symptoms and elbow flexion aggravation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old rugby player's ring finger caught in opposing player's jersey during tackle. Sharp pain and immediate loss of finger flexion. Swelling over proximal forearm developed within minutes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to flex distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint of ring finger (flexor digitorum profundus avulsion). Mild proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint flexion intact (flexor digitorum superficialis functioning). Pain with attempted flexion. Swelling in volar forearm region.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism with finger caught in jersey with sudden traction injury. Injury occurred 2 hours ago. Sport involves high-contact mechanism. Young athletic male with full mobility demands. No prior finger trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ring finger held in extension at DIP joint with inability to actively flex (0/5 DIP flexion). PIP flexion intact at 4/5 strength. Mild swelling noted in volar forearm proximal to wrist. Tender palm with fullness along flexor digitorum profundus tendon region. No sensory loss or vascular compromise. No wound or skin disruption.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows no bone avulsion at distal phalanx base (distinguishes from bony mallet). MRI demonstrates complete rupture of flexor digitorum profundus tendon at level of wrist crease. Tendon retracted proximally with gap of 3cm. Associated edema in forearm soft tissues. Ultrasound confirms tendon discontinuity with fluid collection (hematoma) tracking proximally in forearm.

puzzles.explanation: Jersey finger from acute traction injury during sports with FDP avulsion from distal phalanx.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 67-year-old woman with pelvic floor dysfunction reports mixed symptoms of urgency and stress incontinence. Frequency significant with 12+ voidings daily. Nocturnal symptoms with multiple awakenings.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation from incontinence and frequency. Difficulty with social activities and travel. Sleep disruption from nocturia. Significant quality of life impact.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of vaginal deliveries. Hysterectomy 15 years prior. Menopause 12 years prior. Progressive worsening of symptoms. Previous pelvic floor therapy attempted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Pelvic heaviness sensation. Palpable pelvic floor laxity. Possible mild pelvic organ prolapse. Guarding from pain possible.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound shows weakened pelvic support. Urodynamics shows mixed overactive bladder and stress component. Post-void residual elevated. Defecography shows posterior compartment involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction shows post-traumatic anal sphincter injury with fecal incontinence resulting from significant childbirth trauma and incomplete recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with 6 months of burning pain in the plantar forefoot between the 3rd and 4th metatarsal heads (plantar interdigital space). Pain radiates into the adjacent toes. Tight shoes aggravate the condition markedly.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is worsened by prolonged standing and by wearing tight or heeled shoes. Walking barefoot provides temporary relief. Pain occurs particularly with activities requiring push-off loading of the forefoot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient wears tight high heels 5 days per week for work. A previous history of Morton's neuroma on the opposite foot was treated 8 years ago with conservative management successfully. No significant trauma to the foot is reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Direct compression between the metatarsal heads reproduces sharp pain into the 3rd and 4th toes (Mulder's test positive). A palpable click or 'pop' may be felt with this compression. Plantar palpation elicits exquisite tenderness in the interspace. Sensation to light touch is normal in the affected toes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic nodular mass between the 3rd and 4th metatarsal heads consistent with a neuroma (typically 3-5mm). MRI confirms a focal mass in the plantar intermetatarsal space with T2 signal intensity consistent with nerve tissue edema. Imaging rules out other forefoot pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic or recalcitrant Morton's neuroma may require advanced interventions including injection or surgery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old post-operative patient (day 4 from hip fracture repair) presents with fluctuating confusion, disorientation to time/place, and agitation alternating with lethargy. Acute cognitive changes emerged suddenly post-operatively. Delirium superimposed on dementia must be recognized.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is unable to cooperate reliably with rehabilitation exercises due to inattention and disorientation. Sleep-wake cycle is completely reversed (sleepy during day, agitated at night). Physical activity is limited by safety concerns and inability to follow commands consistently.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has mild baseline cognitive impairment (early dementia). Significant medication changes occurred post-operatively (opioids added, sleep aids prescribed). Dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, and acute infection have been ruled out medically. Hospital environment is unfamiliar to the patient.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cognitive assessment reveals fluctuating confusion with disorientation to time and place. Attention is significantly impaired with inability to focus or maintain concentration. Formal delirium screening (CAM criteria) is positive. No focal neurological deficits are present. Vital signs are stable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain CT or MRI may be performed to exclude structural causes such as stroke, subdural hematoma, or acute intracranial lesion. Imaging is reassuring and shows only chronic small vessel ischemic changes. The diagnosis of delirium is clinical, and imaging serves to rule out structural complications.

puzzles.explanation: Delirium superimposed on dementia has poor prognosis with limited functional recovery potential and high mortality risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old man 12 weeks post-traumatic hip dislocation reduction presents with progressive loss of hip motion and functional decline. Hip stiffness limiting activities of daily living. Swelling and pain in hip region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive loss of hip internal and external rotation. Hip flexion contracture developing (unable to fully extend hip). Abduction and adduction limited. Pain with attempted motion. Functional decline with difficulty dressing and toileting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy trauma 12 weeks prior with hip posterior dislocation and associated femoral head fracture. Complex fracture pattern with soft tissue injury. Delayed presentation to rehabilitation after acute care. Age 42 and male sex associated with higher HO risk. Extended immobilization period.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion contracture present (unable to achieve full extension). External and internal rotation severely limited (<15 degrees each direction). Abduction limited to 10 degrees. Palpable stiffness with firm end-feel on passive motion. Tenderness over hip region. Functional limitation with gait and transfers.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray hip shows extensive heterotopic ossification Brooker Grade III-IV with bridging bone formation across hip joint. CT hip demonstrates mature bone formation in soft tissues surrounding joint capsule. MRI shows ossified bridges with mature bone marrow signal. Radiographs demonstrate hip internal architecture with associated degeneration.

puzzles.explanation: Heterotopic ossification is post-traumatic or post-surgical bone formation in soft tissues causing stiffness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes for 12 years presents with progressive burning pain in the feet extending into the lower legs, beginning distally and advancing proximally. Pain quality is described as burning, electrical, or 'pins and needles.' Diabetic neuropathy is the primary consideration.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is worse at night and is significantly aggravated by touch or light clothing contact (allodynia). Activities requiring prolonged standing or walking exacerbate symptoms. Cool, moist conditions provide temporary relief by reducing allodynia.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Glycemic control has been suboptimal with HbA1c ranging 7.8-8.5% over past 2 years. The patient also takes medication for hypertension. No significant trauma to the feet is reported. Symptoms began insidiously 8 months ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sharp-dull discrimination is diminished in the feet bilaterally with a stocking distribution. Vibration sense is reduced at the ankles. Monofilament testing (10g) shows loss of sensation in the plantar surface. Temperature sensation is mildly altered. Reflexes are diminished at the ankles. Motor strength is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies demonstrate slowing of both motor and sensory nerve conduction velocities in lower extremities consistent with demyelination. EMG shows mild denervation changes in distal lower limb muscles. The DN4 neuropathic pain questionnaire score is elevated. Imaging confirms peripheral neuropathy pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Neuropathic pain presents with characteristic burning/electric quality in nerve distribution with confirmed nerve pathology on testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 78-year-old presents with spouse-reported progressive memory loss and difficulty with complex tasks over 18 months. The patient reports difficulty remembering recent conversations and struggles to manage finances. Early-stage cognitive impairment must be formally assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient has difficulty following multi-step instructions and requires written reminders for appointments. Complex activities (cooking from new recipes, managing medications from multiple prescriptions) are becoming problematic. Independent activity tolerance is declining.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has hypertension and hypercholesterolemia managed medically but with suboptimal adherence. A previous episode of transient ischemic attack occurred 5 years ago. Depression screening has been negative. Education level is college-level.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Formal cognitive screening using Montreal Cognitive Assessment shows score of 22/30 (below age-adjusted norms). Memory is impaired for recent events but long-term memory is preserved. Attention and executive function are mildly affected. Speech is normal and no focal neurological deficits are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Structural brain MRI shows small vessel ischemic changes and mild generalized atrophy consistent with aging but no acute stroke or tumor. Amyloid positron emission tomography may show early amyloid accumulation. Imaging supports vascular and/or neurodegenerative etiology.

puzzles.explanation: Mild cognitive impairment represents decline between normal aging and dementia, with memory and cognitive deficits affecting complex activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman with known osteoporosis fell onto her outstretched hand (FOOSH mechanism) and presents with immediate wrist pain and visible deformity. Swelling is rapid and pronounced. Distal radius fracture is the anticipated diagnosis in this demographic.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot perform any wrist movements due to pain and apprehension about further displacement. Grasping is impossible. The patient experienced immediate functional loss at the moment of injury.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient is a postmenopausal woman with 8 years of documented osteoporosis (T-score -2.8). Falls risk has been increasing due to balance impairment. No significant wrist trauma previously. The fall occurred from standing height.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The wrist demonstrates a 'dinner fork' deformity with dorsal displacement of the distal fragment. Marked swelling of the dorsal wrist is evident. Tenderness is greatest over the distal radius. Radial and ulnar pulses are intact. Hand color and temperature appear normal, and finger sensation is preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain wrist radiographs (posteroanterior and lateral views) show a dorsally displaced metaphyseal fracture of the distal radius with comminution of the dorsal margin. Ulnar styloid fracture is present in 50% of cases. CT may be used to assess fracture complexity for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Complex comminuted Colles fracture from high-energy trauma requiring surgical management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 7-year-old presents with a fourth fracture in 18 months following minor trauma (falling from a low height while playing). The child has a history of multiple fractures and blue-tinged sclerae. Hereditary bone fragility disorder must be suspected.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child is reluctant to participate in normal play activities due to fear of fracture. Protective posturing is evident. Ambulation is affected by pain from recent fracture. Physical activity is significantly restricted compared to age-matched peers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The mother and maternal grandmother have a history of multiple fractures and are presumed to have the same condition. The child's height is below the 10th percentile. Hearing loss was noted at school screening. Dental enamel appears discolored and is prone to wear.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Blue sclerae are visibly evident on eye examination. The child has loose joints and demonstrates increased ligamentous laxity. Muscle tone is diminished. Dental examination shows enamel hypoplasia and discoloration. The recent fracture site shows ecchymosis and swelling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays of the fractured limb show the acute fracture with osteopenic bone (reduced bone density) and thin cortices. Generalized skeletal survey reveals healed fractures in various stages of healing and evidence of previous long bone deformities. Bone density assessment (DEXA scan) shows significantly reduced bone density. Genetic testing confirms collagen I abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta is a genetic connective tissue disorder with severe bone fragility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old presents with 2 months of progressive deep posterior shoulder pain and weakness, particularly with external rotation of the arm. Pain is localized deep in the posterior shoulder. Rotator cuff pathology is a primary consideration.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: External rotation strength has declined significantly, affecting activities like throwing, swimming, and reaching behind the back. Pain is worst with resisted external rotation at 0 degrees abduction. Overhead activities are tolerated but external rotation against resistance is severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: A fall on an outstretched arm with external rotation occurred 3 months ago, initially attributed to a minor strain. Symptoms have progressively worsened rather than improving. No significant prior shoulder history. The patient is right-hand dominant.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Isolated posterior shoulder tenderness is palpable beneath the posterior deltoid. External rotation strength testing reveals marked weakness (1-2/5) with pain. The drop arm test may be positive if full thickness. Supraspinatus strength is normal. Posterior shoulder muscle atrophy may be evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows a full-thickness tear of the infraspinatus with mild retraction of the muscle belly. The supraspinatus is normal. Fatty infiltration is absent, indicating acute injury. Ultrasound confirms infraspinatus tear with gap at the musculotendinous junction. The tear size and patient age determine management approach.

puzzles.explanation: Infraspinatus tear presenting with external rotation weakness and posterolateral pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old previously healthy teacher presents with acute-onset fever, cough, and chest discomfort of 3 days duration. The cough is productive of greenish-yellow sputum and is associated with dyspnea with minimal exertion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is unable to attend work due to dyspnea and general malaise. Climbing stairs reproduces chest pain and worsens cough. Social activities have been curtailed due to fatigue. Sleep is disrupted by nocturnal cough and fever symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset was preceded by a 1-week viral upper respiratory infection with nasal congestion and sore throat. The patient had brief exposure to a coworker with respiratory illness 10 days ago. No significant medical history or immunocompromise. Lives in a suburban home with family.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, temperature is 38.8°C, respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 94% on room air. Auscultation reveals crackles in the left lower lobe with egophony present. Dullness to percussion noted over affected area. Tachycardia present at 102 bpm.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates lobar consolidation in the left lower lobe with air bronchograms, consistent with community-acquired pneumonia. Sputum culture is pending. White blood cell count is elevated at 14,500 cells/mL. Procalcitonin level is elevated supporting bacterial etiology.

puzzles.explanation: Elderly pneumonia presentation with atypical presentation (confusion without fever, no prominent cough). Common in geriatric patients where infection may present as delirium rather than fever.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old presents with swelling over the tip of the elbow (olecranon prominence) that developed over 2 weeks. There is mild discomfort with direct pressure but no significant pain with elbow motion. Mechanical or inflammatory bursal pathology is likely.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Swelling worsens with prolonged pressure on the elbow (resting on elbows at desk). Leaning on the elbow significantly aggravates the bursal swelling. Elbow flexion and extension are painless, but end-range extension is uncomfortable due to bursal compression.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient recalls no specific trauma but notes increased time leaning on elbows while working at a desk over the past 3 months. No fever, chills, or systemic symptoms are reported. No intravenous drug use. Rheumatoid arthritis screening has been negative.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Prominent, soft, fluctuant swelling is visible over the olecranon with normal appearance of overlying skin (non-erythematous). Direct pressure on the bursa produces discomfort. Elbow flexion and extension are full and pain-free. No olecranon bone tenderness is elicited. Lymph nodes are not enlarged.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a fluid collection in the olecranon bursa without internal echoes (simple fluid) or thick septations, consistent with bursitis. Bursal wall is thickened. No osteophytes or bone erosion is evident. Imaging rules out septic bursitis (which would show echogenic debris or thick loculations).

puzzles.explanation: Chronic aseptic olecranon bursitis from repetitive pressure and leaning, classic student presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports pain in the midfoot specifically at the base of the fifth metatarsal. Pain onset may be sudden with specific inciting activity. Functional limitation occurs with weight bearing and running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is exacerbated by running and weight bearing activities. Sudden twisting or push-off movements reproduce pain. Pain worsens with continued activity on fractured bone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Jones fracture occurs at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal. Repetitive stress or acute inversion injury during running is the typical mechanism. The location has poor blood supply increasing healing difficulty.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is specifically localized at the base of the fifth metatarsal. Swelling may be present in the lateral midfoot. Pain occurs with weight bearing and resisted plantar flexion of the foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate transverse fracture at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal. CT imaging assesses fracture displacement and healing. Advanced imaging guides timing of weight bearing progression.

puzzles.explanation: Jones fracture at the metatarsal base is a common sport injury with risk of delayed healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old presents with pain localized to the anterior ear region bilaterally, clicking in both temporomandibular joints, and difficulty with wide mouth opening. Symptoms have developed over 2 months. Myofascial and intra-articular disorders must be differentiated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Jaw pain is aggravated by chewing tough foods and by prolonged talking. Yawning is limited and produces sharp clicking. The jaw deviates to one side upon opening. Morning stiffness of the jaw is reported.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient grinds teeth at night (confirmed by sleep partner), though no formal sleep study has been performed. Stress levels have been high due to work pressure. No history of jaw trauma or previous joint problems. Orthodontic treatment was completed 20 years ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The jaw deviates to the left on opening (10mm at maximum). Bilateral temporomandibular joint palpation elicits mild tenderness and is felt clicking during opening and closing. Masseter and temporalis muscle palpation is tender with trigger points. Maximum mouth opening is limited to 35mm (normal 40-50mm).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the temporomandibular joint shows anterior disc displacement (with or without reduction) and mild joint effusion in both joints. Bony structures appear intact without osteoarthritic changes. Ultrasound may show irregular muscle echogenicity consistent with myofascial pain. Imaging guides conservative versus surgical decision-making.

puzzles.explanation: TMJ disorder presents with disc displacement pattern, clicking, and functional limitation with clear stress-related trigger.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old presents with localized chest wall pain at the costochondral junction (where rib cartilage meets bone) for 3 weeks, worsening with deep breathing and coughing. Pain is anterior and lateral, primarily on the left. Cardiac etiology must initially be ruled out.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are aggravated by deep inspiration, coughing, and physical exertion such as heavy lifting or push-up exercises. Lying on the affected side reproduces pain. Resting and slow, shallow breathing provide some relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient had an upper respiratory infection with persistent cough 2 months ago; however, the cough has resolved, but pain persists. No trauma to the chest wall or weight lifting occurred. Previous episode of similar pain occurred 3 years ago and resolved in 6 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Direct palpation of the costochondral junctions reproduces sharp pain, particularly at the 2nd-5th costochondral junctions on the left. Elicitation of the pain by this specific palpation is the cardinal sign. Chest wall movement is full. Cardiac examination is normal and no murmurs are appreciated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray is normal with no acute cardiopulmonary pathology. ECG is normal, ruling out cardiac etiology. CT or ultrasound of the chest wall may show inflammatory changes at the costochondral junction but imaging is not routinely necessary. Clinical diagnosis is usually sufficient for confirmation.

puzzles.explanation: Costochondritis is the classic mechanical presentation with clear tender points and mechanical reproduction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old presents with a lifetime history of joint instability, frequent dislocations (particularly shoulders and fingers), and diffuse joint pain that worsens with activity. The patient reports being 'double-jointed' since childhood. Systemic connective tissue disorder must be assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities requiring repetitive joint motion (typing, playing instruments) aggravate pain significantly. Joint instability during certain movements creates apprehension and fear. Participation in sports has been limited due to recurrent injuries and dislocations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient's mother also has hypermobility and joint problems. Bruising easily and slow wound healing are reported. The patient recalls being able to perform unusual flexibility feats (bending thumb to wrist, extreme splits) since childhood. Multiple sprains and dislocations have occurred.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Beighton score is elevated (scoring hypermobility at multiple joints: thumbs bend backward to forearm, fingers extend back beyond 90 degrees, forward bending with hands flat on floor, elbows hyperextend, knees hyperextend). Shoulder shrug strength is reduced. Fine motor coordination assessment shows some clumsiness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of affected joints are normal with no evidence of osteoarthritis or structural damage. MRI may show joint capsule laxity and synovial inflammation but findings are non-specific. Genetic testing for collagen disorders (Ehlers-Danlos syndrome) may be indicated based on clinical severity. Imaging is used to rule out structural damage.

puzzles.explanation: Generalised joint hypermobility occurs in an athletic context where flexibility is advantageous until pain develops.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain for 3 weeks. Pain began during overhead serve motion. Reports occasional clicking sensation in shoulder during reaching.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp anterior shoulder pain with overhead activities, serving, and throwing motions. Pain exacerbated by resisted elbow flexion and supination. Discomfort improves with rest and ice. Patient experiences pain at night when lying on affected shoulder.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Repetitive overhead sports participation (tennis 4x/week) for past year. Gradual onset suggesting tendinopathy rather than acute tear. No prior shoulder surgery. Underlying rotator cuff dysfunction noted on screening examination.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Speed's test (supinated elbow flexion against resistance) reproduces anterior shoulder pain. Yergason test (supinated forearm with elbow flexion) also positive. Palpable tenderness over bicipital groove. Apprehension test negative. Rotator cuff strength 4+/5 suggesting dynamic instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening (5mm) with hypoechoic signal and increased vascularity on Doppler imaging consistent with tendinopathy. Subacromial space narrowed suggesting associated subacromial impingement. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration of proximal biceps tendon without full thickness tear.

puzzles.explanation: Biceps tendinopathy associated with rotator cuff pathology, secondary component.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old presents 4 weeks post-operatively from elbow fracture-dislocation with progressive pain and severe restriction of elbow motion, particularly extension. Swelling persists despite appropriate management. Heterotopic ossification must be considered.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Elbow extension is severely limited with active extension reaching only 60 degrees flexion (normal 0 degrees extension). Passive extension is more limited and extremely painful. Functional activities (reaching, eating, grooming) are severely affected.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury was a high-energy trauma (fall from height) requiring open reduction and internal fixation. Significant soft tissue injury and swelling occurred at the time of injury. Post-operative rehabilitation has progressed normally otherwise.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe soft tissue swelling persists over the antecubital fossa and dorsal elbow. Elbow flexion is approximately 90 degrees. Attempts at passive extension reproduce significant pain and apprehension. Skin temperature is slightly elevated. Radial and ulnar pulses remain intact. Fingers are warm with normal color.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show early heterotopic bone formation within the soft tissues around the elbow, visible as cloud-like opacities and beginning to demarcate. CT shows the extent of ossification relative to the fracture fixation. Bone scan shows marked uptake in the affected region. Early recognition and appropriate management (indomethacin or radiation prophylaxis) may limit progression.

puzzles.explanation: Myositis ossificans is pathological bone formation in soft tissues causing significant disability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports shoulder pain specifically at the acromioclavicular joint following direct trauma. Pain is localized to the superior aspect of the shoulder. Functional limitation occurs with overhead activities and crossing the arm over the body.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is exacerbated by adduction of the arm across the body and horizontal adduction. Overhead activities trigger discomfort at the AC joint. Push-up type activities and horizontal pressing movements reproduce pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sprain typically follows direct blow to the shoulder or fall onto the outstretched arm. The injury grades from 1-6 based on ligament disruption and clavicular displacement. Higher grade sprains demonstrate clavicular subluxation or complete dislocation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited directly over the AC joint. Crossover adduction test reproduces pain at the AC joint. The O'Brien test may also elicit AC joint pain with positive result.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate clavicular displacement and AC joint space abnormalities based on sprain grade. Weighted views show degree of clavicular elevation if AC ligament is disrupted. MRI reveals ligament injury and associated findings.

puzzles.explanation: AC joint osteoarthritis from chronic overhead loading in weightlifter with insidious onset.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 64-year-old presents 7 days post-operatively from abdominal surgery with acute partial separation of the incision site. Drainage of serosanguineous fluid from the incision is noted, and the patient reports sudden pain at the surgical site. Post-operative complication assessment is urgent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is unable to engage in physical activity due to pain and risk of further wound separation. Any straining, coughing, or Valsalva movement produces sharp pain and increased drainage. Ambulation is tentative due to fear of disruption.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The initial surgery healed well for the first 5 days with expected surgical site pain. On post-operative day 6, sudden pain and drainage occurred. Risk factors for dehiscence include advancing age, obesity (BMI 32), and cough post-operatively.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visual inspection of the incision reveals partial separation of the wound edges with visible subcutaneous tissue in the area of separation. Serosanguineous drainage is present. The surrounding skin is erythematous. No systemic signs of infection are present (normal temperature, vital signs stable). Some wound edges remain intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the incision site shows separation of the wound layers with fluid collection consistent with seroma. CT may be performed to assess for underlying abscess or significant fluid collection. Imaging guides management decisions regarding conservative wound care versus re-exploration.

puzzles.explanation: Surgical wound dehiscence with infection requires urgent wound care, infection management with antibiotics, possible reoperation and reclosure, infection prevention, nutritional support, and modified rehabilitation with careful wound protection.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old man awakens with severe unilateral neck pain and restricted rotation. Neck held in slight forward flexion and contralateral rotation. Acute onset overnight.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with any neck movement, particularly rotation to one side. Functional limitation with inability to turn head. Pain radiates to upper trapezius region.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No preceding trauma. Slept in unusual neck position. No history of neck pathology. Symptoms began upon waking. Acute presentation with no progressive onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant muscle spasm in ipsilateral sternocleidomastoid and upper trapezius visible. Neck rotation severely restricted in direction of pain, approximately 20 degrees. Palpable muscle tightness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays normal without fracture or dislocation. No acute abnormalities visible. MRI not performed (acute phase). Clinical diagnosis based on acute muscle spasm presentation.

puzzles.explanation: Acute wry neck is the classic acute torticollis with sudden onset and severe movement restriction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents 2 weeks post-operatively from arthroscopic subacromial decompression for chronic impingement syndrome. Shoulder pain persists despite surgery, and range of motion remains limited. Post-operative recovery assessment is necessary.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Arm elevation is still significantly limited with active abduction reaching only 90 degrees. External rotation at 0 degrees abduction is restricted. Pain with any attempt at overhead activities is persistent. The patient is unable to reach behind the back for dressing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pre-operatively, the patient had severe pain with overhead activities and sleep disturbance. Imaging showed subacromial osteophytes and bursal inflammation. The arthroscopic procedure removed the osteophyte and decompressed the space. Post-operative physiotherapy compliance has been adequate.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder range of motion shows active forward flexion limited to 110 degrees with pain at end-range. External rotation at 0 degrees is 30 degrees (limited). Internal rotation is nearly normal. Deltoid strength is adequate at 4+/5. No signs of infection are present (normal temperature, incision healing well).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative shoulder ultrasound or MRI confirms successful decompression with adequate space created. No retracted rotator cuff tear is identified. Bursal fluid is resolved. Imaging confirms appropriate surgical result; pain recovery is within normal timeframe.

puzzles.explanation: Post-subacromial decompression arthroscopy requires early range of motion to prevent stiffness, gradual strengthening of rotator cuff and scapular stabilizers, and activity modification to prevent recurrent impingement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old presents with progressive mid-back pain (thoracic spine) and bilateral lower extremity pain with a girdle-like distribution around the chest at the level of the thoracic spine. Pain is localized to the thoracic region with radiation bilaterally. Myelopathic features must be assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities requiring trunk extension or rotation aggravate symptoms. Prolonged sitting worsens pain. Ambulation tolerance is limited to 30 minutes due to bilateral leg pain. Sleeping is disrupted by pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms developed insidiously over 3 months without trauma. The patient has a sedentary desk job. Previous episodes of mid-back pain have been minimal. No significant trauma or falls. Progressive nature of symptoms is concerning.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Thoracic spine extension is limited and reproduces pain. Trunk rotation to the right reproduces left-sided radicular symptoms. Lower extremity neurological examination shows mild weakness in hip flexors bilaterally (4/5). Hyperreflexia at the knees is present. Clonus may be elicited at the ankle. Mild spasticity is noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows central or paracentral disc herniation at the thoracic level compressing the spinal cord. The cord shows signal change (T2 hyperintensity) consistent with myelomalacia. CT can clarify bone anatomy if surgical intervention is contemplated. Imaging demonstrates cord compression with edema indicating significant pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic disc herniation is a rare and diagnostically challenging condition with serious cord compression implications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old presents with sudden-onset severe vertigo accompanied by nausea and vomiting, presenting 3 days following an upper respiratory infection. Vertigo is rotatory and horizontal, maximized with head movement. Vestibular pathology must be confirmed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Any head movement exacerbates vertigo significantly, causing nausea and vomiting. The patient prefers to remain motionless in a dark, quiet environment. Ambulation is impossible due to severe vertigo and imbalance. Self-care activities are severely compromised.

clues.label: History
clues.text: A URI with cough and nasal congestion preceded the vertigo by 48-72 hours. The infection involved both upper and lower respiratory systems. Hearing loss in the affected ear has been noted. No prior episodes of vertigo. No trauma or vomiting prior to onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nystagmus is present with the fast component toward the affected ear. Positive Dix-Hallpike test reproduces vertigo and nystagmus. Head impulse test shows deficit (unable to maintain focus during head rotation toward affected side). Weber test shows conductive shift toward the affected ear, and Rinne shows air-bone gap indicating hearing loss.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI with contrast shows T2 hyperintensity in the labyrinth on the affected side consistent with labyrinthitis. The vestibular nerve may show enhancement. Audiometry documents sensorineural hearing loss in the affected ear. Imaging and audiometry confirm viral labyrinthitis with associated hearing loss.

puzzles.explanation: Acute labyrinthitis requires vestibular rehabilitation, balance retraining, gait training for safety, psychological support given acute distress, and monitoring for spontaneous recovery while preventing deconditioning and chronicity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with chronic dizziness and vertigo sensation lasting 8 months. Symptoms fluctuate but persistently present. Not true vertigo but persistent dizziness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation from unrelenting dizziness. Difficulty with balance, walking, and visual tracking. Anxiety secondary to symptoms. Avoidance of movement due to fear.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Dizziness onset post-vestibular neuritis 8 months prior. Acute vestibular symptoms resolved but dizziness persisted and worsened. Anxiety developed from symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive head impulse test abnormal (slow eye movements with head turn). Mild postural instability on tandem stance. Normal nystagmus testing. Anxiety evident during examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electro-nystagmography shows mild vestibular asymmetry previously (resolving). MRI head normal. Diagnosis confirmed by symptom persistence without abnormal objective findings.

puzzles.explanation: Persistent Postural-Perceptual Dizziness is a functional vestibular disorder following initial vestibular event, characterized by chronic non-vertiginous dizziness, postural sway, and visual motion intolerance with normal vestibular testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old secretary presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, and middle fingers bilaterally, worse on the right. Symptoms are particularly problematic at night, awakening the patient from sleep. Median nerve compression at the wrist must be confirmed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged typing aggravates symptoms within 30 minutes of work. Gripping objects produces numbness and tingling. Driving worsens symptoms. Night symptoms interfere with sleep quality, requiring frequent position changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works 8 hours daily at a computer keyboard with poor ergonomic setup. Repetitive wrist and finger motion is constant throughout the day. No significant wrist trauma. Thyroid function is normal (ruled out hypothyroidism as contributing factor).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's sign is elicited by percussing over the median nerve at the wrist crease. Phalen's test (wrist flexion held 30 seconds) reproduces numbness and tingling within 10 seconds bilaterally. Two-point discrimination is diminished on the thumb and index finger. Hand strength is normal in the intrinsic muscles tested.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show prolonged latency of median sensory and motor nerves across the wrist compared to the forearm, confirming conduction slowing at the carpal tunnel level. Electromyography shows mild denervation in the abductor pollicis brevis. MRI shows flattening of the median nerve at the wrist. Studies confirm carpal tunnel syndrome bilaterally.

puzzles.explanation: Classic carpal tunnel syndrome with nocturnal paraesthesia in the median nerve distribution, provocable signs, and risk factors of pregnancy and hypothyroidism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old collegiate soccer player reports acute anterior knee pain during game when planting foot during pivot maneuver. Severe pain with sensation of something tearing in anterior knee. Immediate swelling and loss of function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe anterior knee pain exacerbated by any attempt at knee extension. Inability to perform straight leg raise. Swelling rapidly developed over 30 minutes. Pain with palpation of tibial tubercle region. Loss of functional activity with guarding of knee.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young athletic male with high-demand sport (soccer). Acute traumatic mechanism during cutting maneuver. No prior knee surgery or injury. Recent growth spurt (18 years old) with some residual cartilage sensitivity. Full participation in sport up to injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gross swelling of anterior knee with visible hematoma formation over tibial tubercle. Severe tenderness directly over tibial tuberosity. Unable to perform active knee extension against any resistance (0/5 strength). Patellar tendon taut and tender. Quadriceps muscle palpable but non-contractile. Knee effusion present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP, lateral, and Merchant views) shows avulsion of tibial tuberosity fragment with 1.5cm displacement. CT confirms fracture extends into articular surface of tibial plateau. MRI shows complete disruption of patellar tendon at bone insertion with severe edema. Associated bone marrow edema in proximal tibia. Soft tissue trauma with hematoma extensive.

puzzles.explanation: Tibial tuberosity avulsion is a serious injury occurring at the junction of growth plate and tendon insertion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old presents 8 weeks after a relatively minor ankle sprain with disproportionate pain, severe swelling, and skin color changes that are out of proportion to the initial injury. Pain quality is burning and persistent. Complex regional pain syndrome must be considered.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Even light touch to the affected foot triggers severe burning pain (allodynia). Weight-bearing remains impossible despite adequate ligamentous healing. The patient avoids any movement or contact with the foot due to pain severity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The ankle sprain occurred from a simple inversion injury during walking with initial pain that seemed typical. Over subsequent weeks, pain has progressively worsened despite appropriate ankle rehabilitation. The patient reports depression and anxiety due to pain severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Examination reveals bright red, warm, edematous ankle and foot with shiny skin appearance. Sudomotor changes are evident with increased sweating and skin moisture. Vasomotor changes include color variation from red to purple depending on dependency. Nails appear brittle. Pain assessment with gentle touch demonstrates allodynia. Strength testing is limited by pain, not by motor deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs of the ankle show spotty osteoporosis (patchy bone density loss) consistent with reflex sympathetic dystrophy changes. Three-phase bone scan demonstrates increased uptake in delayed phase (blood pooling phase), which is characteristic of CRPS. MRI shows edema in soft tissues and bone. Budapest criteria are met based on clinical findings and imaging.

puzzles.explanation: CRPS presents as classic Type 1 (no confirmed nerve injury) with severe pain, edema, autonomic changes, and motor dysfunction following minor trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old with hypertension and atrial fibrillation presents with acute onset of severe headache, dizziness, vomiting, and gait imbalance. The patient had a sudden fall and is unable to ambulate. Posterior circulation stroke must be rapidly assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Ambulation is impossible due to severe ataxia and vertigo. Sitting upright causes increased dizziness and nausea. Any attempt at coordinated movement results in incoordination and dysdiadochokinesia (inability to perform rapid alternating movements). Self-care activities cannot be performed.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient woke this morning with symptoms without preceding warning signs. Risk factors include hypertension (resistant to medication), atrial fibrillation (not anticoagulated), hyperlipidemia, and smoking history of 40 pack-years. No prior stroke history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The patient demonstrates severe gait ataxia with wide-based, unsteady walk. Nystagmus is evident. Finger-to-nose testing shows dysmetria on both sides. Rapid alternating movements are incoordinate (dysdiadochokinesia). Speech is scanning (cerebellar dysarthria). Mental status is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging shows acute infarction in the cerebellar hemisphere. Vessel occlusion in the superior cerebellar artery or posterior inferior cerebellar artery is identified on CT/MR angiography. CT excludes hemorrhage. Imaging identifies the acute ischemic stroke requiring urgent intervention.

puzzles.explanation: Acute cerebellar stroke with significant edema causing deteriorating consciousness and risk of herniation requiring surgical decompression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old tennis player reports sudden severe calf pain during intense play (explosive plantarflexion during serve). Sharp, tearing sensation was felt, followed by immediate inability to continue playing. Acute myotendinous injury is likely.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Plantarflexion strength is significantly weakened with pain preventing full effort testing. Walking on tiptoe is impossible. The patient stands with the foot in slight plantarflexion, splinting the calf muscle. Stair climbing is severely limited. Running is completely prevented.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during an explosive tennis movement (pushing off during serve). The patient had not warmed up properly before the match. No previous significant calf injuries. Age 52 with sedentary lifestyle between matches.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant calf swelling is immediately visible, with bruising developing within hours and advancing distally toward the ankle. Plantarflexion strength is 0-1/5 (severe weakness). The Thompson test is negative (calf squeeze does not produce plantarflexion, suggesting gastrocnemius-soleus tear rather than Achilles rupture). Calf tenderness is marked in the midsubstance region.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic disruption in the gastrocnemius or soleus muscle with surrounding edema and hemorrhage (grade 2-3 muscle strain). The musculotendinous junction is intact. Achilles tendon is continuous. Imaging grade guides management (conservative for grade 1-2, possibly surgical for grade 3).

puzzles.explanation: Acute calf muscle strains result from sudden forceful plantarflexion or push-off activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 5-year-old boy is referred because he is falling frequently and has difficulty climbing stairs and running compared to age-matched peers. The mother notes progressive decline in function over the past year. X-linked muscular dystrophy (Duchenne type) must be suspected.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child cannot rise from the floor without using his hands to climb up his legs (Gowers sign), which is pathognomonic. Running is clumsy with toe-walking during running. Climbing stairs requires using the bannister. Jumping is not achieved. Generalized weakness is evident.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient is the only affected male in the family; however, maternal uncles are reported to have had similar childhood-onset weakness. Motor milestones were normal, but current function is declining. No significant trauma or falls occurred triggering the decline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant proximal muscle weakness is evident (hip and shoulder girdle strength 2-3/5). The calves appear enlarged (pseudohypertrophy) despite weakness. The child walks with a waddling gait. Lumbar lordosis is exaggerated. Ankle plantarflexion strength is relatively preserved (5/5), which contrasts with hip weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serum creatine kinase level is markedly elevated (>1000 IU/L, often >10,000) indicating muscle damage. EMG shows myopathic pattern with brief, small polyphasic motor units. Genetic testing confirms DMD mutation (dystrophin gene). Muscle biopsy shows absence of dystrophin protein on immunostaining. Testing confirms Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

puzzles.explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy with classic presentation in young boy, marked CK elevation, and confirmed dystrophin deficiency on genetic and histochemical testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old presents 6 weeks after a motor vehicle accident with ongoing headache, difficulty concentrating, and dizziness. A concussion was diagnosed acutely after loss of consciousness. Persistent post-concussion symptoms are limiting return to work. Diagnostic criteria must be confirmed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Return-to-work attempts result in increased headache and cognitive fog, limiting work tolerance to 4 hours daily. Balance difficulties persist with unsteadiness when walking on uneven surfaces. Memory for recent events is impaired. Visual tracking during reading causes headache.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The accident involved moderate-speed motor vehicle collision with loss of consciousness for 15-20 seconds. No focal neurological injury was identified acutely. The patient is slowly returning to activities but symptoms persist weeks beyond expected recovery. Previous concussion history is negative.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cognitive testing shows mild slowing on reaction time tasks and impaired working memory. Oculomotor testing (smooth pursuit) shows minor asymmetry. Balance testing (tandem stance) is mildly abnormal. Vestibulo-ocular reflex testing may show minor impairment. Headache provocation testing reproduces pain with cognitive exertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI is structurally normal with no focal lesions, hemorrhage, or contusion. Diffusion tensor imaging may show subtle white matter microstructural changes but is not routinely indicated. The imaging is reassuring, and diagnosis is clinical using the SCAT5 (Sport Concussion Assessment Tool). Imaging rules out structural complications.

puzzles.explanation: Post-concussion syndrome requires multidisciplinary approach including vestibular rehabilitation, cervical rehabilitation, cognitive rehabilitation, gradual return-to-activity progression, mood screening and management, and monitoring for persistent post-concussive syndrome.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old presents with 3 weeks of intermittent vertigo, head movement intolerance, and oscillopsia (visual blur with head movement) following a viral upper respiratory infection. The patient tilts the head toward the unaffected side to reduce symptoms. Peripheral vestibular dysfunction is likely.

clues.label: Activity
