clues.text: Rapid head movements trigger vertigo and nausea. Visual tracking while walking is problematic with the environment appearing to move. Stairs are difficult to navigate safely. The patient avoids head movements and prefers to keep vision fixed on stable objects.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute vertigo onset occurred 3 weeks prior following a viral URI with vertigo lasting several days. Residual dizziness and head movement intolerance persist. No hearing loss is reported (unlike labyrinthitis). No recent trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive head impulse test (inability to maintain gaze during rapid head rotation toward the affected side, with the eye drifting with the head). Nystagmus may be present, beating away from the affected side. Romberg test shows mild imbalance. Weber test is normal (no hearing loss as in labyrinthitis). Dix-Hallpike test is negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain and internal auditory canal is normal, ruling out retrocochlear pathology. Videonystagmography (VNG) shows impaired vestibulo-ocular reflex gain on the affected side. Caloric testing shows reduced response on the affected side (unilateral hypofunction). Imaging rules out stroke or tumor.

puzzles.explanation: Unilateral Vestibular Hypofunction/Labyrinthitis shows acute viral labyrinthitis with combined vestibular AND cochlear involvement (hearing loss), differentiating it from pure vestibular neuritis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old cyclist presents with progressive weakness and numbness in the hand after a 40-mile cycling event. Numbness involves the ring and little fingers with associated weakness of finger abduction and adduction. Ulnar nerve compression from cycling must be considered.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Gripping handlebars is now painful and results in finger numbness. Fine motor activities (typing, writing) are impaired. The cyclist cannot perform the previously accomplished cycling without finger numbness developing within 30 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-distance cycling is the main recreational activity with recent increase in cycling distance (from 20 to 40+ miles per week). Handlebar type and positioning have not changed. No previous nerve problems reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Finger abduction and adduction strength are reduced (4/5) indicating intrinsic hand muscle weakness. Two-point discrimination is decreased in the ring and little fingers. Sensation to light touch is mildly reduced. The ulnar pulse is palpable at the wrist. No atrophy is evident yet (recent onset).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of ulnar nerve conduction across the wrist and palm, consistent with compression at the wrist level (Guyon canal region). EMG shows mild denervation activity in interossei muscles. Ultrasound may show compression of the ulnar nerve at the wrist from external pressure. Imaging confirms ulnar neuropathy from compression.

puzzles.explanation: Cyclist's palsy shows severe chronic ulnar nerve compression from repeated cycling trauma with muscle atrophy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old male presents with progressive urgency and urinary frequency of 6 months duration. He reports an urgent need to void 10-12 times per day and 4-5 times nightly, with a sensation of incomplete emptying persisting after each void.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient has markedly limited his fluid intake in an attempt to reduce symptoms, but urgency persists even with minimal hydration. He avoids outdoor activities and travel due to frequent bathroom needs. Nighttime symptoms significantly disrupt sleep quality and daytime energy levels.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset was gradual without preceding infection or trauma. The patient has a history of benign prostatic hyperplasia managed medically for 5 years. Recent medication changes included initiation of a diuretic for hypertension management. No family history of urological disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, abdomen is soft and non-tender without palpable masses. Digital rectal examination reveals a mildly enlarged prostate without nodularity. Neurological examination of lower extremities is intact. Pelvic floor muscles demonstrate normal strength on voluntary contraction assessment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-void residual measured by bladder ultrasound is 60 mL, indicating mild retention. Renal ultrasound shows normal parenchyma without hydronephrosis. Urinalysis is normal with negative culture. Prostate-specific antigen level is mildly elevated at 3.5 ng/mL but unchanged from prior measurement.

puzzles.explanation: Overactive Bladder shows idiopathic detrusor overactivity in postmenopausal female with predominant urgency and frequency without stress component.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents with chronic burning pain in the sole of the foot and medial ankle that is worse with activity and at night. Ankle swelling is noted. Tarsal tunnel compression must be excluded. Symptoms are medial and plantar-distributed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Standing and walking aggravate the burning sensation in the foot. Tight shoes worsen symptoms. Sleeping position changes are frequent due to nighttime foot pain. Running is not attempted due to symptom aggravation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a history of ankle inversion sprains and currently has mild ankle instability. Mild varicose veins are present. No significant trauma precipitated current symptoms, which developed insidiously over months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's sign at the posterior tibial nerve (behind medial malleolus) reproduces tingling into the sole of the foot. Pain is localized to the medial ankle and sole of foot (not lateral ankle as in peroneal neuropathy). Toe sensation is reduced, particularly medial toes (plantar surface).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of the posterior tibial nerve conduction across the ankle region consistent with compression at the tarsal tunnel. EMG shows denervation changes in abductor hallucis muscle. MRI may show space-occupying lesion within the tarsal tunnel or thickening of the flexor retinaculum. Imaging identifies the compression cause and location.

puzzles.explanation: Tarsal tunnel syndrome results from posterior tibial nerve compression within the tarsal tunnel causing radiating foot pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 14-year-old girl presents for screening after a health visitor noted uneven shoulders and hips during school health check. Postural asymmetry has been progressive over the past 2 years. Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis must be assessed for severity and progression risk.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports occasional mid-back pain with prolonged sitting or standing. Participation in sports has been unaffected so far. No functional limitation at present, though concerns about cosmetic appearance are high.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Menarche occurred 18 months ago, indicating rapid skeletal growth. The patient is tall for age (85th percentile). No significant family history of scoliosis is reported. General health is good.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Postural assessment reveals right shoulder higher than left and right hip higher than left. Adam's forward bend test shows rib hump on the right side, indicating structural scoliosis. Truncal asymmetry is evident. Neurological examination is normal with no deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Posteroanterior standing spine radiographs show right thoracic curve measuring 28 degrees (Cobb angle measurement). No significant structural abnormalities or vertebral anomalies are noted. Skeletal maturity assessment using Risser sign is 2/5, indicating significant remaining growth. MRI is not indicated given the benign clinical presentation and normal neurological exam.

puzzles.explanation: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis detected on screening, with Cobb angle >25 degrees and significant growth remaining (Risser 1), making this a curve requiring close monitoring or bracing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 36-year-old presents with bilateral frontal and temporal headache described as pressing or tightening quality. Headaches occur most days and have increased in frequency over the past 6 months. The pain is mild to moderate in intensity. Chronic tension-type headache must be distinguished from other headache disorders.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Stress, fatigue, and poor sleep worsen headaches. Prolonged computer work triggers headache onset. Physical activity and rest provide partial relief. Medication is needed 3-4 times weekly to manage pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works in a high-stress job with poor work-life balance. Sleep quality is poor with inconsistent sleep schedule. The patient drinks 4-5 cups of coffee daily and reports high caffeine intake. No migraine aura is reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness of bilateral upper trapezius and cervical paraspinal muscles is palpable. Neck range of motion is mildly limited in all directions with muscle tightness. Neurological examination is normal. No vision changes, sensory deficits, or focal neurological signs are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain imaging (CT or MRI) is typically normal and not routinely indicated unless red flags are present. Imaging is withheld in this case given the clear clinical presentation of chronic tension-type headache without alarming features. Clinical diagnosis is based on symptom pattern and examination findings.

puzzles.explanation: Tension-type headache presents with classic bilateral pressure-type pain, muscle tension, and stress association.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-month-old infant presents with delayed diagnosis of hip dysplasia detected on routine screening ultrasound. No prior interventions. Parents report delayed crawling milestones.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with hip mobility noted. Asymmetric positioning when lying. One leg appears shorter. Asymmetric creases noted by parents.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Delayed diagnosis with child now 6 months old. No prior ultrasound screening. Delayed developmental milestones in crawling. Positive family history. Female infant.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Barlow maneuver positive with dislocation. Ortolani sign positive with reduction clunk. Hip abduction significantly limited bilaterally. Asymmetric skin folds marked.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows severe dysplasia bilaterally with subluxation. Femoral heads poorly covered. Graf classification shows severe dysplasia requiring intervention. Delayed diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Subclinical DDH detected on screening in at-risk infants may not show clinical signs but requires monitoring to prevent progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old develops progressive buttock pain and radiating pain down the posterior leg (pseudoradiculopathy) after prolonged sitting. Pain mimics sciatic nerve radiculopathy but without low back pain. Piriformis muscle compression of the sciatic nerve must be differentiated from true radiculopathy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged sitting exacerbates pain significantly, particularly sitting with hip internal rotation. Activities requiring hip flexion with external rotation (like getting in/out of a car) reproduce symptoms. Running is mildly limited. Sleeping position changes help reduce symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works at a desk with long sitting periods of 4-6 hours daily. No low back injury or trauma occurred. Previous similar episodes occurred but resolved with activity modification. The patient attributes pain to long work hours.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is elicited directly over the piriformis muscle (deeper palpation of the gluteus maximus). Flexion-adduction-internal rotation (FAIR) test reproduces buttock and leg pain. Straight leg raise test is negative (unlike true sciatica). Hip internal rotation is restricted. Sensation and reflex testing are normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine imaging (CT or MRI) is normal with no disc herniation or nerve root compression, helping to rule out true radiculopathy. Diagnostic ultrasound or MRI may show piriformis muscle enlargement or trigger points. Imaging rules out lumbar pathology and supports piriformis syndrome diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Piriformis syndrome causes neuropathic symptoms mimicking radiculopathy but stems from muscle tightness around the sciatic nerve.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports bilateral anterior shin pain that occurs predictably during running activity. Pain develops after consistent running distance and resolves completely with rest. The condition recurs with return to running at similar intensity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducible and occurs during running or intense lower leg activity. The pain is bilateral and symmetric in the anterior tibial compartments. Complete pain relief occurs within 15-30 minutes of ceasing activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset correlates with increased running volume or intensity. The athlete may have history of shin splints or tibial stress. Repetitive high-impact activities such as running on hard surfaces exacerbate symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pre-exercise examination may be normal with no resting compartment tenderness. Post-exercise examination reveals tight and tender anterior compartments. Compartment pressure measurement at rest is normal but elevated post-exercise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Compartment pressure measurement obtained immediately post-exercise (within 1-5 minutes) demonstrates elevated pressures in anterior compartment. Post-exercise pressure exceeds 30 mmHg with slow return to normal. Serial measurements during standardized exercise protocol establish diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic exertional compartment syndrome results from excessive fascia tension limiting muscle expansion during exercise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old office worker presents with acute onset severe shoulder pain yesterday during routine desk work. Sharp anterior shoulder pain unrelated to specific trauma. Pain awakens patient at night with inability to rest arm comfortably.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with all shoulder movements, particularly abduction and internal rotation. Pain exacerbated by lying on affected shoulder or reaching across body. Limited active range of motion due to pain (guarding). Discomfort described as sharp and well-localized anteriorly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset without preceding injury or repetitive activities. Office worker without strenuous overhead activities. Pain intensity disproportionate to functional demands suggesting inflammatory process. Sudden presentation typical for calcific tendinitis acute phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe tenderness over supraspinatus tendon insertion. Passive range of motion better preserved than active motion. Supraspinatus strength testing limited by pain rather than true weakness. Dugas test negative (shoulder can still adduct). Hawkins test markedly positive reproducing sharp pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-ray (AP and scapular Y views) demonstrates dense calcium deposit within supraspinatus tendon measuring 8x6mm. Calcium appears radiodense with well-defined borders. Associated soft tissue swelling visible around deposit. MRI shows T2 hypointense deposit with surrounding fluid signal indicating active inflammation and resorption phase.

puzzles.explanation: Acute calcific tendinitis with severe pain from crystal-induced inflammation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with sensation of bulging or heaviness in the perineum that worsens throughout the day and with standing. She reports discomfort during sexual activity and sensation of something descending into the vagina. Pelvic floor dysfunction from aging and multiparity is likely.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Heaviness sensation worsens with prolonged standing, lifting, or straining. Walking longer distances triggers worsening symptoms. Symptoms improve significantly when lying down. Exercise and physical activity are limited by perineal discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient is gravida 4 para 4 (four pregnancies, four vaginal deliveries). Menopause occurred 8 years ago without hormone replacement therapy. Chronic straining from constipation has been a lifelong issue. Previous pelvic surgery has not been performed.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visual inspection at rest shows the vaginal tissues bulging slightly. Valsalva maneuver exacerbates the bulge significantly with visible prolapse of the vaginal vault. Palpation confirms laxity of the pelvic floor support structures. Pelvic floor contraction strength is reduced (2/5).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound or MRI with Valsalva may show descent of pelvic organs below the pelvic floor. Cystourethrography or defecography may be performed for detailed assessment if surgical intervention is contemplated. Imaging confirms the degree and type of prolapse (cystocele, rectocele, apical prolapse).

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic organ prolapse with classic risk factors of multiparity, menopause, chronic straining, and previous hysterectomy, managed initially with pelvic floor muscle training.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 74-year-old postmenopausal woman with osteoporosis presents with acute-onset mid-back pain following a minor fall from standing height (stepping off a curb). Pain is localized to the thoracic spine. Vertebral compression fracture must be ruled out urgently.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot walk upright due to pain and assumes a flexed posture. Trunk extension is severely limited and painful. The patient is unable to perform self-care activities without assistance. Bed rest provides only partial pain relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has known osteoporosis (T-score -3.2) diagnosed 5 years ago on bone density screening but has poor medication adherence. The patient is a long-time smoker and has low body weight (BMI 19). No previous fractures have occurred.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is elicited over the mid-thoracic spine at the vertebral level. Trunk extension is severely limited. Forward flexion is maintained to some degree but increases pain. Neurological examination is normal with no myelopathic signs. Abdomen is soft and non-tender.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain thoracic spine radiographs show acute vertebral compression fracture with anterior wedging and loss of height. No retropulsion into the spinal canal is present (unlike burst fracture). MRI shows the fracture with bone marrow edema (STIR signal increase) consistent with acute fracture. Imaging confirms acute compression fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture with minimal trauma, multiple risk factors (postmenopausal, low BMI, corticosteroids, prior fragility fracture), and height loss — a classic geriatric presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old presents with progressive weakness and wasting of the feet and lower legs for the past 5 years. The patient reports difficulty with walking and running. High-arched feet and hammer toes are noted. Hereditary motor-sensory neuropathy must be assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running is impossible due to foot weakness and inversion instability. Walking long distances is fatiguing. Fine motor control of the feet is impaired. Ankle instability with frequent inversion sprains is problematic.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The father has similar symptoms and was diagnosed with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A. The patient's symptoms began in late adolescence with gradual progression. No trauma or toxin exposure occurred. Hyperlipidemia and diabetes are not present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lower leg and foot muscle atrophy is prominent with 'stork leg' appearance (wasting of calf muscles). Strength testing shows weakness of ankle dorsiflexors (2/5) and plantarflexors (3/5). Intrinsic foot muscle atrophy and weakness result in hammer toe deformities. Sensation to vibration and position sense is mildly reduced distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slow conduction velocity (demyelinating pattern) in all nerves tested, consistent with hereditary motor-sensory neuropathy. EMG shows denervation patterns in distal muscles. Genetic testing confirms PMP22 gene duplication (CMT1A). DNA sequencing identifies the specific mutation confirming diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Type 2 CMT with axonal features, predominant upper extremity involvement, and genetic confirmation of demyelinating protein gene mutation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old presents 10 weeks post-operatively from wrist fracture fixation with severe hand pain, swelling, and color changes that seem disproportionate to the surgical trauma. Pain is burning in quality and triggered by minimal stimuli. Complex regional pain syndrome type I is suspected.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Even the lightest touch to the hand produces severe pain (allodynia), making it impossible to use the hand functionally. The patient holds the hand immobilized in a protective posture. Attempting any movement triggers severe pain. Participation in physiotherapy is limited by pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: A Colles fracture was treated surgically with open reduction and internal fixation. Immediate post-operative period was unremarkable for the first 2-3 weeks. Subsequently, pain has progressively worsened rather than improved. The patient is anxious about hand recovery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The hand shows marked edema, warmth, and erythema (color changes from red to cyanotic). Sudomotor changes include increased sweating and skin hyperhidrosis. Vasomotor instability is evident with color variation. Skin appears shiny and trophic changes include thin, fragile appearance. Allodynia is prominent with severe pain to light touch.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Three-phase bone scan demonstrates increased uptake in delayed phase (blood pooling phase) consistent with CRPS. Radiographs show juxta-articular osteoporosis ('spotty' pattern). MRI shows edema of hand soft tissues and bones. Budapest diagnostic criteria are met. Imaging supports CRPS diagnosis and distinguishes from other post-surgical complications.

puzzles.explanation: CRPS (Type 2) follows documented peripheral nerve injury with combined neuropathic features and dystrophic changes typical of chronic CRPS.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old overhead athlete (baseball pitcher) presents with episodic shoulder apprehension during throwing, particularly during late cocking phase. The patient reports fear of dislocation. Anterior shoulder instability is the primary concern in this demographic.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Throwing is limited by apprehension when the shoulder reaches 90 degrees abduction and external rotation position. The patient modifies throwing mechanics to reduce apprehension. Reaching behind the head with arm abducted reproduces apprehension. Sleeping on the affected shoulder is avoided.

clues.label: History
clues.text: A previous anterior shoulder dislocation occurred 2 years ago while pitching, which was reduced in the emergency department. Physical therapy was performed but return to competitive pitching resulted in symptom recurrence. The patient continues to play despite apprehension.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive apprehension test (supine, arm 90 degrees abduction and external rotation) reproduces patient fear of dislocation. Positive relocation test (posterior pressure on humeral head) relieves apprehension, confirming anterior instability. Negative anterior drawer test suggests intact capsule. Range of motion may show excessive external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows possible Bankart lesion (anteroinferior glenoid labrum detachment) or reverse Hill-Sachs lesion. 3D CT reconstruction may show glenoid bone loss if engaging Hill-Sachs lesion is present. Imaging identifies labral pathology and glenoid anatomy, guiding management (conservative versus surgical).

puzzles.explanation: Posterior shoulder instability in hypermobile patient, less common but important presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old with known cerebral palsy presents with progressive movement coordination difficulty affecting walking and fine motor tasks. Intention tremor is noted when reaching for objects. Ataxic subtype with cerebellar involvement is evident.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking is unsteady with a wide-based gait. Running is impossible. Fine motor tasks (writing, eating utensil use, building with blocks) are significantly impaired. The child cannot jump or skip with typical age-appropriate coordination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Birth history included prematurity (34 weeks) with intraventricular hemorrhage grade 2. Developmental milestones were delayed with head control achieved at 4 months (normal 3-4 months). The diagnosis of cerebral palsy was made at 18 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pronounced intention tremor is evident when the child reaches for objects with the arms, worsening as the hand approaches the target (cerebellar dysmetria). Wide-based gait with poor balance and trunk control is observed. Muscle tone is low-normal (hypotonia). Reflexes are normal. No spasticity is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI shows cerebellar hypoplasia with reduced cerebellar volume and thin cerebellar folia. The cerebral cortex appears structurally normal. White matter appears appropriately myelinated. The imaging pattern is consistent with cerebellar hypodevelopment from perinatal injury.

puzzles.explanation: Ataxic cerebral palsy involves cerebellar dysfunction causing poor coordination, balance, and fine motor control from brain injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old presents 6 weeks post-operative from lumbar laminectomy for spinal stenosis and nerve root compression with persistent leg pain and weakness. Expected post-operative symptom improvement has not occurred. Post-laminectomy syndrome must be assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking tolerance remains limited to 15 minutes despite expected improvement to 30+ minutes by this point in recovery. Standing from sitting is difficult. Running is not attempted. The patient remains on significant opioid pain medication.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe lumbar stenosis at L3-L4 with left leg radiculopathy was treated surgically with left L4 laminectomy and L4-L5 decompression. Pre-operative imaging clearly showed severe stenosis and neural foraminal compression. The patient had significant pre-operative leg pain (7/10).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left leg pain remains 6/10, significantly improved from pre-operative 8/10 but not the anticipated complete resolution. Left ankle dorsiflexion strength remains 3+/5 (unchanged from pre-operative). Pain with forward bending is improved. Straight leg raise test shows continued limitation. No cauda equina signs are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative MRI shows adequate decompression with relief of stenosis and neural foraminal opening at L4-L5. No hematoma, recurrent stenosis, or missed pathology at other levels is identified. Imaging demonstrates successful decompression; persistent symptoms may suggest post-laminectomy syndrome or inadequate decompression.

puzzles.explanation: Post-laminectomy rehabilitation emphasizes stability training, progressive loading in extension and neutral, and activity modification to prevent hypermobility while managing residual neuropathic symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old man presents with progressive morning stiffness in lumbar spine lasting 2 hours daily for 8 months. Pain improves with activity and worsens with rest. Nocturnal pain awakening him from sleep.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Spinal stiffness particularly in morning and with inactivity. Buttock and lower back pain described as deep ache. Pain improves with activity and exercise. Progressive loss of spinal mobility with difficulty bending forward. Associated fatigue and malaise.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young male age 28 (typical for AS presentation). Insidious onset with progressive spinal involvement. Morning stiffness >1 hour typical of inflammatory pattern. No significant trauma or inciting event. Family history negative but HLA-B27 status unknown. Improved with activity suggesting inflammatory process.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Markedly limited lumbar forward flexion (Schober test: 2cm movement versus normal 5cm indicating rigidity). Reduced cervical spine motion with limited neck extension. Spinal extension limited. Reduced chest wall expansion (3cm versus normal 7cm) from costovertebral involvement. Straight leg raise normal. Neurologically intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI sacroiliac joints shows bilateral sacroiliitis with erosions and inflammatory signal changes. Lumbar spine shows syndesmophyte formation connecting vertebral bodies (bamboo spine early phase). HLA-B27 positive. ESR elevated at 48 mm/hr, CRP 9.2 mg/dL. Complete blood count and metabolic panel normal.

puzzles.explanation: Ankylosing Spondylitis presents as long-standing progressive axial disease with systemic features and structural changes visible on imaging, typical in a younger male.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old overhead athlete (volleyball player) presents with 3 months of deep posterior shoulder pain and progressive shoulder weakness affecting external rotation and abduction. No prior shoulder trauma occurred. Suprascapular nerve compression must be considered.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead activities are now impossible due to pain and weakness. Serving in volleyball resulted in the symptom onset after a particularly intense training session. Reaching overhead is limited. The athlete reports fear of further shoulder damage.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The athlete engages in repetitive overhead activities 5 days per week. High-intensity training with rapid shoulder movements is routine. A previous history of shoulder problems is denied. Backpacking with heavy loads is also performed regularly.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Deep posterior shoulder ache is elicited with palpation over the suprascapular notch. External rotation at 0 degrees abduction is significantly weak (2/5) as is abduction strength (3/5). Supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscle atrophy is evident on visual inspection and measurement. The patient demonstrates wasting of the supraspinous and infraspinous fossae.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows denervation activity in both supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles (indicating suprascapular nerve involvement). Nerve conduction velocity measurements across the suprascapular notch may show conduction slowing. MRI may show compression at the suprascapular notch from ligamentous or bony structures. Imaging and EMG confirm suprascapular nerve compression.

puzzles.explanation: Suprascapular neuropathy from spinoglenoid notch compression in overhead athlete.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old presents 8 weeks post-operatively from open reduction and internal fixation of a bimalleolar ankle fracture (lateral fibula and medial tibia fracture-dislocation). Ankle swelling persists and motion remains significantly restricted. Post-operative complications must be ruled out.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Ambulation without crutches remains limited and causes significant pain. Ankle plantarflexion and dorsiflexion are restricted. Inversion-eversion movements are guarded. Weight-bearing progression has been slower than expected (still on crutches at 8 weeks).

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism (motor vehicle accident) resulted in ankle fracture-dislocation treated urgently in the operating room. Significant soft tissue injury accompanied the fracture. Post-operative protocol included 6 weeks non-weight-bearing followed by progressive weight-bearing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant ankle swelling persists with pitting edema extending into the foot. Ankle dorsiflexion is limited to 5 degrees (normal 15 degrees). Plantarflexion is limited to 25 degrees (normal 50 degrees). Medial and lateral malleoli are tender. Hardware prominence is palpable. No signs of infection (normal temperature, incision healed).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs show appropriate hardware placement with anatomical reduction of the ankle mortise. No articular step-off or subluxation is present. The syndesmotic reduction appears maintained. No hardware loosening or failure is evident. Swelling and motion limitation appear mechanical from post-operative stiffness.

puzzles.explanation: Post-ORIF ankle rehabilitation requires progressive weight-bearing advancement, edema management, restoration of dorsiflexion strength, and proprioceptive training to normalize gait and prevent chronic instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old presents with severe elbow pain and deformity following a fall on an outstretched arm with the elbow extended. The elbow is swollen and bruised. Supracondylar or intercondylar fracture must be immediately assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot perform any active elbow movement due to pain and swelling. Passive motion is guarded and resisted. The arm is splinted at the side. Self-care activities are impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: A fall from a step while descending stairs occurred with landing on the outstretched arm with elbow extension. Known osteoporosis is present. No significant prior elbow pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The elbow demonstrates obvious deformity with posterior displacement of the distal fragment (in extension) or anterior displacement (in flexion). Marked swelling is present with ecchymosis developing. Radial and ulnar pulses are palpable and normal. Sensation is intact distally. Motor function (finger movement) is maintained.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Elbow radiographs (anteroposterior, lateral, and oblique views) show the fracture pattern (supracondylar, intercondylar, or lateral condyle). Comminution is often present in elderly patients. CT may be obtained pre-operatively to plan fixation strategy. Imaging shows the fracture complexity and guides surgical versus conservative management.

puzzles.explanation: Supracondylar fracture in child from fall with critical neurovascular assessment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old presents with progressive right groin pain and pain with weight-bearing that has worsened over 3 years. Morning stiffness in the hip lasting 30 minutes is reported. Hip osteoarthritis with advancing degenerative changes is likely.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking tolerance has decreased from 45 minutes to 15 minutes due to hip pain. Stairs (particularly descending) provoke sharp groin pain. Putting on shoes and socks requires modification. Standing from a seated position causes pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No significant hip trauma or previous hip surgery. The patient has worked a labor-intensive job requiring prolonged standing for 40 years. Obesity is present with BMI 32. Morning pain is worst, improving with activity but worsening with prolonged exertion.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion, abduction, and internal rotation are limited. Thomas test is positive (hip flexion contracture present). Hip abduction weakness is noted (3+/5). Pain is elicited at end-range of hip motion. Palpation over the groin reproduces pain. Trendelenburg test is positive, indicating abductor weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip radiographs show joint space narrowing, osteophytes at the femoral head-neck junction and acetabular rim, and subchondral sclerosis of the femoral head and acetabular roof. Mild acetabular dysplasia may be noted. MRI confirms cartilage loss and bone marrow edema consistent with advanced osteoarthritis.

puzzles.explanation: Hip OA is characterized by progressive cartilage degradation leading to pain, stiffness, and functional limitation. Early morning pain and improvement with movement are classic features.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old athlete (American football) was struck directly to the anterior thigh during game play and presents with immediate pain, swelling, and severe functional loss. Quadriceps contusion with possible myositis ossificans risk is the diagnosis.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Knee flexion is severely limited (0-20 degrees) due to pain and swelling. Ambulation is extremely painful and limping is pronounced. Weight-bearing tolerance is minimal. Running is completely prevented.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The direct blow to the anterior thigh was from contact with another player during an aggressive play. No prior thigh injuries. The athlete is in excellent physical condition with no predisposing medical conditions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Massive quadriceps swelling is immediately visible and palpable over the anterior thigh. Ecchymosis (bruising) is developing and will become apparent over hours to days. The knee cannot flex due to pain and mechanical swelling restriction. Quadriceps strength cannot be reliably tested due to pain. Normal neurovascular status is confirmed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound may show muscle edema and hematoma within the quadriceps. Radiographs are normal acutely (heterotopic ossification develops over weeks). Follow-up radiographs at 2-4 weeks may show early heterotopic bone formation if myositis ossificans develops. Early imaging rules out fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Quadriceps contusion from direct trauma causes immediate swelling and functional limitation in athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old fell from a 10-foot ladder landing on both heels and presents with severe heel pain and inability to bear weight. Marked swelling is immediately visible. Intra-articular calcaneal fracture must be assessed urgently.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: No weight-bearing tolerance is present. Ambulation without crutches is impossible. Foot swelling is severe and rapidly progressive. The patient is in significant pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism (fall from height with landing on heels bilaterally). No prior foot trauma. No significant medical comorbidities. The patient is anxious about functional recovery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe heel swelling is evident bilaterally with ecchymosis developing over the heels and soles. Palpation of the calcaneus elicits severe pain. Neutral ankle alignment may be lost with varus deformity developing. Compartment syndrome signs (excessive pain with passive stretch, numbness, cyanosis) must be monitored for urgently.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Heel radiographs show fracture of the calcaneus, typically intra-articular with loss of the subtalar joint space. CT of the foot shows the fracture pattern, number of fragments, degree of comminution, and extent of articular involvement. Imaging determines if conservative or surgical treatment is appropriate based on fracture severity and displacement.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic calcaneal fracture effects include persistent pain, arthritis, and gait dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents 10 weeks post-operative from lumbar laminectomy and microdiscectomy for severe leg pain from disc herniation with persistent pain and recurrent leg symptoms. Expected symptom resolution has not occurred. Recurrent disc herniation or post-laminectomy syndrome is suspected.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Leg pain remains 7/10 (similar to pre-operative baseline of 8/10), limiting walking, sitting, and standing tolerance. The patient reports pain radiating to the foot. Physical activity is significantly restricted. The patient remains on high-dose medications.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Large left-sided L4-L5 disc herniation with nerve root compression was the surgical indication. Severe leg pain (sciatic distribution) was the primary complaint. The patient expected dramatic improvement after surgery as imaging clearly showed compression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left leg pain persists in the sciatic distribution with radiation to the foot. Left ankle dorsiflexion strength remains weak (3/5) with minimal improvement. Palpation over the surgical incision produces tenderness. Straight leg raise test remains limited on the affected side. Signs of recurrent nerve compression persist.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative MRI shows the laminectomy defect with adequate decompression. However, recurrent disc material at the same level (or different level) may be evident. Re-herniation occurs in 5-15% of cases. Imaging determines if revision surgery is indicated or if conservative pain management should continue.

puzzles.explanation: This presentation of post-laminectomy presents with iatrogenic instability from extensive decompression. Management requires intensive core stabilization and load management rather than progressive extension-based loading.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A worker reports upper extremity pain and dysfunction related to repetitive job tasks. Pain affects the shoulder, arm, or hand depending on workstation demands. Functional limitation restricts job performance and productivity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are exacerbated by the specific repetitive activity required by job demands. Sustained postures and forceful gripping increase pain. Rest breaks and activity modification provide symptom relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Work-related upper limb disorder develops from repetitive overuse in occupational settings. Prolonged computer use, assembly line work, or manual labor are common causes. Poor ergonomics and inadequate breaks contribute to development.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, regional pain and tenderness are found in muscles or tendons involved. Functional testing reveals pain with specific work-related movements. Range of motion may be limited depending on tissues involved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI demonstrates tendon thickening and inflammation at sites of repetitive strain. Imaging identifies specific tissue pathology. Nerve conduction studies rule out compression neuropathy if symptoms suggest neural involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Work-related upper limb disorder shows occupational carpal tunnel syndrome from repetitive computer work.

Benign Nocturnal Limb Pains of Childhood
